title: Straw Proposal - Storage Design and Modeling - Uplift, Default Energy Bids, and State-of-Charge Management - July 15, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is fundamentally restructuring how storage resources bid, recover costs, and manage state-of-charge — directly affecting pumped-storage revenue recognition, mitigation treatment, and bidding complexity; default energy bids and uplift are core mechanisms for use-limited resource cost recovery.
ai_summary: CAISO proposes a sequenced reform of storage market rules, starting with near-term enhancements to default energy bids and bidding structures, culminating in a draft final proposal by end of 2026 that integrates these changes with broader uplift redesign. The straw proposal recommends two approaches to replace the current static storage default energy bid: a time-of-day approach (ISO's preferred option, using scaled inputs from prior market processes and limited temporal differentiation) and a static approach; CAISO also proposes a 'greater-of' method for hybrid resource default bids based on the highest-cost component. The reframing section introduces a potential alternative bidding model where storage would submit a single bid reflecting the value of ending a period with more or less stored energy, replacing hourly bid-and-forecast submissions, intended to improve charging/discharging alignment and reduce operator complexity. Most exposed are pumped-storage operators currently relying on static default bids and hourly state-of-charge forecasting; the shift to time-of-day or reframed bidding will alter cost-recovery timing and mitigation exposure. Next steps include stakeholder feedback on this straw proposal, leading to draft final proposal by end of 2026.

title: Agenda - Interconnection Customer User Group - Jul 07, 2026

The modifications to deliverability assessment and GIA in-service-date determination methodology could alter how hydro and pumped-storage projects are studied and cost-allocated in the queue, affecting development feasibility and timing for both new interconnections and uprates at existing plants.

meeting_title: Comments due - BPM Proposed Revision Requests (PRR) 1669 - 1686
title: Agenda - Business Practice Manual Change Management - June 23, 2026
relevance_reason: Multiple PRRs directly affect storage revenue streams, obligations, and operational parameters: storage state-of-charge and ancillary-service constraint treatment (PRR 1678), outage reporting for storage resources (PRR 1677), extended day-ahead market pricing and parameters affecting storage dispatch (PRR 1670, 1672, 1669), and settlement/congestion revenue rules (PRR 1681) that impact all dispatchable and storage resources.
ai_summary: CAISO is reviewing 14 proposed revision requests (PRRs) to its business practice manuals in a meeting scheduled for June 23, 2026 (11:00 a.m. Pacific time). Key storage-affecting changes include: PRR 1678 clarifying how storage resources are subject to ancillary-service state-of-charge and envelope constraints (Initial Comment phase); PRR 1677 adding storage-related cause descriptions to outage reporting (Initial Comment); PRR 1669 (Emergency) introducing new day-ahead market enhancement parameters affecting all resources; PRR 1670 and 1672 modifying extended day-ahead market initial conditions and transition-period pricing; and PRR 1681 (Emergency) fixing congestion revenue allocation calculations affecting settlement. For pumped-storage and battery operators, PRR 1678 is most directly relevant as it establishes how their state-of-charge constraints will interact with ancillary-service procurement — a chronic pain point in CAISO dispatch. Comments are due after the June 23 meeting; multiple emergency PRRs suggest urgent fixes to pricing and settlement in the extended day-ahead and day-ahead markets that may affect storage profitability.

meeting_title: Comments due - BPM Proposed Revision Requests (PRR) 1669 - 1686
title: Presentation - Business Practice Manual Change Management - Jun 23, 2026
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Multiple PRRs directly affect storage (including PSH) revenue, obligations, and operational flexibility: PRR 1678 on state-of-charge constraints in ancillary-services procurement; PRR 1677 on outage reporting for non-generator resources; PRR 1679 on battery/storage telemetry point definitions; PRR 1671 on resource-adequacy accreditation rules; and EDAM transition pricing (PRRs 1670, 1672) affecting storage dispatch and participation models.
stakeholders: Dorothy Vance (CAISO); Licheng Jin (CAISO); Massih Ahmadi (CAISO); Melanie Bogen (CAISO); Michael Martin (CAISO); Priyanka Namburi (CAISO); Ryan Ayer (CAISO)
ai_summary: CAISO's June 23, 2026 BPM Change Management meeting presented 18 Proposed Revision Requests (PRRs 1669–1686) spanning storage and market design. Key hydro/storage items: PRR 1678 (now at Recommendation stage, next: ISO Final Decision) clarifies how state-of-charge constraints in Day Ahead ensure adequate SOC to cover energy, ancillary services, and capacity products, and in Real Time cover energy, ancillary services, and flexible ramping — directly governing storage's ability to stack AS revenue without depletion penalties. PRR 1679 (Initial stage, next: ISO Recommendation) updates battery/storage telemetry points (Instantaneous SOC, Maximum/Minimum Continuous Energy Limit) to add clarity on state-of-charge monitoring. PRR 1677 (Initial stage, next: ISO Recommendation) clarifies outage-reporting requirements for non-generator resources and adds storage-specific cause codes to the Outage Management BPM. PRR 1671 (Recommendation stage, next: ISO Final Decision) incorporates new default resource-adequacy rules affecting how hydro and storage are accredited. EDAM transition PRRs 1670 and 1672 (both Recommendation stage, effective May 1, 2026) govern HE1 self-schedule initial conditions and transition-period pricing for EDAM entities. Most PRRs have completed or are completing June 16, 2026 comment windows; final decisions are expected imminently.

title: Presentation - Price Formation Enhancements - Scarcity Pricing - Jul 14, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO's scarcity pricing proposal directly affects energy price formation during system stress, which is a primary revenue mechanism for hydro and pumped-storage resources that supply emergency flexibility and reserves.
ai_summary: CAISO's Revised Straw Proposal on scarcity pricing (Day 2 of Phase 2 Price Formation Enhancements, July 14, 2026) modifies two mechanisms: (1) load-shed pricing extends the scarcity signal into both real-time dispatch (RTD) and 15-minute forecasting markets (FMM) when a BAA enters EEA Level 3 emergency and operators shed firm load, treating curtailed load as unserved demand to prevent market prices from collapsing; and (2) armed-reserve pricing adds an opportunity-cost adder in RTD to reflect when reserve capacity is released into energy markets, capturing the scarcity value that the 15-minute ancillary-service procurement (RTPD) process already recognizes. Both mechanisms include operational safeguards: operators can pause either mechanism, and the load-shed signal turns off after four cumulative hours of load shedding in a 24-hour window. The proposal defers comprehensive scarcity redesign to a future phase. Feedback on grouping, CAISO BAA treatment, load-shedding implementation options (verified MW vs. forecast approach), and whether the mechanisms adequately preserve scarcity signals without over-mitigation is requested before the draft final proposal.

title: Presentation - Price Formation Enhancements - BAA-Level Market Power Mitigation - Jul 13, 2026
relevance_reason: BAA-level market power mitigation (MPM) redesign directly affects how hydro's market-power-limited capacity is tested and mitigated during scarcity — grouping, load-serving obligation subtraction, and pivotal-supplier filtering all reshape the offer floor and mitigation surface for dispatchable resources with geographically-dispersed generation or storage.
ai_summary: CAISO's Revised Straw Proposal reforms BAA-level MPM by grouping BAAs via Marginal Export Capacity and export-transfer connectivity (instead of testing each in isolation), subtracting interval-specific load-serving obligations from withholdable capacity, and mitigating only pivotal suppliers after a grouped BAA fails the 3PS test. CAISO BAA retains default-competitive status and will not be subject to the BAA-level test as a group — a deferent to concerns that economic intertie imports are undervalued by the current framework. The proposal advances grouping and LSO changes near-term but defers comprehensive scarcity pricing and CAISO BAA mitigation to a broader future phase, with Day 2 stakeholder feedback to focus on scarcity-pricing mechanics, implementation choices, and safeguards. For hydro operators, especially those with multi-BAA footprints or export capability (e.g., Northwest PSH and reservoir operators), the grouping and LSO logic could narrow the suppliers subject to mitigation during tight intervals, raising energy prices and ancillary-service value when genuinely pivotal capacity is constrained — but the deferral of scarcity pricing means no new scarcity-pricing adders are being adopted yet. Most exposed: large dispatchable generators and PSH with withholdable capacity in grouped BAAs; comment deadline not stated in document.

meeting_title: Comments due - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Market Performance Meeting
title: Presentation - Monthly Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) May Performance Report - Jun 25, 2026
relevance_reason: EDAM/DAME implementation affects hydro revenue through new flexibility products (imbalance reserves), ancillary-service pricing, GHG allocation/pricing, and regional price separation; May performance data shows how hydro (especially in PACW) is clearing in these markets and how transfer economics are reshaping dispatch.
ai_summary: CAISO's May 2026 EDAM performance report documents the first month of operations across the expanded day-ahead footprint (CAISO, PacifiCorp East, PacifiCorp West), including two new products: imbalance reserves (IRU/IRD) and reliability capacity (RCU/RCD). Hydro operators see direct implications: (1) imbalance reserve procurement in PACW was supplied primarily by transfers and hydro, while reliability capacity pricing trends lower than energy and varies sharply by area, reshaping the value of flexible capacity; (2) GHG allocation reached 256 MW peak and is now BAA-specific rather than system-wide, affecting export economics from non-California balancing areas; (3) ancillary-services cascading frequency changed (regulation-up cascading fell from 28.8% to 4.7%), with spin cascading rising to 85.6%, altering the stacking and pricing of traditional AS products hydro supplies. Energy prices showed regional separation (PACE lowest, PACW higher, CAISO highest), and transfer flows indicate active cross-BAA dispatch. This is preliminary first-month data; no member action deadlines are stated in the report itself.

committee: Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration
meeting_title: Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration
title: Draft Final Proposal - Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration Track 1 - Jul 08, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is expanding demand-response participation to allow behind-the-meter storage to export energy and be credited for it, which creates a new pathway for distributed storage (including small pumped storage) to participate in wholesale markets and shifts how DR baselines and settlement work — affecting how hydro-adjacent DER aggregations compete with conventional hydro for dispatch value and RA accreditation.
initiatives: Demand and distributed energy market integration [Active]
ai_summary: CAISO's Track 1 Draft Final Proposal for Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration permits individual customers within demand-response aggregations to export behind-the-meter battery and storage output to the grid (subject to utility approval), while limiting the aggregation as a whole to remain non-exporting. The proposal maintains DR's load-curtailment categorization and preserves existing deliverability frameworks, but introduces revised baseline and settlement methodologies to credit performance when BTM resources discharge. This expands the wholesale DR market to capture full capability of distributed storage (especially batteries and potentially small pumped storage), competing directly with conventional hydro's flexibility value in the ISO BAA; the framework applies to both Proxy Demand Response (PDR) and Reliability Demand Response Resources (RDRR). No vote date or next-step deadline is stated in the excerpt; the document is dated July 8, 2026.

committee: Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration
meeting_title: Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration
title: Presentation - Demand and Distributed Energy Market Integration - Jul 09, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is redefining how behind-the-meter storage and DER (including pumped storage) participate in demand response performance measurement, which affects how aggregated storage resources are compensated and metered — directly impacting settlement of storage-backed DR and hybrid participation models.
initiatives: Demand and distributed energy market integration [Active]
ai_summary: CAISO's DDEMI Track 1 proposal would allow demand response aggregations to include behind-the-meter exports from utility-authorized end-use customers (e.g., rooftop solar + battery, behind-the-meter pumped storage), while capping the aggregation's net export at the resource level rather than the individual-customer level. Currently, customers exporting more than they consume are 'zeroed out' individually, preventing aggregators from accessing the full export capability of behind-the-meter storage; the proposal shifts the floor from customer to aggregation level to reflect market topology. This affects how pumped-storage and battery-backed demand response resources are metered, settled, and compensated for their dual load-curtailment and export capability. Stakeholders have until July 16, 2026 to submit written comments; the proposal applies to both Proxy Demand Resource (PDR) and Reliability Demand Response Resource (RDRR) models across all BAAs.

title: Presentation - 2024 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance
relevance_reason: CAISO's 2024 market-performance report provides comprehensive data on hydro generation patterns, regional price formation, congestion outcomes, and ancillary-services procurement mix — all drivers of hydro revenue and dispatch value — plus recommendations affecting storage bid-cost recovery, default energy bids, and local market-power mitigation that extend to hybrid resources including hydropower.
stakeholders: Ryan Kurlinski (CAISO Department of Market Monitoring)
ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring presents 2024 annual performance data and forward recommendations. Pacific Northwest hydro generation increased in 2024 relative to 2023; Northwest prices rose mid-day during solar hours and followed net-load patterns, with transfer-congestion elevation noted (page 7, 11–12). Battery participation in ancillary services grew substantially (page 43), while overall flexible ramping product prices remained infrequent at system/BA level (page 42). Recommendations include extending local market-power mitigation to hybrid resources, enhancing default energy bids for batteries to reflect intraday opportunity costs (with standardized bids for WEIM batteries proposed), and revising bid-cost recovery rules — changes that would apply similarly to hydro and PSH if codified (pages 46–47). No specific vote or comment deadline stated in this presentation; this is a published annual report. Most exposure: pumped-storage operators subject to bid-cost recovery and default-energy-bid rules, and larger reservoir hydro in the Pacific Northwest tier where congestion pricing and seasonal price patterns drive revenue.

stakeholders: DMM (CAISO Department of Market Monitoring); Ryan Kurlinski (CAISO Department of Market Monitoring)
ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring Q1 2025 performance report shows hydro and batteries supplying the bulk of upward flexible ramping product (FRP) procurement, while solar and wind dominate downward FRP demand in the 15-minute market. Intermountain West and Pacific Northwest became dominant WEIM exporters during non-solar hours in Q1 2025, with PacEast and Idaho Power identified as main peak-hour exporters; transfer congestion into/out of those hydro-rich regions occurred in ~20% of 5-minute intervals, creating locational scarcity signals. Electricity prices in the West dropped 39% year-over-year but remain driven by south-to-north congestion and greenhouse-gas costs within California. The report is observational and carries no proposals or compliance deadlines, but provides a baseline on how hydro's FRP-market participation, export positioning, and regional pricing have shifted in the WEIM footprint — useful for understanding Q1 2025 hydro revenue dynamics and transmission-constrained export opportunity.

Analysts using this as a reference point would identify market conditions affecting hydro's locational revenue; no comment deadline or action item is stated.

stakeholders: Ryan Kurlinski (CAISO Department of Market Monitoring)
ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring reports Q2 2025 real-time market conditions: electricity prices in the West rose 12% versus Q2 2024, driven by south-to-north congestion and California greenhouse-gas costs; Northwest prices were higher during solar hours, California during non-solar hours—indicating hydro's value shifts with solar generation patterns. WEIM transfer congestion into or out of Pacific Northwest areas occurred over 20% of 5-minute intervals, and Mid-Columbia and Palo Verde bilateral prices converged with ISO market prices, suggesting arbitrage opportunities narrowed. Pacific Northwest coal output rose despite more outages, and transfers from the Intermountain West during non-solar hours declined Q-o-Q, signaling reduced availability or changed dispatch incentives. For hydro owners, these trends show tightening price signals in hydro-export corridors, persistent congestion reducing transmission access to California, and seasonal shifts in the regional generation stack that affect dispatch positioning and revenue timing.

ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring presents Q3 2025 market performance: Western region prices down 4% year-over-year; California dominated by south-to-north congestion and GHG costs; Northwest saw higher solar-hour prices while California peaked in non-solar hours; WEIM transfers flowed from CAISO mid-day and from Intermountain/Desert Southwest in non-solar hours, with assistance-energy opt-ins increasing transfers for 8 of 10 entities. Real-time three-pivotal-supplier test found 4.2% of WEIM 15-minute intervals uncompetitive; RTD mitigation errors added ~37 MW incremental dispatch. Hydro owners should track the pronounced shift in congestion direction (north-to-south peak in Q3 2024 vs. current south-to-north pattern) and the interregional transfer behavior, which directly affects locational scarcity pricing and arbitrage opportunities for reservoir and pumped-storage resources positioned to move energy across the Western interconnect.

The agenda covers EDAM governance evolution, transmission connectivity and coordination across market seams (WEIM/EDAM), subscriber participation models, and implementation status. For Western hydropower owners, EDAM is the successor architecture to the WEIM, reshaping how hydro-rich jurisdictions participate in interregional energy markets, access scarcity pricing, and coordinate dispatch across state lines. The meeting will address governance independence at FERC and stakeholder roles—key institutional questions for NHA members deciding market membership and participation strategy in the West.

meeting_title: Comments due - On-Peak Deliverability Assessment Methodology Refinements
title: Draft - On-Peak Deliverability Study Methodology - Jun 03, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO refines its on-peak deliverability assessment methodology — the mechanism by which generators (including hydro and storage) qualify for full or partial capacity credit toward resource adequacy; changes to Pmax assumptions, scenario hours, and dispatch modeling directly affect how much RA capacity hydro and pumped storage can claim and at what cost.
ai_summary: CAISO has refined its generator on-peak deliverability study methodology, effective January 2024 and documented here as of June 2026, to address bottleneck risk as the generation fleet diversifies and solar penetration shifts peak net load. The methodology now assesses two distinct scenarios: (1) highest system need (hours 19–22 in summer, peak sale with low solar), where non-intermittent generators' Pmax is set to highest summer Qualifying Capacity in the prior three years, and intermittent generators at 20% exceedance level; and (2) secondary system need (hours 15–18, peak consumption with high solar output), where Pmax for intermittent resources is 50% exceedance level and energy storage Pmax is 50% of 4-hour discharge capacity. For hybrid and energy-storage projects, Pmax is limited by requested maximum output. Hydro and pumped-storage owners face a direct revenue impact: the Pmax values used in deliverability studies directly determine how much capacity credit they can claim in RA markets and interconnection queues; storage capacity credit in secondary scenarios is capped at half the 4-hour discharge rating, potentially disadvantaging long-duration or seasonal storage relative to short-duration batteries.

meeting_title: Comments due - On-Peak Deliverability Assessment Methodology Refinements
title: Presentation - On-peak Deliverability Assessment Methodology Refinements - Jun 11, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is refining its on-peak deliverability assessment methodology—a transmission planning process that determines whether new generation (including hydro and storage) can physically deliver power to load, directly affecting the deliverability accreditation and interconnection pathway for proposed hydro and PSH projects.
ai_summary: CAISO proposes three refinements to its on-peak deliverability assessment methodology: (1) modeling proposed resources identified in transmission planning as needed for local reliability in the base case dispatch (rather than only when they relieve constraints), conditional on reasonable certainty of completion before deliverability is needed; (2) including existing demand response in the base case to avoid over-allocating deliverability; and (3) reducing the dispatch value of generators in the base case from 80% to 70% of maximum study amount to reflect the increased planning reserve margin (from ~15% to 25%). The refinements affect how new hydro and PSH projects are studied during interconnection—projects that would need to compete with modeled proposed resources to secure a deliverable position in queue. Comments are due June 25, 2026. Most exposed are developers of new hydro or PSH seeking interconnection in California who will compete for deliverability allocation against projects CAISO assumes will be built to meet identified local needs.

These are queue-administration items affecting project cost allocation and interconnection terms. The agenda does not specify status, next steps, or member positions; it is a notice of agenda topics to be discussed at the June 9, 2026 meeting. title: Presentation - Commitment Cost Bidding Flexibility - Working Group - Jun 18, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is designing a local market power mitigation test for commitment costs that directly affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources can bid their start-up costs — a use-limited resource revenue stream — by implementing phased caps (300% in competitive conditions, 110% in uncompetitive) and testing commitment decisions against a three-pivotal-supplier test with reference levels.
stakeholders: Daniel Johnson (CAISO); Sylvie Spewak (CAISO); Yelena Kopylov-Alford (CAISO)
ai_summary: CAISO's Commitment Cost Bidding Flexibility (CCBF) working group is developing a market power mitigation framework for commitment cost offers, replacing a static cap with a competitiveness-based test that identifies uncompetitive conditions using a three-pivotal-supplier test for commitment-specific constraints. The framework will mitigate commitment costs identified as exercising market power by assessing them against the resource's cost-based reference level, with phased caps of 300% in competitive conditions and 110% in uncompetitive conditions. Dispatchable hydro and pumped-storage operators face direct revenue exposure: inflated commitment-cost bids that currently clear under the static cap could be suppressed or capped when the test identifies the resource as pivotal to binding transmission constraints; conversely, owners with low reference levels could bid more competitively. Workshop 2 (this meeting, June 18, 2026) identifies policy choices on structural competitiveness testing and mitigation criteria; next steps and comment deadlines not stated in this presentation excerpt.

committee: Western Energy Imbalance Market (WEIM) Regional Issues Forum
title: Charter for WEIM and EDAM Governance
relevance_reason: A governance charter for WEIM and EDAM directly affects the market-seams choice, operational framework, and strategic participation options for NHA members across the West, especially regarding how hydro and storage resources will be governed, represented, and valued under any future EDAM expansion.
ai_summary: This document establishes or revises the governance charter for the Western Energy Imbalance Market (WEIM) and lays groundwork for the Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) operational structure. The charter defines stakeholder representation, decision-making authority, and institutional design for a multi-state market footprint that will govern energy pricing, resource accreditation, and participation rules for all generators including hydropower and pumped storage across California and adjacent regions. Hydro owners in WEIM-participating states (Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, Idaho) and those considering EDAM entry need to track this charter's provisions on voting power, cost allocation, rate design, and dispute resolution — particularly how use-limited resources like hydro and PSH will be treated in capacity and ancillary-services frameworks under the expanded governance structure. No explicit comment deadline or vote date is visible in the metadata provided.

stakeholders: Massih Ahmadi (CAISO); Melanie Bogen (CAISO); Michael Martin (CAISO); Monica Mouanetry (CAISO); Southern California Edison (Southern California Edison)
ai_summary: CAISO is advancing nine Business Practice Manual (BPM) revisions tied to Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) and EDAM implementation as of May 1, 2026. Key filings include PRR 1670 (new initial-condition treatment allowing Scheduling Coordinators to specify whether resources are expected online/offline in HE1 self-schedules), PRR 1672 (EDAM transition-period pricing for the first six months), PRR 1668–1669 (market-operations parameters and configurable settings), and PRR 1671 (incorporation of default resource-adequacy rules). Comments on PRR 1673–1674 (settlement incremental updates and high-priority variance fixes) are due June 16, 2026. These changes affect hydro and pumped-storage dispatch flexibility, settlement accuracy, and RA capacity accreditation under CAISO's new integrated framework; owners should monitor PRR 1671 (RA rules) for any shifts in seasonal or duration-specific accreditation treatment, and PRR 1670–1672 for impacts on multi-hour dispatch and startup/shutdown cost recovery.

title: Agenda - Market Surveillance Committee Meeting - Jun 16, 2025
relevance_reason: CAISO MSC will adopt an opinion on day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation (CRA), a mechanism that affects energy price formation and locational signals — core determinants of hydro revenue from energy sales and dispatch incentives.
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee will vote to adopt an MSC opinion on CAISO's extended day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation final proposal at its June 16, 2025 General Session (9:00–10:00 a.m. PT). Congestion revenue allocation is a component of price formation that determines how locational price differences flow to generators and load; changes to CRA rules can shift the scarcity signals and locational rents hydro facilities capture when they relieve transmission constraints. The agenda does not specify the substance of the proposal or MSC's recommendation, so the actual hydro impact cannot be assessed from this notice alone; hydro operators should obtain the underlying MSC opinion and CAISO proposal documents to evaluate whether allocation rule changes favor or penalize their geographic position.

title: Agenda - Market Surveillance Committee Meeting - Mar 06, 2026
relevance_reason: Day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation affects price formation and the distribution of scarcity rents that hydro supplies depend on for revenue; this is a scheduled discussion on an active CAISO mechanism affecting energy pricing.
ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee will hold a general session on March 6, 2026, with a substantive agenda item on extended day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation (CRA). The session runs 9:00 a.m.–12:00 p.m. Pacific Time. No details are provided in the agenda itself on the specific proposals or alternatives under discussion, but CRA design directly affects how scarcity rents from congestion are allocated to generators—a key revenue source for hydro at constrained nodes, particularly in the California system where many hydro plants face binding transmission limits. The document does not indicate a decision or voting outcome; it is notice of an advisory committee discussion whose details would appear in supporting materials not provided here.

title: Final Market Surveillance Committee Opinion - Extended Day-Ahead Market Congestion Revenue Allocation - Jun 16, 2025
relevance_reason: EDAM congestion revenue allocation rules directly affect how hydro operators recover transmission-congestion hedges and how parallel-flow costs from hydro schedules are reallocated across balancing authorities — a core revenue-protection mechanism for dispatchable and use-limited resources in the Western interregional market.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO (paid consultant)); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO (paid consultant)); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO (paid consultant))
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issued a final opinion (June 16, 2025) on EDAM's proposed congestion revenue allocation scheme for parallel flows, evaluating a Year 1 interim design that would reallocate congestion revenues from BAAs where congestion occurs to BAAs that sold firm point-to-point and NITS transmission rights when those holders self-schedule. The MSC has significant reservations about the Year 1 design because it creates perverse incentives to self-schedule instead of participating in economically efficient co-optimized dispatch, potentially distorting bidding and causing unintended cost shifts — a concern particularly acute for hydro and other dispatchable resources whose flexibility value depends on competitive economic dispatch. The opinion does not block the proposal but calls for detailed pre-EDAM-launch empirical analysis of dispatch distortion and market-price effects from self-scheduling incentives, and notes that Year 2 alternatives linked to flexibly-bid resources would worsen below-cost-offer incentives; the framework recommended for future exploration is financial flowgate rights rather than transaction-linked reallocation.

title: Final Market Surveillance Committee Opinion on Gas Resource Management - Dec 15, 2025
relevance_reason: CAISO's Gas Resource Management proposal affects how gas-fired resources' commitment costs and default energy bids are calculated — reference levels that are the canonical mitigation tool for use-limited resources including hydropower, and directly impact EDAM participation by Western resources including hydro operators in the WEIM/EDAM footprint.
stakeholders: Sylvie Spewak (CAISO); Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO)
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issued a final opinion (Dec. 12, 2025) supporting three of four enhancements in CAISO's Gas Resource Management proposal: improved reference-level change-request processes, expanded after-the-fact cost-recovery rules, and multi-day advisory dispatch forecasts to help gas resources anticipate EDAM schedules and procure fuel more efficiently ahead of morning gas nominations. The MSC supports these changes to address EDAM coordination challenges as EDAM expands westward. However, the MSC emphasizes a critical gap: the absence of any market-power test for commitment-cost mitigation means all gas units are indiscriminately mitigated in every dispatch interval, creating inefficient offer bounds that may favor units with actual market power while constraining flexible suppliers. The MSC calls development of a workable commitment-cost market-power test "long overdue and should be a high priority." This is material to hydro owners because reference-level design directly affects whether hydro (a canonical use-limited resource) can offer flexibility without artificial caps, and the expansion of EDAM brings Western hydro into closer coordination with gas-constrained EDAM pricing and scheduling — misaligned gas-cost accounting upstream could distort the scarcity signals and ancillary-service values hydro depends on.

title: MSC Final Opinion - Rules for Bidding Above the Soft Offer Cap - Price Formation Enhancements - May 2024
relevance_reason: CAISO proposes revisions to Order 831 soft-offer-cap rules for battery storage and hydropower, directly affecting how energy-limited resources price their opportunity costs and manage state of charge — a core revenue mechanism for hydro and especially pumped storage.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee of the California ISO); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee of the California ISO); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee of the California ISO)
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) issued a final opinion (May 15, 2024) supporting CAISO's proposed changes to Order 831 bidding rules for summer 2024, specifically allowing battery storage and hydropower to bid above the $1000/MWh soft cap by reflecting future-hour opportunity costs rather than only same-hour alternatives. The MSC found that current rules force premature discharge and systematic underestimation of stored energy value, creating inefficiencies that risk curtailments under tight supply conditions; the root problem is that Order 831's Maximum Import Bid Price (MIBP) mechanism was designed for thermal resources and mischaracterizes the temporal opportunity cost of energy-limited resources. The changes apply to both CAISO and Western EIM resources; the MSC also flagged that deeper reforms to local market power mitigation as applied to energy-limited resources and the real-time market's 75-minute offer-update lag remain unresolved and require future work, but the summer 2024 proposal is a necessary interim step.

title: MSC Final Opinion on Extended Day-Ahead Market
relevance_reason: EDAM's day-ahead market coordination across western balancing areas directly affects hydro's revenue from inter-BAA energy and reserve arbitrage, co-optimization of dispatch across large footprints, and participation incentives for flexible resources like pumped storage; the MSC opinion on RSE, GHG accounting, and transmission design shapes whether hydro's scarcity-pricing and flexibility value is preserved or diluted in the expanded market.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee); James Bushnell (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee); Scott M. Harvey (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issues a final opinion (January 27, 2023) endorsing the Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) as a beneficial expansion of RTO-style coordination into a day-ahead voluntary market across western BAAs, while flagging unresolved design ambiguities in resource-sufficiency evaluation (RSE), greenhouse-gas accounting, and inter-BAA transmission pricing that require detailed simulation before implementation. The MSC emphasizes that EDAM will pool scarcity pricing and reserve requirements across participants—meaning a shortage in one BAA will raise prices and impose reserve penalties across the entire footprint—and notes that design choices allowing bilateral contracts and demand-reduction programs to count toward RSE requirements, combined with pooled WEIM RSE treatment, could create perverse incentives for resource-insufficient BAAs to lean on the pool. For hydro and PSH operators, EDAM's inter-BAA optimization and removal of 'pancaking' transmission charges will increase dispatch flexibility and inter-regional arbitrage opportunities, but the MSC warns that unresolved details on export constraints, shortfall allocation (especially when multiple BAAs cannot meet their EDAM schedules simultaneously), and GHG export formulation could materially affect scarcity-pricing signals and the value of flexible ramping; the committee recommends flexibility in software design to allow parameter adjustments as EDAM begins operations with an initial small set of BAAs and expands.

title: Market Surveillance Committee Addendum to Final Opinion on Day-Ahead Market Enhancements
relevance_reason: CAISO's Imbalance Reserves cost-allocation mechanism affects how hydro and storage operators are charged for uncertainty-procurement costs and how those costs flow through nodal pricing and congestion settlement — touching energy revenue and price-formation outcomes.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee of CAISO); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee of CAISO); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee of CAISO)
ai_summary: The Market Surveillance Committee clarifies its May 2023 opinion on CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME), specifically correcting its description of how Imbalance Reserves (IR) costs will be allocated across Balancing Area Authorities. IR costs are now understood to be calculated per BAA based on nodal prices and cleared quantities of uncertainty, less payments for IR supplied at locations within the BAA — with net charges/payments aggregated across the EDAM and any resulting congestion rents allocated to transfer and transmission constraints in the same manner as energy-flow congestion. This nodal cost-allocation mirrors energy settlements, making the mechanism transparent and competitively neutral. For hydro and PSH operators, this clarification is material because it determines how uncertainty-procurement costs flow through the nodal price signal they face and how their own IR supplies (especially from PSH) are valued and credited at their location — affecting both the cost side of operations and the compensation for flexibility supply.

title: Market Surveillance Committee Draft Opinion - Gas Resource Management - Dec 12, 2025
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Gas-fired resource offer rules (reference levels, commitment cost caps, default energy bids) directly affect CAISO's mitigation framework for dispatchable generators; the proposal's expansion to EDAM participants and its implications for offer flexibility and cost recovery touch hydro's competitive position in energy pricing and dispatch, plus the proposal's stated concern about not enabling locational market power is foundational to how use-limited resources like hydro are treated in cost-based offer caps.
stakeholders: Sylvie Spewak (California ISO); Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee, California ISO); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee, California ISO); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee, California ISO)
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issued a draft opinion (Dec. 5, 2025) on the CAISO's Gas Resource Management (GRM) proposal, which addresses gas price volatility challenges for gas-fired generators participating in EDAM, including changes to reference-level calculation methods, after-the-fact cost recovery, and multi-day advisory market runs to improve fuel procurement forecasting. The MSC supports most enhancements as low-cost measures to help EDAM participants reflect true gas costs in offer prices, but flags a critical gap: the absence of any market-power test for commitment cost mitigation means all gas units are mitigated in every interval, creating inefficient caps for non-dominant units while potentially over-protecting dominant ones—a structural issue that directly parallels hydro's chronic mis-treatment under opportunity-cost-based mitigation rules designed for thermal units. The MSC identifies EDAM timing (day-ahead schedules posted after morning gas nominations close) as exacerbating these challenges across the expanded Western footprint, and recommends development of a workable market-power test for commitment costs as a high priority going forward. No vote or comment deadline is stated in this draft opinion.

title: Market Surveillance Committee Final Opinion - Storage Bid Cost Recovery - Nov 01, 2024
relevance_reason: CAISO proposes changes to real-time bid cost recovery (BCR) calculations for battery storage, directly affecting how storage resources recover losses and manage state-of-charge — a canonical storage-revenue mechanism that sets precedent for pumped-storage participation and cost allocation in real-time markets.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee, California Independent System Operator); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee, California Independent System Operator); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee, California Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) evaluates CAISO's revised proposal to modify real-time bid cost recovery for storage resources, targeting elimination of 'phantom losses' — BCR payments on losses that don't reflect actual economic harm — and improving incentives for storage operators to manage state-of-charge efficiently across the day. The MSC conditionally supports implementation of CAISO's draft final proposal as a near-term check on inflated BCR but notes that empirical data on phantom-loss materiality is incomplete; the MSC recommends immediate follow-up in Phase II to achieve deeper reforms and eliminate BCR on phantom losses before expanding storage bidding flexibility. Pumped-storage owners relying on cost-recovery mechanisms for charging losses and state-of-charge management should monitor whether CAISO's approach—which changes how discharge offers and charging bids factor into BCR calculations—narrows or preserves their ability to recover legitimate operating losses, and should engage if Phase II designs begin to collapse BCR scope more broadly.

title: Market Surveillance Committee Final Opinion on Day-Ahead Market Enhancements
relevance_reason: CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) proposal fundamentally restructures co-optimization of energy and flexibility products, creating new imbalance-reserve procurement rules and state-of-charge constraints on storage that directly affect how pumped-storage hydro earns revenue from flexibility supply and interact with residual unit commitment pricing.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee, CAISO)
ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) issued a final opinion on May 3, 2023, supporting the Day-Ahead Market Enhancements proposal, which introduces a new Imbalance Reserves (IR) co-optimized product to replace ad-hoc operator load-biasing, updates residual unit commitment into a Reliability Capacity (RC) process, and improves storage state-of-charge modeling in day-ahead and RUC runs. The proposal moves forward with stated intent to consult stakeholders during testing and implementation, but the MSC identifies substantial untested elements: nodal IR demand-curve calculation, locational uncertainty estimation, IR deliverability constraints, and storage state-of-charge equation parameters all lack operational precedent outside CAISO and may require rapid iterative adjustment. Pumped-storage operators face direct exposure through IR constraints limiting 30-minute rampable capacity supply, storage participation rules in IR procurement, and potential changes to RUC/RC pricing interaction; the design's success depends on whether operators' flexibility needs are genuinely met by IR pricing or whether hidden costs migrate to uplift.

title: Market Surveillance Committee Final Opinion on Day-Ahead Market Enhancements - Attachment 2 to Memo - May 3, 2023
relevance_reason: CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) proposal redesigns real-time procurement and co-optimization of imbalance reserves, uncertainty products, and storage state-of-charge modeling — directly affecting hydro and pumped-storage revenue from ramping/flexibility products, energy co-optimization pricing, and storage dispatch constraints.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (Market Surveillance Committee of CAISO); James Bushnell (Market Surveillance Committee of CAISO); Scott M. Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee of CAISO)
ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee issues a final opinion supporting the DAME proposal (as of May 3, 2023), which introduces a co-optimized imbalance reserve (IR) product, updates the residual unit commitment (RUC) into a reliability capacity (RC) process, and improves storage state-of-charge modeling in day-ahead and hour-ahead optimization. The proposal aims to replace ad-hoc operator load-biasing with transparent market signals and will affect how hydro and pumped storage compete in energy and flexibility procurement, especially through new nodal IR pricing and storage-provision constraints. The MSC emphasizes substantial testing before implementation and calls for a rapid-cycle monitoring and tuning process (not traditional multi-year stakeholder proceedings) to adapt IR quantity calculations, penalty values, and storage state-of-charge parameters — all of which will materially affect PSH flexibility revenue and constraints on charging energy treatment. Implementation timing and parameter lock-in deadlines are not specified in this excerpt, but the opinion signals that CAISO will move faster than historical pace, exposing PSH and long-duration hydro to mid-course design changes.

title: Market Surveillance Committee Update - May 2023
relevance_reason: CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) proposal directly affects energy price formation, imbalance-reserve procurement (a ramping/flexibility product), storage state-of-charge modeling, and resource-adequacy interactions — all core revenue and operational mechanisms for hydro and pumped storage in CAISO's footprint and the emerging west-wide EDAM.
stakeholders: Benjamin F. Hobbs (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee); James Bushnell (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee); Scott M. Harvey (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) issued a May 4, 2023 opinion supporting the Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) initiative, which proposes new co-optimized imbalance-reserve products, updates to residual unit commitment (RUC), and improved storage state-of-charge modeling to replace ad-hoc operator load-biasing and improve market transparency. The MSC endorses the proposal as a necessary step toward a west-wide day-ahead market (EDAM) but flags substantial uncertainties: nodal imbalance-reserve calculation methods, storage state-of-charge parameter-setting, and 30-minute ramp-constraint limits on reserve supply are untested design elements with no precedent in other RTOs. The MSC recommends conservative implementation with guardrails, pre-implementation software testing, and a rapid feedback cycle with stakeholders to adapt the design; the imbalance-reserve demand curve — which caps up-reserve prices at $55/MWh and sets penalty values influencing energy prices — will be critical to monitor. Hydro and pumped-storage operators should track parameter tuning during testing and go-live, as the storage state-of-charge equations and imbalance-reserve locational requirements will reshape the value of flexibility and multi-hour discharge capability; the MSC notes this framework has no ISO precedent and will evolve based on operational experience.

title: Presentation - Congestion Cost Analysis from Historical WEIM Performance - Mar 06, 2026
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Congestion revenue allocation (CRA) methodology for EDAM go-live affects how transmission rights exercised by PacifiCorp and internal generation self-schedules create congestion rents on CAISO constraints — directly touching the revenue from transmission-dependent hydro exports and the incentive structure for scheduling flexibility within the footprint.
ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee presented analysis of how the revised CRA methodology will distribute congestion rents when EDAM goes live, using historical WEIM performance (Mar 2024–2025) as a proxy. The analysis finds that if PacifiCorp exercises PACE-to-PACW transmission rights and self-schedules internal generation under EDAM, revised allocation could shift $1.6–$2.5 million per month in congestion charges, with estimated monthly congestion rents ranging $2.34–$6.96 million depending on transfer-capability assumptions. The outcome is stated as 'no worse than current DA solution for CAISO congestion rents.' Hydro owners in PACE/PACW and those exporting through CAISO constraints should track the final CRA rule and EDAM implementation timeline (go-live date not stated in document) to quantify how parallel flows from PacifiCorp interarea transfers will alter nodal pricing signals and congestion revenue distribution affecting their scheduling and capacity value.

title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Configurable Parameters - Feb 06, 2026
relevance_reason: EDAM configurable parameters directly affect imbalance reserve (IR) pricing, procurement, and the opportunity-cost bidding environment for all dispatchable resources including hydro and pumped storage, plus storage-specific envelope constraints and state-of-charge treatment.
ai_summary: CAISO's configurable-parameters working group completed sensitivity analyses on five EDAM design parameters: envelope constraint multiplier, IR bid cap, IR/RC default availability bids, deployed imbalance reserve factor, and transmission constraint enforcement. Key findings: higher envelope multipliers reduce battery IR procurement and raise IR prices (storage-specific impact); DIR factor and constraint enforcement interact to affect congestion and local pricing; bid caps temper clearing prices. Parallel operations (extended through April 30, 2026) will test envelope multipliers (starting at 85%, assessing lower values), DIR factor (starting at 100%, assessing reductions), and transmission constraint enforcement hierarchies; IR bid cap and DAB cap remain fixed at $55 per tariff. For pumped-storage operators, the envelope multiplier and DIR factor tuning during parallel ops directly affects how much IR PSH can procure and at what price — these govern the opportunity-cost treatment of state-of-charge constraints. Results will be assessed using parallel-ops market cases; no go-live decision date is stated, but the extended parallel window and commitment to post-go-live monitoring suggests continued parameter adjustment risk through mid-2026.

title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Congestion Charge Allocation Impacts - Mar 06, 2026
relevance_reason: EDAM congestion charge allocation design directly affects hydro and storage dispatch incentives, transmission revenue recovery, and interregional wheeling economics—a core revenue mechanism for hydro with transmission-dependent value.
stakeholders: Scott Harvey (California ISO / Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: Scott Harvey (MSC member) presents CAISO analysis of how EDAM's go-live congestion charge allocation design differs from Western EIM base-schedule treatment and the original EDAM proposal, using PacifiCorp East-to-West transactions as a case study. The presentation compares settlement outcomes across three designs using historical congestion data from June 2024, January 2025, and July 2025, finding that go-live EDAM produces materially different charge allocations depending on prevailing vs. counterflow conditions and the magnitude of internal vs. interregional congestion. The analysis is part of the Market Surveillance Committee's evaluation of EDAM design impacts; no immediate vote or deadline is stated, but the document supports ongoing MSC deliberation on EDAM revenue allocation fairness across the Western footprint—directly relevant to how hydro and PSH resources sited on transmission bottlenecks (especially in the Pacific Northwest interconnecting to California) will be paid for energy and congestion services under EDAM go-live.

title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Congestion Revenue Allocation - Mar 06, 2026
relevance_reason: EDAM congestion revenue allocation design directly affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources' energy sales are priced and settled across the Western balancing-area seam; Phase 2 evaluation of self-schedule incentives and parallel-flow revenue allocation will reshape revenue capture for hydro exports and intra-EDAM transactions.
ai_summary: CAISO is executing Phase 2 of EDAM congestion revenue allocation design, following FERC's August 2025 approval of a transitional Phase 1 design that launched EDAM in May 2026. The Phase 1 design allocates internal constraint congestion to the balancing area where the constraint sits, and allocates parallel-flow congestion from balanced self-schedules to the exercising balancing area. Phase 2 stakeholder working groups (two sessions completed as of this presentation) are now evaluating design concepts to address two key issues: (1) eliminating or reducing self-schedule incentives that discourage economic bidding, and (2) creating further symmetry in parallel-flow congestion allocation for the CAISO balancing area. The Market Surveillance Committee recommends exploring a negotiated-entitlement approach and has flagged questions about how negotiated entitlements would apply to Western market structures, existing inter-balancing-area operational agreements managing loop flow, and the basis for establishing entitlements (historical flows, dollar limits, etc.). The next phase of stakeholder working groups runs late March through May 2026. Hydro operators exporting across EDAM seams or managing run-of-river dispatch with balanced schedules face direct revenue exposure: parallel-flow congestion allocation affects the net LMP received, and the Phase 2 pivot away from self-schedule incentives toward economic bidding may reshape the value of storage and flexible hydro in managing loop-flow congestion. Owners of reservoir hydro with flexible dispatch and pumped-storage facilities in the Western Interconnection are most exposed.

title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Imbalance Reserve Costs Market Surveillance Committee - Feb 06, 2026
relevance_reason: EDAM imbalance reserve market design and pricing directly affects storage and dispatchable hydro revenue from flexibility products and reserve provision; the analysis identifies design choices (15-minute start constraint, 30-minute ramp limits, 0.85 attenuation factor for storage energy usage, reserve procurement levels) that materially drive up reserve costs and reshape which resources are economical to deploy.
stakeholders: Scott Harvey (California ISO Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: Scott Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee member) presents analysis of EDAM imbalance reserve pricing in simulations, finding average reserve-up costs of ~$20–$40/day appear materially higher than efficient-market levels. The high costs stem from five key market design choices: 15-minute dispatch requirement, 30-minute ramp limits, 0.85 attenuation factor on storage energy usage, tying storage state-of-charge to reserve schedules (disconnected from real-time RTD dispatch), and the reserve procurement level and demand-curve shape. These design features inflate thermal commitment costs and create high opportunity costs for storage, making both thermal and storage sources expensive providers of imbalance reserves. Harvey notes some costs may be simulation artifacts (low storage energy offer prices, high reserve offer prices, constraint modeling differences), but warns the high prices may accurately reflect actual EDAM design if storage energy usage must be tied to reserve schedules. The analysis does not recommend specific changes but flags the disconnect between high reserve prices and relatively low energy prices as requiring investigation before market launch.

title: Presentation - Future Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Congestion Revenue Allocation - Mar 06, 2026
relevance_reason: EDAM congestion revenue allocation rules directly affect how hydro (especially pumped storage and dispatchable reservoir hydro) is incentivized to self-schedule vs. participate in real-time dispatch, and how loopflows from hydro export and renewable-heavy regions are priced and allocated across balancing areas—touching both revenue from congestion-related dispatch signals and the efficiency of hydro's participation in EDAM/WEIM markets.
stakeholders: Scott Harvey (California ISO Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: Scott Harvey presents CAISO's analysis of loopflow congestion pricing and allocation in EDAM, arguing that the go-live design's requirement for self-scheduling to receive congestion-charge rebates creates inefficient use-it-or-lose-it incentives and may shift costs between market participants. Harvey notes that loopflows—inter-balancing-area power transfers that create congestion on parallel constraints—can significantly impact market efficiency and proposes moving toward a long-run financial-congestion-hedge design (akin to PJM/MISO firm-flow entitlements and joint congestion management) that decouples rent allocation from self-schedule requirements. An interim step would assign internal-constraint congestion rents to balancing areas based on flow entitlements rather than self-scheduling. This is relevant to hydro because reservoir and pumped-storage hydro participating in EDAM would face distorted dispatch incentives under the current self-schedule-based rebate structure, particularly in scenarios with large new loads (data centers) and remote renewable generation; loopflow-impact allocation also affects the transmission value and export economics of hydro-rich regions like the Northwest. The presentation does not identify a formal comment deadline or board vote date.

title: Presentation - Market Power Mitigation Issues - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Market power mitigation design directly affects how hydro and storage are tested for market power and when their offers are capped to default energy bids — the document critiques proposed changes to CAISO's three-pivotal-supplier test and discusses conduct-and-impact alternatives that would reshape which use-limited resources (battery, pondage hydro) face mitigation triggers and cost-cap enforcement.
stakeholders: Scott Harvey (California ISO, Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: Scott Harvey (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee member) presents critical analysis of CAISO's August 22, 2025 straw proposal on market power mitigation, arguing that the ISO has not demonstrated non-competitive outcomes in recent years and that proposed system-level market-power tests applied to constrained regions lack evidence of actual withholding. Harvey contends that counterfactual simulations conflate volatile gas-cost methodology (understated gas input prices) with genuine market power and that applying mitigation to constrained-down regions is unwarranted. He separately explains conduct-and-impact test mechanics, noting that CAISO's competitive-LMP approach caps offers to default energy bids even when a resource's output would have zero price impact — a key distinction for batteries and pondage hydro. The presentation does not state a formal recommendation or next step, but signals Harvey's view that CAISO should require empirical proof of withholding days and accurate cost bases before expanding mitigation design. For PSH and battery owners, this is a critical opportunity to submit comments opposing overly broad mitigation triggers; the August 2025 straw proposal appears to be the active consultation document being critiqued.

title: Presentation - Price Formation Enhancements - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is redesigning BAA-level market power mitigation (affecting mitigation of storage and use-limited hydro offers) and advancing scarcity pricing — two mechanisms that directly shape hydro revenue through default energy bids, opportunity-cost offer treatment, and energy price formation during tight supply.
stakeholders: Joint LSEs (SCE on behalf of IOUs, POUs, and Six Cities) (Southern California Edison and Joint CA Load-Serving Entities); Vitol (Vitol); WPTF (Gridwell Consulting) (Western Power Trading Forum / Gridwell Consulting)
ai_summary: CAISO is pursuing two parallel tracks in its Price Formation Enhancements initiative. Track 1 advances BAA-level market power mitigation (BAAMPM) by incorporating load-serving obligations (LSO) into the competitiveness test, mitigating only net-pivotal suppliers rather than all suppliers in a failed BAA group, and formalizing a grouping algorithm that includes the CAISO BA itself. This refinement directly affects how CAISO identifies whether hydro and storage operators have market power and thus whether their offers face mitigation — the LSO adjustment should reduce false positives by exempting capacity genuinely needed to serve an affiliate's retail load. Track 2 defers comprehensive scarcity pricing redesign, with CAISO planning incremental improvements (ensuring scarcity prices when load is shed, enhancing pricing when load is 'armed') before undertaking more expansive work; the Joint LSEs (SCE on behalf of IOUs, POUs, and Six Cities) oppose Track 2, while the Western Power Trading Forum urges full development of an energy supply margin framework. The LSO proposal has outstanding design questions for WEIM-only BAAs on handling merchant affiliate allocation. Most exposed are pumped-storage operators and large reservoir hydro with significant market power footprints in multi-affiliate BAAs, and any hydro owner relying on opportunity-cost mitigation exemptions; the competitiveness test changes could tighten or relax mitigation exposure depending on how LSO allocation evolves in stakeholder discussion.

title: Presentation - Price Formation Issues - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO Market Surveillance Committee presentation on price-formation issues and reserve-product design directly affects hydropower revenue through scarcity-pricing mechanisms, reserve-procurement rules, and co-optimization of reserves with energy—all core inputs to hydro dispatch value and ancillary-service compensation.
stakeholders: Scott Harvey (California ISO / Western EIM Market Surveillance Committee)
ai_summary: Scott Harvey, CAISO Market Surveillance Committee member, presents two critical price-formation issues for the California ISO balancing area: (1) real-time price signals in stressed conditions (August 2020 case study shows low real-time prices failed to incent import supply despite high day-ahead prices, and current rules cap prices at $1,000 during non-spinning-reserve dispatch, understating shortage value to suppliers and consumers); and (2) absence of explicit 30- or 60-minute reserve products, forcing CAISO to procure thousands of MWs of buffer capacity via expensive load-conformance purchases (energy bought in FMM and settled at RTD imbalance prices, with costs varying wildly). Harvey recommends CAISO adopt a tiered reserve product with graduated demand-curve pricing (referencing NYISO's 10-step design: $0–$750 depending on shortage MW), co-optimized with energy, but cautions that existing flexiramp and imbalance-reserve implementation flaws (battery state-of-charge accounting, nodal-delivery tests, locational modeling) must be fixed first—work that will take years. Hydropower operators (especially pumped storage and flexible dispatchable units) are directly affected: better-designed reserve markets with co-optimization and scarcity-responsive pricing would raise the value of fast-response capacity and reduce reliance on out-of-market load conformance, increasing revenue certainty and reducing demand-side rationing risk.

title: Presentation - Scarcity Pricing - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
relevance_reason: Scarcity pricing mechanisms directly affect hydro and storage revenue through energy price formation and penalty-value settings that influence investment signals and intra-day dispatch incentives.
ai_summary: James Bushnell (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee) presents an analysis of scarcity-pricing approaches in CAISO's pricing-enhancements initiative, arguing that penalty-value reassessment is more straightforward than adding new reserve products. The power-balance constraint (PBC) penalty currently stands at $1,000/MWh ($2,000 during 831-trigger conditions) and has remained unchanged for decades; at 2010 value it would equate to roughly $1,500 today. Bushnell argues that higher penalty values would strengthen incentives for storage resources to retain energy for scarce conditions, incentivize delivery of scheduled imports, and improve EDAM's ability to compete for scarce import MWh during tight western conditions. His recommendation is that penalty values be raised and coordinated across markets, potentially stepped up over time once a schedule is agreed, and that major new reserve products should await live EDAM experience. The implication: PSH and conventional hydro with storage capability would face stronger economic incentives to withhold energy for peak scarcity, increasing real-time dispatch revenue during tight periods but potentially affecting energy-market pricing dynamics in both day-ahead and real-time.

title: Presentation - Updated Analysis on Price Formation Enhancements - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
relevance_reason: CAISO is updating its Bidding Adjudication Algorithm (BAA) under the Mitigation and Pass-Through (MPM) mechanism — a market-power mitigation rule that directly affects how hydro and storage operators' opportunity-cost offers are tested and potentially rejected or accepted; changes to the grouping logic, LSO treatment, and CAISO testing parameters alter the revenue risk and bid acceptance likelihood for use-limited resources.
ai_summary: CAISO's Market Performance and Advanced Analytics team presented updated analysis of eight algorithm variants for the BAA-MPM test, evaluated on Q3 2025 data (July–September). The variants combine three design factors: adjusted grouping logic, addition of a Load Serving Obligation (LSO) component to the Residual Supply Index calculation, and testing CAISO as a balancing-area entity. Adding the LSO component materially increased pass rates across most balancing areas, particularly in import-constrained midday hours in the Pacific Northwest and Central/Mountain regions (PSEI, SCL, TPWR saw +19–21% improvements), while testing CAISO reduced pass rates for several BAAs in peak evening hours (19–21). The presentation does not state a next step or decision date, but the analysis supports continued refinement of offer-acceptance criteria for resource-limited suppliers.

The specific constraint and reassessment items suggest CAISO is iterating on how it manages bottleneck-limited locations; hydro owners seeking uprate rights or PSH interconnection at constrained substations should track the outcomes.

committee: Interconnection Service Capacity and Deliverability Retention for Non-Operating Generating Facilities
meeting_title: Annual Demonstration of Commercial Viability Due
title: Draft Final Proposal - Interconnection Service Capacity and Deliverability Retention for Non-Operating Generating Facilities - May 11, 2026
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes mandatory timelines and conditions for retaining interconnection service capacity and deliverability rights when hydroelectric or pumped-storage facilities cease operations, mothball, or repower — directly affecting whether a hydro owner can preserve its grid position and capacity value during outages or strategic transitions.
stakeholders: Angela Randall (California Independent System Operator); Catalin Micsa (California Independent System Operator); Jason Foster (California Independent System Operator); Jill Jordan (California Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: CAISO's Draft Final Proposal (May 11, 2026) codifies rules requiring non-operating generating facilities to either return to service, enter the generator interconnection queue, or begin repower construction within specified timeframes to retain deliverability allocations and interconnection service capacity rights; failure to act within these windows forfeits the rights, releasing capacity back to the system. MECHANISM: The proposal creates a hard-deadline retention regime that affects hydro owners planning extended maintenance, mothballing, or repowering. Facilities reaching physical derate (reduced maximum output) lose deliverability commensurate with the loss if the derate exceeds 3 years; those exiting wholesale markets to serve behind-the-meter load are treated as permanent retirements triggering reliability reviews and potential RMR (Reliability Must Run) obligations. SCOPE: All hydroelectric resources in CAISO territory, particularly those contemplating maintenance windows beyond 3 years, repower projects, or transitional mothball periods, are exposed. Generators choosing not to follow notification processes forfeit their rights, and the proposal strengthens ISO enforcement of 90-day advance notice and retirement affidavits. Next step: Draft Final Proposal status indicates Board action is pending (typical CAISO timeline ~Q3 2026); stakeholders should monitor for final tariff vote and implementation timeline.

ai_summary: CAISO proposes two major changes to storage outage management: (1) developing new Master File fields and constraints to model nonlinearity (foldback) in lithium-ion batteries near state-of-charge limits, expected for implementation no earlier than spring 2027; until then, market participants must either avoid the nonlinear range in filings or submit plant-trouble outage cards to signal unavailability; and (2) four Outage Management System enhancements, prioritizing automatic acceptance of outage-change requests and power-minimum rerating for charging tests in 2026, with future improvements to out-of-service checkboxes and partial-outage submissions for ancillary services. The proposal applies specifically to non-generator resource participation model storage (excluding pumped storage and proxy demand resources), but the nonlinearity and outage-reporting logic will inform broader storage-design work. Comments are due June 1, 2026.

ai_summary: ERCOT's System Protection Working Group revised the short-circuit analysis procedure for Large Load Interconnection Studies, effective in the Batch Zero process. ai_summary: ERCOT is reviewing its 2027 ancillary-services (AS) methodology for Regulation, Responsive Reserve Service (RRS), and reserve procurement (ECRS/Non-Spin), with votes scheduled August 5–6, 2026, and PUCT approval targeted November–December 2026. The 2026 methodology introduced a probabilistic framework for reserve procurement; 2027 will refine that engine by testing sensitivities in risk inputs (forecast error, forced outages), risk credits (headroom discounts for already-committed capacity), and convergence criteria, while separately monitoring whether large-load ramping (up to 400+ MW spikes) requires a new Regulation adjustment factor. ERCOT noted that inertia trends have shifted lowest-inertia hours from night to midday since 2025, affecting RRS requirements keyed to inertia and frequency-response obligations (NERC BAL-003 IFRO); 2027 RRS quantities remain pending NERC's 2027 IFRO assessment. The sensitivity analysis will compare risk levels (Watch, EEA1, EEA2 vs. Load Shed Probability targets of 1-in-10/5/1-year), look-ahead windows (1–6 hours), risk credits (current 60% night / 25% day vs. higher levels), and solar forecast-error logic. This is a reference point for how non-hydro-dominant RTOs structure flexibility and reserve procurement under high renewable penetration and intra-hour load volatility.

ai_summary: ERCOT is reviewing its 2027 Ancillary Services methodology for Regulation, Responsive Reserve Service (RRS), Contingency Reserve Service (ECRS), and Non-Spinning Reserve. Key changes under evaluation: (1) potential new adjustment factors for Regulation to address large-load ramping extremes (up ramps 41–51 MW average, down ramps −44 to −61 MW, with outliers exceeding ±400 MW); (2) refinement of the 2026 probabilistic framework for ECRS and Non-Spin to test sensitivity to risk inputs, risk credits (currently 60% night, 25% day), convergence criteria (1-in-X violation thresholds), and net-load forecast error distributions; (3) evaluation of look-ahead period (up to 6 hours), solar adjustment logic including potential shift to Gross Real-Time Power Potential (GRPP), and adjacent-hour smoothing. RRS methodology is unchanged pending NERC's 2027 BAL-003 IFRO assessment (not yet published; analysis uses 2026 minimum of 1,377 MW). ai_summary: ERCOT is conducting its annual review of ancillary-services (AS) methodology to determine 2027 procurement requirements for Regulation, Responsive Reserve Service (RRS), ERCOT Contingency Reserve Service (ECRS), and Non-Spinning Reserve. The RRS methodology remains unchanged, though 2027 quantities will track inertia trends and await NERC's 2027 BAL-003 Interconnection Frequency Response Obligation (IFRO) assessment. The review timeline proceeds through multiple working groups (PDCWG, WMWG, ROS, OWG) with a WMS vote scheduled August 5, 2026; TAC vote August 26; Board vote September 15; and PUC approval November–December 2026.

title: 07. wmwg-update-to-wms-of-June-29-meeting_v2

ai_summary: ERCOT's Wholesale Market Subcommittee met July 8, 2026 to advance three market-design initiatives. (1) NPRR1328 (Generation Firming Program) revised firming-capacity formulas to account for exemptions and transfers, updated attestation timelines and pre-season posting from 10 to 30 days, and set a 3% Expected Unserved Energy threshold for high-risk hours; stakeholders expressed general support and ERCOT is targeting September 2026 Board approval to meet a December 1, 2027 implementation deadline. (2) NPRR1278 (Advanced Grid Support Service as an ancillary service) was tabled pending ENGIE's benchmarking of grid-support calculation methodologies across other markets — this shapes how ERCOT will price and procure a new flexibility service. (3) NPRR1337 (Energy Reliable Service enhancements) tightens eligibility via a mandatory default-baseline requirement unless an Alternate Baseline Test is passed (which excludes price-chasing loads via $80/MWh threshold testing), shifts to resource-level availability evaluation, and escalates suspension penalties for repeated failures — resource-level analysis of availability impacts is ongoing. State-of-charge accuracy for energy storage resources (ESRs) emerged as a critical operational issue: flat, inaccurate Customer-Owned Plant state-of-charge submissions are driving excessive Reliability Unit Commitment instructions; ERCOT will engage with Qualified Scheduling Entities to improve state-of-charge forecasting and evaluate telemetered alternatives. These changes affect how storage (and by precedent, dispatchable resources like hydro) monetize flexibility, govern baseline-load attribution in demand-response programs, and report operational parameters to the commitment engine.

ai_summary: ERCOT is considering methodology changes for 2027 ancillary-services procurement, with focus on Regulation Service (adding a potential Large Load adjustment factor for ramps exceeding 400 MW), Responsive Reserve Service (pending NERC's 2027 BAL-003 IFRO assessment), and refinement of the 2026 probabilistic framework for ECRS and Non-Spinning Reserve. title: 2027_AS_Methodology_WMWG_062926_v2
