ai_summary: ERCOT is refining its 2027 ancillary-services methodology with one material change: addition of a Large Load Adjustment factor to Regulation Service requirements to account for extreme 5-minute ramps from data-center and non-conforming loads that can exceed 400 MW and are currently underrepresented in the net-load-forecast-error (NLFE) term. ERCOT is also tuning its probabilistic framework for Contingency and Non-Spinning reserves by testing sensitivity to risk thresholds (Watch / EEA1 / EEA2 / Load Shed), look-ahead periods (1–6 hours), risk-credit levels (current 60% night / 25% day), and tail-event distribution methods; it is investigating migration from HSL (historical scheduled level) to GRPP (Gross Real-Time Power Potential) for solar and wind adjustments. Comparative interest: ERCOT's modeling of large-load variability and its probabilistic criteria tuning (probability of violation targets of 1-in-10-year to 1-in-1-year) show how an inflexible-load-heavy system approaches reserve adequacy differently from hydro-rich grids where demand variability is less extreme. Timeline: WMWG discussion today (June 29, 2026); WMS and ROS votes August 5–6; TAC vote August 26; Board approval September 15; PUC filing November–December 2026.

title: Intra-Hour IRR Forecast Accuracy May 2026
relevance_reason: ERCOT's intra-hour wind and solar ramp-rate forecasting accuracy directly affects how much ramping and flexibility capability must be procured to cover variable-resource uncertainty — a cross-market comparison point for how RTOs handle forecast-driven flexibility procurement, relevant to hydro's role as the primary ramp supplier in markets where it exists.
ai_summary: ERCOT Operations Analysis reports May 2026 performance of its intra-hour ramp-rate forecasting (PWRR for wind, PSRR for solar) used in 5-minute SCED dispatch. Wind ramp forecasts showed 88 MW/5-min mean absolute error (vs. 119 MW for a zero-ramp baseline); solar ramp forecasts showed 222 MW/5-min error (vs. 400 MW baseline). When actual ramps exceed 100 MW, forecast errors widen significantly (wind: 131 MW, solar: 256–257 MW). This is a reference document for how ERCOT's demand for flexibility products derives from variable-resource forecasting — in hydro-rich markets, superior forecast accuracy could reduce the volume and cost of ramping procurements that hydro resources supply. No action items or next steps are stated in the excerpt.

title: Monthly Review of RUC Market Impacts - May 2026
relevance_reason: ERCOT's Reliability Unit Commitment (RUC) market triggers price adders for energy and ancillary services during scarcity; this cross-market comparison shows how ERCOT's commitment and uplift mechanics differ from hydro-centric RTOs, informing NHA's understanding of alternative market-design approaches to reliability procurement and cost recovery.
ai_summary: ERCOT's May 2026 RUC market review reports 386.9 effective resource-hours committed, with 78% addressing capacity needs and most commitments (71%) issued with lead-time margins of 2 hours or less. RUC triggered 181.2 Reliability Deployment Price Adder (RDPA) hours at a time-weighted average of $0.75/MWh for energy, plus ancillary-service RDPAs averaging $0.13–$0.18/MWh across regulation, responsive reserve, and contingency reserve. Make-whole payments totaled $1.14M in May; 77% of committed resource-hours came from generators at least 50 years old, predominantly non-opt-out resources. For hydro-centric markets, this illustrates ERCOT's reliance on must-offer thermal capacity and uplift cost-recovery rather than dynamic AS procurement or storage participation—a design that contrasts with how CAISO, NYISO, and PJM price flexibility and commitment from dispatchable and storage resources.

title: 03. NPRRs for review

ai_summary: ERCOT is advancing four Nodal Protocol Revision Requests through its Credit Finance Sub Group. NPRR1319 expands the winter seasonal-mothball prohibition to December–February (beyond the existing summer ban June 1–Sept 30), and clarifies that ERCOT need not run a Must-Run Alternative RFP when a resource requests seasonal mothball status on short notice (90 days prior), streamlining the mothball evaluation. NPRR1326 adds Energy Storage Resource State of Charge (SOC) information to ERCOT's Ancillary Services Capacity Monitor, improving visibility into storage availability for reserves and ancillary-services procurement. NPRR1329 allows two Resource Entities to jointly manage injection/withdrawal limits at a Point of Interconnection through shared QSE responsibility and attestation. These changes affect seasonal fleet planning (mothball availability narrows in winter), storage dispatch and AS participation (SOC transparency in real-time reserve adequacy), and multi-entity coordination rules. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda item.

ai_summary: ERCOT's Credit Finance Sub Group reviewed five Nodal Protocol Revision Requests. NPRR1326 adds Energy Storage Resource State of Charge information to ERCOT's Ancillary Services Capacity Monitor, improving visibility into storage dispatch constraints during AS procurement. NPRR1320 formalizes reporting of multiple Planning Reserve Margin scenarios (rather than a single point forecast) in ERCOT's capacity and reserves reports, better surfacing seasonal and year-ahead adequacy risk. For hydro-rich markets monitoring cross-RTO approaches: ERCOT's tightened mothball rules and storage-visibility protocols illustrate how RTOs handle use-limited resource retirement and storage operational transparency—relevant as precedent if other RTOs adopt similar frameworks.

title: Resource Integration Topics
relevance_reason: ERCOT interconnection queue mechanics and dynamic-model requirements for generation resources set cross-market precedent for how RTOs handle queue reform, model validation, and resource-integration standards that affect storage and flexible-resource participation pathways hydro monitors for applicability elsewhere.
ai_summary: ERCOT's Resource Integration Working Group presented Q2 2026 status on the Quarterly Stability Assessment (QSA) process, including model-validation deadlines (45 days prior to QSA deadline for FIS studies, 15–30 days for dynamic model review), PSCAD model-quality test requirements effective August 1, 2026, and active revision requests on ESR maintenance exceptions and in-kind generation definitions. As of May 31, 2026, ERCOT has 1,960 active generation interconnection requests totaling 458 GW: 172 GW of battery storage, 163 GW solar, 71 GW natural gas, 48 GW wind, and 5 GW other (including 65 small-gen projects, 37 model-ready). The queue has grown from ~409 GW (May 2025) to ~458 GW (May 2026), with battery capacity in interconnection remaining near 172 GW. For hydro and storage operators, this document signals ERCOT's evolving requirements for dynamic-model validation and queue-management timelines; the large battery-storage queue (172 GW / 412 GWh) demonstrates how a non-hydro RTO is accommodating storage participation and sets a benchmark for comparable participation rules elsewhere. The QSA deadline structure and prerequisite schedules may inform how other RTOs design interconnection-process certainty for flexible resources. No immediate FERC filing or NHA member vote is present, but the document is useful reference material for understanding queue and stability-assessment mechanics in a hydro-free market.

title: 2027_AS_Methodology_PDCWG_061726
relevance_reason: ERCOT's 2027 ancillary-services methodology changes, particularly to Regulation Service procurement driven by renewable ramping and large-load behavior, establish a cross-market comparison point for how non-hydro-rich RTOs structure flexible-ramping products and frequency-response requirements — relevant to NHA's understanding of AS design alternatives and hydro's competitive position in frequency-response markets.
ai_summary: ERCOT is finalizing 2027 ancillary-services (AS) methodology, with one substantive change to Regulation Service computation: incorporation of a potential Large Load adjustment factor to account for extreme 5-minute ramps (up to 400 MW in instances) from data centers and steel mills, which exceed the current methodology's reliance on net-load forecast error and wind/solar ramping adjustment tables. The change reflects ERCOT's focus on dynamic solar-ramp thresholds (now 280 MW/min for sunrise/sunset, up from 80 MW/min when implemented in June 2023) and intra-hour renewable-variability capture via GTBD. Responsive Reserve Service (RRS) methodology remains unchanged pending NERC's 2027 BAL-003 Interconnection Frequency Response Obligation assessment; current analysis uses the 2026 minimum RRS-PFR floor of 1,377 MW. Board vote scheduled September 15, 2026; PUC submission November–December 2026. For hydro asset owners, this demonstrates how RTOs outside the West are handling frequency-response procurement in high-renewable, low-inertia conditions — ERCOT's emphasis on large-load ramping as a regulation driver differs from traditional net-load variability models and may inform future WECC or NYISO redesigns.

title: Non-FMEs for May-26_PUBLIC
relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency event analysis informs cross-market comparison of how RTOs handle frequency response and synchronous-resource ride-through — a key ancillary service and operational-compliance obligation that shapes hydro and storage dispatch across all markets.
ai_summary: ERCOT Operations Planning presented analysis of two frequency events in May 2026: one FME (Frequency Measurement Event) on 5/20 at 23:17:35 UTC and one Non-FME on 5/27 at 12:29:39 UTC, reviewed at the PDCWG on June 17, 2026. The document does not disclose the magnitude, duration, cause, or mitigation outcome of either event in the excerpt provided. As a cross-market reference point, this analysis contributes to understanding how ERCOT — a synchronous-machine-rich, hydro-limited RTO — detects and responds to frequency disturbances compared to hydro-dependent RTOs (WECC, NYISO, PJM); such comparisons inform NHA members' assessment of whether frequency-response procurement and ride-through standards are calibrated consistently across interconnections.

title: PDCWG Frequency Control Report for May 2026
relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency control and inertia metrics establish a cross-market comparison point for hydro's role in frequency response and synchronous generation — showing how a low-hydro system manages frequency stability and minimum inertia thresholds that hydro-rich RTOs can benchmark against.
ai_summary: ERCOT's May 2026 frequency control report documents CPS1 performance of 184.80% (12-month rolling average 181.08%), zero BAAL exceedances, and minimum system inertia of 138.8 GW·s (down from 143 GW·s in 2016 despite 81% non-synchronous generation penetration). The report reveals that ERCOT has reduced its critical inertia threshold from 100 GW·s to 87 GW·s between 2016 and 2026, while deploying Fast Frequency Response (FFR) awards totaling 430 MW by March 2026 to maintain frequency stability as synchronous generation declines. For hydro-rich RTOs, this demonstrates that systems with negligible conventional hydropower must artificially lower inertia requirements and increasingly rely on synthetic-inertia products (FFR), whereas hydro operators in NERC regions with reservoir storage maintain naturally higher inertia margins — a competitive advantage that regulatory design should preserve. The threshold reductions and FFR ramp-up illustrate the cost of losing synchronous capacity; hydro's inherent inertia contribution to regional frequency stability remains a quantifiable asset.

title: Regulation Analysis PDCWG May-26
relevance_reason: ERCOT's regulation service analysis—deployed volume, bias patterns, and 5-min interval performance—is a cross-market comparison point for how ERCOT's ancillary-services market design differs from hydro-rich RTOs and sets precedent for frequency-response procurement and pricing elsewhere.
ai_summary: ERCOT Operations Planning presented a regulation bias and performance analysis covering May 2026 data: regulation deployment trends (yearly and monthly), wind and solar ramp-rate forecast errors, regulation bias across consecutive 5-min SCED intervals, time error accumulation, and load/net-load/ramp profiles. The analysis examines which factors (load variability, wind/solar ramps, startup/shutdown cycles, short-term load forecast error, non-SCED qualified large load ramping) drive time-error accumulation and regulation need. No policy proposals or decisions are stated; this is a diagnostic report to the Performance, Disturbance, Compliance Working Group. Significance for hydro asset owners: ERCOT's regulation procurement model and ramp-rate-driven AS demand are structural comparisons—ERCOT's real-time balancing relies heavily on deployed regulation volume to absorb wind/solar variability, whereas hydro-rich RTOs (WECC, NERC) often use different flexibility mechanisms (flexible ramping, energy imbalance markets). The data provides a market-monitor-like baseline for cross-RTO analysis of ancillary-services value and design; no imminent rule change or NHA member action is signaled.

title: 05. NPRR1214_ERCOTComments_20260610 v6
relevance_reason: ERCOT's proposed changes to NPRR 1214 directly affect price formation (LMP and AS MCPC determination) and indifference-payment settlement mechanics that would apply to any dispatchable or storage resource participating in a similar market structure; the pricing and settlement innovations are instructive cross-market comparison points for how RTOs handle reliability deployments and the distortion between dispatch (actual) and pricing-run (counterfactual) awards.

ai_summary: ERCOT proposes revisions to NPRR 1214 that eliminate energy and ancillary-service price adders by using SCED Pricing Run prices (LMPs and AS MCPCs) directly when a pricing run is active, rather than calculating adders as Pricing Run minus Dispatch Run prices. In parallel, ERCOT introduces an ancillary-services indifference payment (previously energy-only) using actual offer/bid curves instead of linear approximations, and extends indifference-payment consideration to RUC, RMR, and MRA resources whose real-time revenues were previously overlooked. The proposal also expands locational modeling of reliability deployments to Load and SOG resources under certain dispatch scenarios. Although ERCOT has no hydropower, the design—separating binding dispatch awards (from actual grid state) from binding prices (from hypothetical state absent reliability deployments), and compensating the resulting financial mismatch via indifference payments—illustrates an alternative settlement architecture that contrasts with how other RTOs (including those with hydro fleets) handle the same tension between dispatch realism and scarcity-signal accuracy.

ai_summary: ERCOT proposes modifications to NPRR1214 that change how indifference payments compensate resources for reliability deployments (out-of-market actions). Currently, ERCOT calculates energy and ancillary-service price adders to reflect the difference between dispatch-run and pricing-run market clearing; ERCOT proposes to eliminate those adders and instead use pricing-run LMPs and AS MCPCs directly when deployments are active, and to refine the indifference-payment formula to use SCED pricing-run prices rather than approximations. The change applies locational modeling to additional deployment categories (VECL, non-controllable load, must-run and capacity contracts). These modifications simplify settlement accounting and improve accuracy of make-whole payments for resources whose output is constrained by ERCOT dispatch overrides—relevant to storage and dispatchable hydro that compete for scarcity premiums and uplift when system operators invoke out-of-market controls. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in this draft (version 0.7, dated May 21, 2026); this is a technical comments document being developed for PRS review.

ai_summary: ERCOT's April 2026 operations report shows largest net-load ramps nearly tripled since 2015 (18,089 MW in 60-minute ramp vs. 5,330 MW in April 2015), driven by growing wind and solar penetration (80.27% IRR maximum, 69.15% wind record). The system managed two frequency events and 16 RUC commitments without automatic load-shedding via under-frequency relays. Congestion rent on the HCKSW_MR2L constraint alone reached $51.7M for the month. As a hydro-poor system increasingly dependent on fast ramping and frequency stabilization from conventional generators, ERCOT's experience provides a contrast case: where hydro-rich markets (CAISO, NYISO, BPA) use ramping products and flexible capacity mechanisms to harness dispatchable hydro, ERCOT compensates through higher RUC frequency and larger intra-hour ramps. This benchmarks the value proposition of dedicated ramping and flexibility products that hydro markets have adopted.

title: 11. PDCWG-Report-to-ROS_060426
relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency response and inertia management, particularly FFR procurement and PFR performance monitoring, demonstrate a market-design approach to frequency support that contrasts with hydro-rich RTOs and informs NHA's cross-market analysis of how different regions value synchronous and fast-response capability.

ai_summary: ERCOT's Primary Frequency Response (PFR) performance monitoring under BAL-001-TRE has stalled due to too few Frequency Measurable Events (FMEs) — the criteria used to evaluate PFR performance are generating only fewer than the desired 20–35 events per year. ERCOT has proposed revised FME selection criteria to increase event counts and improve oversight of Responsive Reserve Service providers; revised criteria have been shared with the PDCWG and ERCOT has begun using them immediately while retaining discretion in selection. The ERCOT system inertia trends show a structural decline: synchronous inertia at minimum-load points has fallen from 143 GW·s in 2016 to 114.7 GW·s in March 2026, while non-synchronous generation has grown to 81% of system load and Fast Frequency Response procurement has expanded from zero to 430 MW. This illustrates a cross-market contrast — whereas hydro-rich RTOs rely on synchronous hydro for inertia and primary frequency response, ERCOT's low-inertia, high-renewable grid is increasingly dependent on FFR procurement and stricter frequency-event metrics to maintain reserve adequacy and response capability.

NPRR1278 (Establishing Advanced Grid Support Service as an Ancillary Service) and NPRR1255 (Mitigation of Energy Storage Resources) are tabled for vote, addressing how dispatchable storage can participate in ancillary-service markets and how mitigation rules apply to use-limited resources—a canonical problem for pumped-storage hydropower in other RTOs. WMS open action items include establishing performance-review cadences for ancillary-service procurement and investigating impacts of DOE 202(c) emergency authority on pricing transparency, which affects all flexible resources. No specific comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in this agenda extract; the next steps are the WMS meeting votes on June 3, 2026.

title: 05. NPRR1214_ERCOTComments_20260610 v6
relevance_reason: ERCOT's NPRR 1214 proposes changes to energy price formation, ancillary-service pricing, and indifference-payment mechanics for reliability deployments — a cross-market comparison point for how non-hydro-rich RTOs handle price-adder elimination, locational pricing of out-of-market actions, and settlement consistency between dispatch and pricing runs, mechanisms that affect how hydro-dominant RTOs (WECC, NYISO, PJM) structure scarcity pricing and make-whole payments.

ai_summary: ERCOT proposes to eliminate separate energy and ancillary-service price adders by using SCED Pricing Run prices (LMPs and AS MCPCs) directly as binding prices when reliability deployments are active, replacing a previous approach that added the difference between pricing and dispatch runs. ERCOT also refines indifference-payment calculations to use actual offer/bid curves instead of linear approximations, and extends indifference payments to ancillary services and RUC/RMR/MRA resources. The proposal addresses a settlement inconsistency: dispatch-run awards (reflecting real-time conditions) paired with pricing-run prices (reflecting a hypothetical grid absent deployments) can financially harm participants and create perverse incentives to chase prices. This represents ERCOT's approach to neutralizing distortions from out-of-market actions — a design problem that hydro-rich markets handle differently (e.g., PJM's opportunity-cost framework, CAISO's reliability-deployment uplift). No explicit comment deadline or vote date is stated in the slides.

title: 05. NPRR1214_ERCOT_Paper_20260610

ai_summary: ERCOT proposes reforms to NPRR1214 that modify how reliability deployments (out-of-market dispatchable loads, reserve curtailments, and generation adjustments) are priced and compensated. The proposal replaces energy and ancillary-service price adders with direct use of SCED pricing-run locational marginal prices and market clearing prices, and introduces ancillary-service indifference payments to offset the gap between binding dispatch-run awards (reflecting actual reliability actions) and hypothetical pricing-run prices (reflecting the counterfactual absent those deployments). ERCOT positions this as more accurate than prior approximations and states it will be discussed at the Wholesale Market Subcommittee; no vote or implementation date is stated in this draft. Although ERCOT has no significant hydropower fleet, this precedent is relevant to NHA members in other RTOs (especially CAISO, NYISO, and PJM) where similar tensions arise between must-run or must-offer generation commitments and competitive pricing, and where storage and hydro operators increasingly face indifference-payment regimes to offset scarcity pricing or out-of-market dispatch.

title: FERC Launches Aggressive Targeted Action to Speed Large Load Integration
relevance_reason: FERC's show-cause orders to all six RTOs/ISOs on large-load interconnection and co-located generation directly affect hydro's strategic options for siting, co-location, behind-the-meter service, and competitive positioning against data-center load aggregators; also affects transmission planning and deliverability of hydro export capacity.
ai_summary: FERC issued tailored show-cause orders (June 18, 2026) to PJM, MISO, SPP, CAISO, ISO-NE, and NYISO, giving each 60 days to justify or reform tariffs governing large-load interconnection, including data centers and co-located generation. The orders target five categories: transmission study processes, cost-shifting prevention, co-location and behind-the-meter rules, flexible transmission services for large loads, and generation-serving-load study processes. Within 30 days, each RTO/ISO must report how it will ensure adequate generation for existing and new large loads. For hydro owners, this opens strategic pathways: PSH and reservoir hydro can compete to serve large co-located loads, especially where hydro-sited generation can meet 'electrically proximate' criteria; behind-the-meter service rules may create new revenue streams from data centers or manufacturing co-location. However, the rules also intensify pressure on transmission planning—hydro's export capability may face new demand-study scrutiny, and transmission costs will be more transparent, affecting long-distance export margins. Biggest exposure: Northwest hydro seeking interregional transmission for remote load service, and existing PSH/run-of-river plants considering co-location partnerships.

relevance_reason: CAR-SA introduces seasonal capacity auctions with accreditation reforms that directly reshape how energy-limited resources (including hydropower and pumped storage) are credited and valued in ISO-NE's capacity market, affecting accreditation quantities (MRIC vs. QC), offer formulation, and cost recovery for storage.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms–Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) proposal, with Q2 2027 effective date, introduces seasonal capacity procurement tied to accreditation reforms and a winter gas constraint. The document clarifies offer-formulation principles for resources whose accreditation (MRIC) differs from installed capacity, showing that resources exiting energy markets without a capacity sales obligation would face higher per-MW offer prices under MRIC-based accreditation than under prior QC methods. For imports and energy-limited resources, CAR-SA requires demonstration of 16-hour fuel availability for accreditation; external resources using stored fuel must show historical usable-inventory measures over past three like-seasons. Capacity Offers may include risk premia for pay-for-performance (PFP) costs, and the ISO is publishing offer-formulation guidance (contact: Jasleen Singh, jsingh@iso-ne.com) with questions due by the August Markets Committee. Most exposed are operators of energy-limited hydro and storage whose accreditation drops relative to summer nameplate, forcing higher per-MW asks in seasonal auctions to recover fixed obligations.

meeting_title: NEPOOL Markets Committee Summer Meeting Overflow Meeting
title: 2026-07-21 MC A03b - CAR-SA: Offer Formulation Memo
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This memo directly addresses offer formulation rules for ISO-NE's Capacity Auction and Resource (CAR) market, which determines how resources—including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities—calculate and submit capacity bids; the rules govern the cost basis (incremental costs, opportunity costs from Pay for Performance, annual-cost allocation) that hydro operators must use to stay competitive and avoid mitigation challenges, and it explicitly addresses the interaction between offer formulation and market-power mitigation thresholds.
ai_summary: ISO-NE has issued a detailed guidance memo on how Capacity Resources should formulate competitive offers under the CAR-SA (Seasonal Accreditation) framework. The memo teaches that a competitive offer must equal the resource's expected incremental cost of accepting a Capacity Supply Obligation, including the opportunity cost of the PfP (Pay for Performance) scarcity-condition obligation and the cost of any annual-basis expenses allocated across seasonal offerings. The guidance explicitly advises market participants to consult the Internal Market Monitor (IMM) before submitting offers that may exceed the Capacity Offer Price Threshold or involve resource-specific concerns such as risk modeling or annual-cost treatment; the IMM cannot direct offer prices but can validate cost assumptions and whether expectations are reasonably justified. This creates a new compliance pathway for hydro and PSH owners in ISO-NE: operators allocating multi-year capex or seasonal energy availability into capacity-offer costs will need to pre-clear their methodology with the IMM to avoid triggering mitigation and price caps. The memo is comprehensive reference material (no vote or deadline stated) suitable for long-term corpus use by NHA members evaluating ISO-NE's cost-substantiation regime.

meeting_title: Joint NEPOOL Reliability and Transmission Committee Meeting
title: 2026-07-16 TC A00 Agenda
relevance_reason: Surplus interconnection service and large co-located load participation rules directly affect hydro uprate and hybrid-resource strategic options; resource adequacy and transmission planning touch revenue drivers for existing hydro.
stakeholders: Al McBride (ISO-NE); Alex Rost (ISO-NE); Matt White (ISO-NE); Nic Baldenko (ISO-NE); Dave Burnham (PTO AC); Jackie Hansen (PTO AC)
ai_summary: ISO-NE's July 16, 2026 Transmission Committee agenda addresses three mechanisms affecting hydro owners: (1) Surplus Interconnection Service gap analysis and recommendations (Item 3.0, 10:30–12:30) — covering interconnection rules and market participation models for uprates and new capacity; (2) the New England Large and Co-Located Loads Show Cause Order (EL26-72, Items 4.1–4.2), including ISO's Section 205 filing strategy and a resource adequacy informational report — directly relevant because large co-located load interconnection can alter locational scarcity signals and create opportunities for hydro siting behind-the-meter or in hybrid models; (3) Regional Network Service rate notifications for 2027 and a five-year RNS forecast (Items 2.1–2.2), which set transmission-cost foundations for deliverability of hydro output. The resource adequacy report required by the Show Cause Order will inform how load growth and seasonal energy conditions shape RA accreditation for hydro. No comment deadline or vote is stated on this agenda; Item 3.0 is in its third committee meeting, suggesting momentum toward recommendation.

title: 2026-07-16 TC A01.1 June 24, 2026 Joint TC/MC/RC Meeting Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) affects how ISO-NE accredits hydro and storage resources' capacity contribution, touching seasonal RA value and auction parameters for dispatchable and use-limited resources.
ai_summary: ISO-NE staff (Kevin Coopey) presented initial tariff redlines for Sections II.48, II.52, II.53, II.54, and II.55 of the OATT to align resource deactivation processes under a seasonal framework within the CAR-SA initiative. The redlines are under committee review with a future vote scheduled; no substantive discussion or objections were recorded at the June 24, 2026 meeting. The seasonal accreditation framework will reshape how ISO-NE calculates capacity credit for hydro and pumped-storage resources across seasons, directly affecting their RA accreditation and auction qualification; specific impacts depend on the redlined tariff language (not detailed in these minutes) and the voting outcome.

title: 2026-07-16 TC A03 ISO-NE Surplus Interconnection Service Gap Analysis, Part 1 – Interconnection
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Surplus interconnection service rules directly affect hydro owners' ability to co-locate new generation (including pumped storage) at existing hydropower sites without triggering full interconnection studies, reducing development cost and timeline for uprates and repowering — a key strategic option for extending asset life and revenue.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is analyzing gaps in its Surplus Interconnection Service rules (Schedule 22/23 OATT) to streamline co-location of new resources at existing generator sites. The analysis follows NEPOOL's 2025 priority request and builds on FERC Order 2023 compliance filings (May 2024, accepted by FERC in 2025) that allowed surplus service to be used immediately upon interconnection agreement execution. Part 1 identifies rule gaps; Part 2 (separate document) addresses market participation models for co-located resources. Current rules limit surplus service to affiliates or third parties with original customer consent, exclude retiring/repowering facilities, and require commercial operation status for CNR (capacity) service. Hydro owners considering uprates, pumped-storage additions, or hybrid development at existing sites should review stakeholder discussions at NEPOOL Transmission Committee and watch for gap-analysis conclusions and proposed tariff solutions, which will shape interconnection timelines and costs for co-located projects.

title: 2026-07-16 TC A03 ISO-NE Surplus Interconnection Service Gap Analysis, Part 2 – Market Participation and Operational Considerations
relevance_reason: Surplus interconnection service rules directly affect hydropower uprates and co-location revenue options; the document clarifies market participation models (Generator Asset/Capacity Resource constructs) and energy/ancillary service obligations for multiple facilities behind a single point of interconnection—a canonical use case for small hydro expansion and pumped-storage facilities.
ai_summary: ISO-NE released Part 2 of its Surplus Interconnection Service Gap Analysis, clarifying market participation rules for multiple generating facilities co-located behind a single interconnection point, including those using surplus capacity from existing hydro or PSH plants. The document specifies four configuration options (separate or single Generator Assets paired with separate or single Capacity Resources) and maps which Lead Market Participant responsibility applies to each: single-asset constructs consolidate offer submission and dispatch under one participant (reducing autonomy but simplifying coordination), while separate-asset constructs allow each facility independent participation but require multiple participants to coordinate compliance with shared Interconnection Service limits. This guidance is critical for hydro owners considering uprates, pumped-storage co-location, or use-limited resource expansion, since interconnection service constraints will likely force facility-limitation configurations; the document also flags a technical complication with single-asset structures if the original interconnection customer's facility deactivates, which could jeopardize the subordinate surplus resource.

title: 2026-07-16 TC A03 Surplus Interconnection Service Gap Analysis Presentation
relevance_reason: Surplus Interconnection Service rules directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities can add resources, repower, or co-locate at existing interconnection points without triggering new cluster studies — a material option for uprating and hybrid/co-located resource deployment.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Surplus Interconnection Service Gap Analysis (posted July 10, 2026) proposes five interconnection reforms: a new expedited process for additions, replacements, and modifications at existing POIs that don't increase service or require network upgrades; revised definition of Unused Capability to support CNRIS sharing and hybrid-resource accreditation; explicit clarification that energy-storage facilities can be surplus interconnection customers when charging from the grid; public posting of surplus-service requests; and timelines and procedural detail for surplus studies. The analysis also documents market-participation registration options (separate vs. combined generator assets and capacity resources) for surplus and original facilities co-located behind the same POI. Most consequential for hydro owners is the expedited pathway for co-located additions and facility replacements — currently only available through subordinate Surplus Interconnection Service status — which would allow original-level service and alignment with CAR-SA hybrid-resource accreditation rules being developed in parallel. Next steps include stakeholder review and ISO development of specific tariff language; no vote date is stated in this presentation.

title: 2026-07-16 TC A04.1 New England Large & Co-Located Loads Show Cause Order (EL26-72) Compliance: Abeyance Request & Section 205 Filing
relevance_reason: FERC Order EL26-72 mandates tariff reforms for large and co-located loads, including behind-the-meter generation rules and flexible load services that directly affect how hydro and pumped storage can participate in co-location arrangements and transmission service offerings.
stakeholders: Alex Rost (ISO New England); Nic Baldenko (ISO New England); TC Secretary (ISO New England); NEPOOL Counsel (NEPOOL)
ai_summary: FERC issued a Section 206 order on June 18, 2026 (EL26-72) finding ISO-NE's tariff unjust and unreasonable for lack of large and co-located load integration provisions. ISO-NE and the PTOs plan to file a 90-day abeyance request by August 3, 2026, followed by a Section 205 tariff filing on November 16, 2026, addressing five reform categories: efficient study processes, cost transparency, co-location rules (including behind-the-meter generation definitions and netting restrictions), flexible load services (interim RNS, firm and non-firm contract demand services), and generation studies for electrically proximate loads. Hydro and PSH owners should monitor the tariff design details—particularly the BTMG netting rules and flexible service definitions—because these directly constrain or enable co-location and behind-the-meter hydropower arrangements in New England. The stakeholder schedule runs through November 12, 2026 (Participants Committee vote), with TC briefings on August 18–19, Markets Committee and TC reviews in September, and votes in October–November.

title: 2026-07-16 TC A04.2 New England Large & Co-Located Loads Show Cause Order (EL26-72) Compliance: Informational Report on Resource Adequacy
relevance_reason: Large-load growth and co-located load interconnection directly expand the addressable market for hydro's energy and flexibility products, while capacity-market and energy-price signals discussed here determine hydro's revenue sustainability and new-entry incentives.
stakeholders: Al McBride (ISO New England Inc.); Gordon van Welie (ISO New England Inc.)
ai_summary: ISO-NE filed a FERC compliance report (EL26-72) on resource adequacy for large loads (particularly data centers) entering New England. The report affirms that existing competitive energy and capacity markets are well-positioned to attract new supply and retain the existing fleet, citing forward energy prices currently near or at cost-of-entry for new generation. ISO-NE describes several market-refinement projects underway: reductions to the Pay-for-Performance penalty rate (which disproportionately burdens slow-start resources), Capacity Auction Reforms affecting accreditation methodology, improvements to Surplus Interconnection Service, probabilistic forecasting, and uncertainty reserves. For hydro owners, the key mechanics are (1) large-load growth will tighten energy markets and raise scarcity pricing, especially in winter when data centers compete with heating load; (2) co-location rules and interconnection protocols being developed will determine whether hydro can serve load behind-the-meter or tap surplus interconnection at existing plants; (3) PFP rate reduction and CONE updates directly affect the capacity revenues hydro operators depend on for fleet retention; and (4) probabilistic forecasting and uncertainty reserves may create new ramping/flexibility product opportunities. The report signals ISO-NE's expectation of tariff amendments to facilitate large-load entry and expects these changes to be completed within the ongoing stakeholder process.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A00 - Actions Letter
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE is conforming manuals to FERC Order 2222 (DER aggregation) and a Storage-as-Transmission-Only-Asset (SATOA) project—both of which directly affect how distributed and storage hydropower resources participate in New England markets and how pumped storage is treated operationally and financially.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Markets Committee voted to recommend support for manual conforming changes implementing FERC Order 2222 (M-11, M-28, M-RPA) and the SATOA project (M-28, M-RPA). Order 2222 enables aggregation of distributed energy resources including small hydro and storage units into virtual power plants; SATOA redefines how storage assets—including pumped-storage hydro—are operated and accredited when dedicated to transmission service rather than energy arbitrage. The vote passed with no opposition and one End User abstention; recommendation now goes to Participants Committee. For NHA members, this action signals that ISO-NE's rulebook is moving to accommodate smaller hydro through DER pathways and may reshape how pumped-storage assets are contractually and financially treated if designated for transmission-only service—affecting both revenue models and operational flexibility.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A01.a.i - Draft June 9-11, 2026 Markets Committee Meeting Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Day-Ahead Ancillary Services strike-price floor and FER demand quantity adjustments directly affect hydro's ancillary services revenue (regulation, reserves) and energy-price formation through demand-curve parameter changes; FER DQ reduction and NPF changes alter the cost basis for reserve procurement.
stakeholders: Jamie St. Pierre (Central Maine Power Company); Ben Ewing (ISO-NE)
ai_summary: ISO-NE finalized post-implementation adjustments to the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market, including a tariff floor for the DA A/S Strike Price based on distillate-fired combustion turbine cost characteristics (with regional SOx, NOx, CO2 emissions included), reduction of the Forecast Energy Requirement Demand Quantity by expected uncleared wind and solar, a $3/MWh floor on the DA A/S Impact Test Threshold, and updates to NCPC credit calculations for imports. The Non-Performance Factor is set to 115% as of May 1, 2026. The Committee voted to approve these tariff revisions without stated opposition, and implementation follows from this June 9–11, 2026 meeting. For hydro operators offering regulation and reserves, the strike-price floor and FER DQ reduction change both the demand curve and the valuation framework for their ancillary service bids; the NPF update affects the penalty structure for non-performance, relevant to any hydro unit with AS obligations.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A01.a.ii - Draft June 9-11, 2026 Joint Markets and Reliability Committees Meeting Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) directly reshapes how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited and valued in ISO-NE's capacity market, affecting RA showings, auction timing, seasonal MRIC-based accreditation, and revenue recognition under a proposed transition glide path (2-3 years); also introduces winter gas-constraint impacts on capacity procurement.
ai_summary: ISO-NE introduced a potential two-year transition mechanism for its Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) proposal, which shifts resource accreditation from current Qualified Capacity (QC) values to seasonal Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity (MRIC) values. During transition, resources would sell a weighted average of current and MRIC-based accreditation; the full CAR-SA design includes auction seasonality, auction-timing changes, and a winter gas constraint. The Committee raised concerns about equity across resource types (some resources face larger derate than others), lack of concrete year-by-year formulas, and treatment of resources with seasonal asymmetry, higher MRIC than QC, or changing MRIC year to year. The ISO clarified it does not plan to delay the core seasonal framework or deactivation changes, and offered to provide impact analysis and cost information; stakeholders broadly supported a transition for planning certainty but demanded transparent mechanics before final CAR-SA rules are adopted. No comment deadline or vote date was stated in these minutes.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual 11 Revision 63
relevance_reason: Manual 11 Revision 63 implements FERC Order 2222 conforming changes affecting DER aggregation participation rules, which creates a new pathway for small hydropower and run-of-river hydro operators to participate in ISO-NE markets as distributed energy resource aggregations alongside battery and other DER resources.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual 11 Revision 63 (effective November 1, 2026) incorporates FERC Order 2222 conforming changes, establishing market participation pathways for Demand Response Distributed Energy Resource Aggregations and Settlement Only Distributed Energy Resource Aggregations. Small hydropower and variable run-of-river units can now participate through DER aggregation frameworks, submitting supply offers and demand-reduction offers alongside traditional generator-asset pathways. The document specifies bidding timelines, scheduling responsibilities, and settlement treatment for these aggregated resources in both day-ahead and real-time energy markets. This expands strategic participation options for smaller hydro owners (especially run-of-river) who may find aggregation more operationally flexible than standalone resource registration.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual RPA - Revision 22
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Order 2222 conforming changes to DER aggregation (DERA) and Distributed Energy Resource (DER) registration requirements directly affect how small hydropower and distributed storage can participate in ISO-NE markets, including must-offer obligations, dispatch location mapping, and Lead Market Participant roles for aggregated resources.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual RPA (Registration and Performance Auditing) Revision 22, effective November 1, 2026, incorporates Order 2222 conforming changes governing registration of Distributed Energy Resource Aggregations (DERAs), DERs, and alternative technologies. The manual establishes that a DERA must have a single Lead Market Participant (the DER Aggregator) responsible for registering aggregations under one or more participation models, assigning Demand Designated Entities, mapping resources to dispatch locations, and ensuring compliance with all ISO asset requirements. DERs within a DERA cannot transfer between aggregations and must be retired and re-registered to move; the DDE is responsible for RTU installation and mapping DRRs/DRDERAs to remote terminal units. This framework creates new participation pathways for small hydropower and distributed storage but also imposes strict aggregation governance, single-point-of-control dispatch requirements, and metering/telemetry standards that could increase compliance costs for small-to-mid-size hydro operators entering aggregation structures.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes and SATOA Conforming Changes - Motions Letter
relevance_reason: Manual conforming changes for Order 2222 (DER aggregation) and SATOA (storage-as-transmission) directly affect how PSH and other storage resources participate in ISO-NE markets and how their charging and dispatch obligations are defined.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Markets Committee is voting (60% supermajority required) to approve conforming changes to three operations manuals (M-11, M-28, M-RPA) implementing FERC Order 2222 (distributed energy-resource aggregation participation) and the Storage-as-Transmission-Only-Asset (SATOA) framework. The vote is scheduled for July 7–9, 2026. For pumped-storage operators, SATOA conforming changes materially affect state-of-charge management, charging-energy settlement treatment, and transmission-service obligations when PSH is designated for grid-support roles; Order 2222 changes may open new market-participation pathways for smaller hydro and hybrid resources through aggregation, though the manual text itself is not attached to this motions letter.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes and SATOA Conforming Changes - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Order 2222 conformance enables DER aggregations (including small distributed hydro and pumped storage) to participate in ISO-NE markets; SATOA revisions establish storage-as-transmission-asset rules that directly affect PSH participation models, accounting, and registration obligations.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes conforming changes to Manuals M-11 (Market Operations), M-28 (Accounting), and M-RPA (Registration and Performance Auditing) to implement two parallel initiatives: SATOA revisions (effective Sept. 1, 2026) and Order 2222 integration (effective Nov. 1, 2026). SATOA enables storage to be treated as transmission assets in accounting and registration; Order 2222 introduces two new DERA participation models (DRDERA and SODERA) with specific metering and telemetry requirements. For pumped-storage owners, SATOA conformance affects how PSH assets are accounted for and registered; for small distributed hydro, Order 2222 conformance clarifies Lead Market Participant responsibility for metering and DERA aggregation operational rules. The Markets Committee will vote on proposed revisions at this meeting (July 7–9, 2026), followed by Participants Committee vote on August 11–12, 2026. Both mechanisms become operational on their stated effective dates.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes and SATOA Conforming Changes - Voting Memo
relevance_reason: SATOA and Order 2222 conforming changes directly affect how pumped storage and distributed hydro resources participate in ISO-NE markets—SATOA enables storage-as-transmission participation, and Order 2222 integrates DER aggregations (relevant to small hydro and run-of-river resources).
ai_summary: ISO-NE is updating Manuals M-11, M-28, and M-RPA to conform to tariff and operating-agreement changes implementing SATOA (Storage as Transmission-Only Asset) and Order No. 2222 (DER aggregation). SATOA, effective September 1, 2026, permits storage assets to function as transmission infrastructure; Order 2222, effective November 1, 2026, integrates Distributed Energy Resource Aggregations into market operations and registration. The manual updates are required to align operational, accounting, and registration procedures with these tariff revisions and to enable eligible participants to register and dispatch under the new frameworks. The Markets Committee voted on the proposed redlines at its June 9–11, 2026 meeting, with this memo documenting the conforming changes for formal approval.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - SATOA Conforming Changes - Manual 28 - Revision 67
relevance_reason: Manual update adds Storage as Transmission-Only Assets (SATOAs) accounting framework — a new participation model for storage resources including pumped-storage hydro, affecting settlement mechanics, metering domains, and revenue treatment of storage-only operational modes.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual M-28 (Market Rule 1 Accounting) is updated to Revision 67, effective September 1, 2026, to incorporate conforming changes for SATOA (Storage as Transmission-Only Assets) treatment. Section 7.2.5 newly documents SATOAs as settlement-model assets, indicating ISO-NE has established a participation pathway for storage resources (including pumped-storage hydro) to operate as transmission services without energy-market dispatch obligations. The revision adds metering and settlement accounting detail for this mode — critical for PSH owners choosing to use SATOA status for strategic flexibility, interruptibility programs, or transmission-service-only operation. This is the formal accounting manual codification; next steps appear to be implementation at or after the September 1, 2026 effective date.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A02 - SATOA Conforming Changes - Manual RPA - Revision 21
relevance_reason: Storage as Transmission-Only Asset (SATOA) registration and operational requirements directly affect pumped-storage hydro participation in ISO-NE markets, establishing compliance obligations for PSH operators seeking to register as non-energy resources.
stakeholders: ISO New England Inc. (ISO New England)
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual M-RPA Revision 21 incorporates conforming changes to implement Storage as Transmission-Only Asset (SATOA) registration procedures, effective September 1, 2026. SATOA rules allow storage facilities (including pumped-storage hydro) to register as transmission assets that provide grid services without energy arbitrage, requiring Lead Market Participant, asset owner, Host Participant, and Assigned Meter Reader authorization, plus designation of a single Dispatch Location and Designated Entity (DE) to receive ISO dispatch instructions. This creates a new compliance and operational pathway for PSH owners seeking to monetize grid-support services (frequency response, voltage support, flexibility) separately from energy sales, but operators must navigate new registration workflows, dispatch-location requirements, and dispatch-instruction protocols—a shift in how PSH interfaces with ISO-NE's operational and financial systems.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03- CAR-SA: Follow Up Medley - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: CAR-SA (Capacity Auction Reforms–Seasonal/Accreditation) introduces seasonal capacity accreditation and MRIC (Minimum Rated Installed Capacity) requirements that directly affect how hydropower and energy-limited resources—including pumped storage—qualify for and price capacity, with particular impact on hydro's accreditation values and offer formulation under new seasonal-auction structures.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's CAR-SA project (Q2 2027 effective date) implements seasonality in capacity procurement alongside accreditation reforms that shift from QC (Qualified Capacity) to MRIC-based accreditation. The presentation addresses follow-ups on ambient-air offer mechanics for thermal units, seasonal offer construction and mitigation (explaining why reconfiguration auctions remain unmitigated), accreditation impacts on offer pricing, and import-resource MRIC declarations—including a revised 16-hour energy-delivery requirement for external resources to align with internal energy-limited resources. For energy-limited hydro and PSH, the shift from QC to MRIC accreditation is the pivotal change: if accredited capacity declines, offers must recover the same going-forward costs over a smaller certified quantity, raising per-MW offers. The memo on offer formulation (due July 2026) will provide guidance on competitive offers and risk-premium pricing; stakeholders are directed to submit questions to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) ahead of the August Markets Committee, where additional detail will be discussed.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03a - CAR-SA: Proposal Overview - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) restructure capacity accreditation and seasonal procurement to reflect resource-specific reliability contributions, directly affecting how hydro and pumped-storage accreditation values are calculated and the seasonal demand curves that set their capacity revenue.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes moving from annual to seasonal (summer/winter) capacity auctions with accreditation reforms tied to Expected Unserved Energy (EUE) reduction, effective Q2 2027. The design improves resource operational-characteristic modeling—including energy-limited and storage resources—and calculates accreditation values (rMRI) based on margro reliability impact at system-adequacy criteria, rather than uniform assumptions. This revalues hydro and pumped-storage capacity by season and by performance characteristics (outage rates, generation profiles, energy limits); a transition mechanism will phase in the new rules. Key implications for storage and energy-limited hydro: accreditation will reflect their seasonal and state-dependent contributions more precisely, affecting revenue in capacity auctions and long-term investment signals. FERC accepted the prior CAR-PD filing in March 2026; CAR-SA filing anticipated by end of 2026, with key auction activities in first half of 2027.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03b - CAR-SA: Transition Mechanism, cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: CAR-SA's seasonal capacity accreditation and marginal reliability impact (MRIC) methodology directly reshapes how hydro (especially pumped storage and seasonal resources) is credited in ISO-NE's capacity market, altering revenue recognition across seasons and affecting reserve margins that determine must-offer and procurement rules.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing Capacity Auction Reforms – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) with a three-year transition beginning in CCP 2028–29 (Q2 2027 proposed effective date). The reform shifts accreditation from a long-standing annual Qualified Capacity (QC) methodology to seasonal Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity (MRIC) values that reflect resource contributions to each season's adequacy. During transition, each resource's accredited capacity will be a weighted average: Year 1 (2/3 QC + 1/3 MRIC), Year 2 (1/3 QC + 2/3 MRIC), Year 3 (full MRIC). Hydro owners—particularly seasonal resources like winter-peaking or pumped-storage operators—will experience year-by-year accreditation shifts; some resources (e.g. passive demand response and winter solar) will see accreditation rise under CAR-SA, while others (especially summer-peaking hydro) will experience lower recognized capacity. The demand curve for procurement will also shift leftward across the three-year glide path, mirroring supply-side accreditation changes to maintain unit consistency and cost equivalence. Next steps include stakeholder review of transition mechanics and expected board/Markets Committee approval in coming meetings.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03c - CAR-SA: Updated Activity Schedule - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: CAR-SA introduces seasonal capacity auction design and accreditation reforms affecting how hydro's capacity value is calculated and procured; this directly impacts capacity revenue and eligibility rules for both conventional hydro and pumped-storage resources.
stakeholders: Jasleen Singh (ISO New England); Jeff Mislak (ISO New England)
ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing the Capacity, Accreditation and Resource – Seasonal and Accreditation (CAR-SA) reform, proposed effective Q2 2027, which shifts from a single annual capacity auction to seasonal (winter/summer) auctions and incorporates accreditation reforms and a winter gas constraint. The activity schedule governs the timing of resource qualification, market-power mitigation reviews, demand-curve development, and auction windows — all of which directly affect how hydro resources are accredited, how their capacity offers are mitigated, and when they must submit data. Most relevant to pumped-storage and seasonal-constraint hydro: the seasonal framework, the integration of accreditation into RA modeling, the Installed Capacity Requirement process, and the qualification deadlines (resource-qualification data submission one month pre-auction; unexpected full forced outage window tied to seller-side market power close). The presentation is an activity-schedule roadmap with no votes or comment deadlines stated; follow-up questions may be submitted in writing to Jasleen Singh by July 27, 2026, and this is Phase 2 of the CAR workstream, with Final Design and tariff amendments scheduled for Q4 2026.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03d - CAR-SA: Storage and Energy-Limited Resource Modeling and Accreditation Design Updates Cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: CAPACITY ACCREDITATION: ISO-NE is updating how pumped-storage and energy-limited resources are credited toward resource adequacy; the rMRI adjustment mechanism directly affects the capacity value (MRIC) PSH owners receive in the ISO-NE capacity auction.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes an upward rMRI adjustment for energy-limited and storage resources in its Capacity Auction Reforms: Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) initiative, effective Q2 2027. The adjustment corrects a MARS modeling limitation that prevents storage from charging other storage during constrained hours, which currently underestimates storage reliability value. The formula scales adjusted rMRI so that very long-duration storage with zero forced outage rate would receive an rMRI of 1 (fully available). For a 10-hour battery, the winter adjustment increases rMRI from 0.414 to 0.492; for a 12-hour energy-limited resource, summer rMRI increases from 0.839 to 0.851. Class-level impacts show winter MRIC gains of 149.9 MW for energy storage and 140.0 MW for oil/dual-fuel, with larger adjustments favoring longer-duration resources. Next step: committee vote and FERC filing date not specified in excerpt; written questions due to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by July 29, 2026.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03e - CAR-SA: Resource Accreditation Modeling Cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE is modeling resource accreditation (capacity credit) for energy-limited and storage resources under the CAR-SA (Capacity Auction Reforms) proposal, directly affecting how pumped-storage hydro and run-of-river resources qualify for and receive capacity revenue in ISO-NE's forward market.
ai_summary: ISO-NE presents the final Resource Accreditation Modeling Impact Analysis for CAR-SA, effective Q2 2027, which determines capacity accreditation (rMRI) values for energy-limited and storage resources under different weather weighting and system-reliability scenarios. The analysis evaluates FirstLight's alternative load-weighting methodology, finding it understates high-load (peak system-risk) conditions and reduces net ICR by ~280 MW compared to ISO's 70-year methodology, with material impacts on ICR but limited direct impacts on storage accreditation values across durations. Pumped-storage owners in ISO-NE should monitor the final design translation into production software and any further stakeholder-requested sensitivity analyses, as the weighting of historical weather years and the LOLE reliability threshold materially affect the seasonal capacity credit each PSH unit receives and thus its annual revenue.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03f - CAR-SA: FirstLight Power - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's CAR-SA capacity accreditation design—particularly seasonal payment-rate allocation, MRI-based CONE splits, and at-criteria accreditation—directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and seasonal hydro resources are compensated and incentivized to enter, especially when supply is tight in only one season.
ai_summary: FirstLight Power presents technical analysis of interconnected design flaws in ISO-NE's Capacity Accreditation and Reserves-Seasonal Attributes (CAR-SA) proposal to the Markets Committee. The core issue: the current design allocates seasonal capacity payment rates proportional to Marginal Reliability Index (MRI) at Net ICR for each season, driven by an administrative 50/50 seasonal risk split, weather-year weighting that overstates winter event duration, and accreditation at 'at-criteria' conditions (demand-curve location inconsistent with actual clearing risk). FirstLight demonstrates that under this design, a reference resource may not recover Net CONE annually even when the system is tight in one season, suppressing entry signals for seasonal capacity. Potential solutions offered include dynamic seasonal risk splits aligned to actual system conditions, accreditation within a feasible clearing-point range, revised weather-year weighting, and payment-rate approaches that ensure annual Net CONE recovery. No deadline or vote date specified; this is positioned as urgent discussion to prevent compounding design errors before CAR-SA implementation. Pumped-storage operators—particularly those operating in winter-constrained regions or managing seasonal energy constraints—face material revenue exposure: if winter accreditation and payment rates are structurally overstated relative to summer, then summer-constrained capacity and flexibility are undervalued, and winter-heavy PSH assets may receive artificially elevated seasonal payments that don't reflect true entry incentives.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03g - CAR-SA: Flatiron Feedback, cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: CAPACITY/RA accreditation methodology — proposal to reshape how ISO-NE models marginal resource adequacy contributions (rMRI) for all resources including hydro, affecting accreditation values, capacity-market clearing prices, and revenue recovery for dispatchable and storage units under ISO-NE's Competitive Auctions with Reserves (CAR) framework.
ai_summary: Flatiron Energy presents a critique of ISO-NE's proposed Capacity Accreditation and Reserves (CAR-SA) modeling assumptions, arguing that the 'accreditation base case' (which adds 5 GW of load and uses historical 2002–03 extreme-weather years) creates artificially long marginal resource index (MRI) events that over-accredit resources designed for conditions that won't materialize. Flatiron proposes an 'Expected System Conditions' pathway: modeling expected supply and forecasted load without artificial load scaling, which would reduce or eliminate simulated MRI hours in a system with excess capacity. When MRI hours are minimal, Flatiron proposes accreditation shift to MRIC (marginal resource index credit) based on EFORd (equivalent forced outage rate) for most resource types, reflecting contingency-driven rather than energy-shortage-driven reliability events. ISO-NE's capacity demand curve and Net ICR would adjust accordingly, but the price-formation mechanism (reference-unit recovery at Net CONE × rMRI factor) would be preserved. This proposal, if adopted, would fundamentally change how all accredited resources including hydro, pumped storage, and thermal units recover capacity value, with most exposure to resource types whose value depends heavily on modeled energy availability in GE MARS software.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03h - CAR-SA: AEU, cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: Advanced Energy United proposes amendments to ISO-NE's seasonal capacity accreditation model (CAR-SA) that would directly alter how pumped storage and long-duration energy storage are valued in the capacity market, affecting storage MRIC/rMRI accreditation and investment signals.
ai_summary: Advanced Energy United presents three conceptual amendments to ISO-NE's Capacity Accreditation Reforms (CAR-SA): (1) replace peak-day weather-year weighting with multi-day event metrics to avoid overstating winter cold-snap frequency and undervaluing storage; (2) model intertie import profiles hourly rather than as flat capacity blocks to reflect overnight export availability when storage would charge; (3) reclassify storage resources as EL3 (energy-limited, fully rechargeable daily) rather than Type 3 to address systemic understatement of off-peak charging energy and state-of-charge modeling gaps. Under Amendment 3, a 4-hour battery would achieve rMRI of 0.253 instead of ISO's proposed 0.187. These amendments target the root cause that CAR-SA's accreditation model understates storage's realistic value and charging opportunities, thereby depressing investment signals for flexible resources that ISO-NE needs for seasonal adequacy and decarbonization. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in this presentation material; this represents the sponsor's position ahead of further committee consideration.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03i - CAR-SA: Market Clearing Impact Analysis - Preliminary Results, cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) redesigns the capacity market demand curve and accreditation framework that directly affects capacity revenue and seasonal participation incentives for all resources, including hydro and pumped storage.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's CAR-SA proposal restructures capacity-market demand curves to run separately for summer and winter seasons, with Seasonal Payment Rates replacing the single annual Net CONE to ensure cost recovery and constant scaling factors. The proposal derives summer SPR of $8.50/kW-month and winter SPR of $17.88/kW-month from a reference CONE resource's seasonal MRIC values and modeled capacity capabilities. This creates seasonal revenue differentiation: a resource's capacity accreditation and payment will now depend on its seasonal capability profile—critical for hydropower and pumped storage, whose seasonal energy constraints and discharge capabilities vary significantly. The ISO presented updated baseline and sensitivity results; with a proposed effective date of Q2 2027, the design is still in stakeholder feedback and modeling refinement.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03j - CAR-SA: Qualification - Presentation
relevance_reason: CAR-SA resource qualification process directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources demonstrate capacity accreditation and qualify for ISO-NE's seasonal capacity market, with material-change rules determining when resource modifications trigger re-qualification and affect Seasonal Claimed Capability and accreditation outcomes.
ai_summary: ISO-NE presented the CAR-SA (Capacity Accreditation Reform – Seasonal/Accreditation) resource qualification process, effective Q2 2027, which introduces conforming changes to the material-change framework to align with seasonal accreditation concepts. The material-change definition now references Seasonal Maximum Capability (SMCap) instead of Annual Qualified Capacity, and triggers historical-data restart for Maximum Capability and Dependable Capability when deemed material; resource qualification deadlines shift from the original to at least five business days prior to Monthly Capacity Demonstration Deadlines. Hydro and PSH operators—especially those modifying interconnection facilities, changing technology type, or adjusting capacity by >5 MW or 10%—must report material changes, update Qualification Data Submission Packages, and demonstrate updated Commercial Capacity; failure to meet deadlines can defer participation to the next seasonal or monthly auction event. Questions may be submitted to ISO-NE MC Secretary Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by July 27, 2026.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Auditing - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE is redesigning seasonal capacity accreditation rules and resource-auditing frameworks that directly affect how hydro assets (dispatchable and non-dispatchable) are credited in the capacity market and priced by seasonal dependability.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes to sunset existing Claimed Capability and Seasonal DR audits after August 31, 2027, replacing them with three new metrics: Global Maximum Capability (36-month rolling max hourly performance, updated weekly), Seasonal Maximum Capability (same but for seasonally-specific temperature conditions), and Seasonal Dependable Capability (median availability during the 100 highest load hours per season, with a performance factor applied to dispatchable resources). The framework treats dispatchable resources (those receiving electronic dispatch instructions) differently from non-dispatchable resources, applying a performance factor to dispatchable median offers. This is part of ISO-NE's broader Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) initiative scheduled for Q2 2027 implementation. Most exposed are conventional hydropower operators, especially dispatchable hydro in ISO-NE's system, whose seasonal dependable credits will now hinge on demonstrated performance during peak-load hours rather than on-demand auditing — reducing discretion to claim seasonal uplifts and tying accreditation to actual dispatch compliance and offer discipline.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Auditing - Section III.1.5-7 Redlines
relevance_reason: Tariff redlines establish claimed capability audit requirements and durations for hydroelectric resources (conventional daily pondage, run-of-river, weekly; reversible/pumped-storage hydro), directly affecting how hydro assets must demonstrate and claim their capacity values and how those claims feed into RA accreditation and revenue.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is revising its claimed capability audit rules (Section III.1.5–7 tariff redlines), with Claimed Capability Audits and Seasonal DR Audits ending August 31, 2027. The tariff specifies audit duration requirements for hydro by type: hydraulic turbine reversible (2 hours), conventional daily pondage (2 hours), conventional run-of-river (2 hours), and conventional weekly (2 hours); it exempts non-intermittent daily hydro from mandatory Establish Claimed Capability Audits and Seasonal Claimed Capability Audits. Seasonal audits must be conducted at least once per capability year (summer ≥80°F or during ISO-announced window; winter ≤32°F or window, once per three years), with failure to meet requirements resulting in zero seasonal values. This directly determines the claimed capabilities that underpin ISO-NE's capacity accreditation and RA obligations; hydro operators must ensure audits are scheduled and completed within deadlines to avoid penalty (zero seasonal value), making compliance on the August 31, 2027 sunset critical for any asset still accruing seasonal claims.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Qualification - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) directly modifies how hydropower and other resources qualify for and are accredited in seasonal capacity markets, affecting capacity revenue streams and qualification/accreditation obligations for New England hydro operators.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing Seasonal Capacity Auctions and Relative Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI)–based accreditation in place of annual auctions and Qualified Capacity values, with tariff revisions targeting Q2 2027 effective date. The qualification process will occur twice per year (summer and winter), and resources must requalify seasonally with updated Qualification Data Submission Packages including Global Maximum Capability estimates. Hybrid Capacity Resources and Distributed Energy Resources face new submission requirements; material-change procedures are tightened with pre-consultation deadlines, and forced-outage treatment now reduces seasonal MRI Capacity Values to zero for the affected period. Hydro operators—especially those with seasonal peaking patterns or variable output—will need to adapt qualification and capability-demonstration cycles to the new biannual cadence and MRI-based accreditation methodology; tariff amendment votes are expected Q3 2026 with FERC filing to follow.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Qualification- Section III.15.2 Redlines
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Tariff redlines to ISO-NE's capacity qualification rules (Section III.15.2) directly affect how hydro and hybrid resources qualify for the Annual Seasonal Capacity Auction, including seasonal MRI capacity value thresholds, qualification data submission requirements, and hybrid resource configuration rules—all material to hydro's capacity accreditation and revenue.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Markets Committee is advancing tariff redlines to Section III.15.2 (Capacity Resource Qualification) governing participation in the Annual Seasonal Capacity Auction. Key changes include: redefining summer period as June–September and winter period as October–May (except Demand Capacity Resources); clarifying qualification data submission requirements for resources never previously counted as capacity; and adding detailed requirements for Hybrid Capacity Resources (including facility limits, seasonal MRI allocation percentages, storage capability, grid charging, round-trip efficiency, and configuration-type classification). The document also specifies that Import Capacity Resources must be backed by external resources or control areas, and that firm fuel capacity can be qualified in advance of Winter auctions. These redlines materially affect how hydro (especially pumped-storage hybrids and imports) document capability and qualify for seasonal accreditation; next step is Markets Committee decision and board/stakeholder feedback.

title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03l - CAR-SA: Winter Gas Constraint, cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: The proposal establishes a non-firm gas capacity demand curve and bilateral-transaction rules affecting how gas-backed resources—and by extension, competing dispatchable and storage resources—are accredited and compensated in ISO-NE's capacity market, directly impacting hydro's competitive position and RA value.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) introduce a non-firm gas capacity demand curve effective Q2 2027 to reflect winter regional gas-supply constraints in capacity-market pricing. The proposal specifies: (1) gas-contract requirements (16-hour daily firmness for LNG; affidavit requirements dropped); (2) bilateral CSO (Capacity Supply Obligation) transaction limits tied to truncation points on the demand curve, preventing overtrading of constrained non-firm capacity across zones; and (3) restrictions on gas-only resource-backed imports, which cannot sell non-firm CSO in winter. The mechanism directly affects hydro's accreditation and capacity revenue: by capping non-firm gas capacity and pricing it separately, ISO-NE reduces the volume of low-cost gas capacity competing in winter auctions, tightening supply and raising clearing prices for all resources—especially valuable for pumped-storage hydro and reservoir hydro with winter peaking capability. The filing to stakeholders occurred in July 2026; the ISO notes it will "assess potential enhancements... in the months to come and after the CAR-SA filing." Most exposed are PSH and winter-peaking conventional hydro in zonal export-constrained areas, which benefit from reduced gas competition in winter RA auctions.

meeting_title: NEPOOL Power Supply Planning Committee Meeting
title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A00 Agenda
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's annual capacity auction and ICR/capacity accreditation methodology development directly affects how New England's hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited for resource adequacy and capacity revenue; demand-resource performance audits and load forecasting also inform the adequacy construct that drives RA value.
stakeholders: Helve Saarela (ISO-NE); Sam Allen (ISO-NE); Victoria Rojo (ISO-NE)
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Power Supply Planning Committee will meet July 1, 2026, to establish the 2026 schedule for developing ICR-Related Values (Installed Capacity Requirements, Locational Seasonal Reserves, Minimum Capacity Levels, Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Constraints, and MRI Demand Curves) that feed the annual reconfiguration auction, plus review Summer 2025 and Winter 2025–2026 demand-resource performance and update the 2026 CELT load forecast. The ISO's capacity accreditation methodology and demand-resource audit results directly influence the reliability margin and seasonal reserve requirements against which hydro RA value is calculated; changes to the load forecast or demand-resource performance assumptions ripple into the adequacy baseline and thus hydro's capacity accreditation share. The agenda indicates this is a schedule-setting meeting with no proposals tabled yet, so substantive changes are likely to emerge in subsequent PSPC meetings through the 2026 cycle.

meeting_title: NEPOOL Power Supply Planning Committee Meeting
title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A02 2026 Schedule for Development of New England's ICR-Related Values
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) and accreditation values determine how much capacity New England hydro and other resources can claim toward resource adequacy; this schedule governs the development cycle for ARA 3 (2027–2028), which directly affects hydro's capacity accreditation and the revenue hydro receives from New England's capacity auction.
ai_summary: ISO-NE has released the development schedule for Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) and related values—including Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Credit (HQICC), Maximum Capacity Limit (MCL), and Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) demand curves—to be used in the third Annual Reconfiguration Auction (ARA 3) for the 2027–2028 Capacity Commitment Period, scheduled to occur in March 2027.
