The PSPC will review assumptions and proposed values across meetings in August, September, and October 2026; the RC and PC will vote on October 20 and November 12, 2026, respectively; FERC filing is due by November 30, 2026. For New England hydro owners, this proceeding determines the accreditation rules and demand-curve parameters that govern how much capacity they can claim in the auction and the clearing prices they face—particularly relevant for owners supplying reserve margin and for understanding how interconnection credits and seasonal constraints shape their RA revenues in ISO-NE's forward capacity market.

meeting_title: NEPOOL Power Supply Planning Committee Meeting
title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A05 Review Of 2026 CELT Load Forecast
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's 2026 gross load forecast directly drives the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) for the Forward Capacity Market auctions and the Annual Reconfiguration Auction, which sets the demand curve and accreditation targets that determine hydro and PSH capacity revenue and clearing in New England.
ai_summary: ISO-NE presents the 2026 vintage gross load forecast using its pre-existing methodology (pre-CELT 2025) to support calculation of the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) for upcoming Annual Reconfiguration Auctions (ARA). The forecast incorporates load data through end of 2025, reconstitutes for behind-the-meter PV and Passive Demand Resources (PDRs) using FCA 18 CSO values, and applies separate models for energy and seasonal peaks. For the 2027–2028 capacity commitment period, the 2026 forecast yields summer 50/50 and 90/10 peaks of 27,835 MW and 29,778 MW, and winter peaks of 22,461 MW and 23,337 MW, with an annual energy forecast of 136,064 GWh; ARA-specific adjustments are reported separately. This forecast directly determines the capacity-market demand curve and resource-adequacy baseline against which hydro and PSH plants are accredited and paid for capacity in New England's FCM.

title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC Meeting Minutes - Final
relevance_reason: ISO-NE proposes eliminating Estimated QC exceptions and composite offers under Capacity Auction Reforms Seasonal Accreditation, and sets minimum resource-size thresholds (100 kW MRIC) with aggregation allowances — changes that directly affect hydro and small-hydro qualification, accreditation, and participation pathways in the FCM.
ai_summary: ISO-NE presented refinements to its Capacity Auction Reforms Seasonal Accreditation (CAR SA) proposal, addressing resource qualification under the continuous process: resources under 100 kW of Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity may aggregate to meet the 100 kW minimum threshold; prior FCM minimum-size exceptions will not carry forward; the Estimated Qualified Capacity process (which previously addressed limited audit data) will be eliminated; and composite offers will be discontinued due to separate seasonal clearing. These changes reshape qualification burden and aggregation rules for small hydro, run-of-river, and variable hydro operators, most of whom have historically relied on exception pathways or composite bidding. The document does not state a comment deadline or next vote date.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A00 Agenda - Revision
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue applications and transmission planning projects affect hydro deliverability and strategic options for existing plants; PSPC process establishes resource adequacy and seasonal energy assessment inputs that directly influence hydro capacity accreditation and planning horizons.
stakeholders: Bruce Jagolinzer (Central Maine Power); Steve Allen (Eversource Energy); Anthony DeSimone (Flatiron Energy); Andrew Kniska (ISO-NE); Helve Saarela (ISO-NE); Jinye Zhao (ISO-NE); Mike Knowland (ISO-NE); Pradip Vijayan (ISO-NE); Jonathan Kipp (NERP)
ai_summary: ISO-NE Reliability Committee meets June 25, 2026, to review interconnection applications (Hartland Solar 140 MW, Flatiron Battery 200 MW/800 MWh, Milford Power AVR upgrade), transmission cost allocations totaling $448.6 M in rebuild projects (Eversource South Naugatuck–Devon and Hurd State Park area reconductoring), and to kick off the 2026 Power Supply Planning Committee (PSPC) process for Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) and related values development. The PSPC and 2026 long-term energy assessment sensitivity review establish the framework by which hydro capacity is accredited and seasonal adequacy margins are calculated — outcomes that determine revenue exposure during scarcity conditions and baseline planning assumptions for RA. Transmission upgrades affect import/export capability and locational value of existing generation. Items are presented for advisory recommendation and future votes; no specific comment deadlines are quoted in the agenda.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A00 RC Motions Letter - Revision
relevance_reason: Two battery storage project revisions and a synchronous generator control upgrade undergoing ISO interconnection reliability review — battery participation models and synchronous-machine standards affect storage competition and hydro's operating obligations in ISO-NE.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Reliability Committee voted on three interconnection Proposed Plan Application (PPA) revisions: a battery project (QP 1320, Flatiron Energy and New England Power), a solar project with related interconnection updates (QP 1100, Central Maine Power), and an automatic voltage regulator (AVR) upgrade at Milford Power Plant (a synchronous generator). Each motion recommends ISO-NE find no significant adverse effect on transmission stability or reliability. These are routine reliability-screen passes in the interconnection queue, but the battery revision (FLA-24-G03-Rev1, FLA-24-T03-Rev1, NEP-24-T04-Rev1) is substantive: it signals ISO-NE's acceptance of battery participation models and charging-energy treatment precedents that shape how storage competes against pumped hydro for energy and ancillary-services revenue. The AVR upgrade at a conventional plant illustrates ISO-NE's reinforcement of synchronous-machine ride-through and voltage-support standards, which create implicit compliance costs for traditional hydro units. All three items have completed their June 2026 review cycle with approval.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A01.1 MC/RC May 12-14, 2026 Meeting Minutes
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) restructures how hydro and other dispatchable resources qualify for and clear seasonal capacity obligations, directly affecting accreditation thresholds, outage-treatment rules, and capacity-market revenue for smaller hydro assets and pumped storage.
stakeholders: Aleksandar Mitreski (Brookfield Renewable Trading And Marketing LP); Tom Kaslow (Firstlight Power Management LLC); Jeff Mislak (ISO-NE); Jennifer Engelson (ISO-NE); Christopher Sherman (Nautilus Power, LLC); Dan Allegretti (Pawtucket Power Holding Co.)
ai_summary: ISO-NE staff presented the resource-qualification framework for CAR-SA, which eliminates the existing Estimated Qualified Capacity (QC) process, imposes a 100 kW Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity (MRIC) minimum for standalone participation, and removes grandfathering for legacy sub-100 kW resources. Committee members, including representatives of generation owners and municipal utilities, raised material concerns about the disqualification of 60–80 smaller legacy resources unless they aggregate into distributed capacity structures, treatment of planned and partial outages (especially long-term equipment outages), and the risk of seasonal capacity-clearing obligations during known extended unavailability. ISO-NE committed to stakeholder consultation on planned-outage and EFORD (forced-outage-rate) rules with the Independent Market Monitor but did not commit to changes; preliminary activity schedule for CAR-SA implementation is now under development with Summer 2028 as a truncated transition season. Small hydro and PSH operators below 100 kW threshold are most directly affected; aggregation and distributed-resource pathways remain open but require reconfiguration.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A01.1 MC/RC May 21, 2026 Meeting Minutes
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's seasonal tie-benefits and capacity-zone formation methodologies directly affect the accreditation and RA value of hydropower and pumped storage, particularly seasonal capacity imports from neighboring regions and zonal pricing signals that influence dispatch and revenue.
stakeholders: Dan Patnaude (ISO-NE); Dr. Ben Chenault (ISO-NE); Manasa Kotha (ISO-NE)
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Reliability Committee discussed three capacity-market design topics with votes scheduled at future meetings. On seasonal tie benefits, the ISO confirmed that Capacity Import Capability (CIC) will be updated during intervening years using an annual sensitivity-study threshold, and that summer and winter tie benefits are calculated together in a single study aligned triennially with NYISO's model via the Long-Range Adequacy Overview (LRAO). On capacity-zone formation, the ISO proposed removing gas-only resources from objective-criteria testing to trigger price separation signals, but members from the Alternative Resources, Supplier, and End User sectors raised concerns: Alternative Resources opposed removing all gas-only resources; Suppliers requested application of the methodology to FCA 18 for transparency; and one Supplier advocated that all zones be modeled at all times. The removal of gas-only resources could reduce seasonal import capability assumptions and tighten capacity zones in winter months—raising accreditation and RA value for dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped storage during peak-scarcity periods, particularly in constrained zones. Votes on both seasonal tie benefits and capacity-zone formation are scheduled for a future meeting (no specific date stated).

title: 2026-06-25 RC A03.02 Queue Position 1320 (QP 1320) Battery Project Revision PPAs – Level III - FLA-24-G03-Rev1, FLA-24-T03-Rev1, NEP-24-T04-Rev1, ES-24-T44-Rev1
relevance_reason: Battery project interconnection and participation rules set precedent for pumped-storage and energy-storage resource modeling, queue management, and interconnection deliverability — a direct concern for PSH developers and existing hydro owners competing for interconnection queue position and deliverability.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Reliability Committee is reviewing interconnection PPAs and grid-impact assessments for Queue Position 1320, a battery-storage project at Level III of interconnection study. Battery resource treatment — including state-of-charge modeling, charging/discharging rules, and deliverability assessment in the interconnection queue — directly informs how pumped-storage hydro will be modeled and evaluated for network impacts in the same FERC-regulated process. The document itself contains no text extraction; stakeholder names and next steps cannot be determined from metadata alone, but attendance of this RC meeting agenda item will establish which developers, NEPOOL members, and ISO staff are shaping battery-interconnection precedents that extend to PSH queue projects.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A03.03 Milford Power LLC, Milford Power Plant AVR Control Upgrade PPA – Level III - DYN-26-X01
relevance_reason: Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) control upgrade at a synchronous power plant touches synchronous-unit operating standards and reliability requirements that ISO-NE enforces on dispatchable generators, affecting Milford's compliance obligations and operational costs.
ai_summary: Milford Power LLC proposes an AVR control upgrade at its Milford Power Plant, submitted to ISO-NE Reliability Committee as a Level III Voltage and Transient Stability Review (VSTR-26-X01). AVR upgrades are part of the operating standards ISO-NE applies to synchronous generation for voltage support and transient stability — capability that hydro and other synchronous units must maintain. The upgrade's approval or conditions imposed will determine Milford's compliance pathway and any capital requirements associated with maintaining or enhancing its voltage-support obligations.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A05.1 Planning Procedures 5-1 and 5-3
relevance_reason: ISO-NE is creating a new requirement for large load interconnections (≥20 MW) to submit Proposed Plan Applications, which affects hydro owners' strategic options by changing transmission planning visibility for major loads that can significantly impact regional scarcity and dispatch value of existing generation.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes updates to Planning Procedures 5-1 and 5-3 to enhance the Proposed Plan Application (PPA) process, including a new form and requirements for large load facilities (≥20 MW peak demand). The changes formalize how new or materially changed large loads must be evaluated for their impact on system stability and reliability before interconnection, with material changes including capacity increases ≥1 MW and changes to load function (e.g., cryptocurrency mining, AI training). This makes large-load interconnections visible in the planning framework for the first time, allowing hydro owners to anticipate transmission congestion and scarcity conditions that affect energy pricing and dispatch opportunity. The proposal targets September 2026 implementation, with redlines and draft PPA forms planned for the July 2026 Reliability Committee meeting.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A06 Power Supply Planning Committee (PSPC) Kick-Off
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document establishes the development schedule and parameters for ISO-NE's 2027 Annual Reconfiguration Auction (ARA 3), which determines the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR), Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Credit (HQICC), Maximum Capacity Limit (MCL), and demand curves for the 2027–2028 capacity commitment period — directly affecting capacity accreditation, reserve requirements, and revenue for hydro and pumped-storage resources participating in ISO-NE's capacity market.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is launching development of capacity parameters for the 2027 Annual Reconfiguration Auction (ARA 3), with scheduled PSPC reviews from July through October 2026, RC and PC votes in October–November 2026, and FERC filing by November 30, 2026. The values under development include the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR), Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Credit (HQICC), Maximum Capacity Limit (MCL), and System-Wide and Capacity Zone Marginal Reliability Impact demand curves — all determinants of how much capacity must be procured and how hydro and pumped-storage units are credited and compensated in the 2027–2028 auction. Hydro and PSH owners should monitor the July 1 PSPC meeting (load forecast and ADCR performance review), August 27 assumptions discussion, and October 15 proposed values review to understand accreditation adjustments and to submit input; the November 30 FERC filing date is the hard deadline for compliance.

title: 2026-06-25 RC A07 2026 Long-Term Energy Assessments Sensitivity Process
relevance_reason: Long-term energy-adequacy assessment methodology and sensitivity analyses directly inform resource-adequacy constructs and the supply-adequacy signals that hydro operators depend on for scarcity-driven revenue and investment decisions; the sensitivity scenarios (load growth, resource retirements, import constraints) define the conditions under which hydro's flexibility and dispatchability are valued.
ai_summary: ISO-NE has established a Probabilistic Energy Adequacy Tool (PEAT) and Regional Energy Shortfall Threshold (REST) framework to conduct 5- and 10-year energy assessments (study years 2031 and 2036). For 2026, the ISO is running 19 selected sensitivities spanning load variations (+10% net load, +100% large load), resource-mix changes (offshore-wind deletions up to 3,650 MW in 2036; RFO and NG retirements of 2,000–4,000 MW), and fuel/import constraints (-50% imports, ±10% oil/LNG inventory). The replacement strategy for retired resources uses interconnection-queue composition (70% batteries, 15% PV, 15% onshore wind) scaled by nameplate-to-capacity factors, not direct assumption of hydro replacement. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the most material scenarios are the large-load and import-constrained sensitivities, which will reveal energy-shortfall risk periods and durations under high-scarcity conditions — data that affects seasonal accreditation exposure and the likelihood of sustained high-price regimes. The methodology explicitly excludes offshore wind from the replacement portfolio due to buildout uncertainty, leaving a net-generation gap in retirement scenarios that hydro supply could fill. No formal comment deadline or next action date is stated in this overview.

title: 2026-06-24 TC A00 Agenda - Rev. 2
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Capacity auction seasonal/accreditation reforms (Item 2.0) directly affect hydro and pumped-storage resource accreditation, a core revenue stream; Asset Condition Reviewer (Item 4.0–4.2) may impose new operating obligations on transmission-connected dispatchable resources including hydro.
initiatives: Asset Condition Reviewer [Active]; Capacity Auction Reforms [Active]
stakeholders: Fatou Dieng (ISO-NE); Monica Gonzalez (ISO-NE); Drew Landry (Maine Office of Public Advocate (ME OPA)); Jackie Hansen (PTO AC (Participant Transmission Owner, Alternative Consultant))
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Transmission Committee is reviewing two substantive proposals: (1) Capacity Auction Reforms on seasonal and accreditation frameworks (Item 2.0, 2nd committee meeting, future vote pending, WMPP ID 185) — tariff redlines align deactivations in a seasonal structure, likely affecting how hydro and PSH are accredited for seasonal capacity value; and (2) Asset Condition Reviewer updates (Items 4.1–4.2, 6th committee meeting, 66.67% vote required) to the Transmission Operating Agreement and Tariff that would impose new asset-condition assessment and operating standards on transmission-connected resources. The Maine Office of Public Advocate is proposing amendments to the TOA/Tariff related to the Asset Condition Reviewer proposal. Next action: TC votes on both proposals are scheduled for this meeting (June 24, 2026); the seasonal accreditation vote follows a future agenda item.

title: 2026-06-24 TC A02 CAR-SA Deactivations - Section II Redlines
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's deactivation rules directly affect hydro and PSH capacity-market participation, retirement economics, and interconnection-rights termination — core revenue and strategic options for Northeast hydro owners.
initiatives: Asset Condition Reviewer [Active]; Capacity Auction Reforms [Active]
ai_summary: ISO-NE has redlined Section II of its tariff to establish a formal Deactivation Notification process for permanent resource retirements, effective for the Capacity Commitment Period beginning June 1, 2028. Generators seeking to retire must file a deactivation notice by the first business day within 12 months of each CCP start; resources >20 MW are subject to a 90-day reliability review, after which ISO may delay deactivation if needed for local transmission reliability and offer net going-forward-cost compensation. Key mechanics: (1) resources that never held Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNR or equivalent) can deactivate by simple written notice; (2) resources inactive for three calendar years are deemed automatically deactivated unless repower-application protections apply; (3) Lead Market Participants must submit detailed cost workbooks and officer affidavits, subject to Internal Market Monitor audit. Most exposed are Northeast hydro owners contemplating retirement, especially run-of-river or peaking units currently holding CNR capacity — the reliability-review gate and potential delayed-deactivation obligation (with only net-going-forward-cost recovery) can trap owners in the market longer than planned, and the three-year automatic-deactivation clock creates planning urgency for units on extended outages.

title: 2026-06-24 TC A02 CAR-SA: Initial Deactivations Redlines Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE is implementing seasonal capacity accreditation as part of Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited, deactivated, and compensated in the capacity market — a core revenue stream.
initiatives: Asset Condition Reviewer [Active]; Capacity Auction Reforms [Active]
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms–Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) initiative is revising tariff provisions to align deactivation rules, cost-workbook submissions, conduct tests, and portfolio-benefit tests with a shift from annual to seasonal capacity commitment periods and marginal reliability impact (MRI) capacity accreditation. The deactivation redlines (WMPP ID 185), presented to the Transmission Committee in June 2026, conform existing provisions to seasonal terminology and introduce equivalent CNR Capability concepts, with a proposed Q2 2027 effective date. The revisions touch multiple sections of the ISO-NE tariff (II.48–II.55 and III.A.24) that govern how resources request and are approved for deactivation, how their deactivation cost workbooks are evaluated, and how the conduct and portfolio-benefit tests determine whether deactivation is economically justified. Hydro and pumped-storage operators in ISO-NE will need to recalculate opportunity costs, break-even prices, and seasonal cash flows to comply with the new submission requirements; the shift to MRI-based accreditation means their deactivation threshold (20 MW reliability review) and compensation mechanics are redefined in terms of seasonal marginal capacity contribution rather than annual ratings. Next steps include Markets Committee review of accreditation modeling (June–July 2026), tariff redline revisions, and anticipated FERC filing in Q4 2026.

title: 2026-06-24 TC A05 FERC Show Cause Order to ISO-NE & PTOs (Docket No. EL26-72)
relevance_reason: FERC Show Cause Order directing ISO-NE to establish tariff provisions for large-load integration, co-located generation, and behind-the-meter generation—directly affecting how hydro and storage resources can interconnect, take transmission service, and participate in wholesale markets alongside large industrial loads.
initiatives: Asset Condition Reviewer [Active]; Capacity Auction Reforms [Active]
stakeholders: Eric Runge (Day Pitney LLP / NEPOOL Counsel); Margaret Czepiel (Day Pitney LLP / NEPOOL Counsel)
ai_summary: FERC issued a Show Cause Order (Docket EL26-72, 195 FERC ¶ 61,215) finding ISO-NE's Tariff preliminarily unjust and unreasonable for lack of large-load integration provisions, with ISO-NE and PTOs required to respond by August 17, 2026, or request abeyance by August 3 to file a Section 205 tariff proposal by November 16. The Order identifies five deficiency categories: transmission-service application procedures, cost transparency and cost-shifting, co-location and behind-the-meter generation rules, new flexible transmission services, and generator interconnection for load-serving facilities. Most material to hydro and pumped storage: FERC mandates new interconnection service options for generators serving electrically proximate or co-located large loads (50+ MW) and requires tariff rules governing how behind-the-meter generation nets against load for transmission charges and ancillary-service cost allocation. This directly enables co-located storage/hydro at large industrial sites—including data-center clusters—to be studied and interconnected under expedited or load-limited frameworks, but requires ISO-NE to clarify which ancillary-service costs (regulation, black start) large loads cannot avoid and to ensure cost allocation does not shift to other wholesale customers. ISO-NE must also file an informational report on resource-adequacy proposals by July 20, 2026.

title: 2026-06-24 TC A05 NEPOOL Counsel Memo Summarizing FERC Show Cause Order to ISO-NE & PTOs (Docket No. EL26-72)
relevance_reason: FERC's show-cause order to ISO-NE on large-load and co-located-load integration directly affects hydro's strategic options — co-location rules, new transmission service definitions, and generator interconnection pathways for resources serving co-located load will reshape how hydropower (especially pumped storage and run-of-river) can participate in transmission service markets and capture value from load-proximate generation.
initiatives: Asset Condition Reviewer [Active]; Capacity Auction Reforms [Active]
stakeholders: Eric Runge (Day Pitney LLP (NEPOOL Counsel)); Margaret Czepiel (Day Pitney LLP (NEPOOL Counsel))
ai_summary: FERC issued a show-cause order (June 18, 2026, Docket EL26-72) to ISO-NE and Participating Transmission Owners, preliminarily finding the tariff unjust and unreasonable for lacking provisions on large-load and co-located-load integration. ISO-NE has 60 days to propose tariff changes addressing five categories: application procedures for large loads (defined as ≥50 MW peak at single site, ≥69 kV), cost transparency and cost-recovery agreements, rates and terms for co-location (co-located load defined as end-use customer load physically connected to a generating facility), new transmission services (interim NITS, firm and non-firm contract demand), and generator interconnection for resources serving electrically proximate or co-located load. The order defines three interconnection approaches for generators: new study process matching output to load forecast, use of existing service at existing POI, or load-limited service with no injection. ISO-NE must also file a 30-day informational report on resource adequacy for new large loads. This order parallels concurrent FERC actions in PJM and SPP and builds on Secretary of Energy ANOPR RM26-4-000 (October 2025). Hydro owners should note that co-location rules and new transmission service architecture will determine whether existing or new hydropower facilities can serve co-located or electrically proximate loads, and how ancillary services and BTMG netting are treated—directly affecting revenue models for pumped storage and run-of-river plants positioned near emerging data-center loads in New England.

title: 2026-06-23 PAC A03.0 2026 Economic Study - Stakeholder-Requested Scenario Submission
relevance_reason: The Economic Study process directly informs ISO-NE's long-term transmission planning, resource-adequacy decisions, and identification of system efficiency needs—outcomes that shape hydro owners' portfolio positioning, transmission deliverability, and exposure to competitively-procured transmission upgrades; the document opens a submission window for stakeholder-requested scenarios that hydro operators may use to model resource-policy or system-design changes affecting their operations.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is conducting its 2026 Economic Study to model future resource needs, transmission limitations, and regional emissions across 10+ year horizons under multiple scenarios (Benchmark, Policy, System Efficiency Needs, and Stakeholder-Requested). Stakeholder-requested scenarios and sensitivities must be submitted by Friday, July 24, 2026, to pacmatters@iso-ne.com; the PAC will prioritize proposals in Q3 2026, with preliminary Benchmark results presented to the PAC in Q3 2026 and a full report expected by December 2027. Hydro owners and pumped-storage operators can use this window to request analysis of operational policies (e.g., dual-fuel peaker operation, electrification scenarios, variable-resource integration) that would affect their competitive position or regional scarcity signals; the results inform transmission investment, system-efficiency thresholds, and capacity/resource-adequacy assumptions that determine whether hydro faces transmission constraints or peaking-value uplift. The acronym list explicitly includes hydro resource types (HDR, HDP, HY) and Hydro-Québec imports (HQ, PHII), signaling that hydro dispatch and cross-border supply are modeled endogenously.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03 - DAAS Post-Implementation Adjustments - MA AGO Comments in Support
relevance_reason: Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market design changes directly affect reserve procurement costs and pricing — hydro's core revenue stream for regulation and reserve provision — through adjustments to reserve requirements, renewable-integration parameters, and strike-price thresholds.
stakeholders: Massachusetts Office of the Attorney General (Commonwealth of Massachusetts); Emily Laine (ISO New England)
ai_summary: Massachusetts Attorney General's Office supports ISO-NE's proposed post-implementation adjustments to the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services (DA A/S) market, citing cost overruns in the market's first year (actual incremental cost $921–974 million vs. $140 million forecast). The proposals are: (1) reduce the Non-Performance Factor for Total Ten-Minute Reserves from 120% to 115% to right-size reserve quantities; (2) adjust the Forecast Energy Requirement downward to count full expected real-time Front-of-the-Meter renewable generation, not just cleared DA quantities; and (3) set a Strike Price floor based on efficient Combustion Turbine short-run marginal costs, which would better align with actual generator costs and reduce closeout risk. ISO-NE targets October 23, 2026 implementation. The adjustments would have reduced costs by an estimated $292 million (30% of incremental costs) if applied retroactively. These changes alter the supply curves and clearing prices for reserves hydro typically supplies, with greatest impact during low-load hours and periods of tight system conditions.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03a - DA/AS Post-Implementation Adjustments - Presentation
relevance_reason: Day-ahead ancillary services price formation and demand-quantity adjustments directly affect the value of hydro's regulation and reserve products, and the strike-price floor methodology using combustion-turbine marginal cost creates a reference-level precedent for use-limited resource mitigation.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes targeted adjustments to its day-ahead ancillary services (DA A/S) design, effective October 22, 2026, based on February 2026 Internal Market Monitor recommendations. The changes include: (1) reducing the Forecast Energy Requirement Demand Quantity (FER DQ) by expected uncleared front-of-the-meter wind and solar supply to better reflect actual reliability needs; (2) setting a daily floor for the DA A/S strike price (K) based on the marginal cost of an efficient, distillate-fired combustion turbine, including regional emissions costs; and (3) establishing a $3/MWh floor on the Impact Test Threshold. For hydro operators, the strike-price floor methodology is the key mechanic—it ties ancillary-services scarcity pricing to a fossil marginal-cost reference, which will raise the opportunity cost against which hydro's must-offer obligation is evaluated and may increase procurement volume during scarcity events when hydro's regulation and reserve products clear at higher prices. The FER DQ reduction for variable wind/solar will also shift the composition of reliability-based demand, potentially favoring the fast-ramping and continuous-availability profile that hydro supplies. ISO-NE Markets Committee votes on these changes June 9–10, 2026, with Participants Committee vote scheduled June 16–18, 2026.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03a - DA/AS Post-Implementation Adjustments - Voting Memo
relevance_reason: ISO-NE proposes adjustments to day-ahead ancillary services market design—specifically non-performance factor, strike price floor, and forecast energy requirement demand—which directly affect the revenue hydro and other dispatchable resources receive for supplying reserves and flexibility.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes post-implementation adjustments to its Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market following the Internal Market Monitor's February 2026 review. The changes include: reducing the non-performance factor from 120% to 115% (already in effect April 30, 2026); adding a strike price floor tied to distillate-fired combustion turbine costs to reduce the range between short-run marginal costs and strike price; implementing a $3/MWh floor on the Impact Test Threshold to address over-mitigation risk; and reducing Forecast Energy Requirement Demand Quantity by forecasted uncleared wind and solar to better align day-ahead procurement with reserve adequacy analysis. The proposal also modifies Net Commitment Period Compensation to prevent ineligible imports from receiving the FER price. ISO-NE targets implementation October 22, 2026, and is requesting Markets Committee vote. Hydro operators supplying day-ahead ancillary services—especially those exposed to closeout cost risk when the strike price exceeds short-run marginal cost—will see adjusted revenue signals and reserve procurement levels; operators in ISO-NE's footprint should monitor the mechanics of the strike price floor and its impact on their day-ahead AS clearing economics.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03b- DA/AS Post-Implementation Adjustments - FirstLight Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Strike Price Floor in day-ahead ancillary services pricing directly affects hydro and pumped-storage revenue from reserves (regulation, spinning reserve); FirstLight's challenge to the Floor definition and cost–benefit tradeoff is a position-taking document on a tariff amendment in active discussion at ISO-NE Markets Committee.
ai_summary: FirstLight opposes ISO-NE's proposed Strike Price Floor in the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services (DAAS) tariff amendment. The Floor would set a minimum day-ahead reserve clearing price based on an "efficient distillate-fired combustion turbine," but FirstLight argues the tariff language is too vague (heat rate, emissions cost index, fuel-price index unspecified), creates long-term investment uncertainty, and delivers poor cost–benefit: a 2% reduction in DAAS costs while reducing financial incentives for reserves in 41–47% of cleared resources during 50% of hours. FirstLight proposes instead striking the Floor language and replacing it with a filing-letter commitment to improve the Gaussian Mixture Model used to forecast real-time LMP. The document presents ISO-NE's own data showing the Floor would increase strike prices by an average of 238% outside winter months (Dec–Feb), with greatest impact during shoulder seasons when supply is tightest. No explicit vote or comment deadline is stated in this presentation.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04a - CAR-SA: Transition Mechanism Introduction - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's CAR-SA capacity accreditation reform directly alters how seasonal capacity values are calculated and compensated for all resources including hydro, shifting from Qualified Capacity to MRIC-based seasonal accreditation with a multi-year transition glide path that affects revenue stability and clearing outcomes.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes a Capacity Auction Reforms – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) transition mechanism (target Q2 2027 effective date) that will gradually shift resource accreditation from current Qualified Capacity to proposed seasonal MRIC (Minimum Reliability Impact Contribution) values over two transition years (two summer and two winter seasons), with full phased design taking effect in Capacity Commitment Period 21. The transition uses a weighted-average approach: in Year 1, a 100 MW QC resource with 70 MW summer MRIC and 40 MW winter MRIC would be accredited at 90 MW (summer) and 80 MW (winter); by Year 3 it falls to the full MRIC floor (70/40 MW). Hydro owners will face material accreditation volatility during transition because MRIC values depend on seasonal performance during modeled Minimum Reliability Interest hours, potentially creating seasonal mismatches between what resources can offer and what demand curves procure. ISO-NE is still assessing demand-curve transition mechanics and whether to transition the winter gas constraint; comments and questions are due to Markets Committee Secretary Jasleen Singh (Jsingh@iso-ne.com) by June 24, 2026, with further stakeholder meetings scheduled through Q3 2026 before FERC filing.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04b - CAR-SA: Resource Accreditation Modeling cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) establish resource accreditation modeling that directly determines how much capacity hydro and storage resources can sell and at what value — the foundation for their RA revenue.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is modeling accreditation outcomes under its proposed CAR-SA design using a 70-year weather-adjusted load dataset (weighted subset of years 2000/01–2022/23 for winter; 2007–2023 for summer) to capture long-term weather variability and peak-load scenarios. Sensitivity analysis shows that explicitly modeling all 70 years of winter hourly loads produces nearly identical net ICR, reliability metrics (LOLE, LOLH, EUE), and accreditation results (rMRI) across all resource types — including Daily/Weekly Hydro (96.1–96.2%), Energy Storage (20.9–21.7%), and IPR-Hydro (59.9–61.0%) — compared to the ISO's proposed weighted-year approach. The ISO assessed and rejected FirstLight Resource Management's alternate block-based weighting methodology, concluding it ignores inter-block weather correlations and temporal heterogeneity, understating the likelihood of severe winters and distorting the upper tail of the load distribution where resource adequacy is most critical. The proposed framework will remain in place for CAR-SA implementation targeting Q2 2027; FirstLight's alternative approach was a stakeholder submission rather than ISO proposal.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04c - CAR-SA: Storage and Energy-Limited Resource Modeling and Accreditation Design Updates Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE proposes an accreditation adjustment formula for energy-limited and storage resources that directly affects how pumped storage and run-of-river hydro are valued in the capacity market, potentially increasing their accreditation values and revenue.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes a post-RAA accreditation adjustment for energy-limited and storage resources (including pumped storage and long-duration hydro) to account for a model limitation: the RAA model currently prohibits these resources from charging other storage during charging-constrained hours, understating their reliability value. The adjustment formula scales each resource's rMRI upward by a factor that grows with charging-constrained hours (15% winter, 1% summer in the Near-Term Base Case); for a hypothetical 1,000-hour storage resource with zero forced outage rate, the adjustment would boost accreditation from 0.85 (winter) to 1.0. The adjustment is projected to be finalized Q4 2026 with FERC filing and Q2 2027 effective date. Stakeholders should submit feedback to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by June 24, 2026. Most exposed segment: pumped-storage operators and seasonal/long-duration hydro owners in ISO-NE's capacity market.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04d - CAR-SA: FirstLight Power Alternative Load Weighting cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: FirstLight Power proposes revised methodology for winter load-shape and weather-year selection in ISO-NE's Capacity Accreditation Resource (CAR) framework, directly affecting how energy-storage (especially pumped-storage hydro) accreditation is calculated and thus revenue under winter-peak capacity commitments.
ai_summary: FirstLight Power presented a revised approach to winter load-shape and weather-year selection for ISO-NE's CAR-SA (Capacity Accreditation Resource – Storage Accreditation) at the June 2026 Markets Committee. The proposal revises FirstLight's earlier methodology to incorporate aspects of both its own and ISO-NE's original approaches: it targets 5-day blocks (not single days) to capture multi-day consecutive-cold-day effects critical to storage discharge modeling, and it optimizes fit across the entire load distribution rather than only the extreme tail (>95th percentile). FirstLight argues ISO-NE's current methodology over-represents frequency of extreme multi-day events and under-represents fit in non-extreme conditions, leading to material but not massive discrepancies in storage accreditation outcomes, with larger potential impacts under different risk splits or future resource-mix scenarios. The document shows no explicit next-step date or vote, but positions FirstLight as a technical stakeholder in an active accreditation-methodology debate directly affecting how pumped-storage hydro is credited for winter resource-adequacy showings.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04e - CAR-SA: Flatiron RAM cont. - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's proposed resource accreditation model (rMRI methodology) directly affects capacity revenue for all resources by changing how marginal reliability contribution is calculated and how seasonal payment rates are set; the proposal fundamentally alters whether hydro and pumped storage can earn capacity payments based on their actual energy availability and flexibility value during extreme/scarcity conditions versus synthetic stress scenarios.
ai_summary: ISO-NE and Flatiron RAM consulting present a proposal to align resource accreditation modeling with expected system conditions rather than synthetic 'at-criteria' stress scenarios in the Capacity, Accreditation, and Reliance (CAR-SA) initiative. The core concern is that current modeling assumptions over- or under-accredit resources (especially those whose value depends on energy availability in the GE MARS software), and risk paying for a resource mix optimized for conditions that never occur; Flatiron proposes accrediting based on 'expected clearing' or 'forecasted load' scenarios where system visibility is better and resource mix is known. The proposal includes three options to address situations with few simulated resource-adequacy events: load scaling to reliability targets, scaling based on forecasted load growth beyond the delivery year, or setting accreditation floors based on empirical data (e.g., EFORd) when simulated risk falls below threshold. The document flags that any marginal accreditation approach creates volatility and forecasting burden for market participants, since rMRI values are sensitive to resource mix, penetration, interactions, and load shape—factors outside individual participant control. Next steps and comment dates are not stated in this presentation excerpt.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04f - CAR-SA: Impact Analysis, Market Clearing Preliminary Results - Presentation
relevance_reason: CAR-SA fundamentally redesigns ISO-NE's capacity auction accreditation and payment structure for seasonal resources, directly affecting hydro and storage revenue recognition, clearing prices, and capacity market participation rules.
stakeholders: Chris Geissler (ISO-NE); Jake Fabian (ISO-NE); Steph Kang (ISO-NE)
ai_summary: ISO-NE is analyzing market-clearing outcomes under its proposed Capacity Auction Reforms–Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA), with preliminary results presented for three stress scenarios (Lower, Medium, Higher). The reform splits the annual capacity demand curve into separate summer and winter curves with distinct payment rates ($8.82/kW-day summer vs. $19.01/kW-day winter) and introduces a gas-supply constraint in winter clearing. Hydro and storage resources face altered accreditation formulas tied to seasonal peak contribution rather than annual ELCC, and the model shows winter price separation increases as non-firm gas resources displace other capacity. The ISO expects to present sensitivity analyses (80/20 risk split, alternative gas assumptions) in July; the proposed effective date is Q2 2027. Pumped-storage operators and seasonal-variable hydro owners should monitor how the seasonal split and storage accreditation methodology affect their cleared capacity and winter-vs.-summer revenue streams.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04h - CAR-SA: Non-Firm Gas Capacity Demand Curve Follow Up - Presentation
relevance_reason: Capacity auction design for gas resources affects the demand curve methodology and clearing prices that determine system clearing in ISO-NE's capacity market, which directly impacts hydro's energy and capacity revenue through system-wide price signals and accreditation treatment of energy-limited resources.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes not to adjust the non-firm gas capacity demand curve in response to firm gas contracts, treating all firm contracts as fully incremental. The proposal is part of the Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), with effective date proposed for Q2 2027 and expected filing in the coming months. This design prevents gas resources from artificially inflating capacity market clearing prices through uneconomic firm gas arrangements; however, it may result in procurement of more gas capacity than the actual gas available during tight system conditions. The proposal also links gas contract requirements (16-hour hourly minimum for December–February) to energy-limited resource accreditation methodology, meaning hydro and other energy-limited resources will be treated analogously to gas capacity with similar fuel-availability thresholds. Hydro owners in ISO-NE should monitor whether this gas-constraint treatment influences how seasonal accreditation is applied to conventional hydro and how winter capacity pricing evolves.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04h - CAR-SA: Resource Qualification Process Follow Up - Presentation
relevance_reason: CAR-SA capacity accreditation methodology directly affects how hydro and storage resources qualify for and are compensated in ISO-NE's seasonal capacity market, including novel provisions for forced outages and ambient-air deratings that reshape the qualification process hydro owners must navigate.
stakeholders: Jasleen Singh (ISO-NE); Jeff Mislak (ISO-NE); Jennifer Engelson (ISO-NE)
ai_summary: ISO-NE presented refinements to its Capacity Accreditation and Reconfiguration – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) resource qualification process, effective Q2 2027. Three areas affect hydro and storage operators: (1) Ambient Air treatment allows thermal generators to offer de-rated MWs above the cost-offer threshold without justification—hydro owners need to track how this shapes capacity-market offer strategies for their thermal neighbors; (2) the 100 kW minimum MRIC requirement is retained, but small hydro and storage can meet it via aggregation as Distributed Energy Capacity Resources; (3) a new "unexpected full forced outage" provision lets resources remove themselves from the seasonal auction if they face season-long full outages, but only if the outage duration covers the entire six-month season and only before the seasonal auction offer window. The mechanism affects seasonal capacity revenue for any resource subject to forced outages (including hydro with unplanned failures) and clarifies qualification deadlines. Feedback deadline: June 24, 2026; next discussion at July Markets Committee on resource qualification follow-up and resource-attribute update processes.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04j - CAR-SA: Interactions with PFP - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) reshape accreditation methodology and Pay-for-Performance settlement mechanics — directly affecting capacity market revenue and performance-payment exposure for all resource types, including hydropower and pumped storage, through changes to the Balancing Ratio cap, CPP calculations, and seasonal stop-loss limits.
stakeholders: Chris Geissler (ISO-NE); Jasleen Singh (ISO-NE); Megan Sweitzer (ISO-NE)
ai_summary: ISO-NE presents three approaches to cap the Balancing Ratio (BR) under its seasonal capacity auction reform (CAR-SA), addressing a FERC order requiring the BR cap at 1.0 to prevent resources from being obligated beyond physical capability. The migration from Qualified Capacity to Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) accreditation will reduce total capacity supply obligations system-wide, raising BRs and causing the 1.0 cap to bind more frequently, which shrinks Capacity Performance Payments (CPPs) to overperformers due to funding deficiencies. ISO-NE's preferred Approach 3 would cap the BR at a seasonal ratio tied to net maximum capability divided by net MRIC (1.355 in summer, 1.444 in winter), preserving stronger performance incentives for overperformance while complying with FERC's logic. Additionally, ISO-NE proposes conforming the annual PFP stop-loss limit to a seasonal market by capping seasonal losses at two times (instead of three times) a resource's maximum monthly net loss. Stakeholder feedback is due to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by June 24, 2026, with ISO-NE targeting July for its final proposal. Large-duration and seasonal-flexibility resources (including pumped storage and multi-week-capable reservoirs) are most exposed, as their value in CAR-SA depends on accreditation during scarcity hours; how the BR cap settles will determine whether they receive full or reduced CPP rates during performance.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04k - CAR-SA: Capacity Bilaterals - Presentation
relevance_reason: Capacity IBTs and exports rules directly affect how hydro and storage operators can hedge capacity-market revenues and manage export bids—both central to revenue optimization under ISO-NE's seasonal capacity market redesign (CAR-SA).
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes to implement Capacity Internal Bilateral Transactions (IBTs)—an ISO-administered financial instrument allowing participants to hedge capacity-market price risk by fixing capacity payments—and to simplify capacity-export rules under the seasonal Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), effective Q2 2027. Under CAR-SA, capacity clearing occurs ~1 month before each 6-month season (vs. 40+ months ahead under the old FCM), shortening the forward-price visibility window and raising hedging demand. The Capacity IBT would allow a supplier and LSE to exchange fixed-for-floating swaps on capacity obligations, with the ISO settling the floating leg (pegged to seasonal clearing prices or CLO charge rates) and netting it within ISO settlement, while the fixed leg stays off-ISO books. Separately, ISO-NE would discontinue rarely-used export-bid curtailment exemptions (III.1.10.7(f) and III.15.8.1.2) and consolidate export functionality under simpler rules (III.1.10.7(i)), allowing capacity exporters to submit opportunity costs that determine MRIC eligibility. Hydro and PSH operators using export strategies or managing capacity revenues through bilateral hedges will see changes to both pricing instruments and export curtailment priority; the proposal suggests March 2027 implementation and is under stakeholder feedback.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04l - CAR-SA: Initial Tariff Redlines - Presentation
relevance_reason: Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) deactivation tariff redlines fundamentally reshape how ISO-NE's capacity market accredits and clears seasonal capacity commitments, directly affecting how hydro resources (especially reservoirs managing seasonal supply) will be valued, offered, and potentially deactivated under the new framework.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is revising its tariff to implement seasonal capacity accreditation and deactivation rules as part of the broader Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), with a proposed Q2 2027 effective date. The presentation covers initial draft tariff changes to conform deactivation processes from annual to seasonal Capacity Commitment Periods, including revisions to cost workbooks, conduct-test thresholds, portfolio benefit tests, and proxy capacity offer structures, all now indexed to seasonal Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity rather than annual metrics. Hydro owners—particularly those with multi-seasonal storage assets—will see their accreditation, offer-construction flexibility, and deactivation economics reframed around seasonal scarcity and clearing prices; affected segments include reservoir-based resources and pumped storage. The stakeholder schedule shows tariff review and committee votes pending through Q3 2026, with a FERC filing target in Q4 2026.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04m - CAR-SA: Seasonal Offer Construction and Mitigation - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) establish seasonal capacity-market offer formation rules and seller-side market power mitigation affecting how all resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, must construct and submit capacity bids, with direct implications for revenue recovery and mitigation risk.
ai_summary: ISO-NE presented seasonal offer construction methodology and seller-side market power mitigation rules for its Capacity Auction Reforms – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) proceeding. The ISO proposes applying a Capacity Offer Price Threshold (COPT) methodology conformed from the FCA's Dynamic Delist Bid Threshold, capped at 25–75% of net Net CONE, plus a pivotal supplier test that automatically reduces bids above the threshold if the supplier is deemed pivotal. For the first seasonal auctions in CCP19, the ISO will use an initial COPT of 37% of annual Net CONE (derived from historical FCA16-18 ratios) rather than prior-auction pricing. The presentation illustrates offer formulation for generalized resources and gas-constrained resources by showing how next-best-alternative analysis drives capacity bids — resources whose NBA is temporary exit must cover netGFC plus expected PFP costs, while resources profitable in energy markets offer only the PFP common-value component. Hydro operators with seasonal revenue constraints (e.g., run-of-river or PSH facing winter/summer energy-market disparities) will need to validate whether their avoidable-cost calculations and PFP recovery assumptions survive the pivotal supplier test; if mitigated improperly, FERC cost-recovery procedures are available. Status and next steps are not stated in this presentation; the proceeding remains in detail-design phase.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04n - CAR-SA: AEU Conceptual Amendments - Presentation
relevance_reason: ISO-NE's seasonal accreditation methodology directly affects how storage and flexible resources are valued in the capacity market, with specific proposals on weather-year weighting and intertie modeling that would alter the MRIC (Minimum Revenue Impacting Capacity) accreditation signal for pumped storage and other flexible resources.
ai_summary: Advanced Energy United presents two conceptual amendments to ISO-NE's seasonal accreditation reform (CAR-SA). Amendment 1 argues ISO's weather-year weighting based on peak-load days overstates the frequency of sustained cold snaps, causing storage (especially pumped storage) to be disproportionately penalized in accreditation; CAU proposes weighting years by the frequency of multi-day cold-weather blocks instead. Amendment 2 contends ISO's flat import-capacity block for intertie modeling obscures when actual imports are available (often overnight, between MRI hours), understating storage's charging opportunities and thus its MRIC value; CAU proposes modeling hourly import-availability profiles tied to load shape. The submission also flags a potential inconsistency between ISO-NE's use of LOLE for resource adequacy but EUE for accreditation, which could systematically undervalue storage. Storage accreditation is directly material to investment signals and RA sufficiency in New England; these amendments are being advanced in the context of ISO-NE's broader seasonal accreditation reform process.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual 11 Revision 63
relevance_reason: Order 2222 conforming changes to ISO-NE Manual 11 establish DER aggregation participation rules that create a new pathway for small hydropower and pumped-storage resources to participate in wholesale markets, affecting revenue opportunities and operational obligations for distributed hydro assets.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual 11 Revision 63 implements FERC Order 2222 conforming changes for the NEPOOL markets, establishing rules for distributed energy-resource (DER) aggregation participation. The document defines how small hydro, pumped storage, and other DERs can aggregate and bid into energy, capacity, and ancillary-services markets through a lead DER aggregator. Small and medium hydro owners in the ISO-NE footprint gain a new participation model that does not require individual resource registration, potentially lowering barriers to wholesale-market entry and creating revenue streams previously unavailable to sub-threshold hydro units. Manual 11 Revision 63 is being presented to the NEPOOL Markets Committee on June 9, 2026; the specific compliance deadline and effectiveness date are not stated in the extracted text.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual 28 Revision 68
relevance_reason: Manual 28 revision conforming to FERC Order 2222 affects DER aggregation participation rules, which establish pathways for distributed and small hydropower resources to participate in organized markets.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual 28 Revision 68 implements conforming changes to Order 2222 requirements governing DER aggregation participation in the wholesale markets. Order 2222 creates a participation model for distributed energy resources and aggregators, which extends to small hydropower and run-of-river sites that would otherwise lack economic access to RTOs. The revision establishes technical and operational standards for how aggregated DER (including small hydro) can bid, dispatch, and settle in energy and ancillary-service markets. This mechanism expands the universe of hydro assets that can monetize their output — small facilities previously too small to qualify for direct ISO participation now gain a participation pathway through aggregation, though the detailed operational rules (telemetry, dispatch latency, state-of-charge tracking for any hybrid DER) will constrain how flexibly they can operate relative to bulk generators.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual RPA - Revision 22
relevance_reason: FERC Order 2222 conforming changes to ISO-NE's DER aggregation manual directly affect the participation pathway and revenue eligibility of small hydropower and other distributed resources in energy and ancillary-services markets.
ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual RPA (Resource Participation Agreement) Revision 22 updates ISO-NE's tariff to conform to FERC Order 2222 requirements for DER aggregation participation. The manual revision governs how distributed energy resources, including small hydro, can aggregate and participate in wholesale markets under a single operating agreement. This is a compliance filing establishing the operational and settlement framework; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the extracted title. Small hydropower owners considering aggregation as a path to ISO-NE market participation should review the revised manual for eligibility requirements, state-of-charge or operating rules specific to hydro, and settlement treatment of aggregate hydro facilities.

title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes and SATOA Conforming Changes - Presentation
relevance_reason: SATOA and Order 2222 conforming changes directly affect how pumped-storage hydro (and storage generally) registers, participates in energy markets, and settles; Order 2222 also opens DER aggregation pathways for small hydro.
ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes conforming updates to Manuals M-11, M-28, and M-RPA to implement Storage as Transmission-Only Asset (SATOA) revisions (effective September 1, 2026) and FERC Order 2222 requirements (effective November 1, 2026). The SATOA changes establish registration, metering, and settlement accounting for storage assets that operate as transmission-only resources on the 115 kV+ network, affecting how pumped-storage operators register, retire, update SATOA data, and account for injections and withdrawals in the settlement model. Order 2222 integration adds two new DER aggregation models (Demand Response DERA and Settlement Only DERA) into Manual M-11 market operations, creating new participation pathways for distributed and smaller hydro resources. The presentation previews the redlines without a formal comment deadline or vote date stated; next step is NEPOOL Markets Committee discussion and approval of the manual revisions.

title: 2026-06-09-12 MC A00 - Actions Letter
relevance_reason: ISO-NE Markets Committee approved day-ahead ancillary services tariff adjustments affecting how hydro and other dispatchable resources participate in reserve and regulation markets, and FirstLight Power (a major PSH operator) actively opposed one element of the proposal via amendment vote.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Markets Committee approved post-implementation adjustments to the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market and related tariff sections (III.3.2.1(q)(4), III.1.8.2, III.1.8.4, III.A.8.1.2, III.F.2.3.1.3) on June 9, 2026. FirstLight Power submitted an amendment requesting restoration of a strike-price floor in Section III.1.8, arguing against its removal in the ISO proposal; that amendment failed with only 20.83% support (Generation sector split 8.33% for / 8.33% opposed). The unamended main motion passed with 64.54% support. The strike-price floor affects how PSH offering strategies in day-ahead ancillary services are constrained; its removal reduces offer flexibility for storage resources that rely on floor protection to manage opportunity costs. The vote reflects sector disagreement — Generation (including hydro/PSH) largely opposed the unamended version, while Transmission and other sectors favored it.

title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A01 Agenda & Admin
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Interregional transmission coordination (Order 1000 compliance and IPSAC process) affects hydro deliverability across Northeast ISO/RTO seams; agenda includes interconnection queue coordination and regional planning needs that directly shape where hydro projects can interconnect and export power.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Planning Advisory Committee hosts the May 29, 2026 IPSAC (Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee) meeting covering Order 1000 interregional coordination, interconnection queue status, and long-term firm transmission requests across PJM, ISO-NE, and NYISO. The meeting will present coordinated studies on regional transmission needs, discuss potential interregional solutions, and update stakeholder input on projects with cross-border impact. For hydro and PSH owners with assets in or exporting through the Northeast ISO/RTO footprint, this process determines which transmission projects get coordinated study and informs the collective regional planning that gates new interconnections and export capability — particularly relevant for PSH seeking to move energy across seams or conventional hydro seeking firm transmission service for seasonal exports.

title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A02.1 PJM Regional Transmission Expansion Planning Process
relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION PLANNING: PJM's 2025 RTEP window selected backbone transmission projects (765 kV lines, 500 kV HVDC) and load-growth driver analysis affecting interregional transfer capability and hydro deliverability in Mid-Atlantic, South/Dominion, and PJM West regions; hydro owners exporting or operating within these zones depend on these backbone routes for revenue realization.
stakeholders: David Schoon (PJM); Michael Herman (PJM); Nebiat Tesfa (PJM); Nicolae Dumitriu (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM completed evaluation of 134 proposals (57 greenfields, 77 upgrades) submitted in its 2025 Reliability Window 1 and selected regional backbone solutions totaling ~$11.7 billion: a 222-mile 765 kV line from Kammer–Buttermilk–Juniata (Mid-Atlantic), a 185-mile 500 kV HVDC line from Heritage–Mosby (South/Dominion), and three 765/345 kV lines in PJM West (Greentown–Teddy–Marysville, Guernsey–Conesville–West Millersport–Adkins, and 345 kV laterals). The primary drivers are load growth in PPL zone (~5–8.5 GW cumulative), data-center load external to Dominion, and southbound generation flowing to northern VA load centers, all creating 500 kV capacity violations on north–south corridors by 2030–2032. The next FERC 1000 window opens July 2026 with proposal close August/September 2026 and board approval targeted October 2026–February 2027. For hydro operators exporting from the South or West into load-heavy Mid-Atlantic, these backbone augmentations directly increase transmission import capacity and firm locational revenue signals; conversely, the selection of HVDC on the Heritage–Mosby corridor (rather than purely AC) creates a new constraint on traditional hydro exports via 500 kV AC paths.

title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A02.2 ISO-NE Regional Planning Needs and Solutions
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: transmission-planning decisions and constraint analyses affect hydro deliverability, resource adequacy valuation, and revenue-generating scarcity conditions; Loss of Source study baseline, LTTP RFP evaluation, and area studies (Connecticut 2034, daytime minimum load) directly shape transmission availability and locational scarcity signals that determine hydro's economic dispatch and reserve deployment value.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's May 2026 regional planning update covers several transmission-planning initiatives affecting New England hydro deliverability and resource adequacy: (1) Loss of Source study with PJM and NYISO concluded without achieving full objectives but established new baseline for system constraints in neighboring RTOs; report anticipated Q2/Q3 2026. (2) 2025 Longer-Term Transmission Planning (LTTP) RFP evaluation ongoing, final determination by September 2026; stakeholder discussions on FERC Order 1920 compliance (interregional transmission coordination) paused pending RFP completion, resuming Q3 2026 with June 14, 2027 compliance-filing deadline. (3) Connecticut 2034 Solutions Study underway with preferred solution anticipated late 2026; 2035 Daytime Minimum Load Needs Assessment begins post-May 2026 CELT report release, base-case library Q3 2026. These constraint-relief and long-lead-time transmission projects directly affect hydro's ability to deliver reserve and energy products into neighboring markets and hydro's valuation in resource-adequacy showings; owners relying on New England hydro for interregional exports or RA accreditation should monitor LTTP RFP vendor evaluation (financial viability, construction feasibility) and Order 1920 stakeholder discussions resuming Q3 2026.

title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A02.3 NYISO Updates on Comprehensive System Planning Process
relevance_reason: NYISO's system planning process and reliability assessments directly affect hydro and storage dispatch needs, transmission-constrained resource valuations, and generator deactivation/RMR decisions that reshape the market environment for peaking and flexible resources.
ai_summary: NYISO presented its 2026–2027 Reliability Planning Process cycle, including the 2026 Reliability Needs Assessment (covering 2030–2036) and the 2027–2036 Comprehensive Reliability Plan. The 2025 Q3 and 2026 Q1 Short-Term Reliability Assessments (STARs) identified persistent deficiencies in New York City (Zone J) and Long Island (Zone K) that depend on continued operation of fossil peakers (Gowanus, Narrows, Far Rockaway, Danskammer) until transmission solutions energize or demand-side/storage alternatives materialize. Generator deactivations are being withdrawn or held in Interim Service Provider status pending solution entry. These assessments directly affect which generation assets receive RMR contracts or withdrawal approval, which in turn shapes the scarcity pricing, transmission congestion, and reserve-procurement environment in which hydro and storage must compete. Any expansion of transmission or storage-as-transmission alternatives in the planning scenarios could displace fossil peakers and alter the revenue drivers for hydro ramping and energy supply in the constrained zones.

title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A03 Queue Interconnection Studies Update
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue status and interregional coordination affects hydro deliverability, project timing, and transmission access for existing and prospective hydro and storage resources seeking to interconnect or upgrade in ISO-NE, NYISO, and PJM.
stakeholders: Stojan Nikolov (ISO-NE); Supriya Tawde (NYISO); Mojtaba Hoshmand (PJM)
ai_summary: ISO-NE reports on interconnection projects in ISO-NE, NYISO, and PJM queues with potential interregional effects, coordinating studies across RTO borders through the Joint ISO/RTO Planning Committee. The document catalogs active and pending generation and transmission projects (effective queue status as of April 1, 2026), including battery storage, HVDC imports, and solar resources in NYISO and PJM with impacts on neighboring systems. NYISO's Transition Cluster Study Phase 2 is underway with next cluster study anticipated July 31, 2026; this queue coordination directly affects timing and cost allocation for any hydro or pumped-storage project seeking interconnection in the three interconnected RTOs, particularly large-scale storage and transmission upgrades that require multi-RTO impact assessment.

title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A05 IPSAC NCSP25
relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning and coordination directly affect hydro deliverability, resource adequacy needs assessment, and the strategic question of whether interregional transmission investments (vs. regional solutions) will shape hydro's revenue opportunities and grid export capacity in the Northeast.
ai_summary: ISO-NE, NYISO, and PJM completed their 2025 coordinated interregional transmission planning cycle under FERC Order 1000, with draft results posted in early April 2026 and stakeholder comments due April 20–27, 2026. The planning process found no interregional transmission projects that are more efficient or cost-effective than separate regional solutions, meaning each ISO will pursue its own system needs independently rather than jointly. This outcome is significant for hydro owners because it confirms the near-term transmission topology will remain within existing regional footprints; interregional transmission constraints will not be relaxed by new joint projects, and the value of existing hydro resources for intraregional scarcity and export will depend on each ISO's separate needs assessments and queue development. The final NCSP25 report is pending posting.

title: 2026-05-27 PAC A03.0 2026 TPTG Update Presentation
relevance_reason: SATOA (Storage as a Transmission Only Asset) definition and planning-study treatment directly affects how pumped-storage and other dispatchable storage resources participate in transmission planning and potentially compete for transmission-backed revenue or non-wires-alternative roles.
ai_summary: ISO-NE has updated its Transmission Planning Technical Guide (TPTG) to formally document Storage as a Transmission Only Asset (SATOA) — energy storage devices at 115 kV or higher used to address transmission reliability needs post-N-1-1 contingencies and to provide continuous reactive power support like synchronous condensers. SATOA treatment was accepted by FERC on October 19, 2023, and is now being incorporated into planning-study methodology in TPTG Section 2.3.8.1, with conforming changes to Section 2.3.8. This formalizes a pathway for storage (including pumped storage) to compete as a transmission solution in ISO-NE's competitive solutions process alongside traditional wires and other non-wires alternatives, potentially creating revenue streams for PSH owners who can locate facilities to address identified transmission needs. Comments on the draft TPTG are due by June 10, 2026.

title: 2026-05-27 PAC A04.0 2026 Economic Study - Benchmark Scenario Assumptions
relevance_reason: The 2026 Economic Study's benchmark scenario uses historical hydro operations data and models hydro capacity, efficiency, and dispatch; the study also models energy storage (including pumped storage at 74% efficiency) and ancillary services procurement, which affect revenue recognition and reserve accreditation for both resources.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is conducting its 2026 Economic Study to model New England's resource needs, transmission limitations, and emission profiles over a 10-year horizon. The benchmark scenario (preliminary results Q3 2026, final by December 2027) uses 2025 historical hydro operations data integrated into PLEXOS to determine monthly max/min capacity and energy availability; pumped storage is modeled at 74% efficiency with a $3/MWh VOM cost, and both hydro and storage are assigned reserve capabilities (10-minute spinning, 10-minute non-spinning, 30-minute operating reserves). The study will also examine potential System Efficiency Needs through competitive solicitation, which could affect transmission interconnection and deliverability. Stakeholders may submit requests for additional sensitivity analyses by June 2026.

title: 2026-05-27 PAC A05.0 2025 LTTP RFP – RFP Objective Follow-up
relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION PLANNING: ISO-NE's 2025 Longer-Term Transmission Planning (LTTP) RFP evaluation directly affects hydro export capability and interregional transfer limits (Maine–New Hampshire and Surowiec–South interfaces), which constrain the locational value and revenue of New England hydropower and determine whether transmission upgrades will be required to relieve scarcity.
ai_summary: ISO-NE evaluated six Longer-Term Transmission Plan proposals submitted in response to NESCOE's RFP; two proposals (A1, A2) passed RFP Objectives testing (thermal, voltage, and transient stability at ≥3000 MW Maine–New Hampshire and ≥3200 MW Surowiec–South transfer limits) and proceed to No Adverse Impact Screening and Benefits Calculation, while four proposals (B1, C1, D1, D2) failed stability or voltage criteria and remain excluded. After follow-up reviews requested by proposal sponsors, ISO-NE confirmed that C1 failed transient stability testing due to insufficient dynamic reactive support, and D1/D2 sponsors acknowledged instability under single-fault scenarios; both determinations remain unchanged. ISO-NE expects to present its preliminary preferred Longer-Term Transmission Solution to PAC by September 2026. For hydro owners, the outcome determines which transmission projects advance to benefits evaluation — projects that increase Maine–New Hampshire or Surowiec–South transfer limits would reduce congestion and increase the marginal value of New England hydro's energy sales southward; rejection of four proposals may delay relief of these critical constraints.

title: Draft Transmission Planning Technical Guide Rev 8.5 (clean)
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Transmission planning technical guide directly affects hydropower deliverability, interconnection modeling, and system planning study treatment of conventional hydro and energy storage resources — foundational to how hydro assets are rated, tested, and accommodated in the network.
ai_summary: ISO-NE has issued Revision 8.5 of its Transmission Planning Technical Guide, effective September 30, 2025. The guide establishes modeling requirements and study protocols for the region's resource mix, including dedicated sections on conventional hydro generation (2.3.7) and energy storage systems (2.3.8), along with steady-state, transient stability, and short-circuit criteria used in transmission needs assessments. Changes to modeling conventions, reactive power requirements, stability test protocols, or resource-rating methodologies in this revision directly impact how existing hydro plants and new storage projects are characterized in interconnection studies, deliverability analyses, and system planning — affecting both operational flexibility valuations and accreditation inputs to New England's capacity market. The guide is now in effect; any comments on the revision history or requests for clarification should be directed to ISO-NE Planning.

title: Draft Transmission Planning Technical Guide Rev 8.5 (redline)
relevance_reason: Technical guide establishes modeling and study procedures for transmission planning that directly affect how hydropower and energy storage are represented in interconnection and system-adequacy studies—including generator models, maximum power ratings, reactive capability, and stability criteria that shape deliverability assessments and resource-adequacy accreditation for hydro and PSH.
ai_summary: ISO-NE's Transmission Planning Technical Guide (Rev. 8.5, effective 9/30/2025) establishes baseline modeling procedures and criteria for all system planning studies in ISO-NE. The guide includes dedicated sections on conventional hydro generation (§2.3.7), energy storage systems (§2.3.8), and their representation in steady-state and transient stability analyses. Section 2.3.1–2.3.5 cover generator maximum power rating types, models, real power and reactive power ratings—parameters that directly determine how hydro and PSH capacity is credited in resource-adequacy and interconnection studies. The guide also specifies how dispersed energy resources (DER) are modeled and their treatment in transmission element loading calculations (§3.3.5), relevant to small and run-of-river hydro participation through aggregation. This is a technical reference document in active revision; further updates are noted as continuing.

title: Joint Letter on LTTP Solicitation to the PAC
relevance_reason: HVDC transmission expansion and black-start capability affect hydro's regional transmission access, cost recovery, and operational flexibility value; this RFP dispute touches the governance process through which major transmission investments that reshape hydro's market are selected.
stakeholders: David Cavanaugh (ENE Transmission (Energy New England Transmission)); Matthew Ide (Massachusetts Municipal Wholesale Electric Company); Will Hazelip (National Grid Ventures US); Bryan Sanderson (PowerBridge LLC)
ai_summary: A coalition including National Grid Ventures, New England Power Company, and PowerBridge submitted two HVDC-based transmission proposals to ISO-NE's inaugural Longer-Term Transmission Planning (LTTP) solicitation but was excluded from further evaluation after failing stability analysis in March 2026. The coalition disputes ISO-NE's rejection of technical mitigation measures (adding an isolated circuit and transformer to the converter station), arguing the tariff allows non-material modifications and that iterative refinement is standard practice. The broader stakes: all expanded HVDC solutions were eliminated, leaving only HVAC alternatives—eliminating HVDC's claimed benefits of black-start capability, bi-directional power-flow control, and future cost reduction. For hydro owners, HVDC transmission expansion directly affects regional scarcity pricing, deliverability of remote hydro resources, and operational flexibility value; the coalition's exclusion narrows the solutions competitive set and may reduce the scope of future regional investments in transmission features that benefit dispatchable resources.

ai_summary: ISO-NE is developing proposals for seasonal accreditation and import modeling that will affect how external resources (including potential hydropower imports) are accredited and valued in the capacity market through the MRIC framework.
