Firstlight Power LLC, a significant pumped-storage hydropower generator in New England, participates as a Generation sector member.

While this specific project is being cancelled, the withdrawal process and interconnection framework are relevant to how storage resources interact with ISO-NE's reliability and interconnection procedures, which affect both storage and hydropower resources.

relevance_reason: Qualified Reactive Resource (QRR) requests for major transmission projects directly affect resource adequacy, voltage support, and market participation rules that impact hydropower and storage resources in the region.

title: 2026-05-21 RC A05.a Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - DECR Modeling & Accreditation - Memo
relevance_reason: The document discusses capacity accreditation frameworks under FERC Order 2222 that will affect how distributed energy resources including storage are modeled and compensated in ISO-NE's capacity market, which has direct implications for pumped-storage hydropower and energy storage participation.
stakeholders: ISO New England, Inc. (ISO New England)
ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing a new MRI (performance-based) accreditation framework for Distributed Energy Capacity Resources (DECRs) that replaces the previous sum-of-individual-facilities approach. This framework will impact how storage resources and other DERs are credited for capacity contributions, affecting their market revenues and participation incentives—relevant to PSH and battery storage operators.

title: 2026-05-21 RC A05.a Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - DECR Modeling & Accreditation - Presentation
relevance_reason: Document addresses capacity accreditation framework and seasonal auction reforms that directly affect how distributed energy resources—including storage facilities (CSF, BSF)—are modeled and accredited for resource adequacy, which impacts market participation rules for hydropower and pumped-storage resources.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing accreditation reforms for Distributed Energy Capacity Resources (DECRs) under FERC Order 2222 compliance, including Continuous Storage Facilities (CSF) and Binary Storage Facilities (BSF). The framework uses performance-based profiling and Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) Hours modeling to determine capacity accreditation; storage resources aggregated as DECRs must use DERA-level profiles and may be subject to T&D loss adjustments. These rules affect how pumped-storage and battery storage participate in ISO-NE's seasonal capacity auctions effective Q2 2027.

title: 2026-05-21 RC A05.b Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - Tie Benefits Follow-Ups
relevance_reason: The document details ISO-NE's capacity auction reforms including seasonal accreditation and tie benefits methodology that directly affect how hydropower and other dispatchable resources are valued and accredited in the capacity market.
ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing triennial seasonal tie benefits (STB) studies to inform seasonal capacity auction reforms, with sensitivity studies triggered by changes in external Capacity Import Capability (CIC). This affects how hydropower's capacity contributions are valued seasonally (summer vs. winter) and how storage and flexible hydro resources participate in the reformed capacity accreditation framework beginning Q2 2027.

title: 2026-05-21 RC A05.c Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - Capacity Zone Formation
relevance_reason: This document directly addresses capacity accreditation reforms (CAR-SA) and capacity zone formation, which are critical for how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited and valued in ISO-NE's capacity market.
ai_summary: The CAR-SA initiative redesigns capacity accreditation measures and introduces seasonal (summer/winter) capacity auctions with revised zone development processes. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators need to understand how Dependable Capability (DCap) values will replace Qualified Capacity (QC) in import-constrained zone testing, and how seasonal system conditions will affect zonal accreditation and market signals for resource location.

title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A02 - Pay-For-Performance Revisions: Balancing Ratio - Presentation
relevance_reason: Pay-for-Performance capacity market revisions directly affect how dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage resources are compensated and charged under ISO-NE's capacity mechanism.
ai_summary: FERC Order EL25-106 requires ISO-NE to cap the Capacity Balancing Ratio at 1.0 to prevent capacity resources (including hydro and PSH) from being charged obligations exceeding their Capacity Supply Obligation. The proposal reallocates deficient Capacity Performance Payments and discontinues Capacity Performance Bilaterals, materially affecting how hydro/storage resources settle charges during scarcity conditions.

ai_summary: MISO's Distributed Energy Resources Working Group (May 18, 2026 meeting) advanced implementation of FERC Order 2222, covering operational coordination roles for distributed energy resource aggregators (DERAs), demand response resources (DRR), and load-modifying resources (LMR DR). The Locational Enrollment System (LES) multi-phase rollout will enable Distributed Energy Aggregated Resource (DEAR) registration by 2029; Phase 1 (Emergency Demand Response) is underway, with LES access open now to interested stakeholders. MISO also addressed Inter-Control Center Protocol (ICCP) telemetry requirements and network/commercial modeling submission deadlines (quarterly). Small hydropower facilities and PSH units could participate as DERs under Order 2222 through aggregation platforms; the operational coordination framework establishes which transmission owners, local balancing authorities, and distribution companies must coordinate dispatch signals, and the LES enrollment schedule defines when these resources can register. Stakeholders requested feedback on roles and responsibilities (due June 5, 2026) and asked MISO to increase meeting cadence in 2026.

title: 20260722 PSC Item 01c Minutes 20260610
relevance_reason: MISO's proposal to remove storage charging limitations in generator interconnection agreements (PAC-2024-3) directly affects pumped-storage hydropower's operational flexibility and revenue optionality by allowing charging across all dispatch phases rather than restricting it to specific windows.

ai_summary: MISO updated its proposal regarding charging limits for storage units in generation interconnection studies, replacing the Summer Shoulder charging case with a Light Load charging case to permit storage to charge across multiple dispatch phases while remaining subject to violation-based limits from DPP analysis. The revised BPM-015 updates are targeted for effectiveness prior to DPP 2026 kick-off (status: pending July Planning Advisory Committee approval). For pumped-storage operators, this expands the hours and conditions under which they can charge without contractual restrictions, improving their ability to arbitrage between low and high prices and optimize state-of-charge management—though charging remains subject to reliability-driven limits. Separately, MISO invited feedback (due June 26, 2026) on replacing the 2-Year Light Load, 0% wind MTEP scenario with a 2-Year Winter Peak, Low wind case; stakeholders raised concerns about how batteries are modeled under light load sensitivities, indicating sensitivity assumptions may affect resource adequacy accreditation.

title: 20260722 PSC Item 02 Liaison Report
relevance_reason: MTEP26/27 transmission planning cycles and TPL-008 compliance affect MISO's transmission-expansion projects, which determine deliverability and interregional transfer capability for hydro exports and generator interconnection; large-load workgroup expansion affects resource-adequacy forecasting and scarcity signals hydro operators depend on for revenue; stakeholder-feedback window for MTEP27 scope is a submission deadline for hydro fleet concerns.
stakeholders: Arthur Ashby (MISO); Stakeholder Relations (MISO); Trevor Armstrong (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO is executing the MTEP26 reliability-planning cycle (project-recommendation phase underway, board approval targeted December 2026) and requesting stakeholder feedback on MTEP27 scope by October 29, 2026. Separately, MISO and transmission owners are implementing TPL-008-1 (extreme-temperature transmission-planning standard), with R1 enforceable April 2026, R2–R4 compliance required Q2 2027, and R5–R6 by Q4 2027. MISO has resumed the Long Range Transmission Planning study as part of MTEP27. These planning cycles directly shape transmission expansion that controls MISO hydropower generators' export capacity and interregional-transfer capability; TPL-008 compliance may add new transmission needs that alter scarcity patterns. Hydro operators with MISO plants should submit scope feedback to PlanningCoordinator@MISOEnergy.org by October 29, 2026, and track Subregional Planning Meetings (SPM #3 scheduled September 3–4, 2026) where local transmission issues affecting hydro deliverability are debated.

title: 20260722 PSC Item 04a MTEP26 Voltage Stability Analysis Results
relevance_reason: Voltage stability analysis directly informs transmission-transfer limits that constrain hydro export capability and revenue; the Manitoba and Illinois scenarios model interregional transfers where hydro (particularly Manitoba Hydro imports and MISO-generation exports) are material and subject to stability-driven curtailment.
stakeholders: Amanda Schiro (MISO); Andenet Leyew (MISO); Andrew Tiedt (MISO); Nan Duan (MISO); Scott Goodwin (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO presents voltage-stability results for the MTEP26 transmission plan, analyzing two transfer scenarios: Manitoba Hydro exports into the US (540 MW voltage-stability transfer limit in 2031 winter) and MISO Classic into Illinois (constrained to 1.4–7.6 GW depending on coal-plant retirement timing and year). The Illinois scenario is particularly acute: when Illinois retires its largest reactive-power-generating coal units (worst-case), voltage-stability transfer limits collapse from 10.25 GW to 4.78 GW in 2031 and remain tight at 5.4 GW in 2036, materially reducing transfer capability into a load center. For MISO hydro-connected generators (especially in the North, near Manitoba Hydro interface) and any pumped storage considering Illinois or regional export service, voltage-stability limits now compete with thermal and thermal-margin constraints as the binding transfer bottleneck. Final voltage-stability report is due September 30, 2026.

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 01b Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: FERC Order 2023 compliance (BPM-015 and interconnection queue reforms), Inverter-Based Resource (IBR) modeling and system-strength screening, and Generator Interconnection Queue Improvements (ERAS, standardized application data) directly affect how all dispatchable and variable-output resources interconnect and operate in MISO — core obligations and strategic options for hydro and hybrid projects.
stakeholders: Angela Maiko (Interconnection Process Working Group (MISO)); Erin Murphy (Interconnection Process Working Group (MISO)); Kyle Trotter (MISO); Mandi McGirr (MISO)
ai_summary: The IPWG Management Plan outlines MISO's interconnection governance and work calendar through Q4 2027. Key workstreams directly relevant to hydro include: (1) FERC Order 2023 compliance (BPM-015 revisions and queue-reform implementation, PAC-2023-1), with updates scheduled throughout 2026–27; (2) IBR Performance, Modeling, and Conformance (PAC-2024-2), including System Strength Screening for Electromagnetic Transient (EMT) Studies, which will set modeling and equipment requirements applicable to conventional synchronous hydro and all new interconnections; (3) Generator Interconnection Queue Improvements (ERAS, standardized application-data models, PAC-2023-1), which streamline queue administration and deliverability screening; and (4) Generator Interconnection Application Automation (GIAA) Portal updates. The group held 8 meetings in 2025 with ~296 average participants. Status reports and liaison updates on all workstreams are scheduled through the calendar; voting items on charter/management plan renewal are flagged for Q1 and Q4 2026. For hydro owners, the most material near-term actions are Order 2023 BPM-015 updates (October 2026 meeting) and system-strength screening rule development (ongoing through Q1 2027), both of which govern interconnection study scope and may alter equipment or operational-response requirements for conventional and hybrid hydro projects.

ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group met June 9, 2026, to review generator-queue procedures under FERC Order 2023 compliance. Key developments: (1) DPP 2026 cycle application deadline is September 10, 2026, with study cycle kick-off December 9, 2026; application fee (D1) is $7,600, with all projects in the cycle required to recalculate and 'true-up' fees every three years per GIP Section 3.6. (2) East (ITC) Regional Queue Cap has been met; the Queue Cap Tracker includes all submitted projects. (3) Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS) Cycle 4 is underway for six LSE projects (launched June 1, 2026); IPP requests are pending but proceeding as designed. (4) MISO reorganized its Resource Planning Team and is aligning DPP schedule with annual MTEP cycle, with Order 2023 requiring annual cluster studies. These procedural and fee changes affect cost, timing, and queue position for hydro and PSH projects seeking interconnection or uprates in MISO, especially those filing in DPP 2026 or competing within the regional cap; no comment deadline is stated in the minutes.

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 02 Liaison Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue reform (Order 2023 compliance), storage charging limitations in GIAs, and expedited resource addition study directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage projects enter the queue, are studied, and are permitted to operate under interconnection agreements.
ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group is advancing three mechanisms that affect hydro and PSH interconnection and operations: (1) Generator Queue Improvements (PAC-2023-1) and Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS), with ERAS caps met at 50 LSE and 10 IPP projects, and MISO requesting stakeholder feedback on extending the ERAS sunset to December 2027; (2) Removing Storage Charging Limitations in GIAs (PAC-2024-3)—MISO proposes replacing the summer shoulder peak case with a light load case for the charging study scenario, moving to Planning Advisory Committee in August 2026; and (3) Affected System Study tariff alignment (FPA Section 205 filing in late July) to align DPP-2022 and DPP-2023 with Order 2023, avoiding 30–90 day Phase 2 delays. The most immediate deadlines are the DPP 2026 application deadline (September 10, 2026, with updated D1 fee of $7,600) and the PAC meeting August 26, 2026. Pumped-storage projects are directly addressed in the storage charging limitation proposal; all hydro seeking interconnection must comply with queue timing changes.

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 03a FERC Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: FERC Order 2023 and MISO's compliance filing directly affect interconnection procedures and queue management for all generators including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities seeking new or expanded interconnection rights.
stakeholders: Advanced Energy United et al. (Advanced Energy United); AWEA (American Wind Energy Association); Certain MISO Generators (Certain MISO Generators); ITC (ITC Holdings Corp.); Longroad Energy Holdings, LLC (Longroad Energy Holdings, LLC); MISO Transmission Owners (MISO Transmission Owners); National Grid Renewables Development, LLC (National Grid Renewables Development, LLC); Natural Resources Defense Council et al. (Natural Resources Defense Council); Shell Companies (Shell Companies); WATT Coalition (WATT Coalition)
ai_summary: MISO filed Order 2023 compliance on May 16, 2024, addressing FERC's final rule on generator interconnection procedures to reduce queue backlogs and prevent discrimination against new technologies. The Commission accepted MISO's filing in part on June 26, 2025 (effective July 28, 2025) and directed further compliance within 60 days. MISO also faces pending litigation before the D.C. Circuit (docket 23-1282) challenging Order 2023-A. For hydropower and pumped-storage developers, these changes alter interconnection timelines, study processes, cost allocation, and queue advancement criteria; compliance is mandatory and affects the feasibility and cost of new hydro projects and uprates to existing plants.

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 03b DPP Study Schedule.pdf
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue delays and study schedules directly affect hydro and storage project development timelines, deliverability, and capital deployment decisions — a core strategic and operational constraint for NHA members pursuing new capacity or uprates.
ai_summary: MISO's Distributed Project Process (DPP) study schedule, updated July 1, 2026, tracks multiple application cycles across five regions (Central, East ATC, East ITC, South, West) with completion dates extending through 2027. Multiple cycles are experiencing 15-calendar-day delays in DPP Phase 2 completion due to synchronization requirements across regions and cascading delays from the 2022 cycle; some cycles also cite affected-system-study delays and alternative-mitigation volume as causes. Delays in interconnection study completion directly compress the timeline between study completion and Generation Interconnection Agreement (GIA) execution, affecting project financing certainty and investment decisions for hydro and storage projects in queue, particularly those requiring Network Upgrades or Facility Studies (Path 2, which adds 90 days).

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 04 GI Queue Improvements (PAC-2023-1).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is standardizing generator interconnection queue data and modeling requirements (PSSE models, POI specifications, service-type alignment) that directly affect how projects, including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities, move through the interconnection process and timing to meet FERC Order 2023 restudy deadlines.
ai_summary: MISO proposes standardizing the collection and formatting of power-flow modeling data in its Generator Interconnection Queue (PAC-2023-1), requiring interconnection customers to submit PSSE model files (.raw) and adhere to strict data rules for Request Type, Service Type, POI specification, and bus naming conventions. The goal is to automate model building to meet FERC Order 2023 restudy timelines and clear out backlogged older DPP cycles; MISO will download and merge customer-provided PSSE models into its starting MTEP baseline to create study models. For hydro and PSH owners, this tightens the interconnection data discipline and accelerates queue processing, but it also creates new compliance obligations: existing projects and new applications must conform to standardized PSSE formatting, reference-distance alignment with one-line diagrams, and POI bus-numbering rules or risk delays in study cycles. Feedback is due by August 4, 2026.

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 05 MISO-SPP JOA Revision.pdf
relevance_reason: INTERCONNECTION: MISO-SPP Joint Operating Agreement revision affects interconnection queue timelines, affected-system study processes, and facilities construction agreement compliance for all generators (including hydro) seeking interconnection at the seam between these RTOs, and implements FERC Order 2023 compliance in the coordination process.
ai_summary: MISO and SPP are revising Section 9.4 of their Joint Operating Agreement to codify coordination procedures for SPP's new Interconnection Cluster Study (ICS) process, clarify affected-system study timelines and cost allocation on shared tie lines, and add a requirement that interconnection customers comply with Facilities Construction Agreements (FCAs) in the impacted party's tariff. The revision implements FERC Order 2023 compliance, improves process consistency, and reduces financial uncertainty by allowing the direct-connect party to terminate an IC's agreement if FCA execution is not completed timely. Comments are due August 4, 2026; MISO expects to file with FERC by end of 2026 to early 2027, with an effective date 60 days after filing. Hydro generators seeking interconnection at the MISO-SPP seam will face new mandatory FCA execution timelines and clarified cost allocation for tie-line upgrades when both parties identify violations; projects in SPP's ICS clusters (2030 forward) will experience revised study schedules and delay-penalty provisions.

title: 20260721 IPWG Item 05 MISO-SPP JOA_redlines.docx
relevance_reason: INTERCONNECTION: this redline tracks changes to the MISO-SPP Joint Operating Agreement governing interconnection-queue coordination, study processes, and network-upgrade cost allocation across regional boundaries — critical for hydro and storage resources seeking interconnection in either footprint.
ai_summary: The MISO-SPP JOA redline document updates interconnection procedures for requests spanning or impacting both RTOs' transmission systems, including cluster definitions, decision-point sequencing, queue-priority rules, and tie-line upgrade requirements. Key changes include: (1) cluster-definition language split by request date (pre/post April 1, 2026) to align with SPP's ICS terminology; (2) queue-priority rules shifted from Decision Point One/Two closure dates to anticipated start dates for Decision Point Two/subsequent points; (3) new provisions for Joint Targeted Interconnection Queue (JTIQ) portfolio screening and cost allocation; and (4) tie-line upgrade rules requiring the impacted party's result if the direct-connect party's upgrade is insufficient. Study-delay penalties begin with MISO DPP-2026 and SPP ICS-2030 clusters. The document is a redline in active working-group review; no vote date or next steps are stated in the excerpt. Hydro and PSH interconnection requests in either footprint or crossing the seam will face revised queue positioning and cost-allocation rules; generators and storage operators should monitor for final JOA adoption and board approval timelines.

title: 20260717 LME Workshop Item 01c Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Locational marginal emissions accounting may affect how hydro's energy and ancillary-services revenues are valued under carbon-constrained pricing or clean-energy attribution frameworks, and may influence transmission and storage dispatch economics at the margin.
stakeholders: Adam Diamante (EPRI); Daniel Wilson (MISO); Jordan Bakke (MISO); Greg Miller (Singularity)
ai_summary: MISO is holding a workshop on Locational Marginal Emissions (LME) methodology — a framework for attributing emissions to specific generation at specific locations, covering consequential emissions accounting, LME dashboards, and case studies. The workshop is informational (July 17, 2026), with no vote or comment deadline indicated in this agenda document. WHAT: LME is a pricing/accounting system that could reorder dispatch economics when generators' locational emissions are priced differently — potentially affecting the scarcity value and locational signals that hydro and storage operators receive, especially in multi-zone dispatch or when clean-energy or carbon-containment rules interact with locational pricing. The content is exploratory; next steps are not stated in this agenda. SEGMENT: if MISO adopts LME pricing, generators with high dispatch frequency in high-emissions locations would face different marginal revenues, and storage (including PSH) whose dispatch can shift generation patterns geographically would gain or lose strategic leverage depending on implementation.

title: 20260717 LME Workshop Presentation.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's locational marginal emissions (LME) product directly affects how hydro and storage resources are valued in operational decision-making and investment economics, as consequential emissions accounting shapes dispatch incentives and may influence offer strategies for flexible resources.
stakeholders: Adam Diamant (Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI)); Jordan Bakke (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO held a July 17, 2026 stakeholder workshop introducing its Locational Marginal Emissions (LME) dashboard — a new data service going live in August 2026 that provides nodal, near-real-time emissions intensity data to support operational and investment decisions. The workshop presented EPRI's framework for consequential GHG accounting, explaining how marginal emissions rates (MERs) differ from average rates and why they matter for assessing emissions impacts of flexible dispatch or new resource siting. LME complements MISO's existing consumed-emissions and historical-emissions dashboards. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the significance lies in how LME pricing or dispatch signals may incentivize shifting generation to low-emissions hours (a strength of flexible resources like hydro) or de-value output during high-renewable penetration periods (when marginal generation is clean); MISO has not yet disclosed whether LME will be incorporated into energy-price formation, capacity valuation, or settlement, which would determine whether this remains a planning tool or becomes a revenue driver.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load Working Group is addressing co-located load and generation participation (ZGIA), interconnection requirements, and large-load firm service — mechanisms that directly affect hydro's strategic option to serve co-located loads and its interconnection/deliverability pathways.
stakeholders: Mitch Myhre (Alliant); Amanda Jones (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO); Christina Drake (MISO); Joe Reddoch (MISO); Joshua Schabla (MISO); Marc Keyser (MISO); Michael Kessler (MISO); Weiqing Jiang (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is advancing three items material to hydro and storage: (1) Zero-Injection GIA (PAC-2024-4B), which establishes rules for co-located load and generation market participation and registration — hydro owners can use this pathway to serve load behind the meter or coordinate with co-located assets; (2) Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG-2026-3), addressing operational standards for interconnected large loads; and (3) Large Load Firm Service Step Up (LLWG-2026-1), a tariff proposal updating firm transmission-service terms for large loads. The working group is also considering a new Non-Firm Large Load issue (LLWG-2026-5) and a Generation-and-Large-Load Alignment issue (LLWG-2026-6). Next meeting is August 27, 2026. For hydro asset owners, the ZGIA framework and firm-service tariff changes are the primary mechanisms affecting both revenue (through co-location and load-serving options) and strategic flexibility in serving large, high-value loads in MISO.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 01c Action Items
relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection and operational requirements (telemetry, forecasting, dispatch obligations) directly affect hydro's strategic options for co-located load, transmission availability, and grid-following participation in a major RTO.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is managing four active proposals: a ZGIA (Zone Generic Interconnection Agreement) framework, Interconnection Reliability Requirements, Large Load telemetry/PMU standards, and Large Load Operational Forecast Requirements. This July 2026 action-item update shows feedback windows closed (deadlines July 7–15, 2026) and liaison reporting completed. For hydro owners, these rules will set the terms under which large loads interconnect, the real-time operational data MISO requires from them, and forecast obligations—all of which affect transmission availability for hydro export, co-location economics for hydro-plus-load, and MISO's ability to dispatch flexible resources (including hydro) in response to large-load variability and ramp needs. Owners in MISO territory should monitor the outcome of these proposals to understand how large-load requirements (especially telemetry and forecast standards) will reshape dispatch signals and transmission congestion.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260629
relevance_reason: FERC Order to Show Cause on MISO's large-load framework directly implicates hydro's strategic options: large load interconnection and co-located load are squarely relevant to hydro revenue and flexibility value, and MISO's tariff changes to accommodate them will reshape both market-power mitigation rules (which bind hydro) and transmission/deliverability constraints affecting hydro export.
stakeholders: Chris Supino (MISO); Marc Keyser (MISO); Amanda Jones (MISO Stakeholder Relations); Werner Roth (Texas PUC / LLWG Vice Chair); Chris Plante (WEC Energy Group / LLWG Chair)
ai_summary: FERC issued a June 18 Order to Show Cause directing MISO to improve its tariff to better accommodate large loads, recognizing MISO's ongoing efforts but requiring additional filings including an informational report. MISO provided initial legal review at the June 29 LLWG meeting, finding its current approaches directionally consistent with the Order but undertaking further evaluation to determine if additional tariff changes are necessary to meet the Order's requirements and filing deadlines. This matters to hydro because large-load framework changes (load growth, co-location rules, transmission deliverability prioritization) directly shape scarcity pricing and locational signals that hydro depends on, and will likely trigger mitigation-rule adjustments that affect how reservoir and PSH opportunity costs are calculated; the next critical junction is a special Steering Committee meeting on July 7 to consider consolidating all large-load issues under the LLWG.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large load interconnection and firm transmission service rules directly affect hydro's strategic options for co-located load behind the meter and dispatch flexibility, and the working group's agenda items on 'Generation and Large Load Alignment' and firm service tiers shape how dispatchable resources (including hydro) coordinate with large-load integration in MISO's planning and operations.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Eileen King (MISO); Jake Carrico (MISO); Marc Keyser (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO / Large Load Working Group); Werner Roth (MISO / Large Load Working Group)
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is advancing four issues in H2 2026 that reshape how large loads integrate into the market: (1) Large Load Firm Service Step Up tariff revisions, with final redlines due August 27; (2) Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG-2026-2), undergoing cost-allocation review with recommendations expected in Q4 2026; (3) Creation of Large Load BPM 032 governing conditional/nonfirm transmission service; and (4) newly launched 'Generation and Large Load Alignment' (LLWG-2026-6) and 'Non-Firm Large Load' (LLWG-2026-5) issues debuting July 16. These initiatives directly affect hydro owners' ability to serve large co-located loads, secure firm transmission for dispatchable assets, and coordinate generation dispatch with large-load operational requirements. Hydro operators in MISO should monitor the Generation and Large Load Alignment issue (LLWG-2026-6) and the firm service tariff redlines; the alignment framework may establish precedent for how synchronous, dispatchable resources (including run-of-river and reservoir hydro) must adapt real-time operations to synchronize with large-load demand profiles.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 02a Steering Committee Status Update
relevance_reason: MISO's reassignment of two issues to the Large Load Working Group—co-located load/generation participation behind a single POI and large-load definition—directly affects hydro and pumped-storage strategic options by shaping the rules for resource stacking, behind-the-meter service, and participation pathways for distributed or co-located hydro assets.
stakeholders: Brian Drumm (MISO Steering Committee); Dan Scripps (MISO Steering Committee)
ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee approved the 2026 Charter and reassigned two Planning Advisory Committee issues to the Large Load Working Group: 'Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation (behind the same POI)' (PAC-2024-4a) and 'Large Load Definition' (PAC-2024-1). These issues will now be worked through the LLWG rather than PAC. The reassignment is significant for hydro and PSH owners because co-location rules and large-load definitions shape whether (and how) distributed hydro, run-of-river plants, or pumped-storage units can register and participate as integrated load-and-generation portfolios, affecting revenue opportunities from behind-the-meter service and aggregation. The next Steering Committee meeting is August 4, 2026 (virtual), and the LLWG will determine how these participation models are formalized in MISO tariff and registration rules.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 03a Appendix 19 ZGIA Interconnection Agreement Redline
relevance_reason: ZGIA Interconnection Agreement redline affects interconnection queue procedures, deliverability rules, and participation pathways for generating facilities including hydroelectric and pumped-storage hydro seeking to interconnect in MISO, touching obligations and strategic options.
ai_summary: This document is a redlined template for MISO's Zero-Generation Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA), which governs how generating facilities interconnect to serve co-located loads ("ZG Associated Load") without injecting onto the transmission system. The ZGIA is part of MISO's generator interconnection procedures (GIP) and defines terms, study requirements, facilities cost allocation, and operational obligations for interconnection customers. For hydroelectric or pumped-storage projects seeking to serve behind-the-meter or co-located load in MISO — a participation pathway that can unlock value by avoiding transmission congestion and serving critical loads directly — understanding the ZGIA redline signals changes to interconnection timelines, cost responsibility, network-upgrade triggers, or dispute-resolution procedures that would affect project economics and execution risk.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 03a Draft Attachment FF Tariff Redlines ZGIA Market Participation and Registration of Co located Load and Gen
relevance_reason: Draft tariff redlines on co-located generation and load registration under MISO's ZGIA framework directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage facilities can participate in markets when coupled with load or other resources, touching strategic participation options and market eligibility.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is drafting tariff language (Attachment FF) governing transmission expansion planning and the participation of co-located generation and load under the ZGIA framework. This redline excerpt focuses on the MTEP (MISO Transmission Expansion Plan) process, which integrates interconnection planning, load interconnections, and transmission-to-transmission projects. The document includes restrictions on storage-as-transmission-only assets (SATOA): SATOA may only inject/withdraw energy to receive/provide the services for which it was included in the MTEP, and may NOT participate in energy, operating reserve, or PRA markets unless the tariff is later amended to allow cost-based revenue recovery for storage assets in those markets. For pumped-storage operators, this language constrains how PSH co-located with load or generation can use the ZGIA framework to monetize ancillary services, energy arbitrage, or capacity unless MISO later changes the tariff to permit broader market participation for storage cost-recovery assets. The restriction effectively quarantines SATOA participation pending future tariff reform. Most exposed are PSH owners considering ZGIA enrollment to serve co-located load or to participate in MISO markets; redlines are in draft and subject to LLWG review, with final tariff language still developing.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 03a Draft Module A Tariff Redlines ZGIA Market Participation and Registration of Co located Load and Generati
relevance_reason: MISO is establishing tariff rules for co-located generation and load (Zero Injection Generation), a hybrid resource model that directly affects how hydro and pumped-storage owners can participate, charge, and dispatch resources behind the meter or at the same substation.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group has drafted tariff definitions for Zero Injection Generation (ZG) Interconnection Service, which permits a generating facility (including hydro and storage) to connect at the same substation as associated load and serve that load without injecting energy onto the transmission system. The proposal revises offer limits (economic maximum, emergency maximum, regulation maximum) for ZG Resources participating as generation, storage, or dispatchable intermittent resources, capping injection at the demand level of the associated load. This framework enables co-located hydro and pumped-storage operations under state law, directly expanding the strategic options for hydro owners to serve large on-site loads (e.g., data centers) and avoid transmission congestion and market-power mitigation. The tariff redlines are in draft form; next steps and comment deadlines are not stated in this excerpt.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 03a Draft Module C Tariff Redlines ZGIA Market Participation and Registration of Co located Load and Generati
relevance_reason: Tariff redlines governing market participation and registration of co-located load and generation resources, including energy storage and ZG-interconnected resources, directly affect hydro and PSH revenue streams, obligations, and strategic options around hybrid/co-location arrangements and storage participation.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is drafting tariff language (Module C) governing market participation and registration for resources with ZG interconnection service and co-located load and generation. The excerpt specifies criteria for Generation Resources, Electric Storage Resources, Dispatchable Intermittent Resources, and Load-Modifying Resources to qualify as Market Participants, including demonstrations of exclusive rights, compliance with metering/telemetry/communication standards, and credit requirements. A key provision requires resources with ZG interconnection service paired with associated loads >5 MW to register as Generation, Storage, or Dispatchable Intermittent Resources based on operating characteristics. This creates a participation pathway for pumped-storage and run-of-river hydro that are co-located with large on-site loads or that may develop co-location arrangements; it also establishes the operational and registration baseline for storage assets participating in energy and ancillary-service markets. The document is in draft tariff-redline form with no explicit vote or comment deadline stated in this excerpt.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 03a Draft Module E 1 and Sch 53 Tariff Redlines ZGIA Market Participation and Registration of Co located Load
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: tariff redlines to MISO Module E and Schedule 53 on ZG (Zero Generation) interconnection service and co-located load registration directly affect participation pathways for small hydro and hybrid resources under Order 2222 analog rules, transmission-loss treatment of behind-the-meter generation, capacity accreditation thresholds tied to co-located load forecasting, and deliverability determinations for generation and storage resources.
ai_summary: MISO Large Load Working Group has drafted tariff redlines (Module E 1 and Schedule 53) governing ZG interconnection service, co-located load participation, and transmission-loss adjustment for behind-the-meter generation resources. The proposal redefines deliverability pathways for generation, storage, and intermittent resources through Network Resource Interconnection Service, Energy Resource Interconnection Service, and ZG Interconnection Service; establishes transmission-loss gross-up treatment for ZG Resources and non-interconnected behind-the-meter generation (which serve load without incurring transmission losses); and ties seasonal accreditation eligibility for ZG Resources to explicit identification of co-located load in coincident-peak-demand forecasts. Hydro owners evaluating small-hydro or hybrid co-location, or PSH with adjacent loads, must track how ZG interconnection eligibility, transmission-loss rules, and capacity-accreditation linkage to load forecasting affect economics; the tariff language also clarifies deliverability accreditation for energy-storage resources coupled with firm transmission. Document status and next steps are not stated in the excerpt provided.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 03a ZGIA Generator Interconnection Procedures (GIP) Redline
relevance_reason: MISO's Generator Interconnection Procedures (GIP) redline governs how all generating facilities, including hydropower and pumped-storage hydro, enter and progress through MISO's interconnection queue, affecting cost allocation, timeline, facility requirements, and access to surplus interconnection service—core strategic and compliance obligations for NHA members seeking to interconnect or uprate plants.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is reviewing a redlined version of MISO's Generator Interconnection Procedures (GIP) addressing definitions and procedural framework. The excerpt shows definitional updates to terms governing interconnection requests, queue positions, withdrawal penalties, ancillary services eligibility, energy resource interconnection service, and surplus interconnection service through Energy Displacement Agreements. The redline governs how all generators—including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities—qualify for interconnection, achieve Commercial Operation Date certainty, manage contingent facilities in the interconnection queue, and access surplus capacity from existing generators. For hydro owners, the GIP redline is material to understanding cost responsibility for upgrades, queue certainty, and whether existing hydro plants can offer surplus interconnection service to new resources—critical for uprate projects and for existing plants seeking revenue from hosting junior queued resources.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 04 Liaison Report
relevance_reason: MISO's large-load interconnection framework and co-located resource rules (ZGIA) directly affect strategic options for hydropower owners considering co-location with loads, and the firm/non-firm transmission service step-up model creates new revenue and operational pathways for dispatchable resources including pumped storage.
ai_summary: MISO is developing a three-part framework to integrate large loads with accelerated interconnection timelines (120 days to GIA) and flexible transmission service options, including a novel Firm Service Step-Up mechanism and co-located generation-load arrangements (ZGIA). The framework addresses a FERC show-cause order and includes alignment of large-load and generation interconnection studies, new reserve product enhancements for large-load support, and real-time market pricing signals and demand-response products to coordinate large-load variability. Stakeholder feedback on tariff language and process details is due July 30, 2026, with filings on interconnection reliability requirements and ZGIA registration expected July 31, 2026. The Firm Service Step-Up and non-firm service options, combined with co-location rules, could enable pumped-storage and conventional hydro operators to participate as flexible resources backing large-load commitments or to co-locate generation with load behind a single point of interconnection—expanding the strategic toolkit for hydro assets in MISO, particularly those seeking firm transmission service without full generation-only interconnection costs.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 05 Large Load RA Report
relevance_reason: FERC Show Cause Order on large load integration directly affects MISO's resource adequacy rules, which determine how hydro and PSH are accredited and whether existing and new capacity is sufficient—affecting both portfolio participation by hydro owners and the scarcity signals that drive hydro revenue.
ai_summary: FERC issued a Show Cause Order to MISO on June 18, 2026 (195 FERC ¶ 61,212) directing tariff improvements to accommodate large load integration and resource adequacy. MISO is filing an informational report by July 20, 2026 addressing resource-adequacy tools, state roles, and current/under-development initiatives (Seasonal Planning Resource Auction, Reliability Based Demand Curve, Direct Loss Of Load accreditation, and a proposed Generation and Large Load Alignment coordinated study process). For hydro owners in MISO's footprint, this opens the door to rule changes in how resource adequacy is calculated and how large load growth affects accreditation requirements—particularly relevant to pumped storage and conventional hydro seeking to establish or maintain RA capacity value. The July 20 filing date triggers a FERC compliance timeline that will likely spawn tariff proposals through 2027.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 06a Gen and Load Alignment Issue Acceptance
relevance_reason: Large load growth and interconnection alignment directly affect hydro deliverability, transmission planning, and the strategic positioning of existing hydro capacity in load-constrained regions.
ai_summary: MISO proposes aligning large load studies with generation interconnection timelines to reduce delays, optimize network upgrades, and improve reliability assumptions. The proposal addresses a procedural bottleneck where signed Generator Interconnection Agreements (GIAs) are currently required before generation can be included in load studies, creating timing mismatches. MISO reports 27 GW of large load complete or under study in MTEP26, with 34 GW of newly-signed GIAs added in the past year, indicating rapid co-arrival of load and generation. For hydro owners, accelerated load-aligned transmission planning will expose more clearly which regions face generation scarcity (raising scarcity-pricing upside) and where existing hydro capacity becomes strategically constrained or critical to reliability — improving the business case for uprates, resource adequacy accreditation, and interregional export capability. The issue is under Large Load Working Group adoption; no deadline is stated in this excerpt.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 06b Generation and Load Alignment Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Accelerated interconnection and transmission study process for large loads with co-located generation (250+ MW threshold, 120-day combined study timeline) directly affects hydro/PSH revenue optionality—owners evaluating co-location, resource-adequacy coordination, and generation injection rights in ballpark of large industrial loads now face a faster, parallel study pathway that could reshape transmission-cost allocation and permit generation to serve load without full network injection, altering dispatch incentives and scarcity value.
ai_summary: MISO proposes a new streamlined process for studying large loads (250+ MW+) paired with associated generation in parallel, rather than sequentially, aiming to achieve MTEP approval and full study completion within 120 days (vs. 180+ days in current serial process). Outcomes include allocation of generation injection rights, transmission service for reliable load withdrawal, and optimized network upgrades; the proposal harmonizes with FERC's show-cause order (Paragraphs 114–118) on efficient infrastructure build to support large loads. The process imposes a $250,000 nonrefundable application fee per party, requires generation ≤150% of identified load need with firm offtake agreement, full site control through point of interconnection, and ability to reach commercial operation within 3 years; generation must transition to normal interconnection queue if load goes away. This is particularly relevant to pumped-storage and large hydro operators evaluating co-location with industrial or data-center loads—the faster study and clearer transmission-cost allocation could unlock new partnership models—and to all hydro owners tracking transmission-planning acceleration and the precedent it sets for generation-load nexus outside traditional firm-injection assumptions. Stakeholder feedback due July 30, 2026; tariff filing expected September 2026.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 07a Non Firm Large Load Issue Acceptance
relevance_reason: MISO's non-firm large load participation option directly affects hydro's strategic options: expedited large-load interconnection and curtailment rules will reshape local scarcity conditions, frequency response demand, and dispatch signals that drive hydro revenue and dispatch flexibility value in MISO.
ai_summary: MISO proposes a Non-Firm Large Load participation option (LLWG-2026-5) permitting large loads to interconnect ahead of transmission-adequacy studies, accepting curtailment risk under reliability-constrained conditions. The option addresses load desire for expedited interconnection despite insufficient firm transmission service. This changes the operational and economic context for conventional hydro and pumped storage in MISO: faster load growth increases scarcity-pricing opportunities and frequency-response demand during tight conditions, but curtailable load rules may also reduce peak scarcity duration and shift dispatch incentives away from high-margin run-of-river generation toward storage/peaking units. Assignment to the Large Load Working Group indicates continued development; no deadline or vote date is stated in this issue-acceptance document.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 07b Non Firm Large Load
relevance_reason: MISO's non-firm large-load participation option directly affects hydro's strategic positioning and revenue by introducing a new load class that can interconnect without firm transmission, altering system scarcity conditions, reserve requirements, and the capacity/RA framework that values hydro's dispatchability.
ai_summary: MISO proposes a Large Load Participation with non-firm Transmission option allowing data centers and other large loads to interconnect expeditiously without procuring firm transmission service, subject to curtailment under grid stress. The proposal touches resource adequacy (whether non-firm loads have capacity obligations), day-ahead/real-time energy and ancillary-service market participation, curtailment prioritization, and settlement—all of which reshape dispatch incentives and scarcity pricing that hydro relies on. MISO seeks stakeholder feedback by August 7, 2026, and intends to file a tariff no later than Q4 2026. Most exposed are hydro owners in MISO whose revenue from scarcity-driven energy prices and reserve products may compress if large non-firm loads reduce system tightness, and whose must-offer/dispatch obligations may shift if non-firm load is modeled separately in market clearing.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 07c BITS Proposal Alliant Energy
relevance_reason: BITS enables large loads to interconnect and ramp into service on an interim basis while transmission upgrades are constructed, directly affecting hydro's strategic options for co-located load service, behind-the-meter arrangements, and the economics of load growth as a driver of scarcity value.
stakeholders: Mitch Myhre (Alliant Energy); Shivam Sharma (Alliant Energy)
ai_summary: Alliant Energy proposes Bridge Interim Transmission Service (BITS), a non-firm interim transmission product for large loads in MISO that allows service to begin once load interconnection studies are complete and local facilities are built, while required network upgrades are under construction—automatically converting to firm Network Integration Transmission Service (NITS) when upgrades finish. BITS requires load to have metering and telemetry in place, 24/7 contact availability, and ability to reduce load in 30 minutes if curtailed; loads are backed by accredited capacity resources and included in Module E forecasts. This removes a timing mismatch that has deterred large-load interconnection: hydro owners co-locating loads or offering behind-the-meter service can now ramp customers into service faster, capturing scarcity premiums from load growth without waiting years for transmission buildout. The proposal aligns with FERC's Large Load Show Cause Order (EL26-70) for MISO; Alliant seeks stakeholder feedback by August 7, 2026, and proposes FERC filing in Q4 2026 with implementation March 2027.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 08 Firm Service Step Up (LLWG 2026 1) Draft Tariff
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection and co-location rules directly affect hydro's strategic value and deliverability; Firm Service Step Up changes the timing and certainty of transmission service for data centers and industrial loads, which alters demand-side scarcity signals and co-location opportunities that hydro owners can exploit.
ai_summary: MISO is establishing a 'Firm Service Step Up' tariff to allow Large Load customers to secure incremental firm transmission service before full transmission upgrades are complete, in blocks of 25 MW minimum with in-service dates up to 36 months post-approval and aggregate capped at the full-load planning level. This accelerates data-center and industrial-load interconnection phasing, creating intermediate demand milestones that increase the certainty and stacking opportunities for hydro-based co-located or behind-the-meter load supply. Hydro owners (especially those in MISO footprint or considering it) should monitor MISO resource-adequacy and seasonal energy studies to assess whether accelerated load stepping raises scarcity rents during high-load, low-hydro periods, and whether co-location tariffs allow existing hydro to contract with stepping loads at intermediate thresholds.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 09 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements BPM Development
relevance_reason: MISO's proposed Large Load interconnection framework establishes reliability and operational requirements that directly affect interconnection obligations and grid-integration costs for dispatchable resources (including hydropower and pumped storage) co-located or competing for grid capacity with large industrial loads.
ai_summary: MISO is developing a new Large Load BPM-032 (Business Practices Manual) and related tariff framework to govern interconnection and operational reliability requirements for large load customers, with a hub-and-spoke implementation model where the Large Load BPM sets requirements and existing BPMs (BPM-020, BPM-013, BPM-031, BPM-025, BPM-002) supply supporting processes. The framework covers the full interconnection lifecycle from design through in-service operations, with initial focus on interconnection reliability and operational requirements; future phases will address stability assessment, oscillation, PMU, and compliance monitoring. MISO's Tariff filing is targeted for July 2026, and the BPM portfolio is scheduled for finalization by October 1, 2026, with LLWG review cycles on August 27 (first draft) and mid-September (near-final). For hydro and PSH owners, this matters because large industrial loads competing for interconnection queue positions, transmission capacity, and grid reliability resources (particularly voltage support and ride-through capability) may face new facility-data, modeling, and operational verification requirements that shift cost allocation or delay service commencement—affecting the economics of co-located or adjacent hydro/PSH interconnections and increasing modeling/compliance burden on existing hydro plants if brought into Large Load validation scopes.

title: 20260716 MUG Item 08 Large Loads Operating Modeling Requirements.pdf
relevance_reason: Large-load modeling requirements and operational participation rules affect how co-located generation and demand-response resources (including behind-the-meter hydro or storage at large facilities) integrate into MISO's network and commercial models, which shapes strategic options for hydro owners co-locating at large industrial loads.
ai_summary: MISO establishes detailed network and commercial modeling requirements for large load facilities (those where a single breaker opening/closing changes load by >50 MW), requiring explicit device-level representation of generators, battery-energy storage systems, and demand-response resources rather than aggregation. Behind-the-meter generation ≥5 MW must be individually metered and modeled; generation at large-load facilities can participate in markets via traditional GIA, pseudo market generation, or ZGIA (zero-injection generation interconnection), with dispatch instructions flowing through MISO's centralized mechanism. Large loads must establish separate CPNodes (pricing/settlement nodes) to prevent load-distribution skew between day-ahead and real-time models. This directly affects hydro owners with co-located generation or storage at large industrial facilities—they face clearer rules on whether plant-level generation participates in MISO markets or remains behind-the-meter, and how storage (BESS) and ramp-support devices are metered and dispatched. Feedback is due Monday, July 31, 2026.

title: New Issue - Generation and Load Alignment
relevance_reason: MISO's proposal to align large-load interconnection studies with generation-addition studies directly affects transmission planning, generator-interconnection queue timing, and the market and reliability signals that shape hydro deliverability and strategic value — a core strategic option for hydro owners.
ai_summary: MISO is proposing a new process to align large-load studies with generation-addition studies, applying consistent assumptions and reducing timing mismatches to produce more optimized network upgrades while maintaining reliability. The initiative targets the generator-interconnection queue and MTEP/transmission studies, with a resolution deadline of October 30, 2026. For hydro owners, coordinated load-generation planning affects both the pace and location of transmission expansion (raising or constraining hydro export capability) and the visibility of load-driven scarcity that drives energy and capacity revenues; misalignment between load forecasting and generation planning can also distort queue discipline and interconnection costs, which cascade to existing hydro deliverability and valuation.

title: New Issue - Non Firm Large Load
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Non-firm large-load participation design directly affects hydro's resource adequacy accreditation, curtailment exposure, and transmission service value; load growth and co-located demand are strategic options that amplify scarcity and change the value of existing hydro capacity.
ai_summary: MISO proposes a new Non-Firm Large Load participation option allowing large loads to interconnect ahead of firm transmission-service availability, with the understanding that these loads would be curtailed under grid-reliability-risk conditions. The proposal covers energy and ancillary service participation, resource adequacy accounting, transmission and planning, cost allocation, and curtailment procedures — currently at issue-submission stage with no stated deadline. MECHANISM: Non-firm load participation raises two direct concerns for hydro owners. First, if non-firm large loads are NOT counted toward resource-adequacy requirements (or counted with heavy discounts), the RA bar for other resources including hydro rises, while loads receiving scarcity benefit bear no capacity obligation — this inverts normal cost-allocation. Second, if non-firm loads ARE curtailed preferentially during scarcity events, hydro operators face reduced dispatch value in real-time scarcity periods, since the non-firm load absorbs the curtailment instead of pushing higher locational prices. MISO's design choices on RA treatment and curtailment-dispatch rules will determine whether non-firm loads are free-riders on hydro's scarcity value or are appropriately penalized. This affects all hydro in MISO, particularly reservoir and run-of-river operators in tight seasonal periods.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 01c Minutes 20260527.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Long-Range Transmission Planning (LRTP) process directly affects hydro's transmission deliverability and strategic options; interregional coordination with PJM and SPP, load-growth forecasting, and resource adequacy criteria shape the value and revenue exposure of hydro assets across the MISO footprint.
stakeholders: Amanda Schiro (MISO); Jeanna Furnish (MISO); Laura Rauch (MISO); Vikram Godbole (MISO); Cynthia Crane (MISO Planning Advisory Committee)
ai_summary: MISO has launched its 2026 Long-Range Transmission Planning (LRTP) process for the Midwest and South regions, with issue identification and solutions development following a seven-step process; solutions proposals are anticipated in 2027. The LRTP will assess reliability and load-serving needs across a 20-year horizon (CAGR ~1.5% annually) using Future 2 and 3+ scenarios that show resource and energy adequacy under MISO's 1-in-10-years Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) criterion. Hydro owners' revenue and dispatch flexibility are affected because transmission bottlenecks identified in LRTP feed into the solutions phase — constraints on interregional transfers or load pockets in the South region (Louisiana/Texas) directly impact where MISO can call on existing hydro for scarcity pricing and ramping services, and load growth shapes the baseline scarcity against which hydro's flexibility premium is valued. Feedback on Future scenarios is due June 17, 2026; solutions will be developed in 2027, making this the foundational study for MISO transmission expansion over the next decade.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This PAC management plan lists two items directly material to hydro and storage participation: 'Removing Storage Limitations in GIAs' (PAC-2024-3) and 'Transmission Service Requirements for Charging Electric Storage Resources for the Grid' (PAC-2023-2), both of which affect how pumped storage and battery resources are interconnected, charged, and accredited in MISO's queue and transmission-access framework.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Jake Carrico (MISO); Jeanna Furnish (MISO); Cynthia Crane (MISO Planning Advisory Committee); Dave Johnston (MISO Planning Advisory Committee)
ai_summary: MISO's Planning Advisory Committee management plan for 2026–2027 schedules two storage-specific initiatives: (1) 'Removing Storage Limitations in GIAs' (PAC-2024-3), with an update from the Planning Subcommittee expected at the July 15, 2026 meeting, and (2) 'Transmission Service Requirements for Charging Electric Storage Resources for the Grid' (PAC-2023-2), scheduled for October 7, 2026 discussion. Both touch the interconnection and transmission-access rules that govern how pumped storage and battery projects gain capacity and charging capability in MISO's system. The plan also flags 'Generator Interconnection Queue Improvements: Standardizing GI Application Model Data' (PAC-2023-1) for continued discussion, which affects queue clarity for all resource types. Pumped-storage owners should monitor the July and October meetings for specifics on how MISO intends to relax storage GIA constraints and define charging-service tariffs; these rule changes will directly influence PSH project economics and deliverability.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 02a IPWG Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is reforming its generator interconnection process (Attachment GGG and MHVDC procedures) and deploying a new interconnection portal automation system; both affect hydro and PSH developers' queue position, upgrade cost allocation, and delivery timelines — core strategic options for interconnection-dependent expansion or uprate projects.
stakeholders: Ben Scott (Consumers (CMS)); Alan Urban (MISO); Angela Maiko (MISO); Erin Murphy (MISO); Eva Kaso-Collette (Orsted)
ai_summary: MISO finalized tariff and business-practice-manual changes to its MHVDC interconnection procedure and clarified Necessary versus Network Upgrades definitions and Multi-Party Facility Construction Agreement (MPFCA) terms; it also updated Attachment GGG redlines and is targeting two FERC filings in the next 30 days and Q3–Q4 2026. The portal automation system (Generation Interconnection Application Automation, or GIAA) has completed Release 1 (April 2026) and Release 2 (May 2026), with Release 3 scheduled for July 18, September 15, and November 15, 2026; stakeholders requested release notes and front-end sorting/download options. Hydro and PSH developers in MISO—especially those pursuing modular or multi-party transmission solutions—will face revised upgrade-cost responsibility rules and faster application processing; the timing of FERC filings and portal releases will compress the compliance window for projects already in queue or filed under prior rules.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 02b PSC Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's proposal to remove storage-charging limitations in Generation Interconnection Agreements (GIAs) directly affects pumped-storage hydro's ability to participate in interconnection studies and operational planning, and transmission-planning topology updates affect hydro asset deliverability.
stakeholders: Natalie McIntire (MISO Planning Subcommittee); Patrick Jehring (MISO Planning Subcommittee)
ai_summary: MISO is revising its interconnection study process (PAC-2024-3) to replace Summer Shoulder charging cases with Light Load charging cases and will continue studying storage charging across all three phases of the Definitive Planning Process (DPP). The revised BPM-015 changes are expected to take effect for DPP26 and future cycles. This affects pumped-storage operators by expanding the operational scenarios in which their charging patterns are studied during interconnection, reducing prior constraints on when PSH charging can be evaluated. Simultaneously, MISO is updating its Long-Range Transmission Plan (LRTP) base topology using the Summer 2035 February Cycle model and requesting stakeholder review of transmission projects 230 kV and above for inclusion; this determines which transmission infrastructure gets planned around PSH and conventional hydro export capability. The PAC will review the BPM revisions in July 2026.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 02c SC Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is reassigning two high-priority issues to the Large Load Working Group — co-located load/generation participation (PAC-2024-4a) and large-load definition (PAC-2024-1) — both of which directly affect hydro's strategic option to serve co-located load and the eligibility rules for distributed and small hydro under Order 2222-like frameworks.
stakeholders: Brian Drumm (MISO Steering Committee); Dan Scripps (MISO Steering Committee)
ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee approved the 2026 Charter of the Large Load Working Group and reassigned two Planning Advisory Committee issues to it: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation behind the same Point of Interconnection (PAC-2024-4a), and (2) Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1). The next Steering Committee meeting is August 4, 2026. These reassignments materially affect hydro owners' ability to co-locate generation with large loads and behind-the-meter participation models; how MISO defines 'large load' and allows co-located resource registration will determine whether existing or new hydro can participate in this revenue stream. The two issues now move from PAC discussion to focused LLWG workstreams, narrowing the public engagement window for hydro input on what registration rules and definitions will be proposed.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 03 Liaison Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is progressing tariff filings on storage charging in interconnection agreements (BPM-015), queue improvements, and large-load integration rules (ZGIA); storage accreditation studies continue for Futures planning; interconnection-queue delays affect all generators including hydro with projects in DPP cycles.
ai_summary: MISO reports four tariff actions in progress: (1) BPM-015 on removing storage charging limitations in generator interconnection agreements, targeted for PAC Q3 approval—directly affecting how pumped-storage hydro can charge in MISO; (2) GI Queue Improvements (Attachment X), Q4 targeted; (3) MISO-SPP JOA updates (Rate Schedule 06), Q3; (4) ZGIA/Large Load definition reassignment to LLWG, with FERC tariff filing planned July 31, 2026. Separately, MISO is filing a 205 tariff change to decouple Affected System study timelines from DPP-2022 and -2023 queue cycles, aiming to avoid 30–90 day Phase 2 delays and cascading queue congestion. Storage accreditation studies for all four Futures scenarios continue, with final values expected end of summer 2026—these will establish RA accreditation for storage in MISO's long-range transmission plan. The document signals MISO's intent to accelerate interconnection processing and modernize storage-charging treatment, but hydro and PSH owners should track Q3 PAC outcomes on BPM-015 and monitor the 205 filing timeline (end of July) for queue-acceleration details.

title: 20260715 PAC Item 04 Att GGG Improvements (PAC-2024-6)_redlines.docx
relevance_reason: MISO is establishing interconnection procedures for merchant HVDC transmission lines, which affects transmission planning, deliverability of remote generation (including distant hydro resources), and interregional energy flows — a strategic option for NHA members exporting hydro output across MISO seams.
ai_summary: MISO has filed revised Merchant HVDC Connection Procedures (MHCP, Attachment GGG) governing how external HVDC transmission lines interconnect to the MISO transmission system. The procedures establish study, cost-allocation, and injection-rights frameworks for merchant HVDC projects. This matters to Western hydro exporters because merchant HVDC links are a key mechanism for transmitting remote hydro generation to load centers outside MISO (e.g., from the Northwest or Southwest into the Midwest and East); how MISO structures queue, study costs, and interconnection-service terms affects the delivered-cost competitiveness of hydro exports competing against local or MISO-internal generation. The document is a tariff filing with no stated comment deadline or next-step date visible in this excerpt.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: Agenda includes multiple market-design items directly affecting hydro revenue and compliance: reserve deliverability (regulation and operating reserve), FTR-ARR enhancements, ancillary-services pricing mechanics (TCDC evaluation), and a new margin-assurance payment mechanism (DAMAP) that modifies energy/capacity price formation.
stakeholders: Akshay Korad (MISO); Bryan Garnett (MISO); Carrie Milton (MISO); Chris Delk (MISO); Chris Miller (MISO); Chuck Hansen (MISO); Jason Howard (MISO); Joshua Schabla (MISO); Mandi McGirr (MISO); Dr. Patton (Potomac Economics)
ai_summary: The July 9, 2026 MISO Market Subcommittee meeting agenda covers four items of direct interest to hydro operators: (1) Enhance Reserve Deliverability (MSC-2026-2) — MISO will discuss regulation reserve deliverability and performance, directly affecting how hydro's primary AS product is procured and compensated; (2) Evaluation of Updates to Congestion Management and Transmission Constraint Demand Curves (MSC-2026-6) — initial findings on TCDC changes, which shape both energy scarcity pricing and operating-reserve demand curves where hydro earns flexibility revenue; (3) Day Ahead Margin Assurance Payment Reduction Total (MSC-2026-7) — new issue introduction on DAMAP, a capacity/margin-assurance construct that may alter how day-ahead scarcity and adequacy are priced; and (4) FTR-ARR Market Enhancements (MSC-2025-5) — problem statement on transmission-rights market redesign, with feedback requested. The meeting also includes IMM seasonal outlook and market recommendations. No comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in the agenda itself.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 01c Action Items.pdf
relevance_reason: Multiple active MSC items directly affect hydro revenue streams and market obligations: reserve deliverability (capacity and AS), VOLL pricing (energy scarcity signals), HVDC transmission coordination, and market monitoring/mitigation rules for use-limited resources.
ai_summary: MISO's Market Subcommittee closed feedback requests on five active market-design initiatives as of May–June 2026: $10k VOLL Pricing (scarcity pricing mechanism), Enhance Reserve Deliverability for DA and RT Markets (capacity and ancillary-service accreditation), FTR-ARR Market Enhancements (financial transmission rights), Improve Market Monitoring and Mitigation including Module D and Duty of Candor (market-power rules for use-limited resources like hydro and storage), and Transmission Constraint Demand Curve (TCDC) for congestion management. A separate active item incorporates HVDC operations into energy/ancillary-services market processes and transmission planning, including intra-regional HVDC dispatch. No specific comment deadlines or vote dates remain open; all listed feedback requests closed by June 11, 2026. Hydro and pumped-storage operators in MISO are most exposed to the reserve-deliverability and market-monitoring changes, which set accreditation and mitigation parameters for dispatchable and storage resources; the VOLL and TCDC revisions directly affect day-ahead and real-time energy price formation during scarcity.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 01d 2026 MSC Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: This is a procedural management-plan document for MISO's Market Subcommittee; it names multiple active market-design issues directly affecting hydro revenue streams (ramp capability demand curves, reserve deliverability, ancillary-services pricing, FTR-ARR markets, fast-start pricing) and obligations (metering, settlement, uninstructed-deviation penalties), with voting and decision dates through 2027.
stakeholders: Jason Howard (MISO); Mandi McGirr (MISO); Bryan Garnett (MISO Market Subcommittee); Mike Schowalter (MISO Market Subcommittee)
ai_summary: MISO's Market Subcommittee management plan lays out the 2026–2027 agenda for market-design work. Among items directly material to hydropower: ramp-capability demand-curve updates (Q2 ongoing), reserve deliverability enhancements for day-ahead and real-time markets (voting expected Q2–Q4), offline fast-start pricing rules (Q3–Q4), FTR-ARR market enhancements including WPPI feedback (Q2–Q4), meter accuracy standards for small aggregated assets (Q3), congestion management and transmission constraint demand curves (TCDC) updates (Q2–Q4), and evaluation of $10k VOLL pricing under temporary shortage conditions. Key obligations include M2M settlement adjustments, seasonal regulation and spinning-reserve requirements (quarterly), and FERC filing status updates. The plan also tracks MSE (Market System Enhancement) program completion and resolution of inactive/backlog issues post-UDS upgrade. Voting items and feedback deadlines span May 2026 through Q4 2026; next calendar and biennial reviews are scheduled Q3 2027. No specific hydro position-taking is documented here.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 02a SC Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is reassigning governance of co-located load/generation participation and large-load definition from PAC to the Large Load Working Group — directly touching hydro's and PSH's strategic options for co-location, load-growth capture, and hybrid-resource market access.
ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee approved reassignment of two issues from the Planning Advisory Committee to the newly chartered Large Load Working Group: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation (PAC-2024-4a) and (2) Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1). The LLWG will continue work on both, with next Steering Committee meeting August 4, 2026. This shifts governance of rules governing how hydro and PSH can serve load co-located behind a single point of interconnection — a strategic mechanism for hydro owners to capture revenue from adjacent load (data centers, industrial, etc.) and a key enabler of hybrid/storage-as-transmission arrangements. The reassignment signals MISO's focus on large-load participation design; hydro owners pursuing co-location strategies should monitor LLWG meetings for draft participation rules and comment deadlines.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 02c DERWG Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: Order 2222 status update and DER aggregation framework directly affect small hydro and pumped-storage participation options as dispatchable, use-limited resources competing in retail-DR aggregation and VPP models.
stakeholders: Shely Leisz (MISO Distributed Energy Resource Working Group); Zachary Callen (MISO Distributed Energy Resource Working Group); Ted Ko (VPP Convergence Projects)
ai_summary: MISO's Distributed Energy Resource Working Group reported on operational coordination among MISO, distribution companies, transmission owners, aggregators, and load-balancing authorities for DER deployment; the Local Enrollment System (LES) is in multi-phase rollout beginning with emergency DR and expanding to demand-response aggregators (DERAs). FERC issued a Notice of Withdrawal of Inquiry and Termination of Rulemaking on Order 2222 (Docket RM21-14, April 16, 2026), ending the rulemaking on aggregator participation in RTO markets. For small hydro and pumped-storage owners, the termination of RM21-14 closes the federal rule-making pathway for aggregator participation but leaves MISO's tariff-level Order 2222 compliance in place; the LES framework and DERA enrollment phase are the next operational gate determining whether small hydro/PSH can participate at scale through aggregators or remain subject to full-resource-model registration. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this status report.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 03 Liaison Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Multiple items touch hydro/PSH revenue and obligations — ancillary-services pricing (Regulation and Up Ramp Capability demand curves), reserve deliverability for DA/RT markets, and transmission seams management affecting interregional hydro dispatch and revenue.
stakeholders: Akshay Korad (MISO); Christine Ross (MISO); Chuck Hansen (MISO); Congcong Wang (MISO); Daniel Wilson (MISO); Dustin Grethen (MISO); Jason Brown (MISO); Joshua Schabla (MISO); Sarah Fisher (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO filed tariff revisions (ER26-2705-000, effective date August 4, 2026) to update demand curves for Regulation, Spinning Reserves, and Up Ramp Capability (URC) in response to rapid solar penetration and net-load ramping stress. Regulation and Spinning curves are being simplified to a single $98/MWh value, and the URC cap rises to $96/MWh to strengthen market signals during tight conditions.
