Separately, MISO is advancing 'Enhance Reserve Deliverability for DA and RT Markets' (MSC-2026-2) with most stakeholder feedback supportive, and pursuing updates to the Freeze Date congestion-management construct along MISO seams (jointly with PJM, SPP) to improve interregional coordination. These changes directly affect the price and procurement of flexibility and ramping products hydro supplies; URC and Regulation are primary revenue streams for dispatchable hydro and pumped storage. No comment deadline is stated for the demand-curve filing beyond FERC's statutory review.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 04a FERC Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's demand curve updates for regulation, spinning reserves, and up-ramp capability directly affect the ancillary-services revenue hydro operators earn; the co-located resource dispatch rule affects strategic dispatch options for hybrid and co-located hydro; and uninstructed deviation settlement changes alter operational compliance obligations and economic incentives for all dispatchable units including hydro.
ai_summary: MISO filed three tariff changes: (1) Demand Curve update (ER26-2705-000, effective August 4, 2026) revising the demand curves for Regulation and Spinning Reserves and the Up Ramp Capability product in Day-Ahead and Real-Time Markets — hydro's core ancillary-service revenue streams. (2) Interconnection Service Limit Constraint for Co-located Resources (ER26-1915-000, filed 3/25/26, amended 6/11/26 for later implementation) allowing co-located resources to dispatch more efficiently within their interconnection limits — relevant to owners of hybrid hydro-solar or hydro-wind plants seeking optimized dispatch. (3) Uninstructed Deviation Settlement Charges (ER26-1521-000, accepted by FERC 5/29/26, effective 6/1/26) modifying settlement rules to incentivize reduction of uninstructed deviations — affects all dispatchable units including hydro by changing the economic penalty structure for unscheduled output. Ancillary-service demand curves are particularly material to large, responsive generators; co-located rules widen strategic options for mixed-renewable portfolios; uninstructed-deviation incentives reshape operational discipline across all hydro plants.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 04c ARR FTR TCM Quarterly Results.pdf
relevance_reason: ARR/FTR transmission revenue allocation and congestion-rent distribution directly affects hydro's transmission-dependent revenue stream and capacity value in MISO.
stakeholders: MISO Client Services and Readiness (MISO); WPPI Energy (WPPI Energy)
ai_summary: MISO reports quarterly Transmission Customer Metric (TCM) results measuring what portion of day-ahead congestion revenue is returned to transmission customers via ARR/FTR credits versus retained by non-transmission customers. The 2025–2026 planning year shows 60% TCM, with 2026–2027 at 95% (partial-year data through June 29, 2026). The metric tracks total DA congestion revenue ($1.845B in 2025–2026), splits between transmission-customer returns ($1.102B) and non-transmission-customer revenue ($743M). Seasonal variation is substantial: winter 2025 TCM was 50%, while summer 2024 peaked at 92%. This directly impacts reservoir and pumped-storage hydro located in congested zones—a higher TCM means more congestion rent flows back to hydro owners with ARRs, improving revenue from transmission-constrained dispatch; lower TCM diverts that rent elsewhere. The metric has trended upward since 2020 lows (55% in 2020–2021), suggesting improving transmission-customer treatment, but shows high volatility by season and year.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 06 IMM 2025 State of the Market Recommendations.pdf
relevance_reason: Independent Market Monitor recommendations on capacity accreditation, emergency-range penalties, storage dispatch optimization, real-time capacity products, and interchange optimization directly affect hydro and PSH revenue through RA/accreditation rules, ancillary-service and reserve payments, and real-time energy pricing mechanisms.
stakeholders: David Patton, PhD (MISO Independent Market Monitor (Potomac Economics))
ai_summary: MISO's IMM presented 2025 State of the Market findings and six new recommendations to the Market Subcommittee on July 9, 2026. Key proposals affecting hydro and PSH include: (1) improved capacity accreditation with corrections to maintenance-margin calculations and modified off-control unit treatment (2025-5); (2) penalties on units failing to provide emergency output, with estimated impact of $5.6M on coal and $12M on gas/oil emergency-range capacity payments (2025-6); (3) reduced make-whole and reserve payments for deviating resources to strengthen dispatch-following incentives (2025-2); and (4) revival of existing recommendations for a real-time capacity product for 2–4 hour start resources (2020-1) and look-ahead dispatch optimization for storage management (2021-4). The report also flags 26.6 GW of projected load additions by 2030 and evening ramp demands up 50% year-over-year, driven by solar growth. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this summary document, but the recommendations are now before the Market Subcommittee for consideration of tariff filing. Hydro and PSH owners benefit from tighter accreditation rules that reduce overstated capacity claims by competitors, from enhanced real-time products that play to their flexibility, and from optimization of storage dispatch timing; they face potential penalties if emergency-range claims are overstated.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 07 IMM Quarterly Report.pdf
relevance_reason: IMM quarterly market-monitor report documents energy price formation volatility, storage resource participation and dispatch efficiency, contingency reserve shortages, capacity accreditation methodology, and interchange optimization affecting real-time pricing—all directly material to hydro revenue and PSH dispatch strategy.
ai_summary: Potomac Economics' Spring 2026 IMM quarterly report to MISO Market Subcommittee (July 9, 2026) identifies rising evening net-load ramp demand (averaging 15 GW between 3–7 pm, up 45% year-over-year), 20 shortage-pricing intervals (nearly double prior year), and 187 intervals with negative system-wide prices as solar penetration increases. Energy Storage Resources (ESRs) now comprise 1.3 GW capacity, setting system and local prices in 5% and 16% of intervals respectively and providing 28% of regulation; a software flaw caused 7% ESR unavailability when outside state-of-charge limits, dispatching units inefficiently (e.g., above-max ESRs discharging into negative-price hours, below-min ESRs charging at >$200/MWh), now addressed with a July software fix. The report recommends optimizing MISO–SPP/PJM interchange through alternate real-time dispatch cases to lower volatility and average energy prices by ≥$2/MWh (saving >$40 million/year), and shifting existing-solar accreditation from 2–5 pm (68% summer) to 4–7 pm (∼50%) to correct over-crediting that artificially suppressed 2026 PRA clearing prices and reduced summer bids by $240/MW-day. Hydro and PSH owners should track storage accreditation methodology changes and price-volatility patterns as ESR fleet grows; the interchange optimization recommendation indicates MISO may adopt real-time co-optimization mechanics that would affect locational value and ramping-product revenue.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 08 2026 Biennial Review of MSC Inactive Issues on MISO Dashboard.pdf
relevance_reason: Storage-as-transmission asset (MSC-2020-1) and hybrid resource participation rules (MSC-2020-3) are among nine inactive issues under biennial review; both directly affect PSH participation models and revenue-recognition pathways, though both remain deprioritized for 2026.
ai_summary: MISO's 2026 biennial review recommends keeping all nine inactive Market Subcommittee issues in inactive status, including MSC-2020-1 (Using Storage as a Transmission Asset to Provide Market Services) and MSC-2020-3 (Enhanced Hybrid Resource Participation Model). Both issues were placed inactive in December 2022 and remain deprioritized due to competing initiatives—MSC-2020-3 is contingent on separate hybrid-resource work (MSC-2020-2) for which MISO has requested a delayed implementation date beyond June 2026. This preserves the current treatment of PSH and co-located hydro-storage hybrids under baseline participation rules rather than advancing alternative frameworks that could unlock additional revenue streams or operational flexibility. The next biennial review is scheduled for 2028, meaning no near-term market-design changes are anticipated for storage or hybrid resources in MISO.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 08 Improve Market Monitoring and Mitigation (MSC-2026-5)_IMM Module D Stakeholder Feedback.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Independent Market Monitor proposes revisions to market-monitoring and mitigation rules (Module D) that directly touch offer validation, commitment-cost caps during tight conditions, and reference-level development for demand-response resources — mechanisms that affect how dispatchable and use-limited resources (including hydropower and PSH) are mitigated and priced in real-time and emergency conditions.
stakeholders: Carrie Milton (Potomac Economics (MISO Independent Market Monitor))
ai_summary: The MISO Independent Market Monitor (Potomac Economics) presented stakeholder feedback on proposed Module D revisions to market-monitoring authority, duty-of-candor requirements, transmission withholding surveillance, and cost caps on startup/no-load offers during emergency-energy events and tight conditions. Key findings: Entergy and OMS MWG support IMM verification and capping of startup and no-load costs during emergencies (addressing very high offers submitted during winter storms not otherwise validated under tariff); the IMM is developing clarified authority to assess reference levels for demand-response and validate opportunity-cost components of startup offers (including costs from regulatory or emissions-based start restrictions). Redlined tariff language addressing stakeholder concerns on duty of candor scope, transmission "full capability" definition, and rule clarity is expected. Most affected are generators offering into tight/emergency conditions and demand-response resources used to validate reference levels; no vote date is stated.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 09 2026 Biennial Review of MSC Inactive Issues on MISO Dashboard.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's biennial review of inactive market-design issues includes three proposals directly touching hydro and PSH revenue and participation: storage-as-transmission (MSC-2020-1), hybrid-resource participation (MSC-2020-3), and uncertainty products (MSC-2021-9), all held inactive pending other work — their eventual reactivation will reshape how PSH and hybrid hydro participate in MISO's markets.
ai_summary: MISO's 2026 biennial review recommends keeping nine market-design issues inactive, including three with direct hydro impact: MSC-2020-1 (Using Storage as a Transmission Asset to Provide Market Services), MSC-2020-3 (Enhanced Hybrid Resource Participation Model), and MSC-2021-9 (Uncertainty Product in DA, RT, AS). All three remain inactive due to other competing priorities and contingencies—MSC-2020-3 explicitly waits on completion of a separate hybrid-resource initiative (MSC-2020-2) for which MISO requested a delayed implementation beyond June 2026. MSC-2020-1 has been inactive since December 2022; MSC-2021-9 was reactivated in February 2024 but returned to inactive status in November 2024. These issues, once active, would expand PSH's ability to offer grid services beyond energy and ancillary services, unlock hybrid participation in MISO's markets, and introduce flexibility products that could shift PSH's revenue sources—but no work is scheduled before the next biennial review in 2028.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 09 MSC Issue Status Change Recommendations.pdf
relevance_reason: The document addresses the Uninstructed Deviation Enhancement (UDE) Flag, which touches settlement rules and dispatch compliance affecting how hydro and other dispatchable resources are charged or credited for deviations from dispatch instructions—a direct obligation and settlement mechanism for hydro operators.
ai_summary: MISO implemented the Uninstructed Deviation Enhancement (UDE) Flag in November 2025, with settlement enhancements becoming effective June 1, 2026. The MSC recommends closing this issue (MSC-2024-7) as Completed. The UDE flag modifies how deviations from dispatch instructions are metered and settled; hydro and other dispatchable resources must now comply with revised settlement rules that took effect June 1, 2026, affecting how they are charged or credited for off-dispatch performance. The mechanism is live; no further stakeholder action is requested, but operators should verify their settlement systems align with the June 1, 2026 rules.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 11 FTR-ARR Market Enhancements (MSC-2025-5).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is evaluating the Auction Revenue Right (ARR) nomination and Financial Transmission Rights (FTR) allocation process, which directly affects how transmission-hedging revenue flows to generation owners, including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities that rely on point-to-point FTR positions for congestion hedging and revenue certainty.
ai_summary: MISO is conducting a holistic review of its ARR/FTR market under FERC Order 681/681-A compliance constraints, with focus on the ARR nomination process (Reference Year, definition of baseload supply resources), changes to the allocation of ARRs into monthly MPMA auctions, and improvements to transmission-outage reporting incentives. The current ARR allocation uses a fixed Reference Year tied to historical transmission usage to balance stability against gaming and accords long-term (10-year rollover) financial rights to Load Serving Entities for power supply hedging; MISO is evaluating whether the Reference Year, RSP definition, or tariff execution rules should be modified, while ensuring full funding of existing LTTRs and no introduction of new gaming opportunities. MISO requests stakeholder feedback by July 23, 2026, and will present next steps and proposed outage-reporting mechanisms at the August MSC meeting. Most exposed are generation owners (particularly hydropower and storage) whose ARR holdings and FTR self-scheduling optionality depend on stable path valuations; changes to Reference Year or baseload definitions could shift which generation types retain long-term transmission-hedge access.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 12 Enhance Reserve Deliverability for DA and RT Market (MSC-2026-2).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Regulation reserve market performance and clearing mechanism directly affects hydro and pumped-storage revenue from ancillary services (the largest and most volatile reserve product in MISO), the treatment of battery entry into Regulation markets, and potential automation of performance-based disqualification rules that could reshape how dispatchable and storage resources compete for reserve procurement.
ai_summary: MISO staff presents an analysis of Regulation reserve clearing and performance deterioration: Regulation requirements roughly doubled on July 1, 2024 (to ~800 MW base), Regulation reserve prices rose 53% and mileage prices 71% year-over-year in 2025, yet non-performance penalties reached 31% of total Regulation revenue by 2025, with gas resource penalties rising more than 2x year-over-year. The document identifies that (1) Regulation performance is assessed only in settlement, after-the-fact, with no automated real-time feedback loop to disqualify poor performers (unlike offline Supplemental and STR); and (2) coal and gas performance has deteriorated while batteries have entered as significant Regulation suppliers. MISO staff indicate they are evaluating options to improve real-time clearing and performance monitoring, with next steps to follow. Pumped-storage operators should monitor this closely because: (i) rising Regulation prices increase PSH revenue exposure to that product, but (ii) potential automation of performance testing and real-time disqualification could advantage faster-ramping technologies (batteries) and penalize PSH and conventional hydro more heavily if response-time or consistency standards are tightened without accounting for hydro's multi-hour operational flexibility constraints.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 13 Evaluation of Updates to Congestion Management and TCDCs (MSC-2026-6).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is evaluating transmission constraint demand curves (TCDCs) and congestion management during reserve shortages, which directly affects how dispatch and pricing decisions treat dispatchable generation—a core revenue mechanism for hydro—and how make-whole payments (DAMAP) are triggered when congestion and reserve scarcity interact.
ai_summary: MISO is evaluating updates to transmission constraint demand curves (TCDCs), which cap the shadow prices the market will pay to enforce network constraints, with particular focus on how undersized TCDCs interact with operating-reserve shortages to cause manual redispatches and Day-Ahead Marginal Assurance Payment (DAMAP) uplift. The document illustrates how reserve scarcity can overwhelm congestion signals, forcing resources down post-facto and triggering make-whole payments (e.g., Winter Storm Elliott example). MISO is considering expanding TCDC breakpoints, adding steps that increase during reserve shortages, and adjusting weighting between base and contingent constraints; a feedback request and implementation plan will be shared later in 2026. Dispatchable generators (including reservoir hydro) are most exposed: when reserves are scarce, high MEC penalties can force dispatch back to EcoMax despite binding constraints, then manual redispatch down creates the DAMAP liability. Hydro owners should track this work because enhanced TCDCs or dynamic reserve-shortage scaling could improve real-time economic dispatch and reduce the frequency of out-of-market corrections.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 13a DAMAP Reduction Total - New Issue Introduction (MSC-2026-7).pdf
relevance_reason: DAMAP (Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment) and RSG Distribution calculations directly affect revenue sufficiency and make-whole payments for dispatchable units including hydro; overstated Reduction Totals suppress adjusted DA schedules and thus unit revenue when derated or manually redispatched.
ai_summary: MISO proposes correcting the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total calculation, which currently overstates the sum of cleared day-ahead products by double-counting MWs shared across multiple products, resulting in artificially suppressed adjusted DA schedules for derated or manually redispatched units. The fix would base Reduction Totals on physical unit limitations rather than product sums, and would also correct a similar defect in Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee (RSG) Distribution. Both changes require tariff filings; MSC-2026-7 has been introduced for adoption at the July 9, 2026 Market Subcommittee meeting. Any hydro or pumped-storage unit that experiences deratings, manual redispatch, or forced outages would receive more accurate (and typically higher) make-whole compensation under the corrected formulas.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 13b DAMAP Reduction Total - Presentation (MSC-2026-7).pdf
relevance_reason: DAMAP Reduction Total calculation directly affects how derated and manually re-dispatched hydro and storage units are compensated for lost ancillary-service revenue, and the change alters the baseline against which their schedule adjustments are measured.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to revise the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total calculation for derated and manually re-dispatched units, replacing a simple sum-of-cleared-products approach with constraint-based clearing values that reflect physical MW-sharing limits between regulation, contingency reserves, ramp capability, and short-term reserves. Currently, the sum method can overstate total cleared MWs (since ancillary-service MWs can be shared with energy), leading to larger schedule reductions than physical capability warrants; the proposed method uses the maximum of two DART constraint combinations to more accurately cap the Reduction Total. MISO also proposes a parallel change to one Real-Time Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee distribution component (DDC_NDL_CAP_VOL) using the same constraint logic. Stakeholder feedback is due July 24, 2026, with a target Q3 2026 filing and 60-day effective date. The change most directly affects dispatchable hydro, pumped storage, and other use-limited resources that routinely clear multiple ancillary services alongside energy.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 14a DAMAP Reduction Total - New Issue Introduction (MSC-2026-7).pdf
relevance_reason: DAMAP and RSG calculations affect the adjusted day-ahead schedules and revenue guarantees for derated or manually re-dispatched units, which directly impacts revenue sufficiency and dispatch accuracy for dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage resources.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to correct the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total calculation, which currently overstates reductions by summing MWs across shared products when units are derated or manually re-dispatched. The correction would base the reduction on physical unit limitations instead, yielding more accurate adjusted day-ahead schedules and affecting the Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee (RSG) Distribution calculation as well. Both changes require a tariff filing; the Market Subcommittee is adopting this as a new issue (MSC-2026-7) for proceeding to tariff modification.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 14b DAMAP Reduction Total (MSC-2026-7)_redlines.docx
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Alters how electric storage resources (including pumped-storage hydro) are charged for real-time revenue sufficiency guarantee constraint-management deviations — directly affecting PSH revenue settlement and dispatch incentives across commitment states (charge, discharge, available, continuous).
ai_summary: MISO proposes revised rules for allocating Real-Time Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee (RRSG) Constraint Management Charges to electric storage resources, distinguishing treatment by day-ahead and real-time commitment status (Charge, Discharge, Available, Continuous). The rules separately govern deviations prior to and after the Notification Deadline, using Economic Minimum/Maximum Limits and Hourly Integrated Forecast constraints to determine which storage deviations trigger charges. PSH operators face altered settlement exposure depending on whether they are committed to charge, discharge, or remain available — with charges assessed when real-time dispatch deviates from day-ahead schedules in ways that increase active transmission-constraint flow. Most exposed are pumped-storage facilities with multi-hour round-trip commitments, where transitions between commitment states and forecast adjustments near the Notification Deadline will materially affect hourly net revenue. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt; this appears to be a working redline under MSC review.

title: 20260709 MSC Item 14b DAMAP Reduction Total - Presentation (MSC-2026-7).pdf
relevance_reason: DAMAP Reduction Total calculation affects how derated or manually re-dispatched hydro units have their day-ahead schedules scaled, directly impacting revenue recognition and ancillary-services stacking for dispatchable resources including pumped storage and reservoir hydro.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to change how the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total is calculated for derated and manually re-dispatched units, replacing a summation of all cleared day-ahead products (Energy + Regulation + Contingency Reserves + Ramp + Short-Term Reserve) with the maximum of two DART constraint-clearing values that reflect which products can physically share megawatts. The current method potentially overstates the Reduction Total by double-counting shared MWs across products, resulting in lower adjusted day-ahead schedules for affected units; the proposed method aligns the reduction calculation with actual physical constraints that limit product combinations during day-ahead clearing. MISO also proposes a conforming change to one Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee Distribution component (DDC_NDL_CAP_VOL). Stakeholder feedback is due July 24, 2026, with a target FERC filing in Q3 2026 and effective date 60 days thereafter. This principally affects dispatchable thermal and hydroelectric units that regularly clear ancillary services alongside energy, and pumped-storage facilities that rely on accurate product-stacking calculations for profit optimization.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This agenda covers three capacity/resource-adequacy items directly affecting hydro and storage accreditation — solar accreditation for PY27-28, external resource accreditation revision, and a 24-hour lead-time accreditation proposal — plus PRMR allocation methodology that shapes how reserves (a hydro strength) are procured and compensated.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Andy Taylor (MISO); Chris Delk (MISO); Davey Lopez (MISO); Werner Roth (MISO); Zhaoxia Xie (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO Resource Adequacy Subcommittee meeting scheduled for July 8, 2026, with five substantive items: (1) Solar accreditation for planning years 2027-28, (2) External resource accreditation revision (tied to RASC-2019-2 and RASC-2024-5), (3) a 24-hour lead-time accreditation proposal (RASC-2026-1), (4) PRMR (Planning Reserve Margin Requirement) allocation (RASC-2020-4 and 2019-2), and (5) BPM-011 annual review. Items 3, 5, and 6 request feedback; items 6 and 8 are potential voting items. The 24-hour lead-time rule and external resource accreditation overhaul will reshape how hydro and pumped storage compete for RA value alongside solar and batteries; PRMR reallocation directly affects the reserve procurements that hydro supplies. No decision dates are listed, but the feedback-request flag on items 3 and 6 signals imminent drafting or voting.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 01c Action Items.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is managing feedback on resource accreditation rules, including external-resource and solar accreditation, which directly affect how hydro and storage projects are credited toward resource adequacy and revenue in MISO's capacity framework.
ai_summary: MISO Resource Adequacy Subcommittee is processing three concurrent accreditation feedback requests (closing June 10–23, 2026): Solar Accreditation for PY27–28 (RASC-2019-2), 24-Hour Lead-Time in Accreditation (RASC-2026-1), and External Resource Accreditation (RASC-2019-2 and RASC-2024-5, with extended deadline of June 12). These efforts govern how diverse resources—including hydro and storage—are valued and accredited in MISO's resource-adequacy posture; external-resource and lead-time accreditation rules directly shape pumped-storage participation and seasonal/hourly capacity credit. The June deadline windows are the operational focus for NHA members monitoring MISO accreditation mechanics. MISO separately reported on PRMR (Planning Reserve Margin Requirement) allocation results at the LSE/LRZ level as of April 15, 2026.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 01c Minutes 20260520.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is updating resource accreditation frameworks for solar (affecting capacity-RA and accreditation precedent for variable resources), discussing CONE reference-technology retention for capacity markets, and developing PRMR allocation and LOLE adjustment methodologies — all mechanisms that establish accreditation and capacity-valuation precedents applicable to hydro and pumped-storage accreditation, offer development, and adequacy-study participation.
stakeholders: Trey Mayeux (Cleco); Ryan Lowe (MB HYDRO); Sam Kovnats (MB HYDRO); Amanda Jones (MISO); Andy Taylor (MISO); Joshua Schabla (MISO); Ryan Kelley (MISO); Zhaoxia Xie (MISO); Werner Roth (TX PUC)
ai_summary: MISO's Resource Adequacy Subcommittee (May 20, 2026) finalized Module E-2 tariff revisions for new LSE resource-adequacy requirements and advanced two material accreditation proposals: (1) a shift in solar Non-GADS Template accreditation hours (PY27-28) to align with observed reliability-risk windows rather than fixed windows, maintaining existing accreditation percentages (Summer/Fall/Spring 50%, Winter 5%) but adjusting timing, with no tariff change required and stakeholder feedback closing by stated dates; and (2) continued development of PRMR allocation design and LOLE capacity-adjustment methodology with results to be shared at the LSE/LRZ level. The solar accreditation shift is a stepping stone toward a longer-term Dynamic Loss-of-Load (DLOL) design. These accreditation mechanics—particularly the use of observed risk-hour timing and the movement away from fixed templates—directly affect how variable resources and seasonal/peaking-capable resources like pumped storage are credited in adequacy showings; hydro owners should monitor whether similar risk-timing adjustments are proposed for hydro and whether DLOL eventually treats all dispatchable and storage resources under a unified framework that could disadvantage long-duration assets. Action items on PRMR results and volatility-analysis methodology remain open.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: This is a RASC management and work-plan document that governs which resource-adequacy topics MISO will address; it directly impacts hydro accreditation rules, external-resource participation (relevant to run-of-river and PSH), and capacity accreditation reform—all core to hydro revenue and compliance.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Zhaoxia Xie (MISO); Trey Mayeux (Resource Adequacy Subcommittee (MISO)); Werner Roth (Resource Adequacy Subcommittee (MISO))
ai_summary: The RASC Management Plan schedules MISO's resource-adequacy work through Q4 2027, with three items directly material to hydro owners: (1) External Resource Accreditation (RASC-2024-5)—covering accreditation of hydro and storage resources that connect via HVDC or other transmission—appears on the July 8, August 19, and November 11, 2026 agendas; (2) Accreditation Reform BPM Language (RASC-2019-9) is scheduled for July and September 2026, addressing how accreditation calculations apply to use-limited resources like hydro; (3) 24-Hour Lead Time in Accreditation (RASC-2026-1, marked as requesting feedback) appears July 8, 2026 and may alter how hydro must pre-commit to RA showings. The plan also flags CONE Reference Technology updates and PRMR Allocation (RASC-2020-4, RASC-2019-2) as recurring items. Most exposed are pumped-storage operators and external hydro resources (particularly those connected via HVDC) seeking RA accreditation under new rules; next engagement points are July 8, August 19, and September 30, 2026 meetings.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 02a SC Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is reassigning two issues to the Large Load Working Group involving co-located generation/load and large-load definition, which directly affect hydro's strategic options for hybrid projects, behind-the-meter service, and participation models in a high-load-growth environment.
stakeholders: Brian Drumm (MISO Steering Committee); Dan Scripps (MISO Steering Committee)
ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee approved reassignment of two Planning Advisory Committee issues to the Large Load Working Group: Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation (behind the same POI) (PAC-2024-4a) and Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1). These issues address how MISO will define and regulate co-located generation and load at a single point of interconnection, a participation model growing in importance for hydro owners considering hybrid or load-co-location strategies. The LLWG will continue development with next Steering Committee review scheduled August 4, 2026. Hydro owners with projects capable of serving on-site or nearby loads should monitor LLWG progress, as these definitions will shape whether co-located hydro can participate in day-ahead/real-time energy markets and what operational constraints apply.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 02b LOLEWG Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) modeling directly affects hydro and storage resource accreditation under both current ELCC and future DLOL frameworks, which determines capacity value and RA contributions; wind profile enhancements and cross-class accreditation impacts alter the competitive accreditation landscape for storage and thermal resources.
ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group reported advances in wind profile modeling (transitioning to weather-conditioned synthetic profiles) and is conducting PY 2027-2028 LOLE and Capacity/Energy Limited (CIL/CEL) studies. Wind accreditation under the enhanced profiles shows increases in Summer, Winter, and Spring ELCC values; critically, shifts in risk-hour distribution under the future Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) framework will cross-impact accreditation for storage and thermal resources. MISO requested stakeholder feedback on 2027-2028 CIL/CEL study input files by July 7, 2026, and preliminary results will be presented by September 2026. Load growth is unprecedented and calling into question load-forecast uncertainty assumptions in the LOLE process. Pumped storage operators face material changes to seasonal capacity accreditation as MISO refines its RA modeling; the shift from ELCC to DLOL fundamentally redefines how storage is credited during critical risk hours, most likely reducing seasonal/winter accreditation benefits for use-limited resources like PSH.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 03a FERC Monthly Status Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO resource-adequacy tariff proceedings directly affect hydro's capacity accreditation (SAC/deliverability), LOLE methodology, and load-modifying resource rules; multiple open dockets shape hydro's RA obligations and revenue.
stakeholders: Coalition of Midwest Power Producers, Inc. (COMPP) (COMPP); JERA Nex Americas LLC (JERA Nex Americas LLC); Lansing Board of Water and Light (LBWL) (Lansing Board of Water and Light); Pelican Power LLC (Pelican Power LLC); Rainbow Energy Center, LLC (Rainbow Energy Center, LLC); Voltus, Inc. (Voltus, Inc.)
ai_summary: MISO has five active FERC dockets affecting resource-adequacy rules and hydro participation: (1) EL26-70-000 (large load accommodation) — FERC directed MISO to file an informational report on generation adequacy for large loads and interconnection speed by July 20, 2026; (2) ER26-2974-000 (deliverability accreditation) — MISO proposed tariff revisions to account for deliverability in SAC capacity value when implementing Schedules 53A and 53B in Planning Year 2028/2029, with Module A effective September 1, 2026; (3) ER26-2706-000 (LOLE capacity adjustment) — MISO proposed methodology changes to the LOLE study with effective date August 1, 2026; (4) ER26-1882-000 (load-modifying resource penalties) — MISO amended proposed penalty rules for LMRs that fail to respond to Scheduling Instructions, requesting effective date of June 1, 2027, after FERC deficiency letter on May 22, 2026; (5) ER25-1886-000 (demand response and emergency resources) — FERC accepted MISO's reform tariff on June 18, 2026 with compliance filing due July 20, 2026, effective September 1, 2027. Hydro owners are affected by deliverability rules that determine their capacity accreditation value, LOLE study changes that drive RA requirements, and demand-response/LMR rules that define competitive adjacencies and performance penalties.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 03b BPM-011-r35 Resource Adequacy_Redline.docx
relevance_reason: MISO's Resource Adequacy Business Practices Manual (BPM-011-r35) governs accreditation methodology, seasonal capacity credit, must-offer requirements, and RA compliance processes that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited and obligated in MISO's resource-adequacy regime.
ai_summary: This is MISO's annual-review revision (r35, effective September 30, 2026) of BPM-011 (Resource Adequacy manual), the governing document for MISO's seasonal RA accreditation, UCAP/ISAC processes, demand-response and storage qualification, zonal deliverability, and compliance settlement. The document title indicates it is in redline (showing changes), and the revision summary notes edits are pending final summarization. Key prior revisions (r27–r34) addressed seasonal accreditation implementation (FERC ER22-495), electric storage resource accreditation (r26), intermittent-resource UCAP qualification, demand-resource testing and registration, and GVTC (Guaranteed Vendor Test Conditions) language for hydro and run-of-river resources (r10). Hydro owners participating in MISO must track this manual for changes to how seasonal capacity credit, zonal deliverability benefit, run-of-river accreditation, and storage (including pumped-storage) qualification are calculated and verified; the 2026 annual review may contain material changes to these mechanisms, but the summary of edits is incomplete in this excerpt.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 04 Liaison Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection and generation-adequacy planning (strategic option affecting hydro's value through load growth), plus MISO's resource-adequacy compliance calendar and demand-response accreditation rules that directly affect hydro and storage operators' seasonal obligations and capacity claims.
stakeholders: Andrew Taylor (MISO); Daniel Wilson (MISO); Jasmine Davis (MISO); Marc Keyser (MISO); Ryan Darrah (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's RASC liaison report covers four core items: (1) a PRA calendar reminder for Planning Year 2026–2027 with ICAP Deferral deadlines Aug 31, Nov 30, and Feb 26, 2027 for LSEs and resource owners; (2) a FERC Order to Show Cause (EL26-70-000, June 18, 2026) directing MISO to improve large-load interconnection and report by July 20, 2026 on generation adequacy and queue acceleration; (3) a June 18, 2026 FERC acceptance of MISO's DR/ER tariff reforms with compliance filing due July 20, 2026 on emergency-resource switching, baseline backfilling, and demand-resource capability calculations; and (4) announcements on market-data-exchange transitions and workshops (including a Large Load Working Group July 16, Locational Marginal Emissions workshop July 17, and RI initiatives workshop July 29). The large-load FERC order and MISO's generation-adequacy reporting obligation create a material strategic window: hydro owners need awareness that MISO is under commission mandate to accelerate interconnection of large loads (data centers, industrial) and that generation-adequacy studies will now explicitly inform queue timing and resource-procurement urgency — scarcity signals that can raise hydro's energy and capacity value. The seasonal ICAP deadlines directly govern reserve accreditation; the DR/ER reforms affect how demand-resource competition for capacity-market share is structured. Most exposure for pumped-storage and conventional hydro in resource-adequacy portfolios relying on seasonal capacity credits.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 05 Solar Accreditation for PY27-28 (RASC-2019-2).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's decision to maintain fixed solar accreditation windows for PY27-28 and defer to a Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) based accreditation model in PY28-29 affects how non-dispatchable resources are credited in capacity/resource-adequacy calculations, setting a methodological precedent that influences how hydro's own accreditation and capacity value are benchmarked and may be reformed across MISO.
ai_summary: MISO decided to maintain current fixed solar accreditation hours for planning years 2027-28 despite stakeholder concern that observed peak reliability-risk hours for solar have shifted later in the day as installed capacity has grown. The decision defers proposed accreditation updates pending full implementation of Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) based accreditation in PY28-29, when solar class accreditation will be based on critical hours in the loss-of-load-expectation (LOLE) model rather than fixed hour windows. This matters to hydro owners because DLOL methodologies (which use observed loss-of-load events to define accreditation windows) are being adopted to address the divergence between fixed-hour accreditation and actual system scarcity; hydro's own accreditation framework may face similar pressure to move from traditional ELCC windows toward performance-driven models, and precedents set for solar will inform how flexibility and run-of-river resources are credited in MISO resource adequacy.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 06 External Resources Accreditation (RASC-2024-5 and 2019-2).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is reforming accreditation methodology for external resources (pseudo-tied and non-pseudo-tied), which directly affects how external hydropower (including pumped storage participating across seams) receives capacity-value accreditation and participates in the Planning Resource Auction—a core revenue and participation mechanism for hydro owners exporting into MISO or participating via external-resource schedules.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to accredit pseudo-tied external resources using Schedule 53A (real-time offer data, 3-year lookback, 80/20 weighting between Tier 2 RA and Tier 1 non-RA hours) and non-pseudo-tied external resources using day-ahead e-Tag availability (same weighting, no outage exemptions, 3-year lookback). MISO is also eliminating the Use-Limited Resources category, redirecting accreditation of all use-limited assets (including run-of-river and storage hydro) into the Schedule 53A and Schedule 53B frameworks. The proposal shifts external hydro accreditation away from ad-hoc techniques toward seasonal-availability-aligned methods, but non-pseudo-tied resources (which may include many Northwest hydro exports) lose outage exemptions and rely on day-ahead e-Tag offers rather than real-time operational data—potentially reducing accreditation for resources whose actual availability exceeds their day-ahead self-commitments. Stakeholder comments are due July 26, 2026; MISO targets FERC filing in Q4 2026 or Q1 2027 with effective date Planning Year 2029/30 or beyond. Most exposed: pumped storage and run-of-river hydro exporting from adjacent balancing authorities via external-resource schedules; large reservoir hydro with seasonal energy constraints that may be miscaptured by day-ahead e-Tag methodology.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 07 24 Hour Lead Time in Accreditation (RASC-2026-1).pdf
relevance_reason: MISO proposes three alternative rules for how offline resources' capacity accreditation should be calculated based on lead time, which directly affects how thermal and hydro peaking units can be credited toward resource-adequacy showings during emergencies — the key revenue mechanism for dispatchable generation.
ai_summary: MISO proposes three solutions to close a perceived gap in its 24-hour lead-time accreditation rule for offline resources. The problem: thermal generators increased their start-time lead times approaching Winter Storms Fern, Enzo, and Heather, rendering 3.1 GW unavailable for commitment in the Day-Ahead Market and Forward Reliability Assessment Commitment (FRAC) processes while still receiving full accreditation value, despite contributing to emergency deployment of LMRs. Solution 1 (12-hour max lead time) aligns offline-resource accreditation with the Operating Margin calculation but risks uneconomic commitments. Solution 2 (zero availability for lead-time increases within 48 hours of the operating day) would penalize changes near emergencies but allow longer-lead resources to participate in multi-day FRAC. Solution 3 (sliding scale: full availability at 4 hours, declining 5% per hour to zero at 24+ hours) offers graduated incentives. MISO will select a recommended solution in August 2026, file revised tariff at FERC in October 2026. Stakeholders should comment by July 22, 2026. Pumped-storage and conventional-hydro operators should monitor because the same accreditation rules govern their RA value; any tightening of lead-time rules that penalizes start-time flexibility, or that creates asymmetries between thermal and hydro accreditation, could compress the capacity credit PSH and peaking hydro claim during peak-scarcity periods.

title: 20260708 RASC Item 08 PRMR Allocation (RASC-2020-4 and 2019-2).pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: MISO's shift from forecasted load (CPDf) to three-year-lookback settled load for Planning Reserve Margin Requirement (PRMR) allocation changes how reserve obligations are assigned to Load Serving Entities, directly affecting the revenue adequacy and resource-procurement obligations of hydro and PSH owners whose output and strategic flexibility are valued in MISO's resource-adequacy and Planning Resource Auction frameworks.
ai_summary: MISO proposes replacing Coincident Peak Demand forecast (CPDf)-based PRMR allocation with a Resource Adequacy Hour Demand (RAHD) metric using three-year-lookback settled load during Tier 2 RA hours, weighted by effective margin. The proposal aligns PRMR allocation with MISO's updated Resource Accreditation reform and the DLOL framework, tying reserve requirements to actual historical load behavior during highest-risk hours rather than peak forecasts. Hydro and PSH operators benefit when this mechanism improves accuracy of LSE reserve obligations: precise allocation signals encourage load-serving entities to procure capacity-constrained products (like the ramping and storage flexibility hydro and pumped storage supply) at times of actual scarcity rather than all-hours forecasts. MISO requests stakeholder feedback by July 24, 2026, with initial tariff redlines due in August 2026, final redlines in September 2026, and FERC filing targeted for end of 2026.

committee: Expedited Project Review Technical Study Task Force
meeting_title: Expedited Project Review Technical Study Task Force (EPR-TSTF) - July 7, 2026
