stakeholders: Adam Meyer (MISO); Dave Seelye (MISO); Eric Olson (MISO); Nestor Martinez (MISO); Z. Zhou (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's Expedited Project Review (EPR) task force is advancing technical studies for multiple infrastructure projects across four regions, including two data-center load additions in the West Region (Hannover facility projects 51293 and 51404). The EPR process streamlines interconnection review for projects meeting MISO criteria, reducing study timelines. Data-center loads are drivers of regional scarcity and transmission constraint—the addition of these loads in the West Region increases locational price signals and raises the economic value of existing hydro resources that can deliver into that area; simultaneously, transmission upgrades bundled with large-load projects (e.g., the Hannover upgrades) may relieve or shift constraints that affect hydro's ability to move power to constrained nodes. Next meeting is August 4, 2026; this session (July 7, 2026) reviews results and technical findings for Central, East, West, and South region projects.

committee: Expedited Project Review Technical Study Task Force
meeting_title: Expedited Project Review Technical Study Task Force (EPR-TSTF) - July 7, 2026
title: 20260707 EPR-TSTF Item 03 MISO EPR Statistics.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection (data centers and industrial facilities) in MISO creates scarcity and demand growth that raises the value and dispatch frequency of existing hydro resources, while also establishing transmission planning priorities that affect deliverability and strategic co-location opportunities for hydro owners.
ai_summary: MISO's Expedited Project Review process has approved 67 projects through July 2026 representing ~14,000+ MW of new load additions across February, April, and June cycles. Prominent new loads include multiple large data centers (Hannover 430 MW, Project Skyway 780 MW, Project Atom 300 MW, Peninsula DC 430 MW) and industrial facilities concentrated in Central and West regions. These projects trigger transmission build-outs and distribution upgrades that reshape MISO's interconnection queue and establish new load pockets. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, this load concentration raises scarcity rents during peak periods, increases the value of ramping flexibility to serve demand ramps at large loads, and may create co-location or load-serving-entity pathways if MISO permits behind-meter arrangements; it also signals which transmission corridors MISO is prioritizing, which affects where existing hydro deliverability constraints may bind or be relieved.

title: 20260707 RSC Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: The document establishes MISO's Reliability Subcommittee governance and work program, which directly oversees issues affecting hydro operational compliance, winterization requirements, ramping/uncertainty products, and frequency response standards — core obligations and revenue mechanisms for hydro operators in the MISO footprint.
stakeholders: Jane Laskowski-Hart (MISO); John Harmon (MISO); Eric Barry (Reliability Subcommittee (MISO)); Ray McCausland (Reliability Subcommittee (MISO))
ai_summary: MISO's Reliability Subcommittee (RSC) management plan outlines the 2026–2027 governance and meeting calendar for the committee responsible for advising on reliability policies, NERC Reliability Standards, tariff administration, and market processes within MISO. Key topics scheduled include Primary Frequency Response and GIA Limits (RSC 2025-3), Dynamic Ramp Capability and Dynamic Regulation Reserve requirements (RSC-2024-1 and RSC-2024-2), Uncertainty Management (quarterly), annual Generator Winterization Survey, FERC Order 881 compliance, and Reconfiguration for Congestion Cost updates. The document's significance to hydro operators lies in its roadmap for setting and revising rules governing winterization compliance, ramping-product design, regulation reserves, and frequency response accreditation — revenue streams and operational obligations that directly affect how MISO-region hydro (especially run-of-river and storage-dependent generators) are dispatched and compensated. The RSC also reviews event analyses and NERC lessons learned relevant to hydro-dependent grid stability. No specific comment deadlines or votes are quoted beyond the committee's routine Q1–Q2 2027 schedule; the document is procedural rather than action-forcing.

title: 20260707 RSC Item 02d Top 10 Congestion Constraints.pdf
relevance_reason: Congestion cost and flowgate performance directly affects hydro's transmission-constrained revenue from energy and ramping; M2M coordinated flowgates and outage scheduling influence when hydro can deliver value to export/load-pocket markets.
ai_summary: MISO Reliability Subcommittee reports on the top 10 cost-generating congested constraints in its footprint as of July 2026. Seven of the ten constraints are M2M (Midwest-to-Market) coordinated flowgates, and six of the top ten stem from planned outages. The document presents a baseline congestion topology but does not indicate proposed mitigations or next steps. The relevance to hydro lies in flowgate binding and outage scheduling: tight M2M flowgates limit transmission capability for exporting generators (including hydro from the upper Midwest or neighboring regions) into constrained zones, and planned-outage timing can either relieve or exacerbate scarcity pricing windows when hydro's ramping and energy offer highest margin. Operators in hydro-rich neighboring RTOs (e.g., PJM members with hydro capacity, or DSOs considering MISO seams) should track whether MISO's congestion profile shifts—persistent binding on M2M corridors may signal interregional transmission expansion opportunity or pressure for storage-as-transmission alternatives.

title: 20260707 RSC Item 04 Regulatory Compliance Update.pdf
relevance_reason: NERC ride-through standard (PRC-029-2) currently under ballot affects synchronous unit operating standards that hydro must comply with; large-load interconnection Show Cause Order touches hydro's strategic position in resource adequacy.
ai_summary: MISO reports on regulatory compliance status across FERC and NERC activities. A FERC Show Cause Order on large-load integration (Docket EL26-70-000) requires MISO to address generation adequacy and interconnection speed by August 17, 2026, with an abeyance request possible August 2 to allow further stakeholder input on future tariff filings—directly relevant to how quickly new resources (and by extension, existing hydro's scarcity value) are interconnected. Separately, NERC's PRC-029-2 (Ride Through Revisions) is in draft ballot (through 7/13/2026), and PRC-024-4 (Frequency and Voltage Protection for Synchronous Generators) has an October 1, 2026 enforcement date; these impose operating standards on synchronous machines including conventional hydro. Additionally, Project 2025-05 on ride-through revisions and Project 2025-06 on supply-chain management are progressing with comment windows closing 7/13/2026 and 7/17/2026 respectively.

title: 20260707 SC Item 01b Agenda
relevance_reason: MISO is reviewing co-located load and generation participation rules and large-load definitions, both of which directly affect strategic options for hydro owners seeking to serve load behind the meter or participate in emerging co-location markets.
ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee is convening a special meeting to charter a Large Load Working Group and review two active policy issues: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation at the same Point of Interconnection (ZGIA, PAC-2024-4a), and (2) Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1). Both items are flagged as potential voting matters and candidates for reassignment to the new LLWG. These rules determine whether hydro owners can co-locate generation with load for behind-the-meter service and how large-load interconnections are treated, directly opening or constraining pathways for hydro to participate in new market structures as load growth and data-center demand accelerate in MISO's footprint. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda.

title: 20260707 SC Item 01c Management Plan
relevance_reason: MISO Steering Committee is reviewing charters and management plans for entities that will govern large-load and co-located-generation participation (ZGIA PAC-2024-4A and Large Load Definition PAC-2024-1), which directly affect hydro's strategic options to serve co-located load and influence market-design rules that touch revenue and dispatch.
stakeholders: Carmen Clark (MISO); Eileen King (MISO); Brian Drumm (MISO Steering Committee); Dan Scripps (MISO Steering Committee)
ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee is reviewing two proposals on its July 7, 2026 special meeting agenda: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation behind the same Point of Interconnection (ZGIA PAC-2024-4A), and (2) Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1), with the Large Load Working Group charter and management plan also up for review. These proposals will establish how MISO treats behind-the-meter load and generation co-location and defines large loads going forward. For hydropower owners, especially those with run-of-river or reservoir operations, co-location rules determine whether they can serve load behind the meter or co-locate with new load, altering revenue streams and operational flexibility; large-load definitions affect how resource-adequacy and capacity-market rules apply to hydro-serving-large-load arrangements. The Steering Committee will vote on these items on July 7, 2026 (marked as potential voting items), making that date the actionable deadline for stakeholder input if comments are still open.

title: 20260707 SC Item 02 DRAFT 2026 LLWG Charter
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection and co-location can materially alter hydro's strategic positioning and revenue — loads with synchronized timing can drive scarcity pricing, absorb hydro output, and create co-location participation opportunities; the LLWG's charter establishes the forum through which MISO will develop policies on large-load integration affecting hydro's market access and locational value.
ai_summary: MISO is establishing a Large Load Working Group (LLWG) reporting to its Advisory Committee to provide policy guidance on processes and market structures for integrating large loads in the MISO footprint, addressing issues spanning markets, operations, planning, and resource adequacy. The LLWG will convene stakeholders on an as-needed basis to coordinate with other MISO stakeholder groups. For hydro owners, this signals formalization of large-load integration as a strategic market-design priority — policies emerging from the LLWG will directly shape how big loads (data centers, industrial, co-located generation) interact with hydro's locational dispatch value, ancillary-service opportunities, and potential hybrid/co-location participation pathways. The group's scope encompasses resource adequacy and operations, where hydro's role as a flexible, location-sensitive asset will be relevant to load-integration decisions.

title: 20260707 SC Item 02 LLWG Management Plan
relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load Working Group is actively developing market-participation rules for co-located load and generation (including zero-injection GIAs), reserve-product enhancements, and interconnection requirements that directly affect hybrid/co-located hydropower and storage participation options and revenue models.
stakeholders: Marc Keyser (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO Large Load Working Group); Werner Roth (MISO Large Load Working Group); Amanda Jones (MISO Stakeholder Relations); Jake Carrico (MISO Stakeholder Relations)
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is developing several rule packages in 2026–27 that will shape how large loads and co-located generation participate in MISO markets. Key active issues include: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation with zero-injection GIA consideration (PAC-2024-4, feedback requested May 14 and June 10); (2) Large Load Firm Service Step Up (LLWG-2026-1, tariff redlines due August 27); (3) Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG-2026-2, evaluation and cost-allocation recommendations due Q4 2026); and (4) Interconnection Reliability Requirements including stability assessments (LLWG-2026-3). The co-location rule package directly opens a pathway for hydropower and pumped-storage facilities to register and dispatch co-located load behind the meter, potentially unlocking new revenue streams from load service and reducing network-cost exposure. Reserve-product enhancements and firm-service mechanics will change the mix of flexibility and capacity products available to large, controllable loads—affecting how hydro-connected or hybrid-sited PSH competes for reserve and flexibility revenue. LLWG meetings are scheduled through Q4 2026, with tariff redlines and final recommendations due by December 2026.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - July 1, 2026
title: 20260701 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 04 State of the Market Presentation.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's 2025 State of the Market report addresses capacity accreditation reform (marginal DLOL methodology), reserve and frequency response incentives, congestion pricing and transmission management, virtual market liquidity effects on energy pricing, large load interconnection impacts, and renewable resource integration challenges—each directly affecting hydro and PSH revenue, strategic positioning, and system value in a 26.6 GW load-growth scenario.
stakeholders: David Patton, PhD (MISO Independent Market Monitor (Potomac Economics))
ai_summary: MISO's Independent Market Monitor presented findings from the 2025 State of the Market report showing all-in prices rose 67% to $53/MWh, driven by natural gas cost increases and capacity-market reforms. Key findings for hydro owners: (1) MISO transitioned to a reliability-based demand curve in capacity markets and reformed resource accreditation to reflect resources' marginal contribution to reliability in riskiest hours—shifting value from solar/wind with declining accreditation to dispatchable resources, including hydro; (2) shortage-pricing reforms in Fall 2025 improved energy and ancillary-service price signals, and MISO filed recommended penalties to improve incentive for units to follow dispatch setpoints; (3) real-time congestion costs rose 23% to $2.2B, driven partly by conservative transmission ratings and MISO's manual re-dispatch—highlighting transmission constraints that affect hydro deliverability; (4) MISO projects 26.6 GW load additions by 2030 (3.1–5.1% CAGR), with guidance that co-located generation and load should not avoid capacity or transmission costs, and siting at weak network locations should be non-firm pending upgrades—affecting co-location economics and interconnection strategy; (5) renewable penetration peaked at 45.7% in 2025, creating ramp-demand volatility and voltage-support constraints that favor dispatchable resources and storage; (6) marginal accreditation for new renewables is falling sharply, making continued investment in dispatchable generation essential—directly elevating the system value of existing hydro and PSH in MISO's reliability planning.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - July 1, 2026
title: 20260701 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 04 State of the Market Report.pdf
relevance_reason: State of the market report covering MISO energy and ancillary services pricing, resource adequacy trends, transmission congestion, virtual market activity, and recommended market reforms that affect hydro revenue, capacity accreditation, and dispatch performance.
ai_summary: Potomac Economics' 2025 State of the Market Report for MISO examines energy price formation, ancillary services markets, capacity adequacy, transmission congestion, and resource operations across the year, with a forward-looking section on future market needs as wind/solar penetration rises and net-load ramps accelerate. Key findings include uplift costs in real-time markets, changes in MISO's generating capacity mix, congestion dynamics on key transfer constraints, and seasonal adequacy challenges highlighted by 2025 load-shed events and transmission emergencies in MISO South. The report's recommendations (Section X) propose reforms to energy pricing, operating reserves, ramping products, transmission congestion management, and capacity market design — with particular relevance to resource adequacy rules and scarcity-pricing mechanisms that directly influence dispatchable and storage resource revenues and obligations. Most exposed: all MISO hydro and pumped-storage owners subject to MISO's capacity and ancillary services accreditation, and those exposed to real-time pricing and make-whole payment volatility during tight-supply periods.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: Large Load Working Group agenda addresses co-located load-and-generation registration, interconnection reliability, and operational requirements that directly affect hydro's strategic options for serving large loads and co-location arrangements, plus hydro's obligations for operational reliability and dispatch coordination.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Beibei Li (MISO); Chen-Hao Tsai (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO); Chris Supino (MISO); Hemant Patil (MISO); Marc Keyser (MISO); Sandeep Yeleti (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is meeting June 29, 2026, to address market participation rules for co-located load and generation (item 6: Zero-Injection GIA under PAC-2024-2B), interconnection reliability requirements (LLWG-2026-3), large-load telemetry/PMU requirements (LLWG-2026-4C), operational forecasting requirements (LLWG-2026-4A), and reserve product enhancements (LLWG-2026-2). The co-location and zero-injection items directly enable hydro owners to serve large loads behind the meter or as bundled resources, expanding revenue and strategic flexibility; the interconnection and operational requirements impose new compliance obligations on dispatchable generation (including hydropower) serving large loads. Next LLWG meeting is July 16, 2026. No voting or comment deadlines are stated in this agenda.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01c Action Items.pdf
relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection and co-located load affect hydro's strategic positioning in energy markets and scarcity value; this action-items document flags active MISO work on large-load firm service that could shape transmission access and revenue opportunities for hydro.
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group issued feedback requests on June 10, 2026 (closed) for the Large Load Firm Service Step Up proposal (LLWG-2026-1), with comments originally due June 24. The proposal addresses how MISO accommodates large-load customers seeking firm service, a mechanism that intersects hydro's role in providing dispatchable energy and flexibility to serve new demand. Large-load firm-service design can shift the value of hydro by changing transmission constraints, scarcity pricing, and the premium for flexible supply — particularly for pumped-storage operators positioned to serve or compete with large loads. The document provides no detail on the proposal's substance, status, or next steps.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260610.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is developing Zero-Injection Generator Interconnection Agreements (ZGIAs) to enable co-located load-and-generation market participation, which directly affects hydro and pumped-storage strategic options for hybrid/co-located resource models and load-following services.
stakeholders: Marc Keyser (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO Large Load Working Group); Werner Roth (MISO Large Load Working Group); Eileen King (MISO Stakeholder Relations)
ai_summary: MISO is advancing a Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) framework to permit market participation and registration of co-located load and generation resources, with draft tariff language posted and a planned FERC filing at end of June 2026. The proposal follows six months of collaborative stakeholder input and includes interconnection process, market participation, and registration mechanics. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, ZGIAs create a participation pathway for co-located load (e.g., data centers, industrial hydro-adjacent facilities) to be served behind-the-meter or via dedicated interconnection without exporting net energy to the grid, potentially increasing asset utilization and revenue optionality for hybrid facilities and enabling PSH charging load registration. The framework is near completion; the June filing will trigger FERC review and any final stakeholder feedback window.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: This MISO working group management plan addresses large-load integration, co-located generation/load, and operational reliability requirements that directly affect hydro and pumped-storage participation options, interconnection pathways, and reserve-product design.
stakeholders: Marc Keyser (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO LLWG); Werner Roth (MISO LLWG); Amanda Jones (MISO Stakeholder Relations); Jake Carrico (MISO Stakeholder Relations)
ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is tracking six active workstreams through Q4 2026 that reshape how large loads and co-located resources (including hydro-paired load) participate in MISO markets. Key items include: (1) Large Load Firm Service Step Up (LLWG-2026-1) and reserve-product enhancements (LLWG-2026-2), which will define capacity/reserve accreditation and service-level requirements for flexible resources attached to large loads; (2) Interconnection reliability requirements (LLWG-2026-3) and operational-reliability workstreams (LLWG-2026-4A/B/C covering forecasting, network models, and PMU/telemetry) that establish must-offer and real-time operational standards for co-located assets; and (3) Market participation rules for zero-injection GIAs (PAC-2024-4B), affecting how hydro or storage can register co-located load without serving the MISO grid. The June 29, 2026 meeting will surface a tentative FERC action and initial reserve-enhancement evaluation results; final tariff redlines and recommendations are scheduled for August and November 2026. Pumped-storage operators and conventional hydro owners considering co-location or large-load service opportunities need to monitor reserve-product design and the zero-injection GIA interpretation, as these will determine whether co-located capacity can stack ancillary-service revenues or will be constrained by MISO operational requirements.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 04 Liaison Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load Working Group is developing interconnection frameworks, co-located resource rules (ZGIA), and reserve product enhancements that directly affect how pumped-storage and conventional hydropower can participate in markets and operate at existing or new facility locations; operational requirements for large loads including ride-through and stability control establish precedent for dispatchable resource standards.
stakeholders: Daniel Wilson (MISO); Jasmine Davis (MISO); Ryan Darrah (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO is pursuing a three-phase framework for rapid large-load integration: Connect (120-day GIA completion), Transition (flexible service options including firm step-up), and Ensure Long-Term Reliability (real-time coordination and reserve product enhancements). The ZGIA (zero-injection GIA) tariff language covers market participation, resource adequacy treatment, ride-through, and stability requirements for co-located generation and load; initial proposal was filed with FERC in June 2026, with a non-firm service option and firm service step-up option timeline influenced by FERC show-cause order review (target July 2026 filing). Co-located resource rules and the firm service step-up option create new pathways for PSH and hydro to participate in MISO markets with flexible injection/withdrawal capability, while operational reliability requirements (SCADA telemetry, PMU standards, dynamic reserve preparedness) establish obligations that will apply to dispatchable and storage-like resources. The Steering Committee special meeting on July 7, 2026, will determine the forum for continued ZGIA discussion and review LLWG charter.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 05 Legal Review of Show Cause Order Presentation.pdf
relevance_reason: FERC Show Cause Order directs MISO to redesign interconnection processes and transmission service offerings for large co-located generation and load, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities can interconnect, participate in co-location arrangements, and be studied for network impacts—a strategic option under interconnection and deliverability rules.
ai_summary: FERC issued a Show Cause Order (June 18, 2026, Docket EL26-70-000) directing MISO to revise its tariff to accommodate large loads through tailored interconnection processes for electrically proximate and co-located generation-load pairs, transmission service studies that reflect limited network scope, and transparency mechanisms for upgrade costs. MISO proposes a Zone Generation Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) service type and expedited study process, but must reconcile its approach with FERC's specific requirements around interim/permanent service options, remote disconnection capability, and escalating readiness milestones. MISO's informational report is due July 18, 2026, with abeyance request August 2, 2026, and compliance filing August 17, 2026 (absent abeyance)—this timeline affects whether pumped-storage and other hydro facilities seeking co-location or electrically proximate generation arrangements will face revised study procedures and may open faster interconnection pathways for hydro behind large-load nodes.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 06 Market Participation and Registration of Co Located Load and Generation (PAC 2024 4B)
relevance_reason: MISO's Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) framework directly affects co-located generation and load registration, which creates new revenue and settlement rules for hydropower and storage resources operating at or near large industrial loads, and establishes precedent for how RTOs treat behind-the-meter or tightly co-located hybrid resources.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to require Zero-Injection GIAs (generation interconnection agreements where generation is co-located with large industrial loads) whose associated load exceeds the Large Load threshold to register as market resources. Grandfathered ZGIAs already accepted by FERC are exempt. MISO proposes no time limit on ZGIAs and will clarify that loads are firm NITS loads paying gross transmission charges. Market registration will ensure both load and generation volumes are reported separately for accurate ancillary-service charge allocation, affecting settlement and LMP exposure. This proposal responds to FERC's recent Show Cause Order. Stakeholder feedback is due July 15, 2026, with tariff filing expected July 2026. The mechanism matters most to pumped-storage operators and conventional hydro owners considering co-location with or service to large loads (data centers, manufacturing, etc.); the registration and metering rules will determine whether co-located generation can capture locational price signals or is treated as internal to the load node.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 07 Draft MISO Large Load Interconnection Requirement Technical Compendium
relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and operational requirements directly affect hydro's strategic options for co-location, load-following capability valuation, and transmission/grid-stability service revenue through emerging demand for ramp, ride-through, and dynamic-performance capabilities that hydro uniquely supplies.
ai_summary: MISO has published a Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements technical compendium establishing interconnection standards for rapid-growth transmission-connected loads (data centers, AI computing, etc.), with four requirement categories: data/telemetry/forecasting; ramp management; voltage/frequency ride-through; and stability assessment. The framework defines minimum disturbance-performance expectations and dynamic-response capabilities that large loads must meet. This expands MISO's valuation of ramping, frequency support, and voltage stability — services hydro suppliers have historically dominated — and opens strategic pathways for hydro co-location with large loads and behind-the-meter service delivery. The document establishes technical requirements that may inform future MISO ancillary-services procurement and reserve-adequacy modeling as load volatility and grid-stability demands increase; hydro owners should monitor whether MISO translates these reliability needs into new product definitions or accreditation adjustments that reward fast-response, high-ramp-capability resources.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 07 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG 2026 3)
relevance_reason: Large load interconnection requirements and their operational reliability mandates (remote disconnect, ramp-rate controls, ride-through, telemetry/PMU) directly constrain how co-located loads (data centers, AI/HPC) can interact with the transmission system and may affect hydro's ability to serve or coordinate with these loads, plus set a precedent for how RTOs will require dispatchable resources to respond to fast-ramping demand.
ai_summary: MISO is filing revised Tariff language by end of July 2026 to impose interconnection reliability requirements on large loads (≥50 MW new, ≥25 MW cumulative increase) and computational loads (≥1 MW of data-center-type digital demand), implementing FERC's show-cause order. Requirements include basic facility data, modeling, hourly forecasts, telemetry, and PMU data; computational loads face additional phasor measurement, ride-through (voltage/frequency), and ramp-rate controls; MISO will install and charge customers for remote disconnect equipment and control systems. A transitional provision exempts loads with service commencement before 11/1/2027 from ramp and ride-through requirements for their lifetime unless they upgrade by ≥25 MW after 10/1/2026. This affects hydro owners because (1) it establishes a co-location precedent — large loads are now defined as discrete withdrawal points, creating opportunities for hydro to site behind-the-meter generation and enter direct load-service arrangements; (2) the ramp-rate and ride-through enforcement on computational loads (AI, crypto, HPC) creates a demand-side flexibility requirement that could be partially satisfied by hybrid hydro-storage systems and (3) the remote-disconnect and PMU mandates on loads set operational-control expectations that may extend to dispatchable generation. Stakeholders expressed concerns about applicability, transition, and specific language; feedback has influenced the revised Tariff language.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 08a Large Load Telemetry and PMU Requirements (LLWG 2026 4C)
relevance_reason: MISO's telemetry and PMU requirements for large loads will impose new operational compliance obligations on hydropower and storage facilities co-located with or behind the same point of interconnection as computational loads, and may affect dispatch visibility and real-time market participation for generation resources at large-load sites.
ai_summary: MISO proposes mandatory real-time SCADA telemetry (MW, MVAR, breaker status via ICCP) for all large loads and generation resources ≥20 MVA sharing the same point of interconnection, plus PMU installation (sampling at 60 Hz minimum in IEEE C37.118 format) at computational large loads before service commencement. Generation behind the large load's POI cannot be netted with load and must stream individual telemetry including state-of-charge data for battery or storage devices, and control-mode telemetry for market-participating generators. MISO requests feedback by July 13, 2026, with detailed requirements to be drafted in Business Practice Manuals. Pumped-storage facilities and conventional hydro units co-located at large-load sites (especially data-center campuses) will face new capital costs (PMU installation and ICCP comms) and operational constraints (non-netting, continuous dispatch visibility, reduced operational flexibility).

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 08b Large Load Operational Forecast Requirements (LLWG 2026 4A)
relevance_reason: Large load forecasting requirements directly affect hydro's operational flexibility value and dispatch obligations, and the rule design (monitoring, incentives, settlement potential) may establish precedent for how variable/dispatchable resources' forecast accuracy is treated in MISO's commitment and real-time dispatch processes.
ai_summary: MISO proposes to require large loads (data centers, industrial facilities, campuses) to submit short-term operational forecasts at hourly (next 1–168 hours) and 5-minute (next 6 hours) granularities, feeding into MISO's Multi-Day/Next-Day/Intraday reliability commitment and look-ahead commitment/real-time dispatch processes. Forecasts are submitted at the Commercial Pricing (CP) node level by the Tariff Customer; MISO will monitor timeliness and accuracy against telemetered actual load and is considering incentive mechanisms (ranging from escalation to settlement-based penalties). Status: requirements finalized for LLWG feedback by July 13, 2026; accuracy incentives and BPM implementation to follow. For hydro operators, the mechanism is load-side variability management: large loads add volatile demand that MISO's standard weather/calendar forecasts cannot capture; if these forecasts are inaccurate, MISO's commitment stack and reserve procurement tighten or loosen unpredictably, affecting the scarcity signals and ramping/flexibility value hydro receives in real-time and look-ahead markets. The settlement-based penalty option (SPP precedent cited) could create a parallel market-discipline framework applied to demand-side forecast accuracy, potentially informing future treatment of hydro forecast errors and opportunity-cost offers.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 09 Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG 2026 2)
relevance_reason: MISO's revised methodology for quantifying reserve requirements (Regulation Reserve, Ramp Capability, Short-Term Reserve) directly affects how uncertainty and variability from large loads are valued in reserve-product pricing and procurement — a core revenue stream for hydro and pumped storage, which are traditional suppliers of all three products.
ai_summary: MISO has established a dynamic reserve requirement methodology that quantifies net uncertainty and derives seasonal reserve requirements for Regulation Reserve (4 sec–5 min timeframe), Ramp Capability Product (10 min), and Short-Term Reserve (30 min–3 hr), with requirements varying by season, region, and time of day. Large loads exhibiting non-conforming behavior (volatile industrial or storage-adjacent profiles) were evaluated as a source of net-load and tie-line uncertainty; however, MISO's correlation analysis (sampling February–November 2025) found weak or negligible relationships between non-conforming load profiles and reserve-product uncertainty requirements across all three products, suggesting existing large loads have limited impact on reserve sizing to date. MISO will continue to monitor uncertainty patterns periodically and revise reserve methodologies if non-conforming load impact reaches criticality. Stakeholder feedback (six respondents, May 2026) was mostly supportive, and further engagement will occur at the Large Load Working Group.

title: 20260629 LLWG Item 09 Reserve Product Enhancement (LLWG 2026 2) Feedback Response (20260514)
relevance_reason: MISO is evaluating reserve product enhancements and regulation requirements to manage load variability from large data-center and computational loads, which directly affects how much regulation and ramping reserve MISO procures and prices — the demand curve for these products that conventional hydro and pumped storage depend on for revenue.
ai_summary: MISO is developing reserve product enhancements to accommodate fast ramp rates and variability from large loads (data centers, computational facilities), with focus on regulation (4-second dispatch cycle, 5-minute deployment) and contingency-reserve upward and downward directions. Stakeholder feedback from six participants—mostly supportive of MISO's approach—pushed MISO to evaluate whether existing initiatives (RSC-2024-1/2, MSC-2026-1) already suffice before adding new products, and to allocate reserve costs to the loads driving demand rather than socializing them. MISO commits to reserve-enhancement evaluation updates and cost-allocation recommendations at July or August 2026 and later in 2026 LLWG meetings. The mechanism: larger, more variable loads will increase MISO's procurement of regulation and ramping reserves; if MISO adds new reserve products or expands regulation requirements, traditional hydro and PSH owners will face higher competition for those product revenues and greater uncertainty in which resources are called upon to provide regulation—particularly affecting resources with ramp-rate and regulation-eligibility constraints.

title: 20260716 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260610.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) proposal directly affects how co-located generation and load can participate in markets and be registered—a strategic option for hydro owners seeking to site generation with load, and a novel participation pathway that may extend to small hydro and hybrid resources under Order 2222 principles.
stakeholders: Werner Roth (Large Load Working Group); Marc Keyser (MISO); Chris Plante (MISO Large Load Working Group); Eileen King (MISO Stakeholder Relations)
ai_summary: MISO is developing a Zero-Injection Generator Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) framework to enable market participation and registration of co-located load and generation. The proposal reflects six months of stakeholder input and is nearing a tariff filing at end of June 2026, with draft language and Planning Advisory Committee feedback still being incorporated. The framework adapts existing interconnection processes (surplus, replacement, ERAS) to zero-injection scenarios. For hydro owners, ZGIAs represent a new pathway to co-locate generation with large industrial load—potentially raising MW value by shifting pricing exposure, enabling behind-the-meter hydro dispatch, and avoiding transmission constraints; most relevant to pumped-storage operators and small hydro seeking high-load-density sites. Comment deadlines and detailed tariff language appear in related filings (PAC-2024-2B, LLWG-2026-3, LLWG-2026-2) due May 26–June 4, 2026.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) Working Group sets seasonal resource-adequacy targets and load/capacity modeling that directly affect hydro accreditation, must-offer obligations, and the revenue exposure of all dispatchable resources in the region during scarcity periods.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Eric Thoms (MISO); Forrest Hickey (MISO); Jacob Zhang (MISO); Mike Colenso (MISO); Neil Shah (MISO); Ryan Kelley (MISO); Yong Jiang (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's LOLEWG is reviewing the seasonal LOLE-target methodology and the load and capacity models that underpin Planning Year 2027–2028 resource-adequacy calculations. The agenda includes refreshers on seasonal LOLE targets, LOLE load-modeling results, capacity and outage-year assumptions, and wind-profile enhancements. Substantively: LOLE targets and load forecasts drive which resources are needed for RA compliance and how scarcity is priced; hydro's accreditation (seasonal and annual ELCC/UCAP) and the timing of peak-scarcity events are set within these models. Wind-profile enhancements may shift the seasonal shape of reserve requirements, changing when hydro's flexibility commands the highest value. Next meeting is August 11, 2026; the working group is requesting feedback on several items (load modeling, capacity assumptions, wind profiles), though no formal comment deadline is stated in this agenda.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 01c Minutes 20260202.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) study and Reliability Based Demand Curves (RBDCs) directly inform capacity market accreditation and resource-adequacy requirements that affect how much capacity hydropower and pumped storage can sell into MISO's Planning Resource Auction.
stakeholders: Trey Mayeux (Cleco); Eric Thoms (Invenergy); Akshay Korad (MISO); Amanda Jones (MISO); Neil Shah (MISO); Ryan Kelley (MISO); Yong Jiang (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO presented PY 2026-2027 Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) curves and preliminary Reliability Based Demand Curves (RBDCs) showing systemwide summer LOLE risk as highest, but subregional variation (North/Central region peaks in summer; South region peaks in winter, a seasonal shift from prior year). MISO confirmed battery-storage modeling will use 'even loss methodology' and is implementing an enhanced wind-profile model for the PY 2027-2028 LOLE study (near-term), with long-term coordination toward consistent renewable profiles across all planning studies. The wind-profile refinement showed preliminary no material change to LOLE outcomes. These LOLE and RBDC outputs flow directly into MISO's capacity accreditation framework and Planning Resource Auction parameters; seasonal subregional shifts in reliability risk can reset the demand curves that cap how much capacity owners—including hydro and pumped storage—can bid and clear at given prices. Next LOLEWG meeting is June 23, 2026.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) analysis directly determines Planning Reserve Margin (PRM) requirements, zonal Local Reliability Requirements (LRR), and Capacity Import/Export Limits (CIL/CEL) — all foundational inputs to MISO's capacity accreditation, seasonal adequacy assessment, and locational pricing signals that drive hydro revenue and participation incentives.
stakeholders: Amanda Jones (MISO); Eric Thoms (MISO); Neil Shah (MISO); Trey Mayeux (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) manages the LOLE analysis that calculates Planning Reserve Margin requirements, zonal Local Reliability Requirements, and Capacity Import/Export Limits per Module E-1 of the tariff, conforming to ERO and Regional Entity standards. The group held five meetings in 2025 (average 154 participants) and has scheduled work in 2026–2027 including annual wind/solar capacity-credit reviews, LOLE load modeling for PY2027–2028, planned-maintenance modeling, seasonal LOLE targets, and updates to CIL/CEL calculations. These LOLE-derived parameters feed into MISO's capacity accreditation framework and seasonal adequacy mechanisms; changes to load modeling assumptions, wind/solar profiles, or maintenance treatment directly affect the reserve margins and locational signals that determine how much capacity MISO needs from hydro assets and at what premium scarcity pricing may emerge, influencing both RA accreditation levels and energy-market scarcity incentives for dispatchable hydro.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 03 Refresher on Seasonal LOLE Targets.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's seasonal LOLE framework affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources are accredited in capacity adequacy studies and shapes seasonal RA requirements that determine hydro's reliability value and revenue exposure across quarters.
ai_summary: MISO adopted a seasonal LOLE (Loss of Load Expectation) construct in 2023–2024, replacing a single annual 0.1 days/year target with per-season minima of 0.01 days/year. The framework accounts for seasonal variation in peak load (winter mornings, overnight cold events), forced-outage rates (thermal units fail more in extreme cold/heat), planned maintenance clustering (spring/fall), and ELCC values for wind and solar, which differ materially by season. MISO runs an annual LOLE study first, then upwardly adjusts any season falling below 0.01 days/year by adding perfect or proxy units. This document presents the construct as positioned between PJM's simple annual PRMR% approach and ISO-NE's proposed monthly granularity, and includes historical LOLE splits (PY25–26 all summer risk; PY26–27 split summer/winter/spring; PY29–30 nearly even summer/winter) showing how seasonal requirements dampen year-to-year volatility. Hydro operators are most affected because seasonal accreditation directly determines their RA contribution value in each quarter—winter peaking and spring outage windows matter independently rather than being smoothed into a summer-anchored requirement—and because the tariff filing referenced as of June 1, 2026 (adding a perfect positive unit to the LOLE algorithm) may change the study's outcome.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 04 LOLE Load Modeling Process and Results.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) study drives resource-adequacy requirements and seasonal capacity accreditation for all dispatchable units including hydro; unprecedented load growth and forecast-uncertainty revisions directly affect the capacity value and RA showing requirements hydro operators must meet, and seasonal peak-demand modeling shapes winter/spring reliability signals that influence hydro dispatch and scarcity pricing.
ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group is revising its load modeling process and load-forecast uncertainty assumptions for Planning Year 2027–2028, noting that actual loads have deviated from LOLE projections by 4% in shoulder seasons—exceeding current uncertainty bands. MISO is automating the load-development process (replacing NeuroShell with Histogram Gradient Boosting), standardizing extreme-temperature thresholds, and proposing to expand load-forecast error bands (e.g., from ±2% to a range including +3% to +4%) to reflect data-center and electrification-driven load growth. The key mechanism is that higher projected load peaks and wider uncertainty distributions increase the probability of scarcity conditions in LOLE runs, raising capacity-accreditation requirements for all dispatchable resources and making seasonal hydro availability more valuable in winter and shoulder months. MISO is requesting stakeholder feedback on model inputs and uncertainty-assumption options through July 7, 2026, via the Feedback Tool at misoenergy.org.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 05 CIL CEL Model and Input File Overview.pdf
relevance_reason: CIL/CEL study directly affects hydro's capacity accreditation and zone-to-zone transfer limits under MISO's Planning Resource Auction, which determines how much capacity hydro can export across Local Resource Zones and thus what cleared capacity and locational revenue it receives.
ai_summary: MISO is conducting Capacity Import Limit (CIL) and Capacity Export Limit (CEL) studies for Planning Year 2027-2028 across four seasons (Summer 2027, Fall 2027, Winter 2027-2028, Spring 2028). CIL/CEL values constrain capacity transfers between MISO's Local Resource Zones in the Planning Resource Auction and are calculated using transfer-capability analyses that redispatch conventional generators and wind/solar (wind and solar are curtailed, not ramped up). The study uses PSSE power-flow models and contingency files now posted on ShareFile for stakeholder review. MISO requests stakeholder feedback on input files by July 7, 2026, with preliminary results expected in September 2026 and final values in October 2026. For hydro operators, tighter CIL/CEL values reduce their zone's ability to export capacity—lowering locational RA value and export revenue—while looser limits enhance those streams; the redispatch methodology's treatment of hydro as curtailable (not dispatchable) may understate the transfer capability available when hydro can flex output. Most exposed are multi-zone hydro fleets and pumped storage units in export-constrained Local Resource Zones.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 06 Capacity and Outyear Model Assumptions.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) model determines resource adequacy requirements and capacity accreditation thresholds that directly affect hydro and storage revenue through capacity-market clearing prices and RA obligation sizing.
ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group has updated capacity assumptions for the PY 2027-2028 LOLE study, making two key changes: (1) resource retirements from OMS-MISO Survey data will no longer be modeled in outyear LOLE projections—only retirements with approved Attachment Y filings will be reflected—and (2) ERAS resources will now be included in the model based on their Application In-Service date rather than earlier assumptions. These changes affect which resources are counted toward resource adequacy in MISO's planning year, directly influencing the capacity margin and thus the magnitude of capacity-market clearing prices and RA accreditation values for all dispatchable resources including hydro. The shift away from retirement-survey data means retiring units will be assumed available longer in the outyear, potentially depressing LOLE and lowering RA scarcity signals, while ERAS (Energy Resource Aggregation System) inclusion may improve visibility of emerging resources in adequacy. No comment deadline or vote date is specified in this working-group presentation.

committee: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group
meeting_title: Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) - June 23, 2026
title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 07 Wind Profile Enhancement Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's enhanced wind-profile methodology changes how wind and storage resources are accredited in ELCC and DLOL frameworks, directly affecting capacity-value revenue for dispatchable resources including pumped storage competing in MISO's resource-adequacy market.
ai_summary: MISO is adopting enhanced meteorology-driven wind profiles for its PY2027–2028 Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) studies, replacing status-quo profiles with 30-year synthetic data that better capture low-wind conditions and seasonal variability. The new profiles increase wind ELCC values (especially in Spring, rising from 19.3% to 43.8%), but also shift the distribution of critical-event durations by season, causing storage DLOL accreditation to rise significantly in Fall (+12.7 percentage points) while falling in Winter and Spring (−11.2% and −20.0% respectively). Thermal resources' accreditation is affected primarily by shifts in which maintenance-outage hours overlap with newly identified critical hours. MISO plans to move forward with the enhanced profiles subject to final review and will maintain a hybrid approach (synthetic pre-2012, observed post-2012). This directly reshapes the seasonal accreditation profile for pumped-storage hydro competing in MISO's capacity market, particularly for four-hour-plus storage duration valuations.

title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Agenda.pdf
relevance_reason: Freeze Date revision and interchange ramp rules affect interregional transmission of hydro and PSH power between MISO and PJM; interregional planning updates touch transmission deliverability of hydro resources across the seam.
ai_summary: MISO and PJM are revising the 2004 Freeze Date used to determine firm transmission rights on flowgates between the RTOs—a foundational rule for long-distance hydro and storage exports across the MISO-PJM seam. MISO is also updating interchange ramp rules and retiring Coordinated Transaction Scheduling (CTS) processes. Parallel interregional planning updates via the PJM-MISO Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee (IPSAC) will address long-term transmission capability. These changes alter the terms under which hydro operators schedule and export power across the seam and may improve transmission planning visibility for future hydro deliverability; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda, but the freeze-date and ramp-rules discussions are active. Most affected are reservoir and pumped-storage operators selling capacity into PJM or using the MISO-PJM interface for arbitrage.

title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 02 Retirement of CTS Product.pdf
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Retirement of CTS affects interregional energy scheduling and price convergence between MISO and PJM, which directly impacts dispatch economics and revenue for hydro resources located on or serving the MISO-PJM seam; loss of an interchange optimization tool reduces locational price signals and flexibility value for assets arbitraging between the RTOs.
ai_summary: MISO and PJM propose to retire the Coordinated Transaction Scheduling (CTS) product—a coordinated interchange optimization tool approved by FERC in 2016 and launched in 2017—due to minimal usage, underperformance ($740k in annual savings vs. $17–72M projected), and high migration costs ($1.4M to migrate to the new Market Clearing Engine, $0.8M to maintain over 5 years). Stakeholders submitted mixed feedback: some requested a modern replacement (e.g., SPP's Inter-Market Optimization) before retirement to avoid operational gaps, while others supported retirement given CTS inefficiencies. MISO responds that other interchange products (Fixed, Cleared Day-Ahead, and Dynamic schedules) already offer more granular, real-time scheduling (adjustable to 20 minutes before interval vs. 75 minutes for CTS), and MISO is open to pursuing replacement products with PJM and SPP after retirement. MISO and PJM intend to coordinate tariff and Joint Operating Agreement filings later in 2026 to remove CTS. Hydro owners on or serving the MISO-PJM interface lose a tool for optimizing interchange pricing and may see reduced locational value signals for flexibility; replacements under discussion are not yet defined.

title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 03 MISO Interchange Ramp Rules Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO is removing interchange ramp restrictions for day-ahead offers, which directly affects how hydropower and other dispatchable resources can schedule power flows across MISO-PJM seams and influences flexibility/ramping product value.
ai_summary: MISO is eliminating restrictions on interchange ramp calculations for day-ahead market submissions, effective Q3 2026. Previously, MISO enforced limits of ±1000 MW on increased imports/decreased exports and +500 MW on increased exports/decreased imports, enforced via the Ramp Reservation System (RRS) for quarterly schedule changes. Removing these caps increases the scheduling flexibility available to hydro and other resources moving power across the MISO-PJM interface, allowing larger and more frequent interchange adjustments without pre-reservation denials. This particularly benefits reservoir-based hydropower and pumped storage that rely on schedule flexibility to optimize operations across interregional pricing signals; the change reduces a non-price scheduling constraint that previously limited their ability to respond to real-time conditions. Implementation is targeted for Q3 2026.

title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 04 Interregional Planning Update.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO-PJM interregional transmission planning directly affects hydro deliverability, interregional energy flows, and the scarcity signals that drive hydro revenue; the 2026 Targeted Market Efficiency Project (TMEP) study will identify congestion-mitigation upgrades that reshape the value of hydropower export corridors between the two RTOs.
ai_summary: MISO and PJM are conducting a 2026 Coordinated System Plan (TMEP) to study interregional congestion on the MISO-PJM seam, targeting the remaining $681 million of persistent congestion after eliminating $441 million deemed non-persistent or addressable by planned upgrades. Twenty-three TMEP study candidates are under evaluation to identify limiting equipment and potential upgrades; the JRPC will make a recommendation to the Boards by November 2026. For hydro owners with generation located near the MISO-PJM seam or exporting capacity across it, transmission upgrades selected for mitigation will alter locational scarcity pricing and transfer capability — potentially increasing or decreasing the value of that hydro's energy sales into either region depending on which flowgates are relieved.

title: 20260617 AC Item 01b Agenda
relevance_reason: Board session on 'Maintaining Resource Adequacy in a Time of Transition' directly addresses MISO's capacity/RA mechanisms and strategic resource-adequacy constructs that affect hydropower accreditation, accreditation curves, and fleet retirement decisions in the MISO footprint.
stakeholders: Carmen Clark (MISO); Chris Nelson (MISO); Dan Scripps (MISO); Eileen King (MISO); Ted Vatnsdal (MISO); Todd Hillman (MISO); Mitch Myhre (MISO (Finance Subcommittee)); Chris Plante (MISO (Large Load Working Group)); Brian Drumm (MISO (Transmission Owners / Steering Committee)); Tricia DeBleeckere (Organization of MISO States)
ai_summary: MISO's Advisory Committee meeting on June 17, 2026 includes a full board session titled 'Maintaining Resource Adequacy in a Time of Transition,' with discussion between Board and AC members on resource adequacy in the context of ongoing market/grid evolution. The session touches MISO's capacity accreditation frameworks and RA methodology during a period of fleet transition (retirements, storage entry, renewables growth). No specific proposals, votes, or comment deadlines are announced in this agenda document; the session itself is the information node. Hydro owners in MISO should monitor outputs from this session for changes to ELCC methodology, seasonal accreditation, or capacity-auction parameters that would affect how conventional hydro and PSH count toward RA showings.

title: 20260617 AC Item 01d Legal Update
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Document previews near-term tariff filings on co-located resource interconnection limits, capacity accreditation deliverability (DLOL methodology for SAC calculation), large-load reliability requirements, and zone-based generation interconnection (ZGIA) — four filings that will directly affect how hydro (especially pumped storage) competes for capacity accreditation, how co-location affects revenue, and how new load interconnection rules alter dispatch and deliverability constraints.
ai_summary: MISO's Legal Update lists 13 tariff filings anticipated through late July 2026, including four with material relevance to hydro and storage operators: (1) delay of the Interconnection Service Limit Constraint for Co-Located Resources effective date (06/12/2026, docket ER26-1915-000); (2) tariff revisions to establish DLOL-based accreditation methodology for capacity resources beginning Planning Year 2028–2029, with new provisions for how deliverability is accounted for in SAC values for both traditional and co-located resources (06/19/2026); (3) Large Load reliability and informational requirements (06/30/2026); and (4) a non-queue Zone-based Generation Interconnection Approach (ZGIA) for generation co-located at the same substation as load (06/30/2026). The accreditation deliverability filing (item 3) is the most directly material to PSH and reservoir hydro, as it resets how MISO calculates seasonal accreditation value when transmission-constraint-based deliverability limits are incorporated into the accreditation formula — a shift that could reduce accreditation for resources in tight delivery zones. Co-location rules (items 1 and 4) affect PSH and small hydro co-located with load or industrial facilities. All filings are estimated dates unless tied to a FERC deadline; no comment deadlines are stated in the outlook.

title: 20260617 AC Item 05a Current Issue Discussion - Preventing Fraud and Manipulation Questions
relevance_reason: Demand-side resource (DSR) accreditation changes and fraud/manipulation in DSR registrations directly affect how MISO's capacity market and resource-adequacy constructs treat demand-side flexibility—a competing product category that hydro owners bid against for RA value and ancillary-service revenue, and fraud in DSR valuation distorts the pricing signals hydro faces.
ai_summary: MISO's Advisory Committee is discussing fraud and manipulation risks in demand-side resource (DSR) registrations and accreditation, prompted by FERC enforcement actions (Ketchup Caddy LLC, $25M penalty, December 2024; American Efficient LLC, $722M penalty, April 2026). The committee is exploring upcoming MISO accreditation changes and how fraud vulnerabilities in DSR programs intersect with the recent proliferation of large-load interconnection requests. For hydro owners, the relevance is indirect but material: if MISO tightens DSR accreditation rules or validation standards, the capacity value and ancillary-service revenue opportunities for demand-side flexibility may shrink, reducing competition for RA accreditation and improving pricing signals for dispatchable hydro. Conversely, if fraudulent DSRs have inflated RA accreditation or depressed prices, correcting those practices could revalue hydro's true contribution. No specific accreditation proposals or vote dates are stated in this discussion document.

title: 20260617 AC Item 06 MISO Strategy Update
relevance_reason: MISO's strategy update addresses load growth integration, resource adequacy mechanisms, operational risks tied to fleet evolution and large-load interconnection, and storage/hybrid resource participation — all of which touch hydro revenue (seasonal accreditation, RA pricing), strategic options (large-load co-location, transmission planning), and obligations (operational risk categories, interconnection queue management).
ai_summary: MISO reports record 14.7 GW nameplate capacity additions in 2026, with solar and storage dominating the resource mix, while facing accelerating large-load additions (24.9 GW proposed, 8.9 GW approved in Expedited Project Reviews) and evolving operational risks — particularly net-load variability, fewer synchronous resources, and load-pocket adequacy. The Reliability Imperative initiative includes market redesign targeting accreditation/peak-risk alignment, optimized storage participation, dynamic reserves, and congestion-pricing reform, alongside transmission evolution and large-load integration speed-to-power. For hydro owners, the strategic significance lies in (1) seasonal accreditation and capacity-market design — MISO notes summer/winter capacity accreditation at 9.4%–21.2% planning reserve margins, directly affecting what flexibility and seasonal-storage products hydro can sell into RA frameworks; (2) load-pocket adequacy analysis and transmission planning to support large loads, which opens opportunities for run-of-river and reservoir hydro to serve co-located or regionally constrained load; and (3) storage-participation optimization, which will likely reshape how pumped storage competes with batteries in accreditation and ramping products. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated; this is a strategic briefing to the Advisory Committee with implementation timelines for ERAS and market reforms to follow.

title: 20260617 AC Item 07a Session with the BOD Questions - Maintaining Resource Adequacy in a Time of Transition
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Resource adequacy accreditation methodology, auction design, and seasonal capacity markets directly affect how MISO values hydro and storage capacity, and MISO's announced changes to accreditation rules, dynamic loss-of-load methodology, and demand curves reshape the revenue recognition and adequacy contribution of all dispatchable and storage resources in the footprint.
ai_summary: MISO Advisory Committee and Board session reviewing resource adequacy maintenance strategy in light of accelerated load-growth projections since June 2023. MISO has implemented seasonal capacity auctions, reliability-based demand curves, resource accreditation changes, dynamic loss-of-load methodology, and expedited interconnection processes (ERAS). The session will assess whether these changes are sufficient and what additional measures are needed. Directly relevant because accreditation methodology, demand-curve design, and seasonal auction structure set the capacity value hydro and pumped storage receive in MISO's planning reserve auctions; the shift to seasonal accreditation and dynamic loss-of-load modeling will alter how hydropower's seasonal contribution is recognized, particularly in high-stress periods where hydro's flexibility has highest value.

title: 20260617 AC Item 08 Main Parent Entity Status Updates
relevance_reason: Multiple MISO subcommittees report on accreditation rules, resource adequacy, reserve deliverability, interconnection procedures for co-located load and generation, and transmission planning that affect hydro and pumped-storage participation, capacity value, and strategic deployment options.
stakeholders: Bryan Garnett (MISO); Cynthia Crane (MISO); Ray McCausland (MISO); Sarah Martz (MISO); Werner Roth (MISO)
ai_summary: MISO's Resource Adequacy Subcommittee is addressing two accreditation issues that affect all dispatchable and storage resources: (1) Solar accreditation hour-window adjustment for Planning Year 2027/2028, realigning risk hours with observed real-time performance as a glide path to Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) methodology in 2028/2029—a precedent for how MISO recalibrates accreditation as resource mix shifts; (2) Lead-time rules for offline resources, where MISO is tightening the 24-hour lead-time accreditation threshold because some resources have changed lead times approaching or during emergencies, potentially masking unavailability. MISO intends a FERC filing in Q3 or Q4 2026 on the lead-time issue. The Market Subcommittee is also reviewing reserve deliverability in day-ahead and real-time markets (MSC-2026-2), noting that changing resource mix has affected regulation performance and created clearance disparities when the reserve-delivery test (RDT) binds—feedback was due June 5. On the interconnection front, the Planning Advisory Committee is advancing a Zero-Injection Generation Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) proposal for co-located load and generation, with modifications to require substation-level connection and automatic disconnection of injections when load trips; FERC filing is planned before end of June. These mechanisms directly affect accreditation certainty for hydro and pumped-storage capacity showings, regulation/AS revenue exposure if reserve-delivery rules tighten, and strategic options for co-locating PSH or run-of-river hydro with large loads or behind-the-meter applications.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 05 Five Year Plan.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's five-year plan addresses storage participation optimization, accreditation reform, ramping and flexibility products, demand response accreditation, large-load interconnection effects on grid stability, and resource adequacy—all direct levers on hydro and PSH revenue and operational strategy in MISO.
ai_summary: MISO's 2026–2029+ five-year strategic plan prioritizes reliability imperatives driven by solar growth, load growth (especially large commercial/industrial loads), and net-load-shape steepening. Key market-design initiatives affecting storage and dispatchable resources include: (1) storage optimization in markets and multi-interval storage optimization (2027–2029), improving accreditation alignment with peak-risk periods and refining accreditation to include external resources (2026–2029+), and dynamic reserves; (2) ramping-product pricing adjustments, transmission constraint demand curves, linear ramping for fast-ramp resources, and enhanced regional ramp deliverability to mitigate 4–6% ramp shortages during morning/evening peaks; (3) demand-response reforms (2026–2028) with accreditation and participation standardization; and (4) FERC Order 2222 foundational work for DER aggregation (2026–2029+). Operationally, MISO plans centralized control-room modernization (2026–2027) and Ambient Adjusted Ratings (AAR) implementation under FERC Order 881 (2027–2029+). Resource-adequacy ecosystem modernization includes LOLE modeling refinements, large-load adequacy impact assessments, and co-optimized seasonal capacity auctions. Pumped-storage and reservoir hydro owners—especially those holding RA positions or supplying flexibility into ramp markets—should monitor accreditation-reform specifics (Q2–Q3 2026) and storage-optimization market-design details (2027 design phase) for impacts on capacity value and dispatch incentives. Large-load interconnection studies (2026–2027) and ambient-adjusted-ratings deployment affect system stability margins and may require hydro dispatch changes or create scarcity-driven revenue opportunities.

meeting_title: Markets Committee of the Board of Directors - June 16, 2026
title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 06 MISO Operations Report.pdf
relevance_reason: MISO's operations report flags net-load ramp challenges from 20+ GW of solar growth, summer resource adequacy outcomes, and emergency procedure changes that affect hydro dispatch obligations and revenue opportunities from ramping/regulation services.
ai_summary: MISO's spring 2026 operations report shows mild conditions but identifies summer challenges: over 20 GW of solar creating net-load ramp risk, above-average temperature forecasts, and drought conditions in parts of the footprint. The region cleared sufficient capacity (142 GW procured against 132 GW reserve requirement) in the 2026/27 Planning Resource Auction, but real-time metrics show congestion forecasting gaps and RSG (uplift) volatility—in March, $1.7M RSG was paid across three trade dates with 66% concentrated in a single high-priced resource in the South. MISO rolled out revised emergency procedures (June 1, 2026) with simplified capacity-emergency steps and a new Transmission Warning alert to provide earlier non-emergency signaling before load shed becomes necessary. For hydro operators, the ramp-challenge callout signals growing dispatch value from flexible resources; the highlighted congestion forecasting misalignments and congestion-cost shortfalls (ECF ~$10.6M shortfall in March) suggest potential revenue volatility in real-time energy and congestion markets, particularly for resources in the South load pocket.

ai_summary: MISO's Independent Market Monitor reports Spring 2026 performance: real-time energy prices fell 7% despite higher load, but price volatility surged with 20 shortage intervals (vs. 11 last year), driven by evening net-load ramps averaging 15 GW as solar penetration increases and system experiences 187 negative-price intervals in daytime hours. The Monitor identifies a critical ESR state-of-charge (SOC) software flaw that removed 7% of ESR capability from dispatch when outside SOC limits, scheduling discharge during negative prices and charge during $200+/MWh scarcity — a July software fix is underway. Existing solar is over-accredited at 68% summer output (designated 2–5 pm hours) versus actual 4–7 pm peak-stress window, inflating solar's capacity value by ~2.6 GW and depressing summer PRA clearing prices by ~$240/MW-day; the Monitor recommends aligning accreditation to 4–7 pm hours for next year's auction. These findings directly affect hydro's relative market position: expanded solar and ESR penetration intensify evening ramp demand (where conventional hydro excels), yet dispatch rules and accreditation design are not yet optimized for the new resource mix. Pumped storage, if subject to similar SOC constraints, faces the same inefficient dispatch penalty; conventional hydro benefits from scarcity but faces sustained low daytime prices from solar excess.

relevance_reason: MISO's five-year plan directly addresses hydro and storage revenue, obligations, and strategic options through storage resource participation optimization, resource accreditation reforms, large-load interconnection enabling, dynamic reserves design, and capacity-adequacy modeling that will reshape how existing hydro and pumped-storage plants are dispatched and valued.
ai_summary: MISO's System Planning Committee approved a five-year strategic plan (2025–2029+) with major workstreams in market redefinition, transmission evolution, and operations modernization. In Market Redefinition, MISO will redesign storage participation models (including co-located storage optimization), reform resource accreditation to improve peak-risk-period alignment and reflect evolving resource risks, develop dynamic ramp and regulation reserves, and modernize resource adequacy modeling and loss-of-load expectation (LOLE) tooling to account for climate-change impacts and seasonal interdependencies. In Transmission Evolution, MISO will accelerate large-load interconnections (with "speed-to-partial-power" processes), implement FERC Order 2023 generation interconnection reforms, and conduct interregional transmission planning with PJM and SPP. These changes mean storage operators (especially pumped-storage owners) will face new accreditation rules that tie capacity credit to peak-risk periods rather than nameplate, new co-location optimization rules that can improve or constrain dispatch economics, and expanded opportunities to serve fast-growing data-center and large-load demand. Reservoir and run-of-river operators will be affected by changes to dynamic reserves procurement, ramp-product design, and seasonal resource adequacy modeling. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated in this overview document; the plan is framed as approved board guidance for 24–36 month execution cycles beginning 2025.

ai_summary: MISO is executing a three-layered planning framework (local, regional, interregional) to accommodate 26.4 GW of large-load additions projected in MTEP26, with load-growth forecasts ranging 1.1–3.0% CAGR through 2046 (data-center CAGR at 103% through 2030). The South and Midwest Long Range Transmission Planning studies are launching this quarter using updated Futures scenarios to guide solutions for reliability and load-serving needs; outcomes will determine transmission and/or generation solutions in subsequent MTEP cycles. Parallel interregional coordination is underway with PJM (2026 Targeted Market Efficiency Project studying PJM-MISO congestion), SPP (2024–25 CSP yielded two portfolio options improving reliability and transfer capability; 2026–27 CSP announced), and broader alignment with FERC Order 1920. This acceleration of transmission and interregional planning directly raises the strategic value of existing hydro capacity and expands opportunities for hydro to serve as a flexibility and transfer solution, particularly for units near MISO seams or capable of serving co-located load; the 28 GW of joint capacity enabled by JTIQ transmission projects (19 GW approved in MISO-SPP) signals growing reliance on coordinated transmission to unlock interconnection participation.

ai_summary: MISO is deploying SUGAR (Suite of Unified Grid Analyses with Renewables) to automate interconnection study workflows and compress Phase 1 processing from 93 business days (2022 cycle) to 47 days (2023 cycle) to 54 days (2025 cycle), with plans to extend automation to Phases 2 and 3. The system uses agentic AI to automate power flow analysis and network upgrade costing, achieving >99% accuracy match with legacy tools. Faster queue processing reduces time-to-decision for both generator and storage interconnection requests—a material advantage for PSH and conventional hydro seeking expedited network studies and cost determination. MISO's next stage expands automation to stability and short-circuit analysis (Phases 2–3), which historically consumed months; no comment deadline is stated, but this is a board-approved initiative moving into deployment (Q2 2026 status shown).

ai_summary: MISO reports record 2026 capacity additions (14.7 GW nameplate, 10.7 GW accredited) offsetting 2.6 GW of retirements, with 86 GW of approved projects still awaiting commercial operation and 43 GW experiencing delays. Storage projects represent 18–40% of active queue and ERAS applications across cycles 1–4 (as of 6/1/26), advancing through the Expedited Resource Addition Study process designed to accelerate generation additions amid load surge. However, 30 GW (218 projects) have exceeded the standard GIA grace period, with regulatory, contractor, and PPA issues cited as primary delay drivers; projects are securing federal waivers extending timelines 2–7 years beyond COD. For hydropower and PSH owners, the bottleneck between approved capacity and online generation prolongs scarcity conditions that support energy prices and ramping-value realization, while storage's prominent role in ERAS (12–17% of cycles studied) demonstrates MISO's reliance on storage flexibility—though the report does not quantify storage-specific accreditation treatment or ancillary-services pathways. Next steps include MISO exploring modifications to ERAS study cycles (expected Q2 2026) to maximize utilization and GIA negotiations expected over the next two years (920 projected total across Q2 2026–Q2 2027).
