ai_summary: NYISO's Budget & Priorities Working Group is conducting a 2027 market-project prioritization process covering 61 proposed Market projects (16 Capacity Market, 33 Energy Markets, and others). Stakeholder scoring survey opens after June 30 with deadline July 15, 2027; results and high-priority-project constraints reviewed July 29. Among the projects most relevant to hydro/PSH are: Capacity Zone Redesign (locational capacity pricing); Balancing Intermittency Phase 2 (duration in reserves); Dynamic Reserves (operating reserve supplier cost recovery); Flexible Load Models (large loads as new resource type); ICAP Demand Curve Reset methodology; and Aligning Demand Response Baselining with LSE ICAP Obligations. Large-Load Interconnection has been added as a mandatory 2027 project (#60, details pending FERC order expected June 18), directly touching hydro's strategic option to co-locate load and serve it behind the meter. The full project cost and market-design details are posted with BPWG materials; owners should participate in the stakeholder survey by July 15 if seeking to influence 2027 market-design priorities.

title: 3 CEO/COO Report
relevance_reason: Monthly energy market performance metrics show LBMP pricing trends, ancillary-services costs (regulation, reserves, voltage support, black start), and uplift/make-whole payments that directly affect hydro revenue streams; document establishes baseline for tracking real-time price formation and cost recovery mechanisms.
ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 CEO/COO report presents monthly market performance metrics: LBMP averaged $43.63/MWh (up 21% from May 2025); year-to-date average cost is $102.99/MWh, a 27.7% increase over 2025 YTD. Ancillary-services costs include regulation ($0.27/MWh), reserves ($0.96/MWh), voltage support and black start ($0.14/MWh), and negative uplift of ($0.33)/MWh offset by local reliability share of $0.28/MWh. Document also tracks RGGI carbon-allowance auction price ($35.00/ton, up 40% from prior quarter) and natural-gas price trends (Transco Z6 down 13% YoY to $2.03/MMBtu). For hydro operators, rising LBMP and reserve-service costs reflect tightening conditions; the statewide uplift swing from negative to near-zero and the 27.7% YoY cost increase signal potential scarcity pricing opportunities, though negative uplift continues to depress make-whole revenues. This is a management-committee information report with no stated votes or comment deadlines.

title: 3a 2027 Project Prioritization Survey Draft
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Five-minute interchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec directly affects hydro revenue by improving price convergence and real-time flexibility value; demand-response baseline alignment and ICAP surplus rules alter capacity-market participation rules affecting all thermal and storage competitors; DER aggregation integration enables small-hydro participation pathways under Order 2222.
stakeholders: East Coast Power (East Coast Power); JERA Americas (JERA Americas); National Grid (National Grid); NYS UIU (New York State Utility Issues Unit)
ai_summary: NYISO's 2027 project prioritization includes four proposals with direct hydro-market implications: (1) Five-minute interchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec to replace current 15-minute/hourly settlement, improving price convergence between RTC and RTD and increasing flexibility value during renewable ramp events; (2) Demand-response baseline alignment with LSE ICAP obligations to prevent demand-side resources from becoming net ICAP sellers, affecting the competitive position of hydro in capacity markets; (3) Automatic ICAP surplus resale to prevent uplift in ICAP spot prices from unsold capacity, lowering the cost to all capacity suppliers; (4) DER aggregation system integration via API to streamline Order 2222 participation, creating a pathway for small hydro and run-of-river resources to enter wholesale markets through aggregator enrollment. All four are listed as 2027 projects with deliverables ranging from market-design completion to deployment; no comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in this survey draft.

title: 3a Operations Report
relevance_reason: Operations performance metrics reveal hydro and PSH operational patterns, curtailment trends, and dispatch interactions that affect revenue realization and strategic flexibility planning for NHA members.
ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 operations report shows hydro (6,324 MW nameplate including PSH) provided 22% of NYCA's emissions-free generation mix. Wind and solar curtailment reached 2.7% of NYCA intervals in May (13,775 MWh wind, 6,208 MWh FTM solar), driven by transmission-constraint binding at specific interfaces (Meyer 230kV, Montfleur-Codington 115kV, and others). Hydro capacity unavailable in real-time (DAM to RTM) ranged 100–600 MW monthly average, a material baseline for understanding opportunity-cost and dispatch-constraint interactions. The report does not propose market-design changes but serves as reference material for cross-market analysis of how hydro's operational dispatch interacts with wind/solar ramping, transmission scarcity pricing, and reserve-activation patterns.

title: 3c 2027 Market Project Descriptions
relevance_reason: NYISO's 2027 market project portfolio includes multiple projects directly affecting hydro/PSH revenue (capacity accreditation, ancillary services pricing, storage-as-transmission, operating reserves), compliance obligations (FERC Order 2222, transmission planning), and strategic options (demand response baseline alignment, reliability planning with large load integration, seasonal ICAP markets).
stakeholders: CPV (CPV); DPS (DPS); H.Q. ENERGY SERVICES (U.S.) INC. (H.Q. ENERGY SERVICES (U.S.) INC.); ISO-NE (ISO-NE); JERA AMERICAS (JERA AMERICAS); LIPA (LIPA); NATIONAL GRID (NATIONAL GRID); NYS UIU (NYS UIU)
ai_summary: NYISO released its 2027 market project candidate list for Budget & Priorities review, organizing 62 projects across four priority tiers: Prioritize (38 projects), Mandatory (5), Continuing (1), and Future (18). Key projects touching hydro/PSH economics include: 'Balancing Intermittency Phase 2: Evaluating Needs for Duration in Reserves' (ramping/flexibility accreditation); 'Improve Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling'; 'Capacity Zone Redesign'; 'Enhancing Locational Pricing of Capacity'; 'Storage as Transmission'; 'Seasonal ICAP Market Improvements'; 'Dynamic Reserves' phases (operating reserve cost recovery and dispatch); and 'Reliability Attribute-Based Capacity Pricing for Transmission Security'. Mandatory projects include FERC Order 2222 compliance (DER aggregation pathways), FERC Transmission Planning Order 1920 implementation, and 'Hybrid Aggregation Model'. The projects span capacity accreditation and resource adequacy, ancillary services and operating reserves cost recovery, storage participation and transmission service pathways, and price-formation enhancements. No comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in the excerpt.

title: 4 Rate Schedule 1
relevance_reason: Rate Schedule 1 allocates NYISO's annual budget between withdrawal and injection billing units; the allocation directly affects the cost recovery faced by pumped-storage hydropower (which both withdraws for charging and injects for discharge) and by conventional hydro operators, and hydro stakeholders voting in the Management Committee have ongoing interest in whether and how often this cost-allocation study is revisited.
ai_summary: NYISO Rate Schedule 1 governs the allocation of the ISO's annual budget between withdrawal and injection billing units, currently set at 72% withdrawals / 28% injections since January 2012. The tariff requires a Management Committee vote in Q3 each year to decide whether to commission a cost-of-service study to evaluate whether the allocation should change; from 2015 through 2025, successive votes declined to fund a study (most recent study was Black and Veatch in 2011, costing ~$215K over 6 months). The next vote is scheduled for July 29, 2026. Pumped-storage owners are directly affected because the withdrawal/injection split determines their share of NYISO operating costs: a higher injection allocation would raise PSH's charging costs relative to discharge revenue, and a shift toward withdrawals would burden conventional hydro operators more heavily. Management Committee members representing hydro interests should track whether any party moves to commission a 2026–2027 study, since the allocation mechanism is active and the cost-of-service framework can shift under the tariff's standing provisions.

title: 5a MST 26.4 redline
relevance_reason: Credit and collateral requirements for market participation touch all market participants including hydro operators; changes to Operating Requirement calculation methodology, ancillary services credit treatment, and external transaction (import/export) credit requirements affect hydro operator cash-flow obligations and cost of market entry.
ai_summary: NYISO is revising Section 26.4 of its Tariff governing Operating Requirements and Bidding Requirements—the credit and collateral amounts customers must post to participate in ISO markets. The redline modifies the Energy and Ancillary Services Component calculation, introducing a prepayment agreement pathway (reducing credit hold from 16 to 3 days for qualified customers) and altering the Import and Export Credit Requirement formulas to use 98th and 97th percentile price-differential thresholds respectively, with weighted lookback periods (1/3 weight to prior 1 year, 2/3 weight to prior 5 years). For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, lower credit collateral requirements reduce working-capital drag on dispatch flexibility and frequency/ancillary-service provision; the prepayment option may particularly benefit smaller hydro participants. The import/export methodology change affects trading margins for operators exporting power across seams. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in the excerpt provided.

title: 6 Storm Watch Redispatch
relevance_reason: Storm Watch redispatch cost calculation methodology directly affects settlement of Transmission Security Area (TSA) uplift charges, which are allocated to load in Zone J and rebated to NYISO-wide withdrawals — a mechanism that alters the effective energy price hydro and other resources face in real-time dispatch and settlement.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes tariff and manual clarifications to formalize the 'Long-Term Improvement' to Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost calculation, which modifies how Day-Ahead and real-time flows across constrained lines are computed for settlement. The July 2025 TSA events generated over $53 million in settlement costs, triggering the review; the new methodology will use Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass and real-time schedules, departing from the existing tariff language. Tariff revisions and Transmission & Dispatch Manual Section 4.2.9.1 have been approved by the BIC and OC and are proceeding to Board of Directors and FERC filing. Because TSA costs are ultimately allocated to Zone J load (with rebates to NYISO-wide withdrawals), changes to the settlement formula affect the effective price hydro resources receive in real-time and the competitiveness of their energy bids during constrained periods.

title: 6a MST 4.1 redline
relevance_reason: Tariff language on cost-recovery for reliability-committed units and energy storage resources, plus external capacity suppliers — mechanisms that determine revenue recovery for dispatchable hydro and pumped storage in NYISO commitment and real-time dispatch.
ai_summary: NYISO tariff section 4.1.8 establishes cost-recovery rules for units committed by the ISO for reliability. Generating units, energy storage resources, and aggregations including withdrawal-eligible generators (which includes pumped storage) can recover start-up, minimum-generation, and incremental energy costs incurred for ISO reliability commitments when those costs exceed what was bid or recovered in day-ahead and real-time markets. External installed capacity suppliers responding to SRE (Special Reserve Event) requests may also recover incremental net costs including opportunity costs of lost revenue. The redline clarifies that payments are recovered through Rate Schedule 1 OATT and disputes through Rate Schedule 1 Section 6.1.13. This directly affects PSH revenue when dispatched for reliability outside economic dispatch — the rule determines whether owners recover the true cost of water release or lost opportunity value. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

title: 6b Motion
relevance_reason: Storm Watch redispatch tariff revisions affect how NYISO operates and prices dispatch during weather emergencies, directly touching hydro's dispatch obligations, real-time pricing signals, and revenue recognition in emergency conditions.
ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee voted June 24, 2026 to approve tariff revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing Storm Watch Redispatch operations, with recommendation to the Board to file under FPA Section 205. The document does not detail the specific tariff changes, but Storm Watch redispatch is a real-time dispatch override mechanism triggered during severe weather; revisions likely alter how resources (including hydropower) are called, dispatched, priced, and compensated during these events. The Board vote and FPA filing will determine whether the changes take effect. Hydro operators dispatched under Storm Watch should monitor the filed tariff language and any subsequent Board decision to understand revised compensation structures and mandatory dispatch obligations.

title: 7 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Report Presentation
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year System & Resource Outlook directly informs transmission-constrained deliverability of hydro and zero-emissions resources, identifies congestion chokepoints (Central East, Northern New York) where hydro exports and firm capacity face transmission limits, and shapes long-term resource-adequacy strategy affecting hydro's strategic siting and revenue opportunity.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook, presented to the Management Committee on June 24, 2026, projects 20-year system conditions, demand, generation mix, and transmission congestion across New York. Key findings emphasize that transmission capability increasingly determines whether new resources can serve load, and identify the Central East interface and Northern New York as targeted upgrade opportunities for bulk transmission to enable zero-emissions and firm-capacity development. The Outlook recommends establishing a path for firm, dispatchable capacity (including potential hydrogen-based generation) to provide grid services alongside renewables, coordinating resource additions and retirements to avoid tightening system conditions, and enhancing dynamic voltage support across Central East. Hydro owners in New York and the region exporting into NYISO should track the final Outlook (Board approval scheduled for July 2026) because transmission constraints identified here directly govern where new hydro projects achieve economic deliverability, and the emphasis on firm capacity and operational flexibility reaffirms hydro's strategic value in a high-renewable future—but only if interconnection and transmission planning prioritize hydro access.

title: 7a 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource adequacy and transmission outlook identifies hydro imports from Québec as a key variable affecting NY's generation mix, energy pricing, and interregional flexibility — directly shaping hydro-export revenue and strategic positioning of Northeast hydropower in the largest Northeast market.
ai_summary: NYISO's draft 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook models New York's generation and transmission needs across demand and policy scenarios through 2044, explicitly examining 'Energy Interchange with Hydro-Québec' (p. 52) as a key variable affecting system flexibility and resource adequacy. The report identifies fossil-generation retirements, growing large-load demand (data centers, economic development), and transmission constraints in the Central East corridor as drivers of resource gaps; it evaluates how offshore wind, nuclear, and repowered fossil capacity fill these gaps under zero-emissions vs. limited-emissions policy paths. For Northeast hydropower exporters (Québec and regional run-of-river operators), the outlook signals sustained scarcity and interregional transmission bottlenecks that increase the value of flexible dispatch and cross-border energy exchange — directly affecting pricing in New York's energy market and the strategic case for transmission expansion. The report is in draft status for Management Committee discussion as of June 24, 2026; next steps and stakeholder feedback timelines are not specified in the excerpt.

title: 7a 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report - Revised
relevance_reason: Twenty-year resource and transmission outlook directly shapes hydro's strategic options through demand growth, large-load interconnection patterns, interregional transfer capability, fossil-retirements scenarios, and Quebec hydropower interchange—all central to hydro revenue sustainability and portfolio positioning.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook (draft for Management Committee discussion June 24, 2026) models New York's generation and transmission evolution across policy and demand scenarios to 2044. Key findings: growing peak and energy demand, emergence of large loads as significant growth driver, widespread fossil retirements accelerating, and substantial transmission needs—especially Central East congestion and renewable-pocket curtailment—all shape where and when new capacity (including hydro imports from Quebec) is economically viable. The outlook explicitly evaluates Quebec hydropower interchange availability and its system-flexibility impact, directly affecting the value of interregional hydro flows into NYISO. Most relevant to hydro owners are the demand-growth projections, large-load co-location patterns, transmission-congestion forecasts (which determine export/import economics), and scenarios testing new nuclear and offshore wind arrival—all of which reset the scarcity and locational signals hydro responds to in energy and capacity markets.

title: 7b 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly shapes hydro's strategic options by projecting demand growth, resource-adequacy needs, transmission constraints affecting deliverability, and the interplay of policies (clean-energy targets, fossil retirements) that determine where and whether hydro capacity commands scarcity value; large-load growth and transmission pocket analysis are particularly material to hydro owners evaluating whether to invest in or maintain existing capacity.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook projects substantial generation and transmission investment needs across New York under multiple demand and policy scenarios. The draft report (dated June 24, 2026, presented to the Management Committee) models demand growth driven by emerging large loads (data centers), fossil retirements, and clean-energy policy stringency, finding that supply tightens under current commitments and creating upstate generation-pocket curtailment risk and Central East transmission congestion by 2035–2044. Key variables affecting resource value include Hydro-Québec interchange availability, offshore-wind deployment, and nuclear siting. For hydro owners, demand tightness and the scenario-dependent curtailment/congestion patterns establish the planning backdrop for whether existing and new capacity can command reliability and scarcity-driven revenue; the report flags transmission as a binding constraint in upstate generation pockets where much new solar (and by extension, where storage might be sited to firm it) would concentrate, directly affecting the economic case for PSH or reservoir hydro in those zones. No immediate comment deadline or board action is stated in this draft.

title: 7b 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report redline - Revised
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly informs hydro's strategic options—load growth, resource adequacy constructs, transmission constraints affecting deliverability, large-load interconnection (data centers), and interregional flows with Québec hydro—and shapes how much capacity value existing hydro will capture.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook (draft for June 24, 2026 Management Committee discussion) projects substantial transmission and generation investment needs driven by demand growth, fossil retirements, and zero-emissions policy targets. The outlook models four scenarios with varying demand futures (95x and 100x2044 load cases) and policy stringency, examining resource mix, zonal concentration, and transmission congestion—particularly Central East bottlenecks—through 2044. Load growth emerges as a significant constraint driver, with large loads (data centers) cited as a new demand source, and interregional flows with Hydro-Québec are flagged as affecting "system flexibility and risk." For hydro owners, this frames the scarcity backdrop (tightened supply conditions drive higher downstate payments) and identifies where transmission limits will compress their deliverability to load centers; it also quantifies the scale of renewable and nuclear build-out that will compete for transmission headroom and RA accreditation. The document is in draft status for committee discussion with no stated vote date or comment deadline in the excerpt.

title: 7c 2025 - 2044 System Resource Outlook Appendices
relevance_reason: System Resource Outlook appendices inform seasonal resource adequacy, hydro conditions, and energy sufficiency planning that directly shape scarcity pricing, capacity accreditation, and strategic reserve deployment — core revenue and operational drivers for hydro and PSH.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System Resource Outlook appendices provide detailed baseline and scenario-dependent projections of generation mix, load growth, transmission, and resource availability. The document serves as the foundation for NYISO's seasonal adequacy assessments and informs accreditation of hydro and storage resources, transmission-expansion justification, and long-term market-design assumptions. Hydro operators use SRO forecasts to benchmark their own conditions (e.g., precipitation and water-availability scenarios) against ISO projections and to anticipate changes in reserve-margin tightness, scarcity-pricing exposure, and ancillary-service demand. No specific proposals or votes are signaled in the metadata; this is reference material for the corpus.

title: 7c Motion
relevance_reason: The 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook is a long-range adequacy and planning study that shapes hydro's strategic positioning on seasonal energy availability, resource-mix expectations, and transmission/deliverability assumptions driving dispatch and revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO's Management Committee approves the draft 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook for Board consideration at its June 24, 2026 meeting. This multi-year outlook informs resource-adequacy construction, capacity-accreditation assumptions, and interregional transmission planning affecting hydro's energy-value trajectory and seasonal scarcity signals. The report consolidates input from ESPWG and TPAS stakeholder reviews; hydro owners should review the published outlook to assess whether seasonal hydro conditions, ELCC/RA accreditation parameters, and transmission-constrained scarcity scenarios align with their revenue and participation assumptions.

title: 8 MMU Outlook Review
relevance_reason: NYISO Market Monitoring Unit analysis of renewable curtailment, storage economics, and energy price signals directly affects hydro and pumped-storage revenue via Index REC/Storage Credit structures, technology and nodal discounts on LBMP, and the value of storage-based curtailment mitigation.
stakeholders: Joe Coscia (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit); Pallas LeeVanSchaick (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit)
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit analyzed the 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook to evaluate how market signals guide renewable and storage investment. Key finding: energy storage becomes cost-effective for curtailment mitigation only as wind and solar penetration increases and marginal curtailment rates rise; under the Index Storage Credit structure, storage has strong incentives to locate where curtailment risk is highest, but may be unprofitable near profitable renewable sites. The MMU also identifies nodal and technology discounts (differences between zonal average LBMP and generation-weighted LBMP at renewable sites), which reduce wind and solar net revenues below Index REC strike prices—a signal that affects both the competitiveness of all-renewable portfolios and the relative value of storage as a complementary product. For pumped-storage owners, this analysis quantifies the regional and temporal distribution of curtailment opportunities and implied net revenue costs of storage across three policy scenarios (Contract Case 2030, 100×44 High Load 2035, 95×44 Baseline Load 2035), directly informing where PSH investment is most economically justified under NYISO market rules.

title: Final Motions
relevance_reason: Motion #4 approves the 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook, a long-range adequacy and resource planning study that shapes NYISO capacity/RA policy, transmission needs, and seasonal energy-sufficiency outlooks—foundational inputs to hydro fleet valuation, accreditation, and retirement/mothballing decisions.
ai_summary: The NYISO Management Committee unanimously approved the draft 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook on June 24, 2026, following stakeholder review at prior ESPWG (Electricity System Planning Working Group) and TPAS (Transmission Planning Advisory Subcommittee) meetings and Business Issues Committee recommendation on June 10. The Outlook is a 19-year resource adequacy and transmission-planning forecast used to drive NYISO capacity market design, seasonal energy-sufficiency assessments, and infrastructure investment priorities. Hydro owners in NYISO should review this document to understand how their resources are modeled in the long-term adequacy baseline (seasonal accreditation assumptions, drought-stress scenarios, availability profiles) and how the Outlook's transmission and resource-mix conclusions may affect market scarcity signals, RA penalties, and the valuation of dispatchable/storage capacity relative to new renewables and storage.

title: 04 Geospatial Modeling of EV Load Impacts at National Grid
relevance_reason: Load growth from EV electrification drives demand forecasting inputs to NYISO's resource-adequacy and operational-planning processes, which directly affect hydro's strategic value and dispatch scheduling; co-located load and feeder-level demand patterns also inform transmission and storage deployment decisions that touch hydro assets and deliverability.
stakeholders: Hengfang (Alex) Deng (National Grid); John Weis (National Grid)
ai_summary: National Grid's Load Forecasting & Analytics team presented geospatial modeling methods to forecast electric-vehicle load impacts on NYISO's distribution and transmission network at feeder and system levels through 2035+. The work integrates EV adoption propensity models (driven by socioeconomics, vehicle-registration data, program enrollment) with vehicle-specific charging profiles (light-duty, medium/heavy-duty, transit/school bus) to project unconstrained load impacts by location and time. The presentation outlined a hybrid allocation approach (census-tract and parcel-level granularity for subsets like depot-based MHDVs and queued storage/PV) and flagged ongoing challenges in data integration, propensity-model robustness, and governance consistency. No near-term policy decision or vote was announced, but this forecasting framework feeds NYISO's system-planning and resource-adequacy studies—which determine seasonal reserve requirements, transmission expansion triggers, and the scarcity value of dispatchable hydro and PSH.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: NYISO is discussing capacity accreditation modeling and resource-adequacy improvements that directly affect how hydro and storage resources are credited toward ICAP requirements, plus intermittency-balancing rules and transmission-security backstops that shape dispatch and revenue.
stakeholders: Amanda Myott (NYISO); Keegan Guinn (NYISO); Matt Schwall (NYISO); Mike Swider (NYISO); Ross Altmann (NYISO); Vijay Kaki (NYISO); Yvonne Huang (NYISO)
ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Working Group is convening to address three mechanisms affecting hydro and storage operators. First, Mike Swider will lead a session on 'Improving Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling' (10:05–11:05), which signals active revision of how NYISO calculates ELCC or capacity value for dispatchable and storage resources — the foundation of ICAP showings and revenue. Second, Amanda Myott will discuss 'External SRE Opportunity Cost & Penalty' (11:05–11:35), addressing how NYISO applies mitigation to out-of-market resources and opportunity-cost-based offer floors, a chronic pinch point for storage and use-limited hydro. Third, Vijay Kaki's session on 'Balancing Intermittency: Manual Updates' (11:35–11:55) suggests NYISO is refining how it handles forecast error and variability, which affects flexible ramping procurement and settlement. A fourth agenda item, 'Seasonal TSL Floor Kick Off' (Keegan Guinn, 1:10–1:55), signals the opening of a seasonal transmission-security limit study that may alter locational dispatch signals or scarcity pricing. The meeting is structured across the ICAP Working Group, Market Issues Working Group, and Price-Responsive Load Working Group, indicating multi-stakeholder engagement. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated in the agenda itself.

title: 2 Improving Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO is evaluating alternative capacity accreditation methodologies (moving from LOLE to Expected Unserved Energy) that will directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited toward resource adequacy requirements and capacity payments, with implications for accreditation factors (CAFs) and UCAP contributions across different resource classes.
ai_summary: NYISO is conducting a study to increase transparency in resource adequacy modeling and evaluate whether Expected Unserved Energy (EUE)—a magnitude-based metric capturing duration and depth of capacity shortfalls—should replace or supplement the traditional 1-day-in-10-years LOLE criterion for calculating Capacity Accreditation Factors (CAFs). The CAF determines how much Unforced Capacity (UCAP) each resource can provide; EUE would capture reliability contributions differently across resource types, particularly affecting hydro's seasonal contribution and ability to mitigate prolonged weather events as NYISO's winter-risk profile increases (4.4 GW of intermittent resources expected in five years). NYISO has engaged GE to model CAF sensitivity under EUE scenarios and assess accreditation stability; the study is due to complete in 2026, with broader stakeholder assessment of which methodology better reflects NYISO's evolving resource mix to follow.

title: 3 External SRE Opportunity Cost & Penalty
relevance_reason: NYISO proposes to cap opportunity-cost recovery for external ICAP suppliers (including hydropower and storage) called for emergency service, and to add FERC referral penalties for non-compliance — changes that directly affect how external hydro and PSH can recover costs when required to respond to emergency calls, and the penalties they face for non-compliance.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes two changes to external ICAP supplier obligations during System Reserve Events (SREs): (1) cap opportunity-cost recovery at the applicable proxy-generator-bus LBMP minus ATC interface congestion, eliminating pass-through of revenues external resources could earn in neighboring control areas; (2) retain existing ICAP Shortfall Penalties for SRE non-response but add FERC referral threat if an external supplier delivers to another control area while failing to meet its NYISO energy obligation. The proposal reframes opportunity costs as compensation for NYISO's mandatory -$1,000/MWh bidding requirement, aligning external treatment closer to internal reliability suppliers. Next step: NYISO will return to ICAPWG/MIWG with draft tariff and manual language following stakeholder feedback. For external hydro and PSH owners operating at NYISO seams, this eliminates the option to recover the full spread between NYISO and neighboring-market prices during an SRE call, while the FERC referral adds reputational/regulatory risk to non-compliance.

title: 3 External SRE Opportunity Cost & Penalty - Revised
relevance_reason: NYISO proposal to cap opportunity-cost recovery and strengthen defection penalties for external ICAP suppliers affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources located outside NY (notably in PJM or ISO-NE) value their NYISO capacity obligations and manage the trade-off between NYISO SRE calls and higher-priced neighboring-market opportunities — a direct revenue and strategic participation lever for multi-region hydro portfolios.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes to cap opportunity-cost recovery for external ICAP suppliers responding to Supplemental Resource Evaluations (SREs) at the applicable proxy-bus LBMP, excluding ATC interface congestion, and to add FERC referral as a sanction for suppliers that defect to sell energy elsewhere during an SRE obligation. Currently, external suppliers can claim any opportunity costs; the proposal aligns external cost recovery more closely to internal reliability-commitment rules (startup, minimum generation) and removes the subsidy for selling at neighboring-market prices. Status: draft for ICAPWG/MIWG discussion; next step is stakeholder feedback and return with tariff/manual language (no deadline stated). External ICAP suppliers — especially multi-region hydropower and gas operators serving NYISO capacity markets from PJM, ISO-NE, or IESO — face lower opportunity-cost compensation in high-priced hours in neighboring control areas, reducing the financial benefit of maintaining NYISO ICAP obligations; simultaneously, the threat of FERC referral for non-compliance strengthens the incentive to answer SREs even when outside-market prices spike.

title: 4 Balancing Intermittency: Manual Updates
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Uncertainty Reserve Requirements (URR) design directly affects hydro's revenue from ancillary services and flexibility products; URR adds new locational reserve requirements calculated for Net Load, Wind, and Solar forecast uncertainty, requiring hydro and other dispatchable resources to supply additional reserves on top of existing requirements, with deployment in August 2026.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing Uncertainty Reserve Requirements (URR)—a new 24-hour reserve product to account for forecast uncertainty in Net Load, Land-Based Wind, Offshore Wind, and Front-of-Meter Solar. The URR design was accepted by FERC in July 2025 (ER25-1998) and is scheduled for August 2026 deployment. NYISO has incorporated stakeholder feedback from June 2nd meeting and updated the Ancillary Services Manual and a new Locational Reserve Requirements document; notably, the Long Island 30-minute total reserve requirement will vary from 270 MW (off-peak) to 540 MW (on-peak). These additional, locational reserve requirements create new demand for the ramping and flexibility services that hydropower traditionally supplies, raising the value of flexible hydro capacity during high-uncertainty periods (high wind/solar variability). All resources—including hydro and pumped storage—will be obligated to contribute to URR supply. Stakeholder vote scheduled for July 15–16, 2026, at BIC and OC meetings; deployment begins August 2026.

title: 4a Locational Reserves Requirements
relevance_reason: NYISO locational reserve requirements define the operating-reserve products (spinning, 10-minute, 30-minute) that hydropower and pumped-storage units are eligible to supply, and establish locational and magnitude thresholds that affect their reserve-procurement revenue and dispatch obligations.
ai_summary: NYISO specifies locational reserve requirements across five zones (NYCA, EAST, SENY, NYC, LI) for three reserve products: spinning reserve (most restrictive, synchronous units only), 10-minute non-synchronized reserve, and 30-minute reserve. NYCA requires 655 MW spinning and 1,310 MW 10-minute total; EAST requires 330 MW spinning and 1,200 MW 10-minute; SENY requires 1,300–1,800 MW 30-minute (varying by hour, reduced to zero during thunderstorm alerts); NYC and LI have localized requirements of 625–1,250 MW and 120–540 MW respectively. Hydropower and pumped-storage units eligible for spinning reserve (synchronous generation) are the primary suppliers of the 655 MW NYCA spinning requirement and regional spinning reserves; conventional hydro can also supply 10-minute and 30-minute reserves. The document does not indicate a pending vote or comment deadline but establishes the baseline rules governing reserve accreditation and revenue eligibility for reservoir hydro and PSH in New York.

title: 4b Draft Ancillary Services Manual redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's redlined Ancillary Services Manual directly governs how hydro and pumped-storage resources bid, qualify, and are compensated for regulation, reserves, voltage support, and frequency response — hydro's core revenue streams and compliance obligations.
ai_summary: NYISO has released a draft (May 2026) redline to its Ancillary Services Manual 2, governing qualification, performance tracking, settlement, and demand curves for regulation and frequency response service, operating reserves (spinning, non-spinning, quick-start), voltage support, and reactive power capability testing. The manual includes new or revised sections on energy storage resources' reactive power testing (3.6.5), synchronous condenser testing (3.6.3), HVDC VSC technology (3.6.4), and regulation service performance audits (4.11.3). The document is in draft for discussion; it was accepted by BIC on 04/15/2026 and OC on 04/16/2026, with an effective date of 05/01/2026. Hydro and pumped-storage operators must track revisions to regulation demand curves (4.8), performance adjustment rules (4.5–4.6), and resource qualification thresholds (4.11), as these directly affect the value of regulation and reserve services — historically hydro's largest ancillary-service revenue source in NYISO. The manual's treatment of energy storage resources in reactive power testing and performance frameworks may also signal NYISO's direction on storage parity rules relevant to pumped-storage PSH participation.

title: 5 Transmission Security Background
relevance_reason: NYISO's derating-factor methodology and transmission-security-assessment practices directly affect ICAP accreditation for all generators including hydropower, and the document addresses seasonal EFORd calculations and limited-control run-of-river hydro treatment in capacity-adequacy studies.
ai_summary: NYISO staff present transmission-security assessment background and derating-factor recommendations for the Installed Capacity market, including treatment of seasonal forced outage rates (EFORd) for thermal units and reference to run-of-river hydro derating under ICAP Manual Attachment N. The document identifies a methodological gap: 5-year seasonal EFORd averages understate zonal derating factors by roughly 2% compared to 5-year continuous EFORd, and NYISO recommends developing an automated tool to support multi-year continuous seasonal EFORd calculations. The implications for hydro are indirect but material—derating factors determine Locational Minimum Installed Capacity Requirements (LCRs) that shape the ICAP demand curve and pricing hydro and other resources bid into. Run-of-river resources are explicitly noted as following Attachment N methodology, though the document does not propose changes to that treatment in this June 2026 working-group meeting.

title: 6 Seasonal TSL Floor Kick Off
relevance_reason: NYISO's seasonal TSL floor methodology directly affects ICAP accreditation and capacity requirements for all resources including hydro and storage, with implications for winter peak conditions and derating factors for limited-control run-of-river hydro.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing seasonal Transmission Security Limit (TSL) floor values as part of its Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC February 18, 2026, Docket ER26-1431, effective date requested June 28, 2026, for 2027-2028 capability year onward). The seasonal methodology will replace annual calculations with winter- and summer-specific peak loads, bulk transmission limits, equipment ratings, and derating factors. Critically, NYISO proposes to include winter performance data for Limited Control Run of River hydro resources in the seasonal derating factor calculation and will establish winter-specific Special Case Resource (SCR) enrollment, which raises the ICAP addback component. The document indicates that 5-year derating factors will consider winter EFORd for thermal units and seasonal adjustments for intermittent and run-of-river hydro per updated ICAP Manual Attachment N (revised June 2026), and that seasonal External Unforced Deliverability Rights (UDR) elections would be implemented starting 2027-2028. Pumped storage and battery-based energy storage resources (ESRs) currently face no specific UCAP duration-limit adjustment in the TSL floor calculation, though the document notes ESRs are modeled in resource adequacy with full availability subject to duration limits. Run-of-river hydro owners should monitor the winter derating factor methodology to understand how winter-season performance data may reduce their seasonal ICAP accreditation; reservoir and pumped-storage operators should track the seasonal UDR election framework and any future ESR adjustments proposed.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: NYISO is proposing changes to ICAP Manual rules governing seasonal capacity accreditation (UDR/EDR elections) and must-offer requirements for dispatchable resources, which directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage facilities are accredited and obligated to participate in the capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee will vote on proposed revisions to the ICAP Manual and attachments governing Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements, specifically seasonal UDR (Unforced Demand Response) and EDR (Emergency Demand Response) elections and must-offer requirements. The presentation and vote are scheduled for June 18, 2026, 10:05–10:35 am. The mechanic affects hydro and PSH accreditation methodology: changes to seasonal capacity elections and must-offer obligations determine whether and how run-of-river, reservoir, and pumped-storage facilities count toward winter RA showings and what dispatch obligations attach to that capacity. Owners of dispatchable hydro and PSH should monitor this vote, as the revised rules will reset their seasonal capacity election rights and operational requirements.

title: 2 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements – Seasonal UDR/EDR Elections and Must Offer Requirement: Proposed ICAP Manual and Attachment Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO establishes seasonal must-offer requirements and penalties for holders of External Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest of State Deliverability Rights (EDRs), which directly affects how external capacity—including hydropower—can be exported from and must be offered to meet NYCA obligations, and creates an enforcement mechanism (penalty methodology in new Attachment Q) that changes an owner's compliance exposure and dispatch obligations.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing seasonal elections for External UDRs and EDRs starting May 1, 2027 (2027–2028 Capability Year), with elections due August 1, 2026. Holders of External UDRs or EDRs must submit distinct seasonal elections (one for Summer, one for Winter) to return capacity to the NYCA, or face a must-offer requirement: capacity not returned must be offered in each ICAP Spot Market Auction or certified to meet LSE minimum UCAP requirements, and failure triggers a penalty calculated using Market Clearing Price and a new Attachment Q methodology converting ICAP to UCAP. This reshapes the options available to external capacity owners (including hydro exporters to NYCA) by forcing them to commit seasonally to either export or domestic service, with financial penalties for non-compliance; pumped-storage and reservoir hydro operators holding external rights into New York will need to evaluate seasonal dispatch strategies against the penalty risk. The proposal was filed with FERC in Docket No. ER26-1431 with requested effective date June 28, 2026; ICAP Manual revisions become effective one business day after the 10-day appeal period if approved (by July 7, 2026 absent appeal or FERC rejection).

title: 2a Draft ICAP Manual
relevance_reason: NYISO's ICAP Manual governs capacity accreditation, duration-adjustment factors, and reporting requirements that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are valued and compensated in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's draft Installed Capacity (ICAP) Manual 17.0, effective April 2, 2026, specifies minimum capacity requirements, unforced-capacity calculations, and resource-specific test conditions and reporting obligations for all capacity suppliers. The manual explicitly addresses energy-limited resources (including hydro), co-located storage resources (directly relevant to PSH), duration-adjustment factors, and DER aggregations. Sections 4.1.1 and 4.1.4 detail Energy Duration Limitations and Duration Adjustment Factors for installed-capacity suppliers and co-located storage; Section 4.2 governs Demonstrated Maximum Net Capability (DMNC) testing that determines hydro's accredited capacity; and Section 4.4 establishes monthly operating-data reporting requirements (including intermittent power resources and energy-limited resources). The document is in draft for discussion at the Business Issues Committee (BIC) meeting on June 18, 2026, following circulation at the June 9, 2026 ICAPWG meeting (yellow-highlighted changes noted). Hydro and PSH owners need to review the updated duration-adjustment methodology, DMNC test procedures, and state-of-charge rules for co-located storage to confirm accreditation treatment and compliance deadlines.

title: 2b Motion
relevance_reason: NYISO proposes seasonal capacity accreditation and must-offer requirement changes affecting hydro and PSH revenue recognition and operational obligations in the Installed Capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved revisions to the Installed Capacity Manual establishing seasonal Unforced Demand Response (UDR) and Energy Demand Response (EDR) elections and must-offer requirements tied to Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements (FERC Docket ER26-1431). The proposal changes how hydro and storage assets are accredited for capacity in seasonal winter conditions and imposes new must-offer obligations during those periods. The revisions become effective only upon FERC acceptance; rejection triggers automatic rescission. Reservoir hydropower and pumped-storage operators face potential changes to their capacity revenue recognition and dispatch obligations during the winter season, with implementation contingent on FERC's decision in ER26-1431.

title: 2c Draft ICAP Manual edited during BIC
relevance_reason: NYISO's ICAP Manual revision defines unforced-capacity accreditation, duration-adjustment factors, and participation rules for energy-limited resources (hydro) and co-located storage—direct determinants of how much capacity hydro and pumped-storage plants can sell and at what revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO has issued a draft ICAP (Installed Capacity) Manual 04, effective April 2, 2026, for BIC (Business Issues Committee) discussion and acceptance. The manual governs how much unforced capacity hydro, pumped storage, and other resources can supply to the NYCA, including duration-adjustment factors for energy-limited resources (§4.1.1), co-located storage provisions (§4.1.4), and DMNC/DMGC testing procedures (§4.2). Hydro and PSH owners must comply with operating-data reporting (§4.4), bidding/scheduling (§4.8.7 for co-located storage), and maintenance-scheduling rules; the manual sets the technical and temporal basis for their capacity accreditation and revenue in NYISO's ICAP auctions. The document is in draft form and was under BIC discussion as of June 18, 2026—no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

title: Final Motion
relevance_reason: NYISO is approving installed capacity manual revisions for seasonal capacity accreditation elections and must-offer requirements affecting hydro and storage resource participation and revenue obligations.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved on June 18, 2026 revisions to the Installed Capacity Manual implementing Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements, specifically addressing seasonal UDR/EDR (Unforced Demand Response / Equivalent Demand Response) elections and must-offer requirements (FERC Docket ER26-1431). The revisions change the rules governing when and how capacity resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, must be available and offer energy during winter scarcity periods — affecting both their accreditation methodology and their operational obligations. The approval is conditional on FERC acceptance of the filing; if FERC rejects the proposal, the manual revisions will be rescinded without further action. Most exposed are owners with winter-dependent capacity portfolios and PSH facilities subject to new seasonal election and dispatch mechanics.

title: 00 Agenda
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 and Storm Watch Redispatch revisions touch interconnection/deliverability rules and real-time dispatch protocols that affect hydro asset dispatch obligations and transmission-dependent revenue.
stakeholders: Aaron Markham (NYISO); Afreen Vahora (NYISO); Corinne Didomenico (NYISO); Davetta Thacher (NYISO); Ken Buske (NYISO); Mark Domino (NYISO); Prasad Shinde (NYISO); Raj Dontireddy (NYISO); Sarah Rogers (NYISO); William Lawlor (NYISO)
ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee agenda for June 11, 2026 includes two items with hydro relevance: (1) Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 (action item, presenter Prasad Shinde, 10:35–10:50), which may affect interconnection capacity and delivery rights for new or existing hydro resources; (2) Storm Watch Redispatch manual revisions (action item, presenter Afreen Vahora, 10:50–11:05), which modifies real-time dispatch instructions during grid stress and could change hydro's dispatch flexibility and revenue opportunity during high-value periods. Both items are scheduled for action/vote at this meeting. Additionally, the Summer 2026 Operating Study update (Raj Dontireddy) provides seasonal adequacy context relevant to hydro's peaking and flexibility value.

title: 03 Operations Report - May 2026
relevance_reason: Monthly operational metrics tracking NYISO generation mix, hydro/pumped-storage capacity and availability, intermittent resource forecasting, curtailment, and transmission constraints that directly affect hydro dispatch and revenue opportunities.
ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 operations report shows hydro (including pumped-storage hydro) at 6,324 MW nameplate capacity, comprising 12% of NYCA generation resources. The report tracks month-to-month generation mix, day-ahead market capacity unavailable in real-time (broken by fuel type including hydro), wind and solar curtailment by transmission constraint, net load ramps, and reserve activations. Two new transmission projects (Smart Path Connect and additional 345 kV facilities) came online in May, and the Champlain Hudson Power Express merchant transmission facility entered the real-time energy market on 5/13, potentially improving deliverability for hydro exports. Hydro owners use this baseline data to assess system balance, transmission constraints limiting their dispatch, and competitive positioning versus wind/solar capacity (11,547 MW intermittent nameplate vs. 6,324 MW hydro). The report shows May 2026 wind and FTM solar curtailment at 2.7% system-wide, with specific transmission constraints listed, affecting revenue for hydro resources competing in the same nodal zones.

title: 05a Summer 2026 Operations Study - Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Changes in transmission infrastructure (CHPE HVDC addition, Smart Path Connect upgrades, interface limit redistribution) and generation retirements affect hydro dispatch patterns, interregional transfer capability, and revenue opportunity from exports and locational scarcity pricing—all of which touch hydro's strategic deliverability and the value of flexible ramping capacity.
ai_summary: NYISO's Summer 2026 operations study reports 1,225 MW of generation retirements (including large thermal units like Danskammer 532 MW and Narrows 352 MW) offset by 225 MW of solar and storage additions. Major transmission projects—Champlain Hudson Power Express HVDC, Smart Path Connect 345 kV stations, and Hertel–Astoria additions—are now modeled in service. These changes redistribute flows and alter interface thermal limits: Total East interface limit decreased 125 MW, UPNY-ConEd increased 250 MW, UPNY-SENY increased 225 MW, and Moses South increased 250 MW. Sprain Brook Dunwoodie South interface limit was raised to 4,225 MW and Total East to 4,050 MW. The study's status is a June 2026 presentation update to the Operating Committee; no forward deadlines are stated. For hydro owners, the net effect is mixed: retirement of thermal generation reduces dispatchable competition and may elevate real-time energy prices and ramping value, while the HVDC and transmission upgrades improve northern import capability and may compress NY price separations, affecting both in-state dispatchable hydro revenue and Northwest-to-NY export economics. Pumped-storage operators should monitor how the reduced Moses South thermal limits and increased UPNY interfaces change the voltage-support and frequency-response service opportunities in those zones.

title: 05c Summer 2026 Operating Study Appendices Draft - Redline
relevance_reason: Summer operating study details interregional transmission flows, HVdc converter schedules, and phase-angle regulator dispatch that directly affect hydropower export deliverability and revenue from cross-border power transfers — particularly from TransÉnergie (Quebec hydro) into NYISO and ISONE.
ai_summary: NYISO's Summer 2026 Operating Study appendices specify base-case assumptions for generation dispatch, load, and interregional transfers. TransÉnergie is modeled exporting 2,362 MW to NYISO, 1,500 MW to ISO-NE via Sandy Pond HVdc, and 1,250 MW to Ontario (IESO) — all major hydro corridors. HVdc converter schedules list in-service status for Champlain Hudson Power Express (1,250 MW), Sandy Pond (1,500 MW), and four other TransÉnergie HVdc links (total ~3,300 MW). Phase-angle regulator dispatch on key NY interfaces (Ramapo, Uniondale, and cross-Sound) is specified to manage congestion. This study informs summer transfer limits and is foundational for RA adequacy and reserve requirement calculations; export-dependent hydro owners (Northwest suppliers to WECC/California, Quebec hydro exporters) should verify their assumed dispatch levels match actual operating conditions and test sensitivity to transmission constraints.

title: 06a Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 - Presentation
relevance_reason: NYISO Expedited Deliverability Study determines which generation and storage projects can deliver power without system upgrades — directly affecting interconnection timelines, costs, and deliverability certainty for new hydro, PSH, or co-located resources seeking NYISO queue status.
ai_summary: NYISO's Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 analyzes three projects (Curtis/Palmer natural gas/hydro hybrid, Soltage battery energy storage, RP New York BESS) for deliverability at requested CRIS (capacity resource interconnection service) levels without triggering system-wide upgrades. All three projects passed their respective interface and byway tests and are deemed fully deliverable at requested capacity: Curtis/Palmer at 58.8 MW summer, Soltage at 20 MW, and RP New York BESS at 5.1 MW. The study is on track for Operating Committee approval with a 5-business-day decision window for each interconnection customer to accept or reject its deliverable MW; non-acceptance triggers re-study of remaining projects. For any hydro or hybrid generator seeking interconnection in NYISO, this procedural pathway offers faster deliverability determination than full Cluster Study if transmission constraints are minimal — but only for projects already through Class Year or Cluster study phases for ERIS (energy resource interconnection service).

title: 06b Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 - Report
relevance_reason: NYISO interconnection deliverability study determines whether a hydropower project (Curtis/Palmer Hydro) can inject its requested capacity without network upgrades — directly affecting hydro interconnection timelines, costs, and strategic feasibility of delivery into high-value zones.
ai_summary: NYISO's Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 concludes that the Curtis/Palmer Hydro Project in the Rest-of-State Capacity Region is fully deliverable at its requested CRIS level without requiring system upgrades. The study evaluated three projects (Curtis/Palmer Hydro, Soltage battery, RP New York BESS) using 2029 summer peak conditions, transfer-limit analysis across capacity regions, and contingency screening under NPCC/NYSRC criteria. For hydro interconnection customers, a 'fully deliverable' determination removes the risk of being forced to undersize their injection or fund costly network upgrades to achieve their requested MW — streamlining path to commercial operation. The analysis covers methodology, base-case modeling, and transfer-capability assessments but contains no stated comment deadlines or next procedural steps in this excerpt.

title: 07a Storm Watch Redispatch - Presentation
relevance_reason: Tariff and settlement methodology changes to Storm Watch Redispatch (Transmission Security Analysis) cost allocation affect how real-time dispatch costs are computed and allocated to load zones, which can influence uplift/make-whole charges borne by dispatchable resources including hydro.
ai_summary: NYISO is clarifying its Services Tariff (MST 4.1.8) and revising the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual to document a long-term improvement to Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost calculation. The enhancement will use Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass (instead of initial Day-Ahead schedules) and real-time schedules to determine flows across TSA constraints, while keeping the real-time shadow-price methodology and Zone J customer allocation unchanged. This refinement stems from review of July 2025 TSA settlements that exceeded $53 million and aims to more accurately reflect the actual redispatch obligation. NYISO is seeking votes on the proposed tariff revisions at the June 2026 Membership Committee meeting.

title: 07b Storm Watch Redispatch - T&D Manual - Redline
relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch procedures affect real-time dispatch operations, transmission constraints, and emergency redispatch authority — core operational obligations and dispatch mechanisms that touch hydro's real-time pricing exposure and must-offer/dispatch-instruction compliance.
ai_summary: NYISO's Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) redlines the Storm Watch Redispatch procedure, a real-time operational protocol for managing transmission constraints and emergency conditions. The document is in DRAFT status, accepted by the BIC on 04/15/2026 and Operating Committee on 04/16/2026, with an effective date of 05/01/2026. Storm Watch Redispatch is a corrective control strategy that allows NYISO to issue real-time dispatch instructions to manage transmission violations and security constraints — a tool that can override normal market clearing and compel hydro resources to adjust output. For hydro operators, the relevance lies in the operational obligation to accept Storm Watch redispatch directives, which may suppress market-cleared prices during constraint periods or require uneconomic output changes. Reservoir and run-of-river hydro are equally exposed to these dispatch obligations; the mechanism affects real-time revenue predictability and compliance burden.

title: Final Motions
relevance_reason: Motion #2 approves the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02, which directly addresses interconnection and deliverability—a core strategic option for hydro owners evaluating uprates, new resources, and transmission-constrained resource placement.
stakeholders: Greg Yozzo (Central Hudson Gas & Electric Corp.); Jason Ragona (Consolidated Edison Co. of NY, Inc.); Kenneth Buske (National Grid); Scott Knewasser (New York State Electric & Gas (NYSEG) / Rochester Gas and Electric Corp.); Nick Kowalczyk (Orange & Rockland Utilities, Inc.)
ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee approved three routine motions on June 11, 2026: prior-meeting minutes, the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 Report, and revisions to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual for Storm Watch Redispatch. The deliverability study approval is substantive for hydro owners: deliverability studies determine whether generation—including hydro uprates and new hydro interconnection—can physically export power and receive full capacity accreditation, and expedited studies typically flag resource-constraint relief needed for queue-clearing. The Storm Watch manual revisions affect real-time dispatch and redispatch authority during extreme weather, a standing operational obligation for synchronous hydro units. No comment deadlines or further votes are stated in this motion document.

title: Proposed Motions
relevance_reason: Motion #2 approves an Expedited Deliverability Study that affects interconnection and transmission deliverability for new and existing resources, and Motion #3 modifies dispatch operations manual rules that govern how generation responds to grid conditions — both touch hydro's operational obligations and strategic positioning for interconnection and uprates.
ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee approves the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 Report and revisions to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual related to 'Storm Watch Redispatch.' The deliverability study directly affects which new and existing generation (including hydro uprates and storage projects) can physically deliver power; study approval clears the path for resource interconnection decisions. The Storm Watch redispatch manual revisions change how NYISO instructs synchronous generation to operate during weather events — a compliance obligation for conventional hydro units. No comment deadline or vote date beyond the June 11, 2026 approval is stated in this motion summary.

title: 2 Draft April 15, 2026 Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO capacity accreditation and winter ICAP demand-curve methodology directly affects pumped-storage and conventional hydro revenue through seasonal accreditation factors (CAFs) and locational capacity-market clearing prices; the CHPE UDRs decision also touches interregional hydro deliverability and market-seam governance.
stakeholders: Mike Cadwalader (Atlantic Economics); Howard Fromer (Bayonne Energy Center); Doreen Saia (Greenberg Traurig); Mike DeSocio (Luminary Energy); Adam Fam (NYDOS UIU); Alexis Drake (NYISO); Amanda Myott (NYISO); Zach T. Smith (NYISO); Alex Novicki (NYSEG/RG&E); Joe Coscia (Potomac Economics)
ai_summary: NYISO's BIC approved tariff revisions to align winter ICAP demand curves for 2026–2027 to account for potential capacity supply via CHPE (Champlain Hudson Power Express) UDRs from HydroQuébec into New York City, contingent on CHPE commencing summer participation. The proposal resets winter-peak and shoulder ICAP demand curves and their UCAP translations using capacity-accreditation factors (CAFs) that assume CHPE participation ('CHPE-In' parameters). Stakeholders raised concerns that actual winter capacity supply via CHPE could diverge from the 0-MW assumption for winter peaks, potentially inverting NYC winter-to-shoulder price signals and reducing accreditation revenue for existing capacity suppliers. The NYISO rejected a lower-curve stakeholder alternative and declined to impose a prohibition on CHPE winter sales, asserting the proposal maintains revenue sufficiency for the reference technology. The proposal is pending FERC approval under ER26-1431 and will become effective on FERC's permit date; it resets baseline assumptions for the broader Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements design scheduled for 2027–2028. Most exposed are existing hydro and PSH owners in NYC and rest-of-state capacity zones whose seasonal RA valuations and clearing-price signals are recalibrated.

title: 2 Draft May 13, 2026 Minutes
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: capacity market accreditation and participation rules (CHPE UDRs, capacity parameter changes), automatic ICAP surplus-offer rules affecting capacity availability and revenue, and RA/reliability planning updates all touch hydro's capacity revenue streams and strategic options in NYISO; also load-forecasting and DER participation developments relevant to hydro's competitive position.
stakeholders: Howard Fromer (Bayonne Energy Center); Kevin Lang (Couch White); Mike DeSocio (Luminary Energy); Afreen Vahora (NYISO); Robb Pike (NYISO); Zach T. Smith (NYISO); Alex Novicki (NYSEG/RG&E); Matt Cinadr (e Cubed Company)
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved an Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers proposal (Motion #1, May 13, 2026) requiring automatic offering of surplus capacity at $0.00/kW-month with clearing price payment, with May 2027 implementation. Separately, CHPE transmission line commenced real-time energy-market operation May 13, 2026 and capacity-market participation July 2026 delivery month, triggering changes in NYISO capacity-market parameters that will affect existing resources' capacity availability ratings starting July 2026. The committee also reviewed ongoing Electric System Planning and Installed Capacity Working Group activities addressing reliability enhancements (FERC Order 1920 compliance), 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook updates, DER unavailability methodologies, and flexible large-load wholesale participation. For hydro owners, the surplus-offer rule eliminates the option to withhold resale capacity (historically 2.1 MW unoffered Jan 2026), forcing systematic market clearing at spotauction prices; the CHPE parameter shift reallocates capacity value across the system, requiring owners with reduced ICAP ratings to secure makeup capacity or release excess through July 2026 auctions.

title: 3 Market Operations Report
relevance_reason: NYISO Market Operations Report contains energy price formation data, uplift/make-whole mechanisms, ancillary services pricing (regulation, reserves, voltage support, black start), and cost-allocation developments directly affecting hydro revenue.
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Operations Report (June 2026) shows energy and ancillary services cost composition for NYISO customers through May 2026, revealing material shifts in price formation and uplift structures. LBMP (locational energy price) has risen sharply to $43.63/MWh in May 2026 YTD average vs. $36.99/MWh in May 2025; year-to-date NYISO average cost/MWh stands at $102.99 (May 2026) versus $80.72 (May 2025). Uplift (make-whole payments) shows month-to-month volatility, with statewide uplift share swinging between positive $1.68/MWh (January 2026) and negative values in subsequent months. Voltage support and black start services remain stable at approximately $0.14–$0.38/MWh monthly, while reserve and regulation costs track energy scarcity. The report's detailed monthly breakdown by LBMP, NTAC (transmission), reserves, regulation, uplift components, and ancillary services provides visibility into how NYISO's price-formation mechanisms and market-mitigation rules are translating to customer costs — critical for hydro operators managing opportunity costs and dispatch decisions.

title: 4a MST 26.4 redline
relevance_reason: This tariff redline modifies NYISO's credit and collateral requirements for market participants, including specific provisions affecting energy and ancillary services bids, import/export transactions, and capacity market participation — revenue streams and operational obligations that directly shape hydro and PSH participation economics.
ai_summary: NYISO has redlined tariff section 26.4 governing Operating and Bidding Requirements — the credit collateral framework that participants must post to trade energy, ancillary services, and participate in capacity and TCC auctions. The revision clarifies calculation formulas for the Energy and Ancillary Services Component (with separate factors for prepaid vs. non-prepaid customers), refines Import and Export Credit Requirements using 98th and 97th percentile price differentials respectively over rolling one- and five-year lookback windows, and specifies how historical price data is weighted and applied when insufficient data exists at a location. For hydro and PSH operators, this affects the upfront credit burden to bid flexible energy and ramping products, particularly for facilities engaged in import/export arbitrage or frequent real-time adjustment — the collateral requirement formulas directly reduce available capital to deploy in energy or AS markets. Most exposed are PSH and run-of-river operators using interregional transactions to optimize revenue, and any facility with volatile net-settlement exposure. No explicit deadline or next-step action is stated in this redline excerpt.

title: 5 Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) Costs: Tariff & Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost settlement and uplift allocation directly affects NYISO energy market price formation and cost recovery mechanisms that apply to all dispatchable resources including hydropower.
stakeholders: Afreen Vahora (NYISO); Potomac Economics (Potomac Economics (Market Monitor Unit))
ai_summary: NYISO proposes tariff and manual revisions to clarify the calculation of Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) costs—uplift charges triggered by emergency transmission constraints during severe weather. The Long-Term Improvement will enhance Day-Ahead and real-time flow calculations by using Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass and real-time schedules to determine flows across TSA constraints, while keeping the real-time Shadow Price and allocation to Zone J customers unchanged. NYISO will seek votes on manual revisions at the June Oversight Committee and tariff revisions at the June Management Committee. This affects how NYISO recovers emergency redispatch costs from the market and thus the magnitude of uplift/make-whole payments that hydro and other dispatchable resources encounter during scarcity events—clarifying the calculation methodology may reduce settlement volatility following extreme events like the July 2025 storms that triggered $53 million in initial costs.

title: 5a Transmission & Dispatch Manual redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual redline update governs NYISO real-time dispatch, commitment, monitoring, and transmission operations — the core operational procedures that determine when and how hydro resources are called upon, constrained, and compensated for reliability services and energy delivery.
ai_summary: NYISO's redlined Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) updates operational procedures for real-time commitment, dispatch monitoring, transmission operations, and voltage/reactive power control. The manual covers reserve calculations, regulation requirements, corrective control strategies, and phase-angle regulator operations — all of which affect when hydro must respond, how its flexibility is valued in real-time dispatch, and revenue exposure to constraint relief and emergency operations. The document is in draft for Business Issues Committee discussion (BIC acceptance dated 04/15/2026, Operations Committee 04/16/2026) and effective 05/01/2026. Most directly material are sections on real-time commitment procedures (6.1), reserve and regulation requirements (3.1.4), and voltage/reactive support via STATCOM and SVC guidance (4.2.3) — all operational levers that determine whether hydro is selected for ancillary services or energy dispatch in competitive real-time markets.

title: 5b Motion
relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch Costs tariff revisions may alter dispatch procedures, cost allocation, and operational obligations for dispatchable resources including hydropower during weather emergencies — a category of dispatch instruction and settlement changes that affect revenue and compliance.
ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee recommends approval of tariff and manual changes for 'Storm Watch Redispatch Costs,' covering both Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff revisions and updates to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual. The motion advances the proposal to the Management Committee for further approval. The document does not specify the substance of the redispatch-cost changes, their impact on dispatch procedures or settlement, or affected resource classes; the presentation titled 'Storm Watch Redispatch Costs: Tariff and Manual Revisions' (referenced but not included here) would contain the operational and financial mechanism details necessary to assess whether hydro operators face new cost-allocation rules, dispatch constraints, or state-of-charge/operational mandates during storm conditions.

title: 6b Accounting & Billing Manual redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Settlement and billing manual redlines affect how hydro operators' energy and ancillary services revenues are calculated, metered, invoiced, and disputed—foundational operational and financial obligations.
stakeholders: NYISO Customer Settlements (New York Independent System Operator); Randy A. Bowers (New York Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: NYISO has issued a draft redline (version 7.0, effective 04/30/2026) of its Accounting and Billing Manual for Business Issues Committee approval (scheduled 06/10/2026). The manual governs settlement processing, invoicing schedules, meter data management, challenge and dispute procedures, and supplier settlements for energy (DAM and real-time), ancillary services, and demand response. Sections 4 and 5 detail energy and ancillary-services settlement mechanics for generators (including hydro and energy storage resources), encompassing Bid Production Cost Guarantees, balancing-market uplift, and voltage-support and regulation payments—the revenue streams on which hydro operators depend. Hydro operators and pumped-storage owners must track redline changes to settlement rules, metering timelines (including meter-data revision sunset provisions and resettlement procedures in section 1.4–1.5), and dispute deadlines, as errors in these procedures directly affect cash settlement.

title: 7 Proposed Changes to Attachment N of the ICAP Manual
relevance_reason: NYISO proposes seasonal translation factors for run-of-river hydro and intermittent resources affecting UCAP accreditation and capacity-requirement calculations in IRM/LCR studies, directly impacting hydro's RA value and seasonal revenue recognition.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes revisions to Attachment N of the ICAP Manual to establish separate summer and winter translation factors for Limited Control Run-of-River Hydro Resources and Intermittent Power Resources. Currently, translation factors (which convert Installed Capacity to Unforced Capacity for RA calculations) are computed using June–August production data; the proposal adds winter factors using December–February data, weighted by hourly LOLE factors from the most recent LCR study base case. The revision aligns with NYISO's Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC on 2/18/2026, effective 6/28/2026, targeted for 2027–2028 Capability Year implementation) and responds to observed winter LOLE in the 2026–2027 IRM study. The document also proposes a formula clarification to better document the current hourly LOLE-weighted calculation method. Run-of-river and seasonal-variable hydro owners will see their RA accreditation shift between seasons—winter production now counts toward winter capacity needs separately—which may raise or lower total RA credits depending on the seasonal production profile relative to winter LOLE weighting.

The attachment specifies the procedure by which hydro owners request and substantiate the calculation of these factors, affecting which hydro plants qualify as energy-limited resources (Attachment M) and how their seasonal or weather-dependent output profile is translated into a single UCAP value for RA showings. No comment deadline or next step is stated in the excerpt provided.
