title: 8 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Presentation
relevance_reason: 20-year NYISO transmission and resource outlook identifies firm dispatchable capacity requirements, transmission constraints affecting generator deliverability, and resource adequacy challenges in a high-decarbonization future — all determinants of hydro's system value, dispatch patterns, and investment conditions.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook projects electricity demand outpacing resource additions, with firm dispatchable capacity remaining essential across all decarbonization scenarios to provide energy, operating reserves, ramping, regulation, and voltage support during periods of low renewable output. The study identifies Central East and Northern New York transmission interfaces as bottlenecks constraining renewable deliverability and system efficiency; targeted dynamic voltage support upgrades are recommended. Coordinating the timing of resource retirements and new additions is flagged as critical to avoid tightening system conditions. The findings inform NYISO's biennial Comprehensive System Planning Process and identify scenarios for Economic Transmission Project Evaluation and Requested Economic Planning Studies. Board approval is scheduled for July 2026; the full report and 12 technical appendices (including capacity expansion and production cost simulations, renewable generation pocket analysis, and sensitivities) are available in the data catalog.

title: 8a 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Report
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly affects hydropower revenue through capacity-adequacy accreditation, generation-pocket curtailment risk, transmission congestion signals that alter locational value, and strategic location decisions for new capacity — all core inputs to hydro asset valuation and dispatch strategy in the Northeast.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook examines how New York's grid will evolve under multiple policy and demand scenarios through 2044, focusing on generation adequacy, resource-mix composition, and transmission constraints. The analysis finds that renewable generation concentration in upstate pockets creates curtailment risk (especially by 2035–2044) and drives bulk-transmission congestion through the Central East interface, with fossil-generation retirements and large-load growth (data centers, etc.) tightening system conditions and raising downstate capacity payments. For hydropower owners, this outlook establishes the transmission-planning baseline affecting deliverability and locational scarcity value; for pumped-storage operators, it identifies whether seasonal adequacy stress and interregional export opportunities (low-cost clean-energy export potential noted in 2035 scenarios) will raise PSH cycling revenue and RA accreditation premiums. The document is a draft for NYISO Business Issues Committee discussion (June 2026); no formal comment deadline is stated, but future comprehensive reliability plans and transmission procurements will flow from these scenarios.

title: 8d Draft Appendix B 2025-2044 Outlook Production Cost Model Assumptions Matrix - Redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's production-cost modeling assumptions directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources are valued in long-term resource and reliability planning; the document specifies how PSH dispatch, conventional hydro modeling, and battery-storage precedent shape the inputs to capacity-expansion models that drive NHA members' strategic options.
ai_summary: NYISO released a draft Production Cost Model Assumptions Matrix for its 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook, establishing modeling parameters for long-term reliability and resource-adequacy studies. The matrix specifies that conventional hydro (excluding Niagara) is modeled as hourly modifiers based on 5-year historic data with 2018 weather shapes; Niagara is modeled as a pondage unit with monthly energy targets; and pumped-storage hydro is modeled with endogenous optimization of pumping and dispatching. Battery storage similarly undergoes endogenous optimization by the production-cost model. These assumptions feed into capacity-expansion models that determine new-generation requirements and economic retirement risk by scenario (Base, Contract, and Policy cases with lockdown dates ranging 9/1/2025–10/31/2025). The document is a draft for NYISO Business Issues Committee discussion on June 10, 2026, and represents a critical methodological snapshot for how NYISO values existing and prospective PSH and conventional hydro in RA showings and long-run planning.

title: 8e Draft Appendix G 2025-2044 Outlook Production Cost Model Results - redline
relevance_reason: Production cost modeling of NYCA generation fleet, LBMPs, and energy storage utilization through 2044 directly informs hydro asset owners' long-term revenue outlook, reserve-margin tightness, and the competitive positioning of energy storage relative to conventional hydropower in the region.
ai_summary: NYISO's draft Appendix G to the 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook presents production cost model results across four scenarios (Base Case, Contract Case, and two Policy Cases) for study years 2030, 2035, 2040, and 2044. The modeling shows load growth concentrated in downstate zones (NYC and Long Island), with LBMPs rising across the study period and becoming sharply elevated in later years (2040–2044) under the Contract Case due to assumed fossil-generator retirements outpacing new renewable additions; Policy Cases with higher capacity additions substantially mitigate LBMP growth and reduce net imports. Energy storage utilization analysis reveals that battery capacity additions exhibit high zone-level correlation with utilization but experience diminishing marginal utilization per unit as total storage capacity rises, suggesting a saturation point in battery efficacy. This outlook is a reference document for NHA members modeling demand-side scarcity, transmission congestion effects on zonal pricing, and the substitution dynamics between storage and dispatchable hydro in a low-carbon NYCA.

title: 8f Draft Appendix I 2025-2044 Outlook Operational Analysis - redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year operational outlook characterizes hydropower's flexibility role and ramp-following capability relative to fossil generation and renewables, directly informing revenue opportunities for hydro's dispatchable and ramping services as the grid evolves under high-renewable scenarios.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 operational analysis (part of its System & Resource Outlook) models the generation fleet's hourly behavior under Base and Contract Cases, analyzing how different resource types respond to load and net-load (load minus intermittent renewables) changes across daily, hourly, and seasonal timescales. Hydro is characterized as serving both a baseline and dispatchable role with 'limited capability to respond to changes in load/net load' compared to fossil generation, which dominates ramp-following. The analysis shows hydro–load correlation is the second-strongest among generation types (after fossil), and hydro ramps track load ramps more reliably than weather-dependent renewables, positioning hydro as a secondary flexibility resource. This operational characterization — which NYISO will use to assess future resource needs and market design — affects how NYISO values hydro's ramp and ramping-product contributions and shapes transmission and flexibility adequacy planning that influences hydro owners' ability to monetize flexible output. Most exposed are hydro owners with ramp-capable resources; the analysis provides baseline data NYISO may use to set demand curves or capacity factors for flexibility products in forthcoming market-design reviews.

title: 8g Draft Appendix J 2025 - 2044 Outlook Renewable Generation Pockets - redline
relevance_reason: NYISO's renewable pocket analysis identifies transmission constraints affecting hydro deliverability in northern and western New York; hydro resources compete with wind and solar for shared transmission paths, directly impacting energy-delivery value and strategic interconnection/transmission planning.
ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook Appendix J analyzes renewable generation pockets and finds that hydro resources in Pockets W and X (western and northern New York) face rising curtailment and congestion in high-penetration renewable scenarios. Pocket X1 (St. Lawrence hydro corridor) exhibits the second-highest congestion among all pockets; hydro curtails before wind and solar because hydro is ineligible for Tier 1 RECs and therefore carries no negative-bid cost in the production-cost model, putting it at a competitive disadvantage when transmission is constrained. The analysis is draft (dated June 10, 2026 BIC) and used to inform longer-range transmission-upgrade planning; approved Phase 1 and Phase 2 transmission projects in Northern New York (Smart Path Connect) are expected to alleviate some congestion, but Policy Case scenarios with 13–35 GW of additional land-based renewable capacity by 2035 will intensify resource-concentration pressures on hydro energy deliverability.

title: 9 Order No. 1920 Long-Term Regional Transmission Planning
relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 transmission planning and cost allocation framework directly affects hydro deliverability, interconnection, and strategic transmission expansion that determines whether existing and new hydro resources can reach markets; long-term scenarios and resource-mix assumptions will drive transmission investment decisions that enable or constrain hydro export capability.
ai_summary: NYISO is implementing FERC Order 1920 (and subsequent clarifications 1920-A and 1920-B) through a redesigned Long-Term Transmission Planning Process that expands the 20-year planning horizon, incorporates New York Department of Public Service scenario input, and establishes an ex ante cost-allocation methodology for selected transmission projects. The process creates a new Catalog of Transmission Expansion Opportunities, solicits private-sector project proposals competing against NYISO baseline solutions using seven FERC-mandated benefit metrics (avoided reliability facilities, LOLE reduction, production cost savings, reduced losses, outage-congestion mitigation, extreme-weather resilience, and capacity benefits), and gates selection through NYPSC declaration of Long-Term Transmission Needs before cost recovery is allocated. The framework directly affects where transmission investment flows: hydro owners in New York face a new, longer-term planning horizon and competing non-wires alternatives that may or may not prioritize inter-area hydro exports versus local reliability; the cost-allocation methodology (details not yet finalized) determines whether transmission enabling hydro delivery is socialized regionally or assigned to specific beneficiaries. The compliance mechanics are active — NYISO Board approval of the planning structure is imminent, and the first Outlook cycle and PSC filings will begin shortly thereafter.

title: 2 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements – Seasonal UDR/EDR Elections and Must Offer Requirement: ICAP Manual and Attachment Revisions (Incremental Changes)
relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market design adds seasonal must-offer requirements and UDR/EDR elections that directly affect external resource commitments and penalties for underutilized capacity rights — a novel compliance obligation touching revenue and strategic flexibility for hydro and storage resources holding deliverability rights.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes seasonal (Summer/Winter) elections for Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest-of-State Deliverability Rights (EDRs) starting with the 2027-2028 Capability Year (effective May 1, 2027), filed with FERC February 18, 2026 in Docket ER26-1431 with requested effective date June 28, 2026. Holders must submit separate seasonal return-to-NYCA elections by August 1 prior to each capability year; capacity associated with external UDRs/EDRs not returned must be offered in ICAP spot auctions or certified for LSE compliance, with sanctions for failure — including a revised must-offer penalty calculated monthly. This reframes deliverability-rights holders' compliance posture: seasonal elections create a new obligation to commit or forfeit, converting a flexible holding into a binary choice with penalties. Pumped storage and large reservoir hydro holding external rights face the most direct impact; small hydro with external positions must track August elections and ensure compliance or incur monthly penalties.

title: 3 Highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report for the NYISO Markets
relevance_reason: NYISO Market Monitoring Unit analysis of energy price formation, ancillary-services co-optimization, shortage pricing (ORDC), reserve-requirement allocation, and demand-response settlement rules that directly affect hydro revenue through energy and reserve pricing, uplift mechanisms, and locational scarcity signals.
stakeholders: Pallas LeeVanSchaick (Potomac Economics (NYISO Market Monitoring Unit))
ai_summary: Potomac Economics (NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit) presents highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report addressing energy and ancillary-services market performance. Key findings: energy prices rose 66–79% in 2025 due to higher gas prices, peak loads, reduced OOM commitment, increased exports to Quebec, and reduced hydro availability; the MMU identifies $221M in misallocated costs annually, including $40M from statewide allocation of local reserve charges and $38M from largest contingencies not paying their impact on reserve requirements; the MMU recommends urgent reforms to shortage pricing (Rec #2017-2: raising NYCA 30-min ORDC to $2000/MWh up to 150% of largest contingency) to prevent economically unjustified exports to ISO-NE and PJM, and to dynamically adjust reserve requirements and model locational constraints in day-ahead commitment. Hydro operators benefit from higher scarcity pricing and better locational reserve signals, but face exposure to large downward corrections if demand-response baseline issues and cryptocurrency-miner over-consumption (paying full LMP despite uneconomic curtailment) are fixed; the report documents $34M annual uplift from paying demand response as if it generates electricity, which competes with traditional ramping and flexibility revenue.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market and manual updates touching accreditation, intermittency treatment, and modeling rules that directly affect hydro and storage revenue and dispatch obligations.
stakeholders: Amanda Myott (NYISO); Ekene Umeike (NYISO); Sushant Varghese (NYISO); Vijay Kaki (NYISO); Potomac Economics (Potomac Economics)
ai_summary: NYISO Installed Capacity Working Group meeting (June 2, 2026) will address four rule changes: DER market-participation enhancements, ICAP Manual Attachment N updates, revised treatment of intermittency in capacity accreditation, and improved duct-firing modeling for fossil units. The intermittency revisions are most material — they modify how NYISO accredits variable and run-of-river hydro in the capacity market, and how storage is modeled in the reserve-margin calculation. Manual updates will be presented by Sushant Varghese and Vijay Kaki; Potomac Economics will discuss 2025 State of the Market findings, which often include capacity-accreditation trends and hydro-performance analysis. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda.

title: 2 DER Market Enhancements
relevance_reason: DER aggregation participation rules directly affect small hydropower owners' ability to participate in NYISO markets under Order 2222 frameworks; the DADSR concept and proposed telemetry/accreditation changes alter the strategic options and compliance burden for hydro resources enrolled in DER aggregations.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes a Day-Ahead Demand-Side Resource (DADSR) variant within its existing DER & Aggregation participation model, designed to accommodate DSR with longer start-up times and limited real-time flexibility. DADSR would bid day-ahead, lock to a fixed real-time schedule, and be ineligible for operating reserves or regulation service; it would qualify for ICAP participation with 2–24-hour energy duration limitations and potentially reduced telemetry requirements (6-second with 30-second scan rates for sub-1 MW facilities versus current standards for 1+ MW). Small hydropower and run-of-river resources enrolled in DER aggregations face a strategic choice: participation under current DER rules (with full telemetry/ancillary-service obligations) or under DADSR (simpler telemetry, day-ahead certainty, but loss of regulation/reserve revenue and real-time flexibility upside). NYISO is also evaluating MNBT and ECBL enhancements and the 10 kW threshold; stakeholder discussion continues at the July ICAPWG/MIWG meeting.

title: 3 ICAP Manual Attachment N Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO is revising Attachment N methodology for calculating translation factors that convert nameplate capacity to unforced capacity (UCAP) for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources, directly affecting hydro accreditation in resource adequacy and capacity-requirement studies.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes revisions to ICAP Manual Attachment N to implement seasonal (summer and winter) translation factors for intermittent resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro, replacing the current summer-only methodology. The change responds to observed winter Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) beginning in the 2026–2027 IRM study and supports the Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC 2/18/2026, target implementation 5/1/2027 for the 2027–2028 Capability Year). The proposal also corrects the availability-factor formula to apply proper weighted averaging, aligning calculation with current implementation in IRM, LCR, and deliverability studies. For run-of-river hydro owners, this means winter availability will now drive a separate winter UCAP translation factor based on December–February production patterns weighted by winter LOLE distribution, potentially improving winter accreditation if winter production is high relative to winter scarcity hours, or reducing it if winter is a dry season; the revision will be presented to the Business Issue Committee on 6/10/2026.

title: 3a ICAP Appendix N redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Attachment N directly governs how NYISO calculates capacity accreditation for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources—a core revenue determinant—and the redline signals revision to those translation-factor procedures.
ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products is revising Appendix N of the Installed Capacity Manual (draft for discussion, issued June 2, 2026). Attachment N contains the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro, which convert nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (the quantity offered into NYISO's ICAP auction). Changes to these translation factors directly alter the megawatt-accreditation—and thus auction revenue—that run-of-river and variable hydro operators can claim. The document is in draft-for-discussion phase with the Installed Capacity Working Group; the redline indicates active revision, though no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

title: 3b ICAP Appendix N clean
relevance_reason: Attachment N establishes the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources in NYISO's capacity market accreditation, directly affecting how hydro capacity is credited and valued in NYISO's ICAP auctions.
ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual Attachment N specifies the methodology for calculating translation factors that convert nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (UCAP) for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro. This accreditation procedure determines how much capacity credit variable hydro can claim in NYISO's ICAP auctions and capacity showings. The document also references Attachment M (Energy Limited Resource and Energy Duration Limitation procedures) and Attachment D (Dependable Maximum Net Capability test procedures including a hydro-generation form), indicating NYISO uses performance-data-driven methods to credential hydro's contribution to resource adequacy. For run-of-river and variable-output hydro operators in NYISO, the translation-factor calculation directly sets the capacity value realized in auctions and affects their RA contribution and revenue.

title: 4 Balancing Intermittency Manual Updates
relevance_reason: NYISO's Uncertainty Reserve Requirement (URR) design directly affects how intermittent resources and ramping/flexibility are procured and priced in both day-ahead and real-time markets, creating new reserve products that hydro will be called upon to supply and creating new demand curves that establish payment terms for flexibility hydro traditionally provides.
ai_summary: NYISO is finalizing manual revisions to implement its Uncertainty Reserve Requirement (URR), a new 24-hour reserve product designed to hedge forecast uncertainty for net load, land-based wind, offshore wind, and front-of-meter solar. The design was accepted by FERC in July 2025 (ER25-1998) and is scheduled for deployment in August 2026. The updated manuals clarify URR calculation methodology (incorporated in new Attachment D to the Ancillary Services Manual), specify that URR can be met using 30-Minute Reserve in the day-ahead market, introduce locational demand curves with a new Long Island requirement row (270 MW and 540 MW separately), and refine operating-capacity accounting for seasonal differences. Stakeholder votes are scheduled for June 10–11, 2026 (BIC and OC); comments can be submitted at the June 4 SOAS meeting. For hydro owners, URR creates both a revenue opportunity (new procurement of a flexibility product hydro naturally supplies) and a compliance obligation (must-offer rules in the 30-minute reserve product that backs URR); pumped-storage facilities are particularly positioned to capture value from locational reserve requirements, especially on Long Island where the demand curve now explicitly separates 270 MW and 540 MW tiers.

title: 4a T&D Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual revision affects hydro compliance obligations: reserve calculation, regulation requirements, outage scheduling, dispatch procedures, and real-time commitment processes that directly govern how hydropower units are monitored, operated, and settled in the market.
ai_summary: NYISO is issuing a revised Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) effective May 1, 2026, following committee acceptance on April 15–16, 2026. The manual covers operational monitoring (real-time data, security assessment, reserve and regulation requirements), transmission operations (operating limits, voltage control, phase angle regulators), real-time commitment and mitigation processes, and scheduling procedures. Hydro operators must comply with the manual's reserve calculation methodology, regulation-requirement definition, outage scheduling processes, and real-time dispatch instruction protocols—changes to any of these affect when hydro units are called on, how their output is metered and settled, and the operational flexibility they must maintain. The document's status as draft-for-discussion suggests revisions are still in circulation; the June 2, 2026 Installed Capacity Working Group meeting will likely discuss final language.

title: 4b Day Ahead Scheduling Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: Day-ahead scheduling manual revisions affect hydro's energy commitment rules, storage constraint evaluation processes, and ancillary service demand curve participation—core elements of revenue and operational flexibility in the day-ahead market.
ai_summary: NYISO has issued revisions to its Day-Ahead Scheduling Manual (Manual 11, effective 10/29/2024, accepted by BIC 08/14/2024 and OC 08/15/2024). The manual governs SCUC initialization, unit commitment of dispatchable resources including hydropower, energy storage resource constraint evaluation, ancillary service demand curves, and bilateral transaction evaluations that directly shape day-ahead market dispatch and pricing. For hydro owners, key mechanics documented include scheduling of 'must run' resources, multiple response rates for resources, energy storage limits constraints, and limiting participation constraints—all of which define the operational envelope within which hydro's dispatch and AS offerings are optimized. Hydro and PSH operators rely on these rules to understand how their bids enter unit commitment and how their flexibility is valued in the scheduling process.

title: 4c Ancillary Services Manual Revisions
relevance_reason: NYISO's Ancillary Services Manual revisions directly govern how hydropower and pumped storage are compensated and must perform for regulation, frequency response, voltage support, and operating reserves — the core revenue streams and operational obligations for dispatchable hydro and PSH.
stakeholders: NYISO Operations Engineering (New York Independent System Operator)
ai_summary: NYISO issued a draft Ancillary Services Manual (v10.0, effective May 1, 2026) with revisions to regulation and frequency response service, voltage support, operating reserves, and energy imbalance rules. The manual specifies qualification criteria, performance-tracking methods, settlement mechanics, and testing procedures for generators, synchronous condensers, HVDC with voltage-source converter (VSC) technology, and energy storage resources providing these services. Hydro and PSH operators must comply with reactive capability testing protocols (section 3.6), regulation service prequalification and performance audits (section 4.11), and operating-reserve supplier eligibility and day-ahead/real-time bidding rules (sections 6.2–6.4). This is a working draft for discussion at the Installed Capacity Working Group (committee acceptance 04/15/2026, OC acceptance 04/16/2026); the effective date is May 1, 2026, making this a compliance-critical update for all NYISO hydro and storage participants.

title: 6 2025 State of the Market Report
relevance_reason: NYISO's 2025 State of the Market Report addresses capacity-market design, seasonal accreditation, resource-adequacy signals, and locational pricing — all mechanisms that directly shape revenue for hydro and pumped-storage resources in NYISO and influence investment and retention decisions.
stakeholders: Joe Coscia (NYISO Market Monitoring Unit / Potomac Economics)
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit (Potomac Economics) identifies structural gaps in the capacity market that undervalue reliability contributions and distort investment incentives. Key findings: (1) demand curves based on 2-hour battery cost understate Net CONE, risking premature retirements even as load grows; (2) seasonal incentives are misaligned—firm fuel/UDR elections locked 16 months in advance don't respond to winter market prices, and elected capacity for 2026/27 (15.2 GW) exceeds planning assumptions (12.3 GW), creating cost-allocation risk; (3) transmission security is undercompensated—downstate LCRs increasingly bind at floor, overpaying bottled generation (SCRs, large units) and underpaying non-firm gas units while creating $74M annual inefficiency post-CHPE. Recommendations filed or pending include raising the demand-curve floor (Rec. 2025-1a), moving seasonal-election deadlines closer to winter (Rec. 2022-2), introducing locational CAFs (Rec. 2025-2), and expanding capacity zones. These touch pumped-storage accreditation (2-hour battery CONE undermines longer-duration PSH value), seasonal energy adequacy (firm fuel elections timing), and locational value recognition (hydro deliverability and transmission-security contribution). No formal comment deadline is stated in this highlights presentation, but the full 2025 SOM Report (posted May 19, 2026) and tariff filing ER26-1431 are the operative documents.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: ICAP (capacity market) surplus-offer rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources bid and earn capacity revenue; this agenda item flags an active NYISO discussion and vote on capacity-market mechanics.
stakeholders: Afreen Vahora (NYISO); Emilie Nelson (NYISO); Matt Antonio (NYISO); Rich Dewey (NYISO); Potomac Economics (Potomac Economics)
ai_summary: NYISO's Management Committee will vote on changes to automatic ICAP surplus-offer rules on May 27, 2026. The mechanism affects how capacity is offered into NYISO's capacity market when resources have surplus capacity beyond their committed obligations — a setting that shapes net-revenue outcomes for hydro and PSH that participate in ICAP. The specific proposal details are not disclosed in the agenda, but this is a live vote item; no comment deadline is stated in the document.

title: 1 Agenda
relevance_reason: The document addresses ICAP (Installed Capacity) market rules and winter reliability enhancements that directly affect hydropower's capacity accreditation and resource adequacy participation in NYISO.
stakeholders: Julia Popova (NYISO); Michael Swider (NYISO); Tiffany Sharp (NYISO)
ai_summary: This agenda covers ICAP Manual revisions related to winter reliability capacity enhancements, which are directly relevant to how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are accredited for capacity value and contribute to resource adequacy in NYISO. Changes to ICAP rules materially affect hydro participation and compensation.

title: 2a Operations Report
relevance_reason: Hydro asset owners use monthly operational metrics to understand system conditions affecting their revenue (scarcity pricing opportunity during stress events, curtailment risk, interchange patterns) and obligations (reserve activations, load-following demand). This April 2026 report includes hydro outage data, net-load-ramp trends affecting flexibility value, wind/solar forecast performance and curtailment (competitive pressure on hydro's flexibility premium), and transmission changes (CHPE MTF, Smart Path Connect) affecting hydro deliverability and inter-regional pricing.
ai_summary: NYISO's April 2026 operations report tracks system reliability and market outcomes. Hydro-relevant findings: (1) Monthly generation mix shows 6,324 MW hydro nameplate (including pumped storage); (2) Hydro and other resources showed measurable day-ahead capacity unavailability in real time (charted but values not extracted due to daily volatility); (3) Net load ramps driven by wind and solar variability remained significant (15–35 MW hourly average), sustaining demand for flexible ramping; (4) Wind and FTM solar curtailment totaled 1.5% system-wide in April (wind 8,453 MWh, FTM solar 2,486 MWh), indicating transmission constraints that compress energy value for all resources in congested zones; (5) Champlain Hudson Power Express (CHPE MTF) merchant transmission completed integration testing in late April and began commissioning, with Smart Path Connect incrementally entering service throughout the month—these transmission additions will improve hydro export paths from Quebec and reduce NY congestion costs. No member action required; document is reference material for understanding April revenue drivers and seasonal resource-adequacy conditions.

title: 3 Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO is revising the ICAP market's surplus-offer rules to automatically resell capacity that load-serving entities and ICAP suppliers procure but do not need, at zero-dollar bids. For hydropower and storage owners selling capacity into NYISO's ICAP market—whether as primary capacity suppliers or via bilateral trades—this rule removes their option to withhold surplus capacity and directly affects the monetization path and price realization for unsold UCAP.
ai_summary: NYISO proposes a new tariff section (5.14.4) that automatically submits zero-dollar offers for any Unforced Capacity (UCAP) surplus held by load-serving entities, ICAP suppliers, and ICAP marketers after the monthly certification deadline, effective May 1, 2027 (Board approval and FERC filing required). Currently, surplus UCAP often goes unoffered, potentially raising market-clearing prices; the automatic zero-dollar offer guarantees resale at the auction clearing price. Hydropower and pumped-storage owners—particularly those acquiring capacity via bilateral trades or selling excess UCAP—will face mandatory resale of any certified surplus, eliminating the ability to withhold capacity or negotiate off-market prices for unexpectedly available MW; however, capacity associated with a physical transmission interface (PTID) and 'unoffered MW' remain subject to physical-withholding rules and are excluded from automatic resale.

title: 3a 2026 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements: ICAP Manual Revisions - Presentation
relevance_reason: This document addresses ICAP capacity market rules, seasonal capacity requirements, and deliverability rights (UDRs/EDRs) that directly affect how hydropower and energy storage resources participate in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO is proposing seasonal ICAP minimum requirements, seasonal elections for Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest of State Deliverability Rights (EDRs), and must-offer requirements effective May 1, 2027. These changes establish distinct Summer and Winter capability periods and impose penalties for failure to offer or certify capacity, which affect how dispatchable resources like hydropower and pumped-storage can deliver capacity across seasonal variations and transmission constraints.

title: 3a MST 5.14 redline
relevance_reason: ICAP capacity-market accreditation and demand-curve methodology directly affect hydro and PSH revenue from capacity markets, particularly the periodic review process that sets accreditation reference points and the demand-curve adjustments that drive cleared capacity prices.
ai_summary: NYISO's tariff Section 5.14 establishes the ICAP Spot Market Auction mechanics and demand-curve parameters that clear monthly unforced-capacity obligations. The document redlines the periodic review methodology (every four years, next review for 2025/2026 capability year), which assesses the levelized cost of a peaking plant, net energy and ancillary-services revenue offsets, and seasonal differences in capacity availability. Beginning with 2025/2026, demand curves will account for seasonal variations in capacity availability—summer and winter curves now reflect different excess-capacity assumptions. The 2025/2026 NYCA summer curve peaks at $21.69/kW-month; winter at $16.39/kW-month, with locational adders (NYC summer peak $41.30). Hydro and PSH capacity accreditation is measured against these ICAP demand curves translated to unforced-capacity terms; seasonal demand-curve splits will alter how seasonal hydro availability and storage round-trip efficiency factor into capacity showings, potentially reducing winter accreditation if available supply diverges seasonally from the reference peaking-plant assumption.

title: 3b Attachment Q - Draft
relevance_reason: Document establishes capacity accreditation and penalty calculation methodologies for external unforced capacity deliverability rights, which directly affects how hydropower and other resources are valued and incentivized in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: This attachment details UCAP calculations for External UDRs/EDRs using capacity accreditation factors, forced outage rates, and derating factors—mechanisms that apply to hydropower resources delivering capacity over external transmission interfaces. The must-offer requirement and associated penalties (1.5× spot market price) create compliance obligations for hydropower operators holding external deliverability rights.

title: 3b Motion
relevance_reason: NYISO proposes automatic ICAP (capacity) surplus offer rules, which directly affects how capacity accreditation and surplus management operate—a core revenue mechanism for hydro and PSH capacity participation in NYISO.
ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee approves revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing automatic ICAP surplus offers, with the Board of Directors to authorize filing under FPA Section 205. The proposal changes the rules by which capacity surplus is offered into the market automatically, affecting how hydro and pumped-storage units manage their capacity accreditation obligations and surplus positioning—particularly relevant for PSH operators managing state-of-charge and capacity ratings. The full tariff language and detailed mechanism are presented in the accompanying slide deck 'Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers' (May 27, 2026); the proposal moves to Board filing stage pending Board authorization.

title: 3c ICAP Manual Revisions - Draft Redline
relevance_reason: This NYISO Installed Capacity Manual addresses capacity accreditation, energy duration limitations, and specific provisions for co-located storage resources—all directly affecting hydropower and pumped-storage participation in the NYCA capacity market.
ai_summary: The manual establishes ICAP requirements, unforced capacity calculations, and duration adjustment factors applicable to installed capacity suppliers. Section 4.1.4 explicitly addresses co-located storage resources, and section 4.1.1 covers energy duration limitations and adjustment factors critical to how pumped-storage hydropower is accredited in NYISO's capacity market.

title: 4 2025 State of the Market Report
relevance_reason: NYISO's 2025 State of the Market Report analyzes energy and ancillary services pricing, capacity market performance and locational incentives, demand response, transmission planning, and external transactions—all material to hydro and PSH revenue signals, obligations, and competitive position in the Northeast market.
stakeholders: David B. Patton (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit); Jie Chen (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit); Joseph Coscia (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit); Pallas LeeVanSchaick (Potomac Economics / NYISO Market Monitoring Unit)
ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit releases its 2025 annual State of the Market Report, covering wholesale and retail costs, energy and ancillary services market performance, capacity market results and locational pricing assessment, transmission congestion management, demand response, and competitive dynamics. The report includes analysis of fossil and non-fossil generator performance during peak conditions, assessment of transmission security limits' impact on capacity payments, evaluation of excessive qualification of conventional generators, and critique of the large load interconnection process. For hydropower and PSH owners, this report provides the baseline market-monitor assessment of (1) whether ancillary services pricing is adequate to support reservoir and storage operations, (2) locational capacity-market signals and whether transmission constraints are suppressing payments in hydro-rich zones, (3) performance metrics that may inform cost-of-service or mitigation rules, and (4) findings on demand-response and load-growth trends that shift the value of flexibility and scarcity-price exposure. The document is posted to NYISO's Management Committee agenda; no specific vote or comment deadline is stated in the excerpt.

title: 4a 2025 State of the Market Highlights
relevance_reason: The document directly addresses hydropower availability impacts on NYISO energy prices, capacity market performance affecting generation investment incentives, and ancillary services market design—all critical to hydro operations and revenue.
stakeholders: Pallas LeeVanSchaick, Ph.D. (Potomac Economics (NYISO Market Monitoring Unit))
ai_summary: The 2025 State of the Market Report identifies reduced hydropower generation availability as a driver of 2025 energy price increases (66–79%), and highlights planning-market gaps and low net revenues for flexible generation types that affect hydro investment incentives. The report recommends capacity market reforms and improved energy/ancillary services price formation to enhance incentives for flexible and available units, which are essential for hydro profitability and retention.

title: Final Motion
relevance_reason: NYISO approves revisions to automatic ICAP surplus offers, which directly affects capacity accreditation and revenue for dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities participating in NYISO's capacity market.
ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee unanimously approved revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers on May 27, 2026, with the Board of Directors now authorized to file the tariff revisions under FPA Section 205. The mechanism affects how ICAP (Installed Capacity) surplus—the cushion above actual resource adequacy requirement—is offered and priced in the capacity market. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators rely on ICAP accreditation for capacity revenue; changes to surplus-offer rules can shift the timing, volume, or price-formation signals that determine when and how much capacity-constrained resources are called upon, altering the effective capacity value and bidding strategy for hydro and PSH assets.

title: 03 2026 RNA: Scenario Assumptions

title: 04c - Order 1920 Compliance - Comments from NRDC and Earthjustice
