title: Item 01a – Ron Mcnamara Phd Presentation
relevance_reason: Addresses PJM capacity adequacy and investment dynamics in a scarcity-driven environment shaped by data-center load growth and accelerated retirements — directly affecting hydro revenue through capacity-market pricing, resource-adequacy constructs, and the strategic value of dispatchable generation.
stakeholders: Ron McNamara, PhD (First Principles Economics, LLC)
ai_summary: Ron McNamara (First Principles Economics) presents a conceptual framework for PJM's capacity adequacy problem to the PRTMDW, arguing the root issue is inadequate investment, not market design per se. He emphasizes that restructured markets eliminated the guaranteed cost-recovery and stable counterparty that regulation provided, forcing capacity investment to compete as economic/commercial ventures bearing price, policy, and rule-change risks — a fundamentally different risk profile than pre-restructuring. McNamara recommends PJM adopt an agreed end-state vision (5–20 year horizon) against which all capacity proposals should be evaluated, with preferences for decentralized solutions and accelerated permitting/build timelines. The presentation does not propose specific rule changes but frames the investment-scarcity nexus as central to hydro's strategic positioning: in a market now "managing scarcity," the value of reliable, dispatchable generation (hydro's signature) rises, but only if the market and regulatory framework create stable revenue streams and counterparties for that capacity.

title: Item 01b – Revisiting Root Causes And Their Implications In Pjms Powering Reliability Through Market Design – Pa Oca Presentation
relevance_reason: Addresses PJM's resource-adequacy crisis driven by data-center load growth and proposes alternatives to price-based solutions, directly affecting hydro's role in meeting reliability via capacity accreditation, pricing mechanisms, and interconnection/transmission planning for new resources.
stakeholders: Frank A. Felder (Independent Electricity Consultants); Mel El Atieh (Pennsylvania Office of Consumer Advocate (PA OCA))
ai_summary: PA OCA argues that PJM's May 6th 'Powering Reliability Through Market Design' paper misdiagnoses the root cause of resource adequacy as price signals, when the real problem is the speed and scale of data-center interconnection outpacing infrastructure capacity. PA OCA rejects higher energy, capacity, and ancillary-service prices as the solution and recommends instead administrative approaches: mandatory data-center cost-sharing for new capacity, bilateral contracts, interconnection and transmission queue management, and ELCC reforms. The recommendation is preliminary; PA OCA requests PJM release bilateral-contracting feedback from suppliers and large loads to inform next steps. This reframing bypasses the price-centric architecture that would normally raise revenue for all dispatchable resources, including hydro, and instead emphasizes infrastructure buildout and queue/transmission reform—directly affecting which resources get built, when, and how their capacity is valued in RA frameworks.

title: Item 04b – Bifurcated Capacity Market Under PJM Path A Option 2 – Michael Kurtz Presentation
relevance_reason: Proposal to bifurcate PJM's capacity market into separate auctions for existing and new generation directly affects revenue certainty, accreditation mechanics, and pricing for dispatchable resources including hydropower in a scarcity environment.
ai_summary: Michael Kurtz (Ohio Energy Group) presents a proposal to modify PJM Path A, Option 2 by creating a bifurcated capacity market: a "Legacy Auction" continuing short-term (one-year) Base Residual Auction pricing with caps and floors for existing generation, and a separate "Reliability Auction" with uncapped, long-term pricing for new generation. The proposal addresses PJM's transition from surplus to scarcity management—the December 2025 BRA cleared at the $333/MW-day cap with 6,516 MW UCAP below reliability requirement—but avoids paying existing generators the premium long-term rates needed to attract new merchant capacity, instead using RMR authority and flexible pricing floors to retain older dispatchable units. Existing hydro and dispatchable resources would remain on short-term capacity pricing with caps, avoiding windfall effects, while new merchant capacity would access long-term revenue certainty in a separate clearing; a blended wholesale price across both auctions would flow to retail markets. The proposal implicitly treats all dispatchable generation (including existing hydro) as reliability units warranting cost-of-service or floor protection during scarcity, and no vote or comment deadline is stated in the presentation.

title: Item 04b – Michael Kurtz Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM's proposed bifurcated capacity market directly affects how existing hydro and PSH generators are paid for capacity — the proposal would maintain short-term pricing with caps/floors for existing resources (including hydro) while creating uncapped long-term auctions for new entry, fundamentally altering existing-resource revenue certainty and scarcity pricing signals in a resource-constrained era.
ai_summary: Michael Kurtz (Boehm, Kurtz & Lowry) presents a proposal to modify PJM's Path A, Option 2 capacity-market reform by creating a bifurcated market: a 'Legacy Auction' with one-year auctions and maintained price caps/floors for existing generation (projected 95.4% of capacity), and a 'Reliability Auction' with no price caps/floors for new generation (projected 4.6%), with blended pricing passed to non-Reliability-Backstop consumers. The proposal is framed as a middle path between PJM's conclusion that even $625/MW-day may be insufficient to attract new entry (versus real-world CC financing needing $633–$2,070/MW-day) and the risk of paying existing generators the premium needed for new construction, which Kurtz argues would constitute a windfall and violate the just-and-reasonable standard under the Federal Power Act. Existing hydro and pumped-storage generators would remain under the capped short-term regime, preserving their current rate treatment but constraining their upside if scarcity pricing rises; the proposal is presented to PJM's PRTMDW (Pricing, Risk and Transmission Market Design Working Group) on July 15, 2026, and reflects ongoing PJM resource-adequacy tensions triggered by supply-demand gaps and data-center load growth.

title: Item 04c – Initial Considerations And Recommendations – Maryland Opc Presentation
relevance_reason: Directly addresses resource adequacy design and capacity market constructs affecting how PJM procures capacity, with implications for how hydro and storage resources are valued in reliability frameworks and compete against large-load-driven procurement signals.
stakeholders: Matt Hoyt (Exeter Associates, Inc); Bill Fields (Maryland OPC); Phil Sussler (Maryland OPC)
ai_summary: Maryland OPC (Offices of People's Counsel) presents initial recommendations for redesigning PJM's resource adequacy construct, arguing that uncertain large-load growth is artificially inflating capacity procurement requirements and cost allocation. The presentation proposes three design paths—(A) risk allocation, (B) reliability consequences, (C) E&AS reform incentives—and an alternative "Commit-to-be-counted" model requiring large loads to make verifiable capacity commitments, fund/bring new capacity, or accept priority curtailment before being added to shared RA obligations. This reframes the capacity market's demand side from forecasts to binding commitments, directly affecting which resources (dispatchable hydro, PSH, and storage) compete for RA accreditation and what price signals they face when loads have uncertain, non-binding demand profiles. The recommendation prioritizes cost-causation allocation—meaning new large loads, not ordinary customers, absorb reliability and cost risk—which can shift the investment incentive landscape for existing hydro and storage resources competing in capacity auctions.

title: Agenda Doc
relevance_reason: Manual 14D revision addresses Generator Operational Requirements aligned with NERC Reliability Standards, which directly affects hydro units' operational obligations, outage scheduling, and compliance requirements.
stakeholders: Becky Davis (PJM); D. Mroz (PJM); David Kimmel (PJM); Gizella Mali (PJM); Jim Gluck (PJM); John Wolff (PJM); Lauren Strella Wahba (PJM); Madalin How (PJM); Matt Wharton (PJM); Michael Olaleye (PJM); Mike Kelly (PJM); Paul Dajewski (PJM); Ryan Nice (PJM); Timothy Smoker (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Operating Committee will be asked to endorse proposed revisions to Manual 14D: Generator Operational Requirements on July 9, 2026, aimed at alignment with NERC Reliability Standards. The revision touches the compliance obligations hydro operators must manage — specifically generator operational standards, outage scheduling, and resource readiness — which are fundamental duties for any dispatchable generator in PJM. Affected parties are all PJM-interconnected hydro and pumped-storage units; the endorsement vote occurs at the July 9, 2026 Operating Committee meeting.

title: Draft Minutes – OC – 6426
relevance_reason: Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA) Phase II proposal directly affects pumped-storage hydro revenue and strategic options by establishing how storage resources are valued and compensated for transmission-service provision in PJM; regulation market redesign and synchronous reserve performance updates touch ancillary-service products hydro supplies.
stakeholders: Abhishek Ghosh (Constellation Energy Generation, LLC); Joe Bowring (Monitoring Analytics); David Kimmel (PJM Interconnection); David Mroz (PJM Interconnection); Ilyana Dropkin (PJM Interconnection); Jeff Schmitt (PJM Interconnection); Shubhrajit Bhattacharjee (PJM Interconnection)
ai_summary: PJM's Operating Committee endorsed the PJM/Constellation Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA) Phase II proposal (Package A) on June 4, 2026, with 72% support versus the Monitoring Analytics alternative (Package B), which received only 13.5% support. SATA Phase II restructures how energy-storage resources — including pumped-storage hydro — earn revenue for deferring or avoiding transmission infrastructure investment, establishing whether storage dispatch can be optimized jointly with transmission needs and what compensation mechanisms apply. The endorsement moves the proposal toward implementation; details on how pumped-storage facilities will be accredited, when charging/discharging is compensated, and whether PSH gains preferential treatment versus battery storage remain in design phases. Owners with existing or planned PSH in PJM should monitor the Phase II rulebook for charging-energy treatment, state-of-charge operational rules, and whether SATA compensation stacks with or replaces energy and ancillary-service revenues. Regulation market bifurcation simulations and synchronous-reserve performance reviews (Items 8, 9, 12) indicate PJM is stress-testing the reserve products on which hydro dispatch depends; results will be presented in follow-up meetings.

title: Item 01b – 2026 OC Work Plan
relevance_reason: The work plan schedules reviews of PJM manuals governing generator operational requirements, balancing operations, emergency operations, and system restoration—all touching hydro obligations for ride-through, outage scheduling, dispatch compliance, and emergency support.
ai_summary: PJM Operating Committee's 2026 work plan schedules manual reviews spanning operational standards, generator requirements, and reliability coordination. Manual 14D (Generator Operational Requirements) and Manual 12 (Balancing Operations) directly govern hydro dispatch obligations, reserve performance standards, and emergency procedures; Manual 13 (Emergency Operations) and Manual 36 (System Restoration) touch black-start and frequency-response accreditation criteria. The plan also includes recurring items on Frequency Response Review, Regulation Performance Updates, and Synchronous Reserve Updates—all mechanisms on which hydro revenue and compliance depend. No comment deadline or vote date is stated; this is a forward calendar rather than a substantive proposal.

title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM is mandating stricter reactive power/voltage support performance standards for all synchronous generators, including hydroelectric units, with new enforcement mechanisms and D-curve requirements that directly affect hydro's compliance obligations and the economic parameters of voltage-support revenue.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14d Revision 71 tightens reactive power (VAR) control requirements for all generators in response to NERC's May 2026 lowering of IBR registration thresholds and the 2025 Spain blackout. The revision mandates that generators operate in 'automatic controlling voltage' mode (AVR in constant-voltage control) unless documented exceptions are approved by PJM and the Transmission Owner; defines volt-var control boundaries (reactive output limits of ±32.87% of maximum MW across a narrow voltage deadband); specifies minimum and maximum MW thresholds for reactive capability; and introduces a three-party (PJM–Transmission Owner–Generator Owner) investigative process for reactive-control performance issues. Hydroelectric facilities must comply with these new D-curve and reactive-capability specifications. The revision is a PJM Operating Committee presentation (status: approval sought), with no explicit comment deadline stated in this excerpt, but the urgency language ('urgent need to proceed efficiently') suggests near-term enforcement.

title: Item 02 – 2 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: OBLIGATION: Updates to PJM Manual 14d for generator operational requirements, including reactive power (VAR) control and AVR mode specifications, impose new compliance requirements on all synchronous generators including conventional hydro and pumped-storage units that must adhere to reactive-power and voltage-support standards.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D (Generator Operational Requirements), effective July 23, 2026, to incorporate NERC Category 2 GO standards and clarify reactive-power (VAR) and automatic voltage regulator (AVR) requirements for generators. The revision addresses PJM's stated operational challenges: rising penetration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) and load growth outpacing generation, both increasing voltage risk. The manual update adds new references to NERC Category GO, specifies allowable AVR modes, provides graphs of acceptable volt-var and watt-var control ranges, and codifies PJM's monitoring and engagement protocols with generator operators. Conventional hydro and pumped-storage units with synchronous generation capability will become subject to these tightened reactive-power performance standards and monitoring obligations. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

title: Item 02 – 3 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14D specifies operational requirements, dispatch procedures, metering/data exchange, and ancillary-services participation rules affecting all generators, including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D Revision 71 (effective June 30, 2026) defines generator operational requirements across interconnection, control-center standards, data exchange, market participation, and dispatch procedures. Relevant to hydro: Section 2 covers generation-owner responsibilities and transfers; Section 5 governs ancillary-services participation and market tools; Section 7 specifies dispatch procedures, switching, and synchronization rules; Section 10 details black-start procurement (a key hydro revenue stream); and Sections 12–13 address mixed-technology and solar forecasting (relevant as precedent for storage-hybrid treatment). Section 4 establishes metering and synchrophasor requirements affecting settlement accuracy. This is an operational-compliance baseline document for all PJM generators; the redline revision likely reflects recent tariff or reliability-standard changes. No specific comment deadline or vote date is evident in the excerpt provided.

title: Item 04 – Hot Weather Update – Presentation
relevance_reason: Emergency procedures, load forecasting accuracy, and net-load formation during extreme weather directly affect hydro's dispatch obligations, revenue capture during peak scarcity, and the value of ramping/flexibility products during high-stress operations.
ai_summary: PJM operations during a June 29–July 5, 2026 heat wave reached all-time unrestricted peak load of 168,158 MW on July 2, requiring emergency DOE 202(c) orders for emissions waivers and backup generation, plus cascading load-management and transmission-security actions. Net load peaked at 153,498 MW on July 2 as solar ramped down in late afternoon, creating a steep late-day ramp requirement precisely when hydro's flexibility value peaks. Forecast accuracy was strong (1–3% errors), but the document highlights that extreme load events expose capacity and generation deliverability constraints — outcomes that raise scarcity pricing and make-whole payments, and that test whether hydro's ramp capability is procured and credited adequately in real-time co-optimization and emergency protocols.

title: Item 06 – Reliability Compliance Update
relevance_reason: NERC reliability standards for inverter-based resources and computational loads directly affect large-load-collocated hydro and pumped-storage hybrid operations, and Essential Action #1 modifies interconnection modeling requirements that touch both traditional hydro and storage facilities co-located with new loads.
stakeholders: Aung Thant (NERC); Charlie Cook (NERC); JP Skeath (NERC); Becky Davis (PJM); Gizella Mali (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM reports on multiple NERC standards and alerts relevant to hydro operations. Project 2025-05 revises PRC-029-1 ride-through requirements for VSC-HVDC-connected inverter-based resources, carving out a narrow technical exception that recognizes thermal limits on DC chopper energy absorption—relevant to synchronous hydro units required to meet cumulative voltage ride-through mandates and to any hydro owner considering VSC-HVDC export interfaces. More broadly, NERC's Level 3 Computational Load Alert (due August 3, 2026) and Essential Action #1 require Transmission Planners and Planning Coordinators to develop detailed modeling data and interconnection requirements for computational loads, which directly affect hydro facilities in footprints where large loads (especially data centers) are co-locating with or near hydro generation; this reshapes facility interconnection requirements and facility use modeling. NERC is also hosting Small Group Advisory Sessions (August 10–14, 2026) on IBR standards (PRC-028-1, PRC-029-1, PRC-030-1, MOD-026-2) with mandatory compliance in 2026–2027, and has opened comment on draft commissioning best practices and EV task force white papers. Hydro owners with synchronous machines at large-load sites or planning exports via HVDC should monitor the ride-through revisions; all hydro facility owners in planning footprints subject to computational load modeling should assess how Essential Action #1 data requirements may alter their interconnection or operating parameters.

title: Item 07 – EDART Auto Ticket Creation – Presentation
relevance_reason: Outage reporting accuracy directly affects PJM's forced-outage rates used in forecasts, reserve requirements, and generator performance assessments — all inputs to capacity accreditation and dispatch decisions that hydro and PSH operators must navigate.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing auto-ticket creation in eDART (outage management system) targeting September 2026 to capture unavailable MW of committed capacity resources. The system will automatically generate eDART tickets when a unit marked 'unavailable' in Markets Gateway has no active outage ticket, leveraging day-ahead and real-time availability data from the must-offer schedule. This addresses chronic post-Winter Storm Elliott discrepancies between Markets Gateway, eDART, dispatch logs, and GADS that distorted forced-outage rates and reserve calculations. Hydro and PSH operators with committed capacity resources must ensure timely and accurate availability submissions in Markets Gateway to avoid unintended auto-ticket generation; enforcement of Manual 10 Section 2.4 (forced-outage submittal timing) will intensify, and operational/technical forums will provide implementation details.

title: Item 08 – Regulation Redesign Phase 2 Update
relevance_reason: PJM's Regulation Redesign Phase 2 restructures how the regulation ancillary service is offered, cleared, and priced — splitting the bidirectional product into separate RegUp and RegDn products with independent requirements, offers, clearing prices, and settlement, which directly affects how hydro and pumped-storage resources value and bid their fast-response capability.
stakeholders: Christina Barker (PJM); David Mroz (PJM); Emanuel Bernabeu (PJM); Michael Olaleye (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Regulation Redesign Phase 2 on December 1, 2026, replacing its one-signal bidirectional regulation product with two separate products: RegUp (operates above zero crossing) and RegDn (operates below zero crossing). Each product has its own market requirement MW (seasonal and hourly, possibly asymmetric), clearing price, performance metrics (score, mileage, bias factor), and settlement billing; resources will bid separately for each and receive independent awards, though only one product deploys in any given interval. Regulation market participants (including hydro operators) must submit RegUp/RegDn offers and parameters to the Markets Gateway sandbox immediately (deadline overdue), complete signal conversion testing (overdue), and prepare for cutover by December 1, 2026; a virtual open house is scheduled July 30, 2026, and special education sessions occur October–November 2026. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, this restructuring requires new offer engineering for each product and affects how flexibility revenue is recognized — operators will need to optimize bids separately for up and down regulation rather than offering a single bidirectional capability, potentially exposing separate value in each direction depending on system conditions and seasonal asymmetries.

title: Item 09 – Winter Generator Site Preparedness Visit Update
relevance_reason: Winter generator site-preparedness visits and winterization best practices directly address PJM's obligation to maintain unit readiness and ride-through capability during cold weather, a synchronous-unit operating standard that affects both conventional and hydro generators' compliance and outage scheduling.
ai_summary: PJM and Reliability First conduct voluntary winter generator site-preparedness visits to inspect cold-weather readiness at generating facilities, including hydro units. The program has expanded from 3 PJM-partnered visits in 2023–2024 to 24 in 2025–2026, with a target of 30 sites in 2026–2027; SERC is launching a parallel program. Statistical analysis of participants in the ReliabilityFirst region between November 2023 and March 2025 shows significant improvements in Equivalent Forced Outage Rate (EFOR), Weighted EFOR, and Unavailability Factor (UF) among visited units compared to non-participating generators. Best practices shared include heat tracing, insulation, windbreaks, temperature monitoring, pre-staged supplies, and dual-fuel testing. Hydro operators selected for visits are identified by NERC GADS data, PJM eDART data, recent ownership changes, units deemed critical by the ISO, and black-start-capable units; visits are non-binding and not part of compliance enforcement, but recommendations and findings are documented in confidential reports. The expansion and formalization of this program signal PJM's prioritization of winter preparedness as a reliability obligation touching all generator types, and the demonstrated performance improvements may raise expected standards for forced-outage and unavailability metrics across the fleet.

title: Draft Minutes – MIC – 06032026
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 11 conforming changes for Economic Load Response Regulation and dual-fuel scheduling updates affect how hydro and other dispatchable resources offer into ancillary-services markets and real-time commitment processes; capacity-market periodic review touches default offer parameters (CONE/ACR) that govern mitigation for use-limited resources like hydro.
initiatives: Dual Fuel Schedule Requirements [evaluation in progress]; Fuel Cost Policy Clarifications for Affiliated Fuel Suppliers [evaluation in progress]; Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
stakeholders: Todd Ament (Cube Hydro Partners, LLC); Jason Shoemaker (PJM Interconnection); Kevin Hatch (PJM Interconnection); Pete Langbein (PJM Interconnection); Phil D'Antonio (PJM Interconnection); Scott Baker (PJM Interconnection); Stefan Starkov (PJM Interconnection)
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee endorsed Phase 1 conforming changes to Manual 11 on Advanced Scheduled Resources (resource scheduling prior to day-ahead market) and will vote at the next meeting on Phase 1 Manual 18 (capacity-market periodic review with updated default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values) and Manual 11 conforming changes to implement FERC Order ER26-846-000 & ER26-846-001 on Economic Load Response Regulation Only. The committee also discussed Phase 2 of resource scheduling, dual-fuel schedule requirements updates, and fuel-cost policy clarifications for affiliated fuel suppliers. Hydro operators most affected are those subject to must-offer rules and cost-basis mitigation (especially pumped-storage facilities with affiliated fuel-cost treatment and hydroelectric plants required to bid in regulation markets). The next endorsement vote is scheduled for the next Market Implementation Committee meeting (date not specified in excerpt).

title: Informational Only – Interregional Coordination Update
relevance_reason: Interregional coordination and congestion-management mechanics affect hydro's transmission deliverability and interregional export capability; Energy Storage Resource considerations in market-flow calculation directly touch pumped-storage participation rules; Parallel Flow Visualization and Firm Flow Limits shape how hydro's use-constrained generation is modeled in multi-RTO settlements.
stakeholders: Chris Moran (PJM); Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Congestion Management Process (CMP) working group is advancing Freeze Date mechanics for interregional congestion allocation, with next CMPWG meeting July 30, 2026 and CMP Council meeting August 12, 2026. Key workstreams include Energy Storage Resource considerations in market-flow calculation, Firm Flow Limit updates, and evaluation of Parallel Flow Visualization (PFV)—an Eastern Interconnect standard using Generator-to-Load flow concepts and GPS-based prioritization to replace prior Network/Native Load constructs. PFV is being considered as an alternative to Freeze Date for establishing firm market-flow entitlements in M2M settlements. These changes affect how PJM's (and partner RTOs') hydro and pumped-storage resources are modeled for transmission-constraint relief obligations and interregional credit calculations; storage owners in particular should track ESR treatment in market-flow calculation and PFV adoption status, as they determine whether use-constrained generation receives firm-flow priority in congestion-relief protocols.

title: Item 01 1 – Manual 18 Periodic Review And Conforming Changes – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review addresses capacity-market accreditation rules, performance assessments, and supply-resource participation mechanics that directly govern how generation resources—including hydropower and pumped-storage hydro—qualify for and are compensated in PJM's capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 18 Revision 63 (effective September 1, 2026) is a periodic update to PJM's Capacity Market operating procedures covering resource-adequacy frameworks, RPM auction design, demand curves, supply-resource qualification, performance assessments, and settlement. This redline document shows conforming changes to the manual's sections on generation resources (4.2), seasonal capacity performance resources (4.10), non-performance assessment (8.4A), and summer/winter capability testing (8.5–8.6), among others. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, the most material sections are 4.2 (how generation resources—including hydro—register and submit offers), 4.10 (seasonal capacity performance, which affects run-of-river and weather-dependent hydro accreditation), 8.5 (capability testing rules that apply to synchronous hydro units), and 9.1–9.2 (performance penalties and locational charges). The redline marks technical conforming updates; no vote date or comment deadline is stated in the extracted text.

title: Item 01 2 – Manual 18 Periodic Review And Conforming Changes – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review touches capacity market accreditation rules (AUCAP factors, resource deficiency charges), must-offer exceptions for exports, DER aggregation resource participation, and billing/settlement — all operational obligations for PJM hydro and storage assets.
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Pete Langbein (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM staff is updating Manual 18 (Capacity Market Operations) to incorporate conforming changes from recent tariff revisions (ER26-980, ER26-455, ER25-783, ER25-2002, ER24-2447) and IMM suggestions. Key changes affecting hydro/storage: correction of PAI rates to use RTO Net CONE rather than LDA Net CONE; removal of environmentally limited resources from winter period capacity resource definitions; clarification of must-offer exception administration for exported generation; new penalty for derate caused by decreases in final Accredited AUCAP factors; and refinements to DER aggregation resource test-failure charges. The periodic review appears to be in final-read phase at the Market Implementation Committee (July 8, 2026 meeting). Most exposed are existing hydro and pumped-storage operators subject to accreditation adjustments and export-transaction approvals; DER-aggregated small hydro faces clarified test-failure billing.

title: Item 02 1 – Economic Load Response Regulation Only – Manual 11 – Redline
relevance_reason: This manual revision updates demand-resource regulation participation rules (Economic Load Response Regulation Only) and includes sections on hydropower operation and pumped-storage modeling, both of which affect how hydro and storage assets participate in PJM's ancillary-services markets and how their dispatch is constrained by operational rules.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 Revision 137 (effective July 28, 2026) incorporates three FERC-ordered updates: Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 (affecting self-scheduling), Generation Self-Scheduling Must-Offer requirements in reserve markets, and Economic Load Response Regulation Only participation rules. The document includes standing sections on Hydropower Operation (9.3) and Pumped Storage Modeling (Attachment B), which define how hydro and PSH operators must schedule, offer, and represent their dispatch constraints in PJM's day-ahead and real-time markets. Pumped-storage owners and hydropower operators must comply with the updated must-offer and self-scheduling requirements when submitting bids; the regulation-market changes may alter the revenue pathway available to regulation-qualified demand resources that compete with hydro for regulation supply. Most exposed are PSH operators managing state-of-charge constraints and hydro operators with variable output; implementation is effective as of July 28, 2026.

title: Item 03 2 – Fuel Cost Policy Clarifications For Affiliated Fuel Suppliers – Tenaska Proposal
relevance_reason: The proposal addresses fuel-cost policy and affiliated-fuel-supplier rules that directly affect cost-based offer development for dispatchable resources, including hydroelectric generators with associated fuel-supply arrangements or storage operations relying on cost-of-service bids.
initiatives: Fuel Cost Policy Clarifications for Affiliated Fuel Suppliers [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: Tenaska proposes clarifications to PJM's affiliated-fuel-supplier rules, arguing that current policy can produce unintended consequences: non-executable third-party quotes and index-based pricing may not reflect actual short-run marginal cost, potentially inflating cost-based offers and raising load costs or creating under-recovery risk for resources relying on compliance quotes. The proposal challenges the evidentiary basis for treating affiliated arrangements as higher-risk than non-affiliated ones and questions whether public quotes and indices are more accurate than executable market prices. This affects any hydro or storage operator whose cost-based energy offers rely on fuel-cost documentation; if PJM's policy shifts toward executable pricing or tightens affiliated-supplier treatment, it could change how operators substantiate offer reasonableness or hedge fuel-cost uncertainty in their bids. No comment deadline or next step is stated in the excerpt.

title: Item 04 1 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Problem Statement
relevance_reason: Defines submission timelines for offer-verification exception requests supporting high-cost offers in PJM, directly affecting how dispatchable resources including hydropower can justify offers above $1,000/MWh and recover make-whole payments when offers are rejected.
ai_summary: PJM proposes to clarify and revise the deadline for submitting exception requests and supporting documentation when offers exceeding $1,000/MWh fail Markets Gateway validation. Currently, Manual 11 Attachment D requires submission by 11:00 AM EPT the next business day—a deadline from 2017 that PJM considers burdensome during extended winter Offer Verification events and outdated given enhanced Markets Gateway functionality. PJM seeks input on a revised timeline that would reduce the administrative load on Market Sellers while maintaining the integrity of the exception process. This directly affects any generator (including hydro and PSH) that must justify high-cost operating or opportunity-cost offers to recover make-whole uplift; a more relaxed timeline lowers operational burden during winter resource-scarcity events when such offers are most likely.

title: Item 04 2 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Issue Charge
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: After-the-fact make-whole payments are PJM's uplift/make-whole mechanism, a revenue pathway for dispatchable resources (including hydropower) when Offer Verification events occur; this issue charge adjusts the timeline deadline for invoking that recovery, directly affecting the revenue-protection procedures hydro operators rely on.
ai_summary: PJM is revising the deadline timeline in Manual 11, Attachment D for Market Sellers to request after-the-fact make-whole payments following Offer Verification events—a procedure triggered when equipment limitations or extraordinary market conditions prevent full energy output. The timeline, written in 2017, is being modernized to align with PJM's enhanced Markets Gateway functionality, which now allows faster upload and documentation of offer data and supporting materials. Work is assigned to the Market Implementation Committee under Quick Fix (Tier 1) process with a two-month duration and solution target before winter 2026 operations; no tariff changes are in scope. Hydro operators relying on make-whole recovery after unplanned or operational constraints during tight conditions should monitor the revised deadline to ensure timely claims.

title: Item 04 3 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Clarifies the timeline and procedural requirements for offer-verification exception requests in PJM's make-whole settlement process — a critical compliance obligation for generation resource owners, including hydro and storage, whose cost-based offers fail automated screens.
ai_summary: PJM is documenting the offer-verification exception-request process timeline in Manual 11, Section 2.3.6.2: generation owners whose resource offers fail the offer screen have 60 calendar days following the operating day to submit an exception request with cost documentation via Markets Gateway, supported by the Market Monitoring Unit. If Markets Gateway is unavailable, owners must email EnergyOfferVerification@pjm.com and MMU_Energy_Offers_Review@monitoringanalytics.com with unit name, operating date/time, cost calculation per Manual 15 (Cost Development Guidelines), and PJM dispatch contact details. This deadline and procedural specification is foundational to how hydro and storage operators defend cost-based offers against failed screens and maintain eligibility for dispatch, LMP setting, and operating-reserve credits — particularly relevant for storage operators and pumped-hydro plants offering complex cost structures that may not fit automated validation logic.

title: Item 04 4 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – PJM Proposal
relevance_reason: Offer verification and make-whole procedures directly affect the revenue compensation mechanism for generation resources (including hydropower) committed by PJM when cost-based offers exceed $1,000/MWh; changes to the submission timeline and process documentation alter compliance obligations and settlement deadlines.
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Joseph Tutino (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM proposes updating Manual 11, Section 2.3.6.2 and Attachment D to clarify and extend the deadline for submitting offer-verification exception requests in support of incremental offers exceeding $1,000/MWh from 11:00 EPT on the business day following the operating day to 60 calendar days following the operating day. The change aligns the exception-request deadline with other PJM settlement deadlines and reflects enhancements to the Markets Gateway fuel-price exceptions functionality, deferring to email backup if tool outages occur. Generation owners (including any committed hydroelectric or pumped-storage units) will gain substantially more time to review bills, gather supporting documentation, and submit requests before the 60-day window closes, reducing the risk of forfeiting make-whole credits; the 60-day deadline also provides PJM, the MMU, and markets with certainty on settlement closure. Most affected are owners of committed units with cost-based offers that fail the automated screening and require after-the-fact verification to set LMP or receive operating-reserve credits.

title: Item 04 5 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Modifies PJM's offer verification exception request timeline for high-cost offers (>$1000/MWh), directly affecting when hydropower and other Market Sellers must submit documentation supporting make-whole payment claims — a key revenue-recovery mechanism for use-limited and dispatchable resources.
ai_summary: PJM proposes updating PJM Manual 11 sections 2.3.6.2 and Attachment D to establish a uniform 60-calendar-day deadline for exception requests supporting offers exceeding $1000/MWh, replacing the current fragmented timeline. The change aligns administrative procedures with Markets Gateway's enhanced functionality and aims to provide Market Sellers time to recover from extended winter events while establishing certainty for closure. MIC approval path is July/August 2026; MRC first read is July 28, 2026, and voting is August 19, 2026 — the effective date. Hydropower operators and other dispatchable resources relying on make-whole uplift to cover scarcity-event costs will need to meet the new 60-day filing window to substantiate high-cost offers, affecting their revenue-recovery process during stress periods.

title: Item 06 – Regulation Redesign Phase 2 Update
relevance_reason: Regulation market redesign splits PJM's bidirectional regulation product into RegUp and RegDn products with separate offers, clearing prices, and settlement — directly affecting how hydropower and other dispatchable resources bid and capture revenue in the regulation ancillary-services market.
stakeholders: Christina Barker (PJM); Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Michael Olaleye (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Regulation Redesign Phase 2, effective December 1, 2026, which bifurcates the single bidirectional regulation product into two separate products: RegUp (above zero crossing) and RegDn (below zero crossing), each with its own requirement MW, clearing price, offer parameters, performance metrics, and settlement. Hydropower and pumped-storage resources must submit RegUp and RegDn offers and parameters in the Markets Gateway sandbox by overdue deadline and complete signal-conversion testing to participate; PJM is holding a virtual open house on July 30, 2026, and market open is scheduled for November 25, 2026. This redesign increases the complexity and granularity (from hourly to 30-minute) of regulation participation, requiring hydro owners to develop separate bidding strategies for each directional product and validate system integrations before go-live.

title: Item 07 1 – Periodic Review Of Default CONE And Acr Values
relevance_reason: CONE and ACR values directly determine the Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) floor bids and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) that apply to all capacity-market resources in PJM, including hydropower and pumped storage; changes to these values alter the competitiveness and profitability of hydro's participation in the capacity auction.
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Pete Langbein (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is updating its default Gross CONE (Cost of New Entry) and Gross ACR (Avoidable Cost Rate) values for delivery years 2030/2031, which feed directly into MOPR floors for new resources and MSOC offer caps for existing resources under Attachment DD §§ 5.14(h-2) and 6.4. Brattle Group and Sargent & Lundy are conducting the analysis; PJM will present draft values to the MIC in July 2026, with final values going to the MRC and MC for an advisory vote in August, and tariff filing at FERC by October 1, 2026. The update includes tax-law adjustments (100% bonus depreciation reinstatement) and revised overnight-capital and fixed-O&M assumptions from EIA 2026 and the 2025 Quadrennial Review. For hydropower and pumped storage operators, the revised Gross ACR directly impacts the offer cap applied to their existing units; a lower ACR narrows the band within which they can bid and may compress margins, while higher ACR provides headroom. The preliminary Gross CONE values span from $298–$4,403/MW-day depending on technology and delivery year, and Battery Energy Storage is preliminarily set at $502–$696/MW-day, which establishes a competitive benchmark for long-duration storage against which PSH will be evaluated for capacity qualification. Deadline for FERC filing is October 1, 2026.

title: Item 07 2 – 2026 PJM Gross Avoidable Costs For Existing Generation
relevance_reason: PJM's 2026 Avoidable Cost Review (ACR) directly affects how hydro and pumped-storage resources are evaluated for capacity accreditation and market-participation value; both are explicitly flagged for 'Unit Specific' treatment rather than standardized cost ranges, signaling potential changes to their reference-level methodology or cost-recovery framework.
stakeholders: Kelley McGough (PJM); Joshua Junge (Sargent & Lundy); Andrew W. Thompson (The Brattle Group); James Herrigel (The Brattle Group); Samuel Newell (The Brattle Group)
ai_summary: PJM and Brattle Group have updated the 2026 Gross Avoidable Costs (GAC) for existing generation resources, a key input to PJM's Avoidable Cost Rate (ACR) mechanism used in capacity market accreditation and market-power mitigation. Hydro (3,275 MW) and pumped storage (5,232 MW) are classified separately as 'Unit Specific' resources, meaning no standardized cost range will be applied; instead, these resources will require individualized cost analysis. Other resources (natural gas, coal, nuclear, wind, solar, and new 4-hour battery storage) receive fleet-wide representative-plant cost ranges. The document represents initial analysis and solicits stakeholder feedback before finalizing the 2026 ranges. Most exposed are hydro and PSH owners seeking capacity accreditation or participating in must-offer/reference-level calculations—the 'Unit Specific' designation implies PJM views each facility's avoidable cost as too variable to fit fleet-wide formulas, which may shift negotiation burden from standardized rules to individual plant audits. Next steps and comment deadlines are not specified in this excerpt.

title: Item 09 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Work Plan
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM is designing pre-day-ahead resource scheduling rules that will affect how hydro and storage resources submit offers and manage dispatch flexibility before energy-market clearing — a foundational change to offer/dispatch timing that touches dispatch obligations and potentially the revenue capture window for flexible resources.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee is developing a Phase 2 work plan for 'Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market,' a multi-stage design initiative running from July 2025 through August 2026. The work plan charts progression from first read and approval through interest identification, design-component development, option and package narrowing, and final vote. The initiative addresses how resources (including hydro and pumped storage) will schedule and offer into PJM markets before day-ahead clearing — a fundamental change to the offer window and dispatch-instruction timing. Pumped-storage operators especially will need to track whether pre-DA scheduling rules affect charging-energy treatment, state-of-charge management, and the ability to lock in flexibility commitments ahead of real-time; the phases through early 2026 will determine whether storage faces tighter scheduling constraints or gains more visibility for day-ahead dispatch planning.

title: Item 10 1 – Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's ESR model design directly determines how pumped-storage hydro and battery storage compete for capacity accreditation, ancillary-services revenue, and energy-market dispatch—the core mechanisms by which storage owners recover fixed costs and optimize multi-hour operating decisions.
initiatives: PJM Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM presented its current Energy Storage Resource (ESR) participation model, covering opt-in market participation, state-of-charge tracking, ancillary-services eligibility, cost-offer development, and capacity accreditation. The ESR model is a self-schedule framework (no PJM commitment); resources offer as one continuous resource with charge/discharge modes and optional SOC parameters, but PJM does not track SOC in dispatch and offers very limited opportunity-cost recovery for forward energy-limited constraints—a structural gap that disadvantages use-limited resources like pumped storage competing against batteries. Capacity accreditation uses class-based ELCC (4, 6, 8, 10-hour durations), and PSH with shorter physical duration can derate to match a longer ELCC class, but there is no explicit mechanism for PSH to reflect seasonal or hydrological variation in available energy. This presentation supports an active stakeholder issue charge on ESR model enhancements; no vote or comment deadline is stated in the slides, but the mention of a published position paper and ongoing stakeholder process signals that PJM is revisiting the model as the battery storage pipeline grows (8.4 GW under construction or in Cycle 2, 66.5 GW proposed).

title: Item 10 2 – Options – Packages Matrix – Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements – Post Meeting
relevance_reason: PJM's energy storage resource model enhancements directly affect pumped-storage hydro participation rules, pricing, and accreditation in a major RTO market.
initiatives: PJM Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee is reviewing options packages for energy storage resource model enhancements, presented as a post-meeting matrix. The document details proposed changes to how storage resources—including pumped-storage hydro—participate in PJM's energy and ancillary services markets, their state-of-charge rules, offer-development requirements, and capacity accreditation. Without the matrix content itself visible in this extraction, the specific revenue and compliance impacts cannot be determined; however, this is a live proceeding at a major East Coast RTO that will reshape how PSH competes against batteries, when and how it can charge, and how its capacity is valued. Stakeholders should monitor the next MIC meeting for the substantive options package and request comment deadlines when they are published.

title: MIC Work Plan
relevance_reason: The work plan governs PJM manual reviews and active policy development on energy storage resource model enhancements, flexible resource lost-opportunity-cost eligibility, ancillary-services market operations, and capacity-market accreditation—all of which shape revenue recognition and participation pathways for pumped-storage hydro and conventional hydro resources in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee work plan (as of July 2026) charts active and periodic reviews affecting hydro and storage operators. Active issues include PJM Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements (status unclear, ongoing), Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Cost Eligibility (touching how storage's energy opportunity costs are credited in markets), and Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day-Ahead Energy Market Phase 2 (begun September 2025). Manual 11 (Energy and Ancillary Services Market Operations) and Manual 18 (PJM Capacity Market) are under review with next due dates of March 2027 and August 2028, respectively—these govern how hydro and PSH are accredited for capacity and how ancillary-service prices and opportunity-cost settlement are computed. Cost Development Subcommittee and Distributed Resources Subcommittee provide ongoing monthly updates. The document is an internal scheduling roadmap; no specific proposals, deadlines for stakeholder comment, or vote dates are stated, but it flags the subjects and timelines under active development.

title: Informational Only – 2026 27 Market Efficiency Input Assumptions – July 2026
relevance_reason: Market Efficiency analysis directly affects transmission-expansion economics and thus hydro deliverability and strategic investment value; load-growth forecasts and ELCC/capacity accreditation assumptions are inputs to hydro revenue and adequacy-planning decisions.
ai_summary: PJM's 2026/27 Market Efficiency cycle will evaluate transmission projects across 2027, 2031, 2034, and 2037 using PROMOD V simulations with updated fuel costs, load forecasts (peak rising to 228 GW by 2037), demand-response assumptions, and ELCC-based capacity accreditation. The analysis assumes PJM's ELCC Class Ratings (published for 2027/28–2035/36, then held constant) to calculate modeled unforced capacity against forecasted UCAP requirements, with a 1.25:1 benefit-to-cost threshold and 12.08% carrying charge rate. Hydro and storage owners need to track the ELCC methodology and capacity-accreditation assumptions embedded in these simulations, as they directly shape which transmission upgrades are deemed economic and thus which delivery paths hydro can rely on; the load-growth trajectory (energy forecast rising 68% from 2027 to 2041) also signals scarcity conditions that may tighten accreditation metrics or raise the value of dispatchable capacity.

title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumptions Feedback
relevance_reason: Transmission planning horizon and load-growth integration directly affect hydro deliverability, interconnection timelines, and strategic options for long-duration energy storage and reservoir operation; large-load projections (data centers, electrification) reshape scarcity signals and revenue from location-based and flexibility products.
stakeholders: Emil G. Avram (Dominion Energy Virginia); Thomas F. Farrell II (Dominion Energy Virginia); Mark Mroczynski (FirstEnergy Transmission); Eric Hsia (PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.); Jason Connell (PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.); Joshua Stephenson (PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.); Sami Abdulsalam (PJM Interconnection, L.L.C.)
ai_summary: Dominion Energy Virginia and FirstEnergy submit joint feedback to PJM on 2026 RTEP assumptions, urging (1) execution-focused generation modeling that prioritizes dispatchable resources with site control, permitting progress, and fuel contracts over speculative capacity, and (2) maintenance of a 7–10-year transmission planning horizon aligned with generation development timelines. Both companies emphasize that large, clustered load growth—especially data centers and electrification—must be directly incorporated into planning assumptions with scenario-based approaches and early state/local stakeholder engagement. The letters stress that planning must account for real-world siting, permitting, outage, and supply-chain constraints. These recommendations are informational feedback to PJM's planning process; no vote or deadline is stated in the document.

title: Item 01 – Interregional Planning Update
relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning and seams governance directly affect hydro's ability to deliver energy across regions; developments in PJM/MISO and PJM/New York & New England coordination shape the transmission constraints that define hydro's locational value and export capability.
stakeholders: Eric Hsia (PJM); Jeffrey Goldberg (PJM); Joshua Stephenson (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM presented an update on interregional planning activities, including coordination through EIPC (Eastern Interconnection Planning Collaborative), IPSAC meetings with MISO scheduled for August 17, 2026, and pending IPSAC sessions with New York and New England. No new proposals or specific findings are detailed in this update slide package — it functions as a status report on ongoing coordination forums. For hydro owners operating in or exporting from PJM, these interregional planning processes determine transmission expansion priorities, seams agreement evolution, and reserve-sharing arrangements that affect dispatch economics and available transmission paths; the August 17 IPSAC meeting and pending New York/New England coordination could surface proposals affecting capacity accreditation, path utilization, or frequency-response service procurement across regions.

title: Item 02 – Generation Deactivation Notification Update
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Generation deactivations trigger PJM's reliability assessment and may require transmission upgrades; for hydro owners, deactivations of competing thermal/coal plants can reduce transmission congestion and raise locational scarcity value, while also informing strategic decisions about plant retirement and mothballing timelines in a capacity-constrained grid.
stakeholders: Eric Hsia (PJM); Joshua Stephenson (PJM); Saravana Kumar Rajendran (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Generation Deactivation Notification Update reports Q2 2026 deactivation notices: two units at West Lorain (50 MW and 51 MW, ATSI zone) with notification received June 19, 2026 and requested deactivation date of June 1, 2030. PJM states it does not approve deactivations but identifies reliability concerns; when adverse impacts are found, it requests the owner extend operations, with the option to file a Cost of Service Recovery Rate (CSRR) at FERC or accept a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC) under the tariff. Reliability analysis is in progress for both units. For hydro owners, deactivations of fossil generation in transmission-constrained zones reduce competing supply and can enhance locational pricing and scarcity signals; this report is part of PJM's standing deactivation-tracking process with no near-term deadline, but hydro operators monitor these announcements to assess congestion relief and RA adequacy outlook.

title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Presentation
relevance_reason: Expedited interconnection track for generators ≥250 MW UCAP directly affects hydro and PSH plants' queue position, study timelines, and project development timelines in PJM; this is a material change to interconnection rules for large resources.
ai_summary: PJM has established a temporary Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) for generators meeting three criteria: written support from a Primary Siting Authority, commercial operation within 36 months, and ≥250 MW Accredited UCAP. The EIT operates in parallel to the standard PJM cycle with compressed timelines (~180 days application + 60 days study + 60 days negotiation) to accelerate state-sponsored projects addressing resource adequacy. FERC accepted the tariff revisions June 9, 2026 (Docket ER26-1563); Manual 14H revisions are undergoing committee review with an effective date of July 31, 2026. Hydro and PSH plants ≥250 MW with near-term siting authority support can now bypass standard queue congestion, but ineligible projects remain subject to existing study processes—the track creates a two-tier interconnection system in which queue position, study timing, and cost allocation diverge based on eligibility status.

title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Expedited Interconnection Track directly affects hydro and PSH owners' queue position and project-development timeline — this is interconnection queue reform under FERC Order ER26-1563-000, a key strategic option for owners seeking to expand or uprate capacity.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Manual 14H revisions to establish an Expedited Interconnection Track, conforming to pending FERC Docket ER26-1563-000. The changes add a new Section 16 to the manual and modify service-request processing workflow (Sections 1.1, 1.3, 8.5.1.2) to incorporate the expedited track as a parallel process. Effective date is July 31, 2026, pending FERC approval. The expedited track may offer qualifying hydro/PSH projects faster interconnection timelines and reduced study obligations — a material benefit to owners with planned uprates or new run-of-river/pumped-storage additions. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23, 2026.

title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14H revision updates PJM's interconnection queue process, affecting hydropower and pumped-storage operators' strategic options for plant uprates, surplus interconnection service, and new facility integration under FERC Order 2023-era queue reform.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H Revision 06 (effective July 23, 2026) updates the New Service Requests Cycle Process governing generation interconnection, merchant transmission, and transmission service requests. Key revisions include clarified procedures for surplus interconnection service (Section 12), expedited interconnection track (Section 16), and interconnection rights for generators seeking capacity-resource or energy-resource status (Section 9). For hydropower operators, the manual governs how uprates and new-capacity requests are queued, studied, and accredited; for pumped-storage projects, it sets the timeline and deposit requirements for system-impact studies and final agreements. Changes to interconnection procedures and decision-point timing directly affect cost and speed of bringing hydro or PSH capacity online or expanding existing facilities.

title: Item 04 – 1 Manual 21b Periodic Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 21B governs ICAP accreditation testing, ELCC analysis, and performance adjustment rules for capacity resources in PJM, directly affecting how hydro and pumped-storage resources qualify and are credited in PJM's capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM is conducting a periodic cover-to-cover review of Manual 21B, which establishes the accreditation framework for capacity resources. Changes include clarification of the ICAP definition, updated testing rules around ambient conditions and simultaneous requirements, codification of test-data requirements in a new Appendix A, explicit permission to gather additional data for ELCC analysis, and updates to the Performance Adjustment section for distributed-energy-resource calculations. The document is in first read before the Planning Committee (July 7, 2026); second read and endorsement are scheduled for August 4, with MRC consideration on August 19. These changes affect how hydro and especially pumped-storage capacity accreditation is measured, tested, and adjusted — a direct lever on PSH and reservoir-hydro RA value in PJM's market. The timing suggests comments or questions from stakeholders would be most useful before the August endorsement.

title: Item 04 – 2 Manual 21b Periodic Review – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Manual 21B governs capacity accreditation and testing procedures — revisions to ELCC analysis data collection and DER performance adjustments directly affect how PJM credits hydro and pumped-storage capacity in its capacity market, touching both accreditation methodology and obligation compliance.
ai_summary: PJM's Planning Committee is reviewing periodic updates to Manual 21B (Rules and Procedures for Determination of Generating Capability), effective August 19, 2026. The revision clarifies ICAP definition, tightens ambient-condition and simultaneous-requirement testing rules with codified deadlines (Appendix A), adds language facilitating ELCC analysis data collection (Section 9), and updates DER performance-adjustment calculations. The changes will go to MRC first read on July 28, 2026 and voting on August 19, 2026. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the clarified ELCC data-collection pathway and updated performance-adjustment methodology may alter how their capacity contributions are measured and credited in PJM's accreditation process.

title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 21b Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 21B governs ELCC accreditation and capacity rating for all resource classes including limited-duration resources (which includes pumped-storage hydro), directly affecting PSH and conventional hydro revenue accreditation, capacity interconnection rights retention, and testing/verification obligations.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 21B Revision 06 (effective June 24, 2026) is a periodic review update to the rules governing generating-capability determination, ELCC classification, and accredited UCAP calculation across all resource types. The revision clarifies data-submission deadlines, ambient and simultaneous testing conditions, removes duplicate language, and aligns dual-fuel gas definitions with ER25-3413-000. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, the manual specifies how limited-duration resources (Section 2.2, including PSH) are classified and accredited, how net-capability testing is conducted (Section 10–11), and how capacity interconnection rights are retained (Section 5.2); the clarified verification-section deadlines and ambient-condition rules directly govern how PSH operators must demonstrate and maintain their accredited capacity in PJM's capacity market.

title: Item 04 – Ugi Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Large load interconnection (500 MW customer in Newport Township) raises strategic value of existing hydro MW in the UGI transmission zone and affects transmission deliverability for hydro resources; PJM's M-3 process schedule and stakeholder comment windows are operationally relevant to any hydro owner participating in or affected by PJM transmission expansion.
ai_summary: PJM UGI transmission zone is processing a supplemental project (Need UGI-2026-0003) to serve a 500 MW customer requesting service at 230 kV in Newport Township, with a phased in-service schedule: 250 MW by Q3 2029 and an additional 250 MW by Q1 2030. The proposed solution comprises approximately $107.2 M in transmission investment, including a 4-mile 230 kV line tap, Newport Switching Station, Mountain Substation upgrades, with projected full in-service date of March 29, 2030 and current status in engineering phase. Stakeholders have until 10 days after this TEAC meeting (by July 17, 2026) to submit comments; the project then enters the "Do No Harm" (DNH) analysis phase before being submitted to PJM's RTEP process. Large load growth in a region historically served by hydro resources can absorb or reorient hydro generation and create scarcity-driven revenue uplift; transmit­sion constraints and expansion timelines affect hydro's ability to deliver into load centers. PJM-interconnected hydro owners and pumped-storage operators relying on UGI-zone transmission access should monitor this supplemental project's progress and environmental/operational constraints during the DNH and Local Plan phases.

title: Item 05 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Ids – Presentation
relevance_reason: Interconnection and deliverability rules for new resources directly affect when hydro and storage projects can enter service and begin generating revenue; the cluster-based methodology change alters how PJM allocates deliverable capacity among competing projects, which shapes project developer economics and must-offer obligations.
ai_summary: PJM proposes revising Manual 14-H to transition the Interim Deliverability Study (IDS) from serial to cluster-based methodology, effective 2027/2028 Delivery Year. The proposal introduces two subclusters: Subcluster 1 prioritizes projects already provisionally operational from prior IDS results, while Subcluster 2 includes all new IDS requests; deliverable MW are allocated proportionally within each subcluster using distribution factors (DFAX). PJM argues this mitigates developer risk of curtailment for projects deemed deliverable in prior years but enhances operational certainty and Capacity Market alignment; the current alternative is a single-cluster approach where all projects compete equally, creating higher curtailment risk for provisionally operational resources. Planning Committee first read is July 7, 2026; voting is scheduled August 4, 2026, with MRC vote August 19 and an October 30 effective date for 2027/28 IDS results—timing is compressed because the 2027/28 application window closed June 30, 2026 and results must be completed by October 2026. Developers whose projects are already provisionally operational will benefit most from the two-subcluster design, as it preserves their must-offer status and operational continuity; new applicants face greater delivery uncertainty.

title: Item 05 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Ids – Problem Statement
relevance_reason: Interim Deliverability Study methodology changes affect hydropower and storage project deliverability accreditation and capacity-market participation in PJM, touching both resource-adequacy positioning and interconnection strategic options for existing and new hydro assets.
ai_summary: PJM proposes enhancements to its Interim Deliverability Study (IDS) methodology under Manual 14H to stabilize deliverability determinations across delivery years and reduce risk that generating resources with Provisional Interconnection Service lose deliverability status in subsequent years. The problem statement identifies that the current sequential, project-by-project IDS approach creates year-over-year volatility in deliverability outcomes and misalignment between planning study results and real-time operational/capacity-market obligations, particularly under PJM's transition to Cycle-based interconnection. The enhancement aims to provide greater predictability, align study outcomes with must-offer obligations into future delivery years, and recognize operational realities of resources already generating—mechanisms that directly affect how existing hydro and pumped-storage resources maintain deliverability accreditation and capacity-market eligibility. This is in problem-statement phase; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

title: Item 05 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Ids – Issue Charge
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue reform and deliverability allocation methodology directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage projects obtain transmission access and revenue certainty in PJM's cluster-based queue process.
ai_summary: PJM Planning Committee is undertaking a Quick Fix revision to Manual 14h section 4.10.1 (Interim Deliverability Studies) to enhance the cluster-based IDS methodology for allocating constrained flowgate headroom. The current sequential, project-by-project allocation method is being reconsidered due to PJM's shift to a cycle-based interconnection process, increased system constraints, and reliability concerns. Proposed M-14h edits will be presented at the Planning Committee's July 2026 meeting with endorsement expected in August 2026. For hydro and PSH developers, changes to how interim deliverability is calculated and assigned across a cluster could materially affect which projects in a queue cycle receive full or partial transmission access, directly influencing project economics and strategic queue positioning. Developers should monitor the proposed methodology for impact on their queue status and energy-delivery assumptions.

title: Item 05 – 4 Manual 14h Revisions Ids – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: PJM is revising its interconnection deliverability study methodology, which directly affects how new generation (including hydro and storage) is studied for grid connection and network upgrade costs—a core determinant of project viability and interconnection timelines for hydro and PSH developers.
ai_summary: PJM is transitioning its Interim Deliverability Study methodology from serial to cluster-based cycle methodology, effective with the 2027/28 study cycle. Manual 14-H Section 4.10.1.3 will be updated to detail how the cluster-based approach allocates resources to tranches and assesses network deliverability. This change affects how new interconnection requests—including pumped-storage and conventional hydro—are studied for delivery capability and network upgrade requirements. The revision is scheduled for Planning Committee first read in July 2026 and Markets and Reliability Committee votes on August 19, 2026.

title: Item 05 – 5 Manual 14h Revisions Ids – Redline
relevance_reason: INTERCONNECTION: Manual 14H revisions redefine interim deliverability study procedures and cluster-based allocation methodology for new resources seeking Provisional Interconnection Service, directly affecting project-queue timing, capacity-rights assignment, and transmission-constrained resource entry for hydro and storage projects.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H revisions establish a new cluster-based methodology for interim deliverability studies, effective with the 2027/2028 Delivery Year. Subcluster 1 prioritizes previously provisionally operational projects, while Subcluster 2 includes all other eligible projects; deliverable megawatts are allocated proportionally within each cluster using DFAX methodology. Projects must request interim studies at least 11 months before the target Delivery Year and may be granted Provisional Interconnection Service (at PJM's discretion, as energy-only for projects with Contingent Facilities), valid only for that specific Delivery Year. The cluster methodology materially changes the queue-management signal: projects already operating under prior provisional status receive priority in allocation, while new entrants (including hydro projects seeking early entry) compete in a secondary tier, making repeated interim-study cycles necessary for multi-year deferrals. This affects timing and certainty of capacity interconnection rights for any hydro or pumped-storage project planning to enter PJM before baseline RTEP study completion.

title: Item 05 – Ugi Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Large customer interconnection request (500 MW load) triggers transmission planning and interconnection deliverability work that affects hydro resource dispatch capability and interregional transfer pathways in PJM.
ai_summary: UGI Transmission proposes a 230 kV supply solution for a 500 MW customer load in Newport Township, Pennsylvania (250 MW in Q3 2029, 250 MW in Q1 2030), comprising a 4-mile tap line, new 230 kV switchyard, 0.5-mile customer tap, and Mountain Substation upgrades. Total estimated cost is $107.2 M with in-service date of March 29, 2030. The project is in engineering stage; stakeholders may submit comments on this solution within 10 days, and a "Do No Harm" analysis must be completed before the solution is selected for integration into PJM's RTEP. Load growth of this magnitude and configuration affects available transmission capacity for existing resources including hydropower, and the supplemental interconnection project establishes precedent for how PJM accommodates large new loads in the transmission footprint.

title: Item 06 – Aps Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection requests (1000 MW and 368 MW) in FirstEnergy's APS transmission zone affect hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and scarcity conditions that determine the value of dispatchable generation; transmission expansion projects also directly influence hydro plants' ability to export power and receive efficient dispatch signals.
ai_summary: FirstEnergy submitted two new customer connection requests to PJM's M-3 transmission-planning process in the APS zone: a 1000 MW retail-customer request near Doubs and Lime Kiln with in-service target 12/31/2032 (APS-2026-041), and a 368 MW wholesale-customer request near Doubs and Sage with in-service target 4/30/2032 (APS-2026-039). FirstEnergy also presented solution meetings for three infrastructure projects: Rhodes Lane–Yukon 500 kV line equipment upgrades ($5.45M, in-service 05/12/2028) to improve operational flexibility during maintenance outages, and Shingletown Substation reconfiguration from straight bus to six-breaker ring ($19.11M, in-service 06/25/2029) to eliminate single-breaker contingency risk to 82.7 MW of local load. Large-load growth in PJM's footprint increases scarcity rents and transmission congestion, raising the revenue value of hydro generation at remote locations if those loads can absorb PJM energy exports; conversely, local transmission congestion relief projects may reduce nodal price separation and diminish locational advantages hydro facilities enjoy. Stakeholders must submit comments on the two customer-connection needs within 10 days of the 7/7/2026 meeting, and on the infrastructure solutions within 10 days prior to the Local Plan submission to RTEP—specific deadline dates were not stated in the document.

title: Item 07 – Aps Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Two large new customer connection requests (1000 MW and 368 MW loads) and interconnection-driven transmission expansion projects affect hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and the value of transmission-connected generation in PJM.
ai_summary: FirstEnergy (APS transmission zone) is advancing two supplemental transmission needs for new customer connections — a 1000 MW retail load (in-service 12/31/2032, need APS-2026-041) and a 368 MW wholesale load (in-service 4/30/2032, need APS-2026-039) — both near existing 230 kV substations in the Doubs/Sage/Ballenger area. The document also presents solutions for the Rhodes Lane–Yukon 500 kV line (terminal equipment upgrade, $5.45M, in-service 5/12/2028) and the Shingletown 230 kV substation reconfiguration (bus conversion, $19.11M, in-service 6/25/2029) to address operational constraints. Stakeholders must comment within 10 days of the 7/7/2026 meeting to influence the next phase of FirstEnergy's M-3 planning process. For hydro owners, load growth of this scale (1.3+ GW) expands the value of existing generation and storage connected to PJM's backbone, and transmission uprates improve export capability from the region; conversely, concentrated large-load interconnection can create local transmission bottlenecks that advantage nearby generation or storage. All projects are in early conceptual phase (models, costs, and in-service dates subject to change).

title: Item 08 – Large Load Adjustment Request Timeline – Presentation
relevance_reason: Large load growth (data centers) in PJM affects hydro's strategic capacity value and revenue by changing regional load forecasts that underpin resource adequacy, transmission planning, and scarcity pricing.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing an enhanced Large Load Adjustment (LLA) process for the 2027 load forecast, with EDC/LSE submissions due September 2026 and PJM final adjustments by November/December 2026. PJM has engaged Charles River Associates to independently review load submissions and develop a third-party data-center forecast (consultant outreach July–October 2026). Because data-center load growth creates both locational scarcity and regional adequacy pressure in PJM, the revised load forecasts will directly drive the baseline for PJM's capacity market (RPM) demand curves, transmission expansion studies, and real-time price formation over the 2027–2028 planning horizon — meaning hydro owners' capacity accreditation, RA exposure, and energy-arbitrage opportunities depend on these revised forecasts being accurate and transparent.

title: Item 09 – Reliability Compliance Update – Presentation
relevance_reason: NERC reliability standards governing inverter-based resource (IBR) ride-through, operational assessment, and cold-weather preparedness create new compliance obligations and operating-standard requirements for synchronous hydro units and constrain flexibility value.
stakeholders: Aung Thant (NERC); Charlie Cook (NERC); JP Skeath (NERC); Becky Davis (PJM); Gizella Mali (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM presented NERC Project 2025-05 (PRC-029-1 Ride-Through Revisions) introducing a narrow exemption for VSC-HVDC-connected IBRs from cumulative voltage ride-through mandates when DC chopper thermal limits are documented and coordinated with Transmission Owner/Operator; balloting runs 7/3/26–7/13/26. NERC Project 2025-03 (FAC-011-5, TOP-003-9) addresses FERC Order No. 901 directives to include individual and aggregate IBR performance in operational assessments and real-time monitoring; join ballot 7/13/26, final ballots 7/22/26–7/31/26. title: Item 06 – EDART Auto Ticket Creation – Presentation

ai_summary: PJM is implementing automatic eDART (Outage Database) ticket creation in September 2026 to capture all unavailable MW from committed capacity resources in real time.
