This directly affects hydro and PSH operators' obligation to report unavailable capacity timely and accurately under Schedule 1 Section 1.10.1A(d) (must-offer rules); the auto-creation logic will flag operators whose availability data in Markets Gateway diverges from eDART submissions, and forced-outage rates derived from eDART feed into capacity accreditation, reserve calculations, and PJM's forecast accuracy to stakeholders. Hydro and PSH owners dependent on accurate outage reporting for capacity value and performance assessment should monitor the Tech Change and eDART forums as details are formalized before the September 2026 implementation.

ai_summary: PJM Interconnection has posted Transition Cycle 2 Phase II Studying & Information (SIS) reports covering 270 projects (June 5, 2026) and opened Decision Point II (DP2) from June 8–July 7, 2026, during which project developers must confirm participation and modifications via NextGen to proceed to Phase III SIS (starting July 8). title: Item 04 – Market Participation Options For Mixed Technology Facilities
relevance_reason: Defines participation pathways (co-located vs. hybrid models) and capacity/must-offer rules affecting how mixed-technology resources—including pumped-storage hydro paired with other resources—can be classified, accredited, and dispatched in PJM markets, with direct implications for hybrid PSH revenue and operational flexibility.
stakeholders: Amanda Martin (PJM); Dave Miscavage (PJM); Maria Belenky (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Interconnection Process Subcommittee presented market participation options for Mixed Technology Facilities (MTF)—facilities with multiple generation or storage types behind a single interconnection point. Resources can participate as independent Co-Located Resources (each a separate market unit) or as a Hybrid Resource (single market unit with unit-specific accreditation). A closed-loop hybrid with storage can charge from grid; an open-loop hybrid cannot. Facilities with independent components can elect either model; those with significant interdependency (e.g., storage unable to charge from grid) must use the hybrid model. Classification must be declared 6 months before in-service date; capacity resources must choose the same model for both capacity and energy markets and must re-elect within RAA Schedule 9.2 deadlines; energy-only resources can change election once per calendar year with notice by May 30. For any MTF, the site Maximum Facility Output (MFO) cannot be exceeded in real time and capacity-must-offer obligations apply to the original Certified Interconnection Rights. The four use cases illustrate that inverter-gen + storage (like solar + battery or wind + battery) and non-inverter gen + storage (gas + battery) can form hybrids, while non-inverter gen + inverter gen (gas + solar) must participate as co-located resources. This framework directly affects pumped-storage hydro owners considering co-location with other generation or considering participation in paired-resource models, constraining how PSH state-of-charge management interacts with capacity accreditation and must-offer rules.

title: Item 05 – Nextgen Updates Dp2 Reminders
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document describes updates to PJM's NextGen interconnection application system, which directly affects the operational and compliance obligations for all interconnection applicants including hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking queue entry, site-control documentation, and progression through decision points.
ai_summary: PJM has released updates to its NextGen interconnection platform affecting how applicants submit and manage interconnection requests. Key changes include: (1) change-history tracking by page with user attribution; (2) PJM's ability to edit certain application fields (company profile, applicant information, and banking data excepted) with email notification to applicants; (3) kickoff-call notes now visible to all parties (PJM, transmission owner, project developer); (4) restricted editing until Decision Point 2 election is made; (5) limited document uploads during DP2 site-control and supporting-documents phases; and (6) stricter submission validation requiring all sections saved and deficiency comments resolved before the submit button enables. A known bug affecting nearly all TC2 projects (insufficient-funds-deposit warning for TC2 study deposits) does not invalidate DP2 submissions. Timeline: Replacement Generation went live April 2026; Expedited Interconnection Track is July 2026; Surplus goes live August/September 2026; Upgrade track rollout TBD. Hydro and PSH applicants must follow these new workflows to advance projects through the interconnection queue; any deficiencies in the submission process or misunderstanding of the new validation requirements could delay or jeopardize project advancement.

ai_summary: PJM is running the Effective Load Carrying Capability (ELCC) and Forced Penetration Ratio (FPR) analysis for the 2029/30 Delivery Year BRA (Capacity Auction) with updated load forecasts. No procedural changes to the ELCC methodology are planned relative to the prior run. For hydro and PSH owners, the winter-heavy load shape directly influences accreditation of winter-peaking and seasonal-flexible resources; PJM expects to release the IRM and FPR first read and endorsement at the MRC/MC in July/August 2026. Deadlines for resource-portfolio lock-in include Notice of Intent to Offer (June 12, 2026), Dual Fuel Attestation (June 15, 2026), and Transitional Deliverability Study and Announced Deactivation Review (both June 2026).

title: Item 02 – Pjms Rcstf Proposal Summary
relevance_reason: PJM's proposal restructures reserve products and requirements, introducing new ramping and uncertainty reserves (10-min and 30-min RUR) with locational procurement and 10-min response times that will directly affect hydro and PSH revenue and dispatch obligations.
initiatives: Reserve Certainty and Resource Flexibility Incentives [solution alternatives proposed]
ai_summary: PJM proposes restructuring its reserve construct to align scheduling with operational needs and address growing ramping and uncertainty requirements. The proposal introduces three new reserve products—10-minute Ramp and Uncertainty (RUR), 10-minute Ramp and Uncertainty Down, and 30-minute RUR—each with specified offer caps ($10/MWh day-ahead, $0 real-time), balancing settlement, and locational procurement. These products carry fast response requirements (10–30 min) that hydro and PSH are positioned to supply, and are priced at 1.25× the clearing price or cost-to-provide, whichever is higher. The proposal also shifts Day-Ahead Scheduling Reserve from a fixed 4.51%-of-peak formula to a dynamic, risk-based framework classified by operating-day conditions. Hydro and pumped-storage operators will directly participate in procurement of these ramping/flexibility products, which represent new revenue streams for fast-responding resources; exposure depends on portfolio flexibility and locational advantage. The document does not state a vote date or comment deadline.

title: Item 03a – Imm Rcstf Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's reserve product redesign proposal directly affects hydro revenue through reserve-clearing incentives, online/offline reserve mix, and reserve performance accountability — reserve-eligible hydro and PSH earn revenue from reserve provision and market-efficiency changes that alter clearing prices and call-out frequency.
initiatives: Reserve Certainty and Resource Flexibility Incentives [solution alternatives proposed]
ai_summary: The Independent Market Monitor (IMM) presents its interim reserve-redesign proposal for PJM's Reliability Committee, opposing PJM's parallel proposal. The IMM recommends three sequential implementation packages: (1) incorporating net load forecast uncertainty into reserve calculations (near-term priority); (2) reserve performance measurement and settlement changes; (3) reserve eligibility and market-clearing engine changes. The critical difference is the online/offline reserve mix: PJM proposes all 10-minute reserves be online-only, significantly raising production costs and clearing prices; the IMM proposes maintaining offline reserve capability (primary reserves) and synchronized (online) reserves separately. The IMM contends PJM's approach reduces market efficiency and jeopardizes reliability if current fast-start generators drop fast-start capability to avoid the online-reserve requirement. Solar generation in PJM has grown dramatically (14.3 GW maximum hourly output in 2026, 2.4% of annual generation), driving net-load uncertainty that both proposals address but with different reserve adequacy methods — the IMM's approach captures generator performance uncertainty in 30-minute reserves and includes demand response as eligible supply. No vote date or next-step deadline is stated; the presentation positions the IMM proposal as the operational-necessity baseline against PJM's preferred-design alternative.

title: Updated Information Only – Rt Market Pricing Impact Multi Step Ordcs With Extended Requirements 06092026
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Operating Reserve Demand Curve (ORDC) restructuring changes ancillary-services revenue for reserve suppliers; multi-step ORDCs and unnesting of synchronized reserves affects how hydro's reserve products are priced and when they clear, with particular impact on 10-minute and 30-minute reserve pricing and frequency of scarcity events.
initiatives: Reserve Certainty and Resource Flexibility Incentives [solution alternatives proposed]
ai_summary: PJM's Advanced Analytics team analyzed 2025 real-time market pricing impacts from proposed multi-step Operating Reserve Demand Curves (ORDCs) unnesting 10-minute Synchronized Reserve from 30-minute reserves and creating explicit 30-minute and 10-minute Online Reserve products. The Full Case scenario (updated ORDCs with Primary Reserve not clearing) showed 10-minute Online reserve prices rising from $10.25/MWh to $13.04/MWh (+$2.79), energy LMPs rising $0.80/MWh, uplift declining 3.8%, and load reserve payments rising $216M for 10-minute Online reserves—with greatest price pressure during peak hours when reserves are most needed. The simulation found that prepositioning units with ramping products in the multi-step ORDC case reduced scarcity-event frequency compared to the base case, particularly during two modeled shortage events (3/3/2025 and 8/12/2025), suggesting improved reserve-positioning efficiency, though the analysis carries limitations (PROBE PD simulation tool, combustion-turbine-only commitment, 30-minute Online requirement added to SR requirement rather than separately modeled). Document presented to RCSTF with no stated vote or deadline; further results posted post-presentation.

title: Consent Agenda B – 1 Manual 11 Revisions Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 – Presentation
relevance_reason: Advance scheduling of resources with >24-hour start-up times affects how dispatchable generation (including hydroelectric units and pumped-storage plants with slow start times) are committed, offered, and compensated in PJM's day-ahead and real-time energy markets, directly impacting revenue realization and offer-management obligations for slow-start hydro and PSH.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM has formalized Manual 11 revisions implementing advance pre-scheduling of generation resources with start-up or notification times exceeding 24 hours, committing them to cost-based offers prior to the day-ahead market close to ensure resource availability during forecasted system emergencies (lessons from Winter Storm Elliott). Resources pre-scheduled in advance must be committed on cost-based schedules subject to Fuel Cost Policy rules; those with >24-hour start times cannot update cost-based offers after advance scheduling, while shorter start-time resources may update cost-based offers only through the day-ahead market. The revision locks the 'Switch to Cost Schedule' flag automatically for advance-scheduled units, preventing market sellers from reverting to price-based schedules for the scheduled operating days. Most exposed are slow-start hydroelectric and pumped-storage units (>24-hour commitment lead time) in PJM's footprint, which lose offer flexibility during emergency conditions but gain certainty of commitment; the practical revenue effect depends on whether cost-based offer caps reflect true opportunity cost. Implementation timeline: MIC first read May 26, 2026; MRC first and second reads June 24, 2026; effective date and FERC notice scheduled thereafter.

title: Consent Agenda B – 2 Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Cost-capping and offer-placement rules for resources with long startup/notification times (>24 hours) scheduled before the day-ahead market affect how hydro and pumped-storage operators with extended lead times structure bids and recover costs in PJM's energy market, with direct implications for revenue recovery under cost-based dispatch.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) to FERC approval order ER26-527-000 (approved January 13, 2026) under Phase 1 of an issue charge addressing resources scheduled prior to the day-ahead market. The revisions clarify cost-capping of resources with startup or notification times exceeding 24 hours when called in advance of the day-ahead market, specify how such resources are placed on the cheapest available cost-based offer, and refine the application of the "switch to cost" flag in both day-ahead and real-time operations. Pumped-storage hydro with extended notification or startup times, run-of-river plants with multi-day scheduling constraints, and other hydro resources that may be committed ahead of the day-ahead market could be affected by these offer-placement and cost-recovery rules; the revisions were approved by the Market Implementation Committee on June 3, 2026 and are scheduled to take effect June 24, 2026.

title: Consent Agenda B – 3 Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 11 Revision 137 includes updates to hydropower operation rules (Section 9.3) and pumped storage modeling (Attachment B), plus regulation redesign and advanced scheduled resources affecting ancillary-service revenue and dispatch obligations for hydro units.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 Revision 137 (effective June 1, 2026) incorporates three market-design updates relevant to hydro: (1) Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 updates (FERC ER26-0527, Section 2.3.3) affecting how scheduled resources participate in energy markets; (2) Regulation Redesign Phase 1 (FERC ER24-1772, Sections 2.5, 3, 4.4, 9.3) reshaping the regulation market that hydro traditionally supplies; and (3) Economic Demand Response parameter updates (FERC ER24-3135, Section 10.3) with secondary effects on energy and reserve clearing. The document confirms revisions to hydropower operation rules (Section 9.3) and pumped storage modeling assumptions (Attachment B) are now in effect. For conventional hydro and pumped-storage operators in PJM, the regulation redesign and ASR Phase 1 changes alter both offer/bid mechanics and mileage/opportunity-cost calculations; the effective date is June 1, 2026, with no comment deadline noted in this agenda item (revision was approved September 30, 2025).

title: Consent Agenda C – 1 Manuals 20a – M22 Periodic Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 20A defines ELCC Class Rating and Resource Specific ELCC Rating methodologies that directly determine hydro's and PSH's capacity accreditation in PJM's resource-adequacy framework, affecting their RA revenue and portfolio showings.
ai_summary: PJM is conducting periodic cover-to-cover reviews of Manual 20A (Resource Adequacy Analysis, Revision 3) and Manual 22 (Generator Resource Performance Indices, Revision 19), with administrative and definitional updates. Manual 20A adds two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE) and reformats ELCC Class Rating and Resource Specific ELCC Rating calculations from plain text to formula presentation—clarifying but not changing the hydro-specific capacity accreditation methods. Manual 22 updates cross-references and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor to its introduction. The manuals passed first read at PC on May 8, 2026; second read/endorsement vote is scheduled for June 24, 2026 at the Markets & Reliability Committee, with final timeline through June 2026.

title: Consent Agenda C – 3 Manual 20a Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: ELCC accreditation study methodology directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited in PJM's capacity market and resource adequacy framework, determining their participation value and revenue exposure.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A (Revision 3, effective May 20, 2026) documents the Reserve Requirement Study (RRS), Effective Load Carrying Capability (ELCC) Study, and Capacity Emergency Transfer Objective (CETO) methodologies that determine capacity resource accreditation for the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM). Revision 3 adds two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE), converts ELCC Class Rating and Resource Specific ELCC Rating calculations to formula-based presentations, and incorporates conforming changes from FERC Order 2222 on DER aggregation (Revision 2). The ELCC accreditation rating directly sets how much capacity credit hydropower and PSH can claim in PJM's auction, affecting both reserve-margin requirements and individual resource pricing signals—hydro's accreditation depends on how the study models seasonal availability and effective contribution to peak load carrying. No comment deadline or vote date is stated; this is an approved operational manual effective as of the stated dates.

title: Consent Agenda C – 4 Manual 22 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 22 defines generator resource performance indices (forced outage rates, availability factors, accreditation metrics) that directly affect how PJM calculates UCAP accreditation and capacity resource performance — a key revenue and compliance driver for all dispatchable generators including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 22 Revision 19 (effective May 20, 2026) updates generator performance-index definitions and calculations used to establish resource adequacy and capacity-market accreditation. The revision adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor metrics to the Introduction and cross-references updated PJM Manuals 20A and 21B governing resource adequacy analysis and capability determination. These indices — including Equivalent Availability Factor, Equivalent Forced Outage Rates, and accreditation factors — feed directly into UCAP calculation and capacity-auction participation for all generating units, including hydro and pumped-storage plants. Andrew Gledhill (Resource Adequacy Planning) manages the manual; no external stakeholder positions or comment deadlines are identified in this redline agenda item.

title: Item 01 – 1 Schedule 2 Penalties – Presentation
relevance_reason: MITIGATION-OFFERS: This modifies PJM's penalty formula for cost-based offers that violate approved Fuel Cost Policies, directly affecting how use-limited resources (including hydropower and pumped storage) are subject to default-energy-bid and opportunity-cost enforcement—a core market-power mitigation mechanism for storage.
initiatives: Schedule 2 Penalty Calculations [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM proposes revising Operating Agreement Schedule 2 section 6.1 penalty formulae to prevent Market Sellers from receiving credits (negative penalties) when cost-based offers are non-compliant and real-time LMPs are negative. The revised formula introduces an error-identification factor (E = 0.25 for self-reported errors, 1 otherwise), a market-impact factor (I) that escalates penalties if non-compliance continues after PJM or MMU notice, and a duration multiplier (d) capping at 15 days. This change tightens enforcement of offer compliance for all dispatchable and storage resources subject to cost-based offer rules—hydro and PSH are typically among resources required to submit cost-based offers, so stricter penalties and elimination of negative-penalty loopholes increases the cost of offer errors and reinforces compliance discipline. First Read was May 20, 2026; Markets & Reliability Committee vote scheduled June 24, 2026.

title: Item 01 – 2 Schedule 2 Penalties Oa Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Cost-based offer penalties directly affect hydro operators' compliance obligations and how they manage offer submissions under PJM rules; the penalty structure and escalation mechanics impact use-limited resource mitigation and offer-development incentives.
initiatives: Schedule 2 Penalty Calculations [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM's Markets and Reliability Committee is revising Schedule 2 penalties for non-compliant cost-based offers. The redline text establishes a two-tier penalty structure: (1) a non-escalating penalty using a formula weighted by LMP, available capacity, a self-identification factor (0.25 if self-reported, 1.0 otherwise), and a market-impact factor that ranges from 0.1 to 1.0 depending on whether the resource cleared, ran, passed pivotal-supplier tests, or submitted offers above $1,000/MWh; and (2) a daily escalating penalty (multiplied by day count d, starting at 2 and capping at 15) for continuing violations after PJM notification. Hydro operators subject to cost-based offer requirements—particularly pumped storage and run-of-river resources with use-limited characteristics—face heightened compliance costs if offer errors persist; the escalating penalty's cap at day 15 and the incentive structure (lower penalties for self-reported errors) will shape whether operators err on the side of conservative offers or market-responsive pricing. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this redline.

title: Item 01 – 4 Schedule 2 Penalties Issue Charge – Informational Only
relevance_reason: Cost-based offer compliance and penalty calculations under PJM Schedule 2 directly affect how dispatchable and storage resources can submit competitive energy bids; the mechanics of penalty enforcement—especially the treatment of negative LMPs—touch revenue at the point of bid validation, a compliance obligation for all Market Sellers.
initiatives: Schedule 2 Penalty Calculations [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM is addressing a technical flaw in the penalty formula for Market Sellers who violate approved Fuel Cost Policies or Schedule 2 requirements: when negative LMPs occur, the existing penalty calculation under Operating Agreement Schedule 2, section 6.1 can produce negative (and thus uncollectible or perverse) penalties. The work is high-priority and immediate; the Market Implementation Committee will develop a proposed solution for tariff revision and manual updates. Hydro and storage operators subject to cost-based offer requirements (especially pumped storage in cost-based mode, and any unit filing a fuel-cost policy) should monitor the proposed fix to confirm it preserves penalty enforceability without creating unintended side effects in negative-price periods—a key risk during high-renewable, low-load hours when hydro's opportunity cost is most relevant.

title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 13 Annual Review Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 13 Revision 98 updates PJM's emergency operating procedures with direct implications for hydro and PSH dispatch obligations, reserve contributions during capacity/transmission emergencies, and the new Capacity Advisory mechanism that signals scarcity conditions affecting real-time operations.
stakeholders: Lisa Drauschak (PJM); Mike Handlin (PJM); Paul Dajewski (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM approved Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updating emergency operations procedures to align with NERC EOP-011 standards and address capacity-deficient conditions occurring outside traditional weather-alert triggers. Key changes include: (1) a new Capacity Advisory mechanism providing advance notice when available generating capacity approaches demand requirements, leveraging forecasted peak load, day-ahead reserve requirements, and net interchange; (2) Attachment O establishing a conditional procedure for emergency use of backup generators (triggered only by DOE Section 202(c) orders) with coordinated PJM and Transmission Owner actions during capacity and transmission emergencies; (3) clarifications separating transmission-security emergency procedures from capacity-based procedures, removing capacity-specific actions (Emergency Bid Process, export recall, capacity load shed) from transmission-security triggers; and (4) updated language for Advanced Scheduled Resources under cold- and hot-weather alerts. Hydro operators must understand the Capacity Advisory's forecasting inputs and threshold logic, as early signals of tight capacity may activate voluntary or mandatory dispatch obligations; pumped-storage operators should note the manual's treatment of generation scheduling during emergencies and any implications for charging-window availability. Most exposed: all dispatchable hydro and PSH participating in PJM's real-time market and ancillary-services offerings. No member comment deadline is stated; the revision was endorsed by the Operating Committee on June 4, 2026.

title: Item 02 – 3 Manual 13 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 13 establishes PJM's emergency operations procedures including capacity emergencies, reserve requirements, and load management actions that directly govern how dispatchable resources including hydropower respond to system stress, affecting must-offer obligations and revenue opportunities during scarcity events.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13: Emergency Operations (Revision 98, effective July 23, 2026) establishes procedures for capacity emergencies, reserve management, and transmission security including load shedding and emergency generation dispatch. The revision reorganizes transmission-security emergency procedures to separate them from capacity-focused actions, redefines several alert/action sequences, and adds a new attachment on emergency use of back-up generators. For hydro and PSH operators, Manual 13 defines the operational obligations during emergency conditions—including minimum generation requirements (Attachment H), load-dump capability assessment and allocation (Attachments E, F, N), and dispatch directives for transmission relief—that interact with revenue accrual during scarcity pricing events and state-of-charge management for pumped storage. Operators in the PJM footprint should review Revision 98 for changes to alert sequencing and reserve/generation requirements that may affect their emergency-response positioning and ancillary-service obligations.

title: Item 03 – 1 Flexible Resource Loc Eligibility – Presentation
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Flexible Resource definition refinement affects lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) crediting eligibility for dispatchable resources, including hydro with fast-start capability; the proposal tightens which resources qualify for LOC payments when held out of real-time operation, altering revenue certainty for flexible hydro units and changing the operational expectations PJM will impose on fast-start dispatch.
initiatives: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM proposes to refine the definition of "Flexible Resources" to require that all available operating schedules meet the flexibility criteria (combined startup and notification time ≤ 2 hours, minimum run time ≤ 2 hours), rather than permitting flexibility on only some schedules. Under current rules, a unit committed day-ahead on a flexible schedule but holding an inflexible backup schedule can still receive Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Cost (LOC) credits if not called in real-time, even though it may be unable to start under real-time dispatch due to its inflexible schedule(s). The proposal would classify such units as inflexible for real-time and LOC purposes, restricting LOC eligibility to units that meet flexibility on all schedules and are not called due to transmission or reliability constraints. Tariff and Manual updates (Attachments K, 11, and 28) are under review; the Markets and Reliability Committee is scheduled to endorse the proposal on July 28, 2026, with FERC filing to follow. Fast-start hydro and PSH units with multiple offer schedules are most exposed: those unable to configure all available cost-based schedules as flexible will lose LOC protection and be held to inflexible day-ahead commitments.

title: Item 03 – 2 Proposed Tariff Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Proposed tariff revisions redefine how hydroelectric resources are scheduled and compensated for lost opportunity costs when curtailed for reliability, directly affecting hydro revenue under PJM's pool-scheduling and real-time dispatch framework, with special provisions for hydroelectric and pumped-storage units.
initiatives: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM proposes tariff changes to its pool-scheduling rules and lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) compensation framework. Key provisions: (1) hydropower units can only be pool-scheduled if they are pumped-storage units and use PJM's hydro optimization tool in the Day-ahead Energy Market; (2) hydroelectric resources that are pool-scheduled or self-scheduled and curtailed by PJM for transmission or reliability reasons receive LOC compensation under a formula detailed in section 3.2.3(f-2), aligned with methodology in sections 3.2.2(d) and 3.2.3A(f); (3) a new section 3.2.3(e-2) applies Tracking Balancing Net Revenues to Flexible Resources (startup and notification ≤2 hours, minimum run ≤2 hours) that are self-scheduled but committed in Day-ahead, modifying how their real-time compensation is calculated when PJM does not call them to run. The document is a redline tariff revision presented to the Markets and Reliability Committee on June 24, 2026; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 11 And 28 Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM's changes to lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) eligibility rules for 'Flexible Resources' (fast-responding CTs and diesels) directly affect how PJM compensates use-limited resources—hydro and pumped storage—when held out of the market despite day-ahead commitment, a canonical revenue stream for dispatchable resources and the structural analog to how hydro's opportunity costs are treated under market power mitigation.
initiatives: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM is amending Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) and Manual 28 (Operating Agreement Accounting) to clarify lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) eligibility for 'Flexible Resources' (fast-response CTs and diesels) committed day-ahead but not dispatched in real-time, or denied startup for reliability/transmission reasons. The changes memorialize the expectation that Flexible Resources wait for PJM dispatch instructions before running, clarify offer-capping applicability, and specify LOC credit eligibility and ineligibility conditions. The tariff and manuals are in concurrent FERC approval, with MRC voting scheduled July 28, 2026. The LOC framework—compensating resources for foregone opportunity when constrained out despite a day-ahead commitment—is the same mechanism PJM uses for hydro and storage under mitigation rules; these revisions to Flexible Resource LOC treatment establish precedent and may signal how PJM intends to handle similar situations for dispatchable hydro, pumped storage, or other use-limited resources facing transmission or reliability constraints.

title: Item 03 – 4 Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 11 revisions to market seller rules, offer-capping procedures, and flexible-resource definition directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources submit offers, self-schedule, and face market power mitigation in PJM's energy and reserve markets.
initiatives: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM's Manual 11 redline updates the business rules for Market Sellers, with particular emphasis on Hybrid Resources and Energy Storage Resources (ESR) participation, flexible-resource definitions, and offer-capping procedures under the Three-Pivotal Supplier Test. Hybrid Resources and ESRs may now self-schedule only (not offer into day-ahead), must specify hourly operating mode and range, and trigger notification requirements when >10 MW. Offer-capping rules are clarified for combustion turbines and diesels designated as Flexible Resources—these units are now expected to await dispatch instruction rather than run automatically, preserving their flexibility value but exposing them to lost-opportunity-cost credits if not called. For non-CT resources and non-Flexible CT/diesel units, offer-capping carries through from day-ahead to real-time at the same schedule; Flexible Resources face re-evaluation for market power at real-time commitment. The revision enables cost-based offer stickiness (once dispatched on cost basis, unit remains cost-capped regardless of updated offers) and clarifies the initial capping determination period. Most exposed are pumped-storage and other dispatchable hydro resources that compete with CTs/diesels for flexibility revenue; any PSH owner using PJM's ESR model or treating a hybrid asset will need to audit self-scheduling constraints and mode-change notification procedures. The document does not state a comment deadline or vote date.

title: Item 03 – 5 Manual 28 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 28 governs the accounting and settlement of ancillary services (regulation, operating reserves, synchronized reserves, non-synchronized reserves, secondary reserves), transmission congestion and losses, and energy storage charging — all critical revenue and obligation mechanisms for hydro and pumped-storage resources in PJM.
initiatives: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility [awaiting senior committee approval]
ai_summary: PJM Manual 28 (Revision 106, effective July 29, 2026) is the definitive operating manual for PJM's market accounting and settlement procedures. This redline revision updates rules for regulation accounting (Section 4), operating reserve accounting (Section 5), synchronized and non-synchronized reserve accounting (Sections 6–7), secondary reserve accounting (Section 19), transmission congestion and loss accounting (Sections 8–9), and notably for pumped-storage operators, charging energy accounting for open-loop hybrid and energy storage resources (Section 22). Because the document is a redline, specific substantive changes are not visible in the table of contents alone; hydro and PSH owners must review the marked-up text to identify which revenue-settlement formulas, accreditation criteria, charge/credit calculations, or metering reconciliation procedures have shifted. The effective date is July 29, 2026.

title: Item 04 – 3 Iprtf Gdecs Iii April 2026 Chart
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: interconnection queue and application-process reforms (IPRTF GDECS III) directly affect how new hydro and PSH projects navigate PJM interconnection requirements, queue cycles, and approval timelines — this is clean-up/clarification to Parts VII–IX tariff language governing the interconnection process that all new generation (including pumped storage) must comply with.
stakeholders: PJM Legal Service Agreement Team Attorneys (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Legal Service Agreement Team has proposed clean-up, clarifications, and corrections to Tariff Parts VII–IX governing the interconnection reform process (Interconnection Reform Process Task Force GDECS Number III), with revisions submitted April 17, 2026. The document addresses definitional and formatting corrections across 43 numbered items spanning application requirements, interconnection facilities cost allocation, and generator interconnection agreement language. The proposals include minor grammatical edits, corrected cross-references, and clarifications to terms like "Application and Studies Agreement," "Cancellation Costs," and "Decision Point I" that establish timelines and cost responsibility rules for Project Developers (including hydro and PSH developers) entering new service requests. This is a tariff clean-up rather than a substantive redesign, but it codifies the procedural and cost-allocation framework through which all new hydro interconnections in PJM flow — corrections to Decision Point definitions, cancellation-cost allocation, and obligation language will apply uniformly to pumped-storage and conventional hydro developers submitting applications.

title: Item 05a – Destf Phase Ii Scope Status Report – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's Deactivation Enhancement Task Force Phase II addresses RMR (Reliability Must-Run) contracts and alternatives including energy storage and transmission solutions, which directly affects how PJM will procure reliability services from retiring generators and whether hydro/storage assets can compete as alternatives—a strategic option and revenue mechanism for NHA members in PJM.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Deactivation Enhancement Task Force Phase II completed scope development for six work areas, with three proposals advancing: Scope Areas 4/5 (accelerating transmission solutions and alternatives to RMR contracts) failed to achieve >50% support and remain unresolved; Scope Area 6 (pro forma RMR arrangements and capacity market rule updates for retained generators) received PJM's proposal with >50% support and was offered for first read at the June 24, 2026 Markets & Reliability Committee meeting. The Phase II effort will determine PJM's framework for evaluating energy storage, demand-side solutions, and transmission enhancements as alternatives to long-term RMR contracts when generators retire—a direct pathway for hydro and pumped storage to compete for reliability-driven revenue in place of mothballed conventional units, though the lack of consensus on Scope 4/5 signals continued disagreement on how to fairly compare and procure such alternatives.

title: Item 05b – 1 Pro Forma Rmr Agreement – Presentation
relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must-Run) agreements are a key strategic and financial tool for hydroelectric operators facing deactivation; this presentation establishes PJM's pro forma RMR framework, which determines the path by which hydro owners can recover costs and remain operational when transmission constraints require their continued service.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM presented its pro forma Reliability Must-Run (RMR) Agreement framework, streamlining the process by which generators—especially old or transmission-constrained units—can receive cost recovery to continue operating after leaving the market to address thermal, voltage, or stability violations. The pro forma approach pre-approves non-rate terms and conditions, requiring FERC acceptance of only the rate component (either the Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit formula or a full cost-of-service filing), eliminating the need for hearing and settlement judge procedures and reducing process friction. Hydroelectric facilities, particularly older reservoir-based units prone to becoming stranded as markets shift, may use this pathway to recover operating costs when transmission upgrades are pending; revenues from energy, capacity, and ancillary-services sales are credited against RMR charges, creating a hybrid cost-plus mechanism. The framework applies to units regardless of fuel type and is effective only upon FERC approval; PJM terminates the RMR when it determines the constraint is resolved. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in this presentation slide deck.

title: Item 05b – 2 OATT Att Dd53 – Redline
relevance_reason: CAPACITY-RA MECHANISM: this tariff redline modifies how generation capacity resources (including hydro) are accredited, committed, and compensated in PJM's capacity market (RPM), directly affecting resource-adequacy obligations, accreditation rules, and the revenue treatment of must-run/reliability-retained resources—a canonical hydro participation pathway.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's tariff amendment (Attachment DD, section 5.3) revises the commitment and compensation rules for Generation Capacity Resources retained under must-run or transmission-reliability cost-of-service agreements. The redline clarifies that such resources must offer their accredited UCAP at $0/MW-day into the Base Residual Auction (not subject to market-clearing mechanisms) and requires PJM to backstop any unmet capacity via the Third Incremental Auction. Critically, the amendment limits this automatic $0 offer through delivery year 2028/2029 (unless PJM and FERC extend it), and excludes such resources from performance-bonus eligibility and balancing-ratio calculations. All capacity revenues flow to the zone that funded the asset's reliability mission, not to the resource operator. This affects any hydro asset retained for transmission reliability or deactivation-avoidance—particularly reservoirs and run-of-river plants in constrained zones—by locking them into below-market revenue recovery and removing upside from performance incentives. The document is in first-read status at the June 24, 2026 MRC; next steps and comment deadlines are not stated in this excerpt.

title: Item 05b – 3 Pro Forma Rmr – Redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: RMR (Reliability Must Run) agreements directly affect hydro and fossil generators facing deactivation — this pro forma tariff controls when generators can be forced to stay online, project-investment approval (critical for maintaining aging hydro units), compensation mechanisms (Section 4), and termination rights that constrain a hydro owner's exit decisions and asset management.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM is revising its form Reliability Must Run Service Agreement for first read at the June 24, 2026 Markets and Reliability Committee meeting. The redlined pro forma establishes the terms under which PJM can require a generation owner to continue operating a resource designated as critical for transmission reliability, including scheduling/dispatch authority (Section 3), project-investment approval requirements (Section 4, not shown here but referenced), and termination triggers (Sections 2.2–2.3). Key hydro-specific provisions: Generation Owner may terminate only on "Catastrophic Force Majeure" or if PJM denies necessary Project Investment, with a 61-day notice requirement; PJM may extend the term unilaterally if reliability need persists; and the resource must be offered into Day-ahead and Real-time markets at "Unavailable" status until PJM dispatches. For hydro operators, this agreement determines whether aging run-of-river or reservoir plants facing retirement can be forced to remain online, what capital repairs PJM must fund, and how long the contract lock lasts — directly affecting retirement-planning flexibility and revenue certainty during the reliability-need window.

title: Item 05b – 4 Tariff Part V Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Generation deactivation rules and mothballing/RMR processes directly affect hydro owner fleet strategy, retirement timing, and must-run revenue recovery — a core strategic option for asset owners facing obsolescence or economic pressure.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM Part V tariff revisions (first read at June 24, 2026 MRC) govern generation deactivation notice, reliability-impact review, and continued operation under must-run arrangements. Generation owners must provide 12 months' notice; PJM then has 4–5 months to assess whether deactivation would harm transmission reliability. If reliability impacts are found, owners can elect either cost-of-service recovery or a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC) formula—a monthly payment that escalates from 110% in year one to 150% by year four of the deactivation-delay period, less actual net market revenues. The formula applies to generation (including hydropower) that continues operating beyond its desired retirement date to support grid reliability. Most relevant to aging hydro plants facing retirement but needed for transmission support or seasonal energy adequacy; the DACC structure and multiplier schedule directly determine whether an extended operating life is economically viable.

title: Item 05c – 1 Destf Ceg Proposal – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's Reliability Must-Run (RMR) agreement framework directly governs retirement and mothball processes for dispatchable resources, a key strategic option for hydro and storage owners; the proposal affects cost recovery, termination rights, and capacity-market treatment of retained units.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: Constellation Energy presents proposed amendments to PJM's Pro Forma RMR Agreement (used when PJM retains a retiring unit for transmission system reliability) addressing cost-recovery certainty, termination rights, and capacity-market participation. The proposal would allow asset owners to terminate if PJM does not approve Project Investment, upon catastrophic force majeure, or with 90 days' notice under OATT Section 113.3, provided all prudently incurred costs are recoverable; it would also prevent blanket inclusion of RMR resources' capacity accreditation in RPM auctions unless specific resource-level terms are met and revenues credited to cost-payers. The changes aim to ensure RMR units do not face performance obligations without clear cost-recovery pathways prior to FERC rate-setting. This reshapes the economics of mothballing versus operational reserve retention for owners considering exit — a material decision point for aging hydro and storage assets in PJM.

title: Item 05c – 2 Ceg Pro Forma Rmr – Redline
relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must Run) agreements are a critical strategic option and revenue mechanism for hydropower and pumped-storage operators facing deactivation but offering essential grid stability services; this redline form shapes the terms under which hydro/PSH plants can be retained and compensated for reliability service.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM has circulated a revised pro forma Reliability Must Run Service Agreement (tariff Part V, Subpart A) for discussion at the June 24, 2026 Markets and Reliability Committee. The agreement establishes the contractual framework by which PJM can compel continued operation of generation resources (including hydro and PSH) that have submitted deactivation notices but are deemed necessary for transmission system reliability. Key terms include: (1) Generation Owner may terminate only on Catastrophic Force Majeure, if PJM denies necessary Project Investment, or by exercising standard deactivation rights with 90 days' notice; (2) PJM may terminate on 61 days' notice if the RMR Resource is no longer needed, with full recovery of prudently incurred costs; (3) PJM may extend the term unilaterally, requiring Generation Owner response within 14 days and permitting renegotiation of compensation; and (4) PJM retains dispatch authority over the RMR Resource for transmission limits, system restoration, capacity emergencies, or operational testing, subject only to owner-disclosed operating limitations. The document is currently in working-draft, FOR DISCUSSION PURPOSES ONLY status. For hydro and PSH owners, this revised form directly affects exit optionality: the extension clause (Section 2.5) and PJM's unilateral termination right (Section 2.3) determine how long a resource can be held in operation and whether compensation can be renegotiated mid-agreement. Owners with plants facing retirement but offering essential voltage support, black-start capability, or ramping—a common profile for aging hydro and PSH—should monitor whether Section 4 (compensation mechanism, not fully excerpted here) provides adequate coverage of Project Investment (repairs, NERC compliance, maintenance with overtime) and whether the 60-day termination notice gives sufficient runway to execute alternative retirement or repurposing strategies.

title: Item 05d – 1 Destf PJM Package April 2026 – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's Deactivation, Energy, Storage, Transmission, and Flexibility (DESTF) Phase 2 proposal directly affects hydro and storage RMR (Reliability Must Run) processes and creates expedited interconnection pathways that touch resource-adequacy and retirement-planning strategy for all generation including hydropower.
initiatives: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules [evaluation in progress]
stakeholders: David Anders (PJM); Jonathan Ruffin (PJM); Michael Herman (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM proposes a two-path framework to reduce or eliminate Reliability Must Run (RMR) obligations when generation deactivates. Path 1A/1B relies on PJM-led transmission alternatives (including demand-side and generation-from-transmission mechanisms like GET/ATTs and SATOA); Path 2 allows states to sponsor replacement generation with PJM technical support, with both paths requiring the deactivating unit to remain under RMR until the replacement is energized. The proposal incorporates Coalition stakeholder input on proactive 5-year deactivation analysis and dual competitive windows but explicitly excludes generation solutions from PJM's Path 1 procurement, citing FPA jurisdictional limits. Vote is scheduled for after May 19, 2026. This affects hydro owners subject to must-offer rules or considering retirement/deactivation by changing the duration and conditionality of RMR obligations and opening state-led generation procurement as an alternative to transmission reinforcement—relevant to both conventional hydropower and pumped storage considering fleet decisions in PJM.

title: Item 06b – 1 Manual M14d Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM Manual M14D Revision 71 mandates reactive power control requirements for all synchronous generators, including hydroelectric units; this directly affects hydro operators' compliance obligations and may constrain operational flexibility in reactive support provision, a traditional revenue stream.
stakeholders: Dave Anders (PJM); Lisa Drauschak (PJM); Ryan Nice (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual M14D to clarify and tighten reactive power (VAR) control requirements for all generators in response to the May 2026 NERC ERO Enterprise CMEP Practice Guide (which lowered IBR registration threshold to 20 MVA/60 kV) and emerging low-voltage stability risks highlighted by the 2025 Spain blackout. The revisions require: (1) generators operate exclusively in automatic voltage-control mode unless documented exceptions are approved by PJM and the Transmission Owner; (2) define acceptable volt-VAR boundaries and VAR control ranges per MW output (32.87% of max MW capacity); and (3) establish a three-party investigative process (PJM, TO, and generator owner/operator) for reactive control performance issues, with PJM empowered to direct AVR and inverter settings changes. For conventional hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities, this narrows operational flexibility in reactive-power dispatch—generators can no longer operate in constant-MVAR, constant power-factor, or manual modes without PJM/TO approval—potentially reducing the ability to optimize reactive output for revenue or water management. The revision moves to OC and SOS for first read June 24, 2026, with MRC review June 24, and endorsement timeline extending through July.

title: Item 06b – 2 Manual M14d Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: OBLIGATION: PJM is updating generator operational requirements for reactive power (volt-var and watt-var behavior) under NERC Category 2 GO standards, which applies to synchronous generators including conventional hydro and pumped-storage units operating in PJM's footprint, affecting their compliance obligations for reactive-control modes and VAR output.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual M14D (Generator Operational Requirements), sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D, effective July 23, 2026, to incorporate NERC's new Category 2 GO standard and clarify reactive-power control requirements. The revision adds references to Category 2 GO generators, specifies allowable automatic voltage regulator (AVR) modes, and introduces volt-var and watt-var performance graphs with acceptable operating ranges, along with monitoring procedures where PJM may direct gen owner/operators to address voltage issues. The change is driven by PJM's dual operational pressures: rising solar/IBR penetration and load growth exceeding new generation, both exacerbating voltage stability risks. Hydro operators in PJM (primarily PSH and conventional generation) must update their VAR control procedures and performance commitments to comply; MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

title: Item 06b – 3 Manual 14d Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14D prescribes operational compliance obligations for all PJM generators including hydroelectric facilities—metering, dispatch, data exchange, ancillary-services participation, black-start capability, cold-weather readiness, and deactivation processes that directly affect hydro revenue and operational flexibility.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D Revision 71 (effective June 30, 2026) consolidates generator operational requirements across metering, data exchange, dispatch protocols, ancillary-services participation, black-start procurement, and resource deactivation. The redline updates sections on EMS data-exchange requirements (Section 4), ancillary-services marketing (Section 5.2), unit-commitment and scheduling (Sections 5.6–5.7), cold-weather resource readiness (Section 7.5 and Attachment N), and generator deactivation compensation (Section 9.2)—all of which set mandatory operational and reporting standards for conventional hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities operating in PJM. Specific attention to cold-weather preparation for conventional generators and new sections on mixed-technology facilities and behind-the-meter/microgrid business rules (Appendices A–C) signal evolving compliance expectations. Hydro owners must comply with all must-offer and dispatch rules outlined, and those seeking black-start contracts or deactivation compensation navigate the defined processes in Sections 10 and 9 respectively.

title: Item 06c – 1 Manual 14h Eit Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) is a new queue-reform pathway affecting interconnection and deliverability timelines for eligible generators ≥250 MW UCAP, including hydro and PSH projects that can meet 36-month COD and secure state siting support.
ai_summary: PJM has adopted a new Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) as a parallel process to the standard interconnection queue, effective July 31, 2026. Eligible generators must have ≥250 MW accredited capacity, state siting-authority support, and achievable commercial operation within 36 months; the process compresses review/study/negotiation to approximately 300 days total. This creates a strategic option for pumped-storage and large conventional hydro projects with confirmed state backing and shovel-ready status to bypass queue congestion and accelerate deliverability. Hydro developers should assess whether existing or planned projects meet the 250 MW threshold and can secure the requisite siting support; the process is now open for applications.

title: Item 06c – 2 Manual 14h Eit Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: PJM's new Expedited Interconnection Track affects hydro and PSH owners' ability to queue and connect generation to the grid, directly impacting interconnection timelines and deliverability — a strategic option for hydro fleet expansion and uprates.
ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) to FERC Docket ER26-1563-000, which establishes an Expedited Interconnection Track as a parallel process option alongside the standard interconnection queue. The changes add a new Section 16 describing the expedited process and update process diagrams and milestones. The revisions take effect July 31, 2026; the Markets and Reliability Committee votes July 28, 2026. Hydro and PSH owners in PJM can potentially access faster queue processing and interconnection timelines under the expedited track, reducing capital-deployment delays, though the summary does not detail eligibility criteria or specific timeline reductions.

ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H (Revision 06, effective July 23, 2026) is a redline update to the New Service Requests Cycle Process, the standard governing interconnection applications and studies for generation, transmission, and merchant facilities in PJM. The manual covers application, phased system-impact studies (Phase I–III), deposit and site-control requirements, interconnection agreements, and special pathways including surplus interconnection service and replacement generation. The extracted text provides the table of contents and structural overview; the substantive revisions and their specific impacts are not visible in this excerpt. Hydro owners and PSH developers in PJM should review the full redline to identify changes to queue processing timelines, deposit rules, study scope, site-control timing, or surplus interconnection eligibility — all of which affect project economics and timing for uprates and repowering.

title: Item 07 – 1 Storage As A Transmission Asset – Presentation
relevance_reason: Storage-as-Transmission-Asset (SATA) framework directly affects PSH and storage revenue recovery, operational dispatch rules, and market participation eligibility—establishing a non-market pathway for storage discharge that bypasses capacity and energy markets but triggers automatic discharge to solve transmission violations.
initiatives: Storage as a Transmission Asset [on-hold]; Storage as a Transmission Asset - Phase II [evaluation in progress]
stakeholders: Dave Anders (PJM); Jeffrey Schmitt (PJM); Lisa Drauschak (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Planning and Operating Committees have jointly approved a Storage As Transmission Asset (SATA) framework, now moving to Markets and Reliability Committee review (first read June 26, 2026, endorsement vote scheduled August 26, 2026) with FERC filing to follow. The design creates a distinct operational mode for energy storage—including pumped hydro—dedicated to mitigating RTEP-identified transmission violations: discharge occurs automatically when the violation occurs, charging is coordinated post-discharge, and the asset is settled under transmission cost-of-service (Schedule 12 Transmission Enhancement Credits) rather than energy or capacity markets. Critically, SATA resources cannot participate in PJM markets, earn no capacity value, and are excluded from the interconnection queue, effectively creating a bifurcated storage regime. Most exposed are pumped-storage owners considering or operating transmission-constrained locations; the framework could redirect PSH toward guaranteed cost-recovery transmission-relief roles at the expense of merchant energy and ancillary-service revenue streams. The mechanism locks storage owners into a single operational purpose (voltage/flow support) tied to planning-study assumptions, with tariff conforming language expected September–November 2026.

Gross CONE is the levelized cost benchmark for a new reference resource; Gross ACR is the proxy capacity value of existing resources. Changes to either reset the economic entry/exit threshold for new capacity and redraw existing-resource accreditation levels — directly affecting reserve-margin adequacy determinations and capacity revenue for all dispatchable and storage units. title: Item 01 1 – Gross CONE And Gross Acr Updates – Preliminary Update Of Default CONE Values
relevance_reason: CONE and ACR are the foundational inputs to PJM's Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC), which directly set offer-floor rules for all capacity resources including hydropower and pumped storage; this periodic review updates default values for the 2030/2031 delivery year that affect how hydro operators must bid and what the floor to market participation is.
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Pete Langbein (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is updating its default Gross Cost of New Entry (CONE) and Gross Avoidable Cost Rate (ACR) values, effective for the 2030/2031 capacity auction, informed by consultant analysis of capital and fixed O&M costs from EIA and prior Quadrennial Review data. These default values set the floor for existing hydro and storage offers under the Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) and for new-entrant MOPR bids; PJM will file final updated values with FERC by October 1, 2026, following an Advisory Vote at the MRC/MC in September 2026. The update applies the same methodology as prior cycles but includes preliminary CONE values for all resource types (e.g., Battery Energy Storage $502–$799/MW-day for 2026/27 and 2030/31); existing hydro assets will be affected to the extent their marginal avoidable costs are benchmarked against these updated ACR defaults when calculating unit-specific or default-option MSOC bids.

title: Item 01 2 – Gross CONE And Gross Acr Updates – Avoidable Cost Study Methodology
relevance_reason: PJM is updating Gross Avoidable Cost Rates (ACRs) used in the Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC), which directly affects how hydro resources' market-power mitigation default offers are calculated and whether unit-specific cost reviews are triggered — a core revenue-protection mechanism for dispatchable resources.
stakeholders: Dr. Andrew W. Thompson (Brattle Group); Dr. Sam Newell (Brattle Group); Joshua Junge (Sargent & Lundy)
ai_summary: PJM and Brattle/Sargent & Lundy are updating the Gross ACR study (last done in 2022) to take effect in delivery years beginning 2030/31. The 2022 study covered ~94% of the PJM fleet and established resource-type median costs (e.g., coal at $94/MW-day, natural gas CC at $113/MW-day, onshore wind at $147/MW-day) that feed into default Net ACRs, which are then applied as the MSOC threshold for supplier-side market power mitigation in the capacity market. The 2026 update will review whether current fleet characteristics have shifted the median cost profiles, solicit stakeholder feedback on whether to add a 4-hour BESS as a reference type, and present draft ranges and fleet analysis at the July 8, 2026 MIC meeting. Hydro resources are not explicitly listed as a reference type in either study; the mechanism is indirect — if the MSOC thresholds shift downward relative to hydro's actual avoidable costs, hydro owners will face more frequent unit-specific cost reviews to justify offers above the default, raising compliance burden and regulatory uncertainty around scarcity-related revenues.

title: Item 02 – Options – Packages Matrix – Storage As A Transmission Asset – Sata – Phase 2

This proposal would create a new participation pathway and potentially a new revenue stream for pumped-storage hydro by allowing storage facilities—including PSH—to be credited and compensated for deferring or replacing transmission infrastructure upgrades. title: Item 02 – Storage As A Transmission Asset – Sata – Phase 2 – Package Summary
relevance_reason: SATA establishes a new participation model for energy storage as a regulated transmission asset in PJM, fundamentally altering how storage resources (including pumped-storage hydro) can be deployed and valued — either enrolled in SATA (transmission-only, cost-of-service recovery, no market participation) or remain in competitive markets, creating a strategic fork for PSH owners.
initiatives: Storage as a Transmission Asset - Phase II [evaluation in progress]
stakeholders: David Mroz (PJM); Emanuel Bernabeu (PJM); Jeffrey Schmitt (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Operating Committee reviewed the Phase 2 framework for Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA), which allows energy storage (including pumped-storage hydro) to operate as regulated transmission facilities to address localized RTEP violations without participating in competitive markets. Under SATA Phase 2, eligible storage assets would discharge automatically to mitigate identified contingencies, charge via owner coordination afterward, and recover costs through transmission rates (Schedule 12 Transmission Enhancement Credits) rather than market revenue, with metering and telemetry matching standard energy-storage requirements. The framework explicitly prohibits SATA resources from queuing for interconnection or claiming capacity value, and expects assets to be small (≤10 MW) and short-duration (≤4 hours). The document advances to MRC review in July 2026, with FERC submission expected September–November 2026. This creates a new revenue pathway for pumped-storage owners willing to accept regulated-utility-like cost recovery in exchange for operational control and transmission-rate certainty, but forecloses market-participation optionality (energy, AS, capacity) for enrolled assets.

title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 13 governs PJM emergency operations procedures that directly affect hydro and other generating resources' obligations during system emergencies, capacity shortages, and transmission security events — particularly dispatch instructions, must-offer requirements, and emergency bid processes that touch hydro revenue and compliance.
stakeholders: Emmanuel Bernabeu (PJM); Mike Handlin (PJM); Paul Dajewski (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 (Emergency Operations), Revision 98, underwent annual review with substantive changes to emergency procedure taxonomy and triggering thresholds effective July 23, 2026. Key changes separate transmission-security emergencies from capacity emergencies, clarify that transmission-security procedures do not trigger Energy Emergency Alerts (EEAs) or Persistent Advisories (PAIs), and add a new Capacity Advisory to provide advance notice when forecasted capacity approaches operating requirements. A new Attachment O codifies procedures for emergency use of backup generators under DOE 202(c) Orders. The revision also updates Advanced Scheduled Resources language for cold/hot-weather alerts and adds RCIS notification to Pre-Emergency Load Management procedures. For hydro operators, the distinction between transmission- and capacity-triggered emergencies affects when they will receive dispatch instructions and emergency bid solicitations — transmission emergencies no longer trigger EEAs, which may narrow the scope of emergency-reserve procurement that traditionally favors hydro's rapid-response capability; the Capacity Advisory may provide earlier signaling of system stress, allowing better strategic positioning. Most affected are large, flexible generators and storage operators participating in PJM's emergency-bid and reserve markets. Revision endorsed by Operating Committee June 4, 2026, with final approval timeline shown through June 24, 2026 (MRC second read).

title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Establishes procedures for emergency dispatch of large loads (data centers) on backup generation during capacity/transmission emergencies, creating new operational constraints and dispatch prioritization rules that affect hydro's role in reserve provision and emergency response.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) introduces a new Emergency Use of Back-up Generators procedure allowing data centers and large loads to disconnect from the grid and operate on-site backup generation during capacity or transmission emergencies—after NERC EEA2 is declared and a DOE 202(c) Order is obtained. PJM will direct Transmission Owners to coordinate disconnection of large loads within 15 minutes, with capacity-deficient control zones exhausting all available large-load capacity before requesting disconnection in zones with excess capacity. This reshapes hydro's strategic value during emergencies: where previously all generation resources (including hydro's full output) would be called to meet contingency reserves, large-load disconnection now becomes an alternative resource, potentially reducing dispatch calls on conventional generators and changing the economic incentive structure for hydro emergency participation. Most exposed are reservoir and pumped-storage operators supplying contingency reserves in constrained zones; run-of-river hydro with fixed output faces less direct impact. The manual becomes effective immediately; no comment deadline is stated.

title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 13 covers emergency operations including capacity emergencies, weather/environmental procedures, and transmission security — obligations that hydro operators must comply with during system stress, plus strategic implications for hydro revenue during scarcity events (shortage pricing, load shedding, emergency procedures that affect dispatch and pricing).
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updates the operator emergency procedures manual with changes to capacity emergency classifications, weather alerts, transmission security procedures, and minimum generation requirements. Key changes include addition of a Capacity Advisory level to the emergency-level structure (Section 2.3), updated language for Advanced Schedule Resources during cold and hot weather alerts, clarification that transmission security procedures do not trigger PAIs or EEAs, and a new Attachment O on emergency use of backup generators. For hydro operators, these procedures determine obligations during system stress (e.g., must-offer and minimum generation requirements), affect the timing and magnitude of scarcity-pricing events and emergency bidding, and establish the dispatch scenarios in which hydro's flexibility is most valued or constrained. All hydro-owning utilities in PJM footprint must comply with these operating standards.

title: Item 04 – 1 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's revision to Manual 14d establishes binding reactive power control requirements for all generators, including hydroelectric units, affecting their compliance obligations and operational constraints on voltage support capability provision.
stakeholders: David Mroz (PJM); Ryan Nice (PJM); Emanuel Bernabeu (PJM Operating Committee)
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d Section 7.1.2 to clarify and strengthen reactive power (voltage support) control requirements for all generators in its footprint, driven by NERC's May 2026 lowering of IBR registration thresholds to 20 MVA and PJM's identification of increasing low-voltage risk (citing the 2025 Spain blackout). The revision mandates automatic voltage control mode as the default for all generators; defines volt-var control boundaries and reactive output ranges tied to MW output; specifies two new required reactive capability curve data points ('Maximum AVR capable MW Output' and 'Unit Minimum MW Output with Reactive Control'); and establishes a three-party investigative process (PJM, transmission owner, generator owner/operator) for reactive control performance issues. For hydroelectric owners, this tightens the operating envelope for reactive power supply: hydro units must now document and receive explicit approval for any AVR mode other than voltage control, and must define their reactive capability limits with new precision, constraining dispatch flexibility and potentially requiring retrofits or AVR reconfiguration on legacy units. The revision proceeds through SOS (June 15), MRC (July 1 and July 9), and OC endorsement (July 23), with education and Q&A sessions between June 4 and July 9. The compliance timeline is not yet stated in this first-read presentation.

title: Item 04 – 2 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: reactive power and voltage support obligations — PJM is clarifying VAR control requirements and AVR mode directives in Manual 14d, which directly affects how synchronous hydro and PSH units must operate their excitation systems and reactive-capability dispatch; this tightens an existing compliance obligation and may constrain flexibility in real-time volt-var/watt-var behavior.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d (Generator Operational Requirements), sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D, effective July 23, 2026, to add NERC Category 2 GO references, clarify allowable AVR (automatic voltage regulator) modes, specify acceptable volt-var and watt-var control ranges, and formalize PJM's monitoring and engagement authority over reactive-control behavior. The revision responds to NERC's May 2026 Category 2 GO initiative (expanding inverter-based resource reliability standards) and PJM's operational challenges from rising solar/IBR penetration and load growth outstripping generation, both elevating voltage risk. For synchronous hydro and PSH operators, this tightens the specification of reactive-capability dispatch and AVR operating ranges — reducing ambiguity about allowable volt-var curves but also narrowing operational flexibility in voltage control. Most affected are large reservoir hydro and PSH facilities with synchronous generators supplying reactive power in constrained voltage zones; smaller or run-of-river plants with limited reactive margin may face tighter conformance audits. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14D governs PJM generator operational and data-exchange requirements, metering standards, and dispatch procedures that directly affect hydro and storage operators' compliance obligations, participation pathways, and black-start procurement eligibility.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D (Revision 71, effective June 30, 2026) is a comprehensive operational and technical standard for all generating resources in PJM, including hydro, storage, and hybrid facilities. The redline reflects updates to control-center requirements, EMS data exchange and synchrophasor protocols, metering standards (with specific exhibits for energy-storage and hybrid resources), cold-weather resource preparation checklists for conventional and inverter-based units, black-start procurement processes, and behind-the-meter and microgrid business rules. Hydro and pumped-storage operators must comply with metering precision, reactive-capability testing and reporting (Attachments D–E), dispatch-instruction protocols, outage-scheduling, and cold-weather operational readiness (Attachment N) — and may be selected for black-start service under the revised incremental RFP and reliability backstop processes (Section 10). Mixed-technology facilities (solar–storage hybrids) now have dedicated marketing and metering rules (Section 13, Exhibit 7–8), setting a template that extends to other hybrid and co-located configurations. Revision 71 is already effective as of the meeting date, with no further comment period or vote indicated in the excerpt.
