ai_summary: PJM's Performance Compliance team reports on regulation-market operations as of May 2026, including a nomenclature change (Regulation Market Mileage Clearing Price, effective October 1, 2025) and an update to regulation requirements for summer 2026 (effective May 1). Hydro resources averaged 89% performance score in May 2026 with 967 MW qualified for regulation, compared to storage at 94% score and 230 MW qualified. This metric directly affects hydro's regulation-market revenue; operators should track the new mileage-based pricing framework and summer requirement levels to assess whether pricing or dispatch patterns have shifted under the post-October 2025 rules.

title: Item 09 – Synchronous Reserve Update
relevance_reason: Synchronous reserve performance and penalty administration directly affect ancillary-service revenue and compliance obligations for dispatchable resources, including hydropower units that carry synchronized-reserve assignments in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM's 2026 Q1 synchronized-reserve performance report shows 8 events (1 ≥10 minutes) with 48% average generation response to assignment and 66% demand-side response. March 1 event triggered $330K in penalties (first of the quarter), indicating tightened enforcement of reserve-response obligations. The report documents response-performance metrics by resource type (generation vs. demand-side resources) and region (RTO vs. MAD zone), establishing baseline compliance data that operators—particularly synchronous units like conventional hydro—must track for performance assessment and penalty exposure.

title: Item 10 – Reliability Compliance Update
relevance_reason: NERC large-load interconnection guidance and inverter-based resource ride-through standards directly affect how synchronous hydropower (and competing generation) must operate during grid disturbances, and large computational loads affect hydro's strategic positioning in a scarcity-prone grid.
ai_summary: NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert (due August 3, 2026) requiring registered entities to address risks from customer-initiated large load reductions and oscillations that threaten BPS reliability, alongside a Reliability Guideline recommending risk mitigation for emerging large loads. NERC also released implementation guidance for PRC-029-1 (inverter-based resource ride-through design), which sets standards hydro must comply with if required to operate in grid-support modes. The large-load alert is strategically relevant because rapid load swings create scarcity windows that reward flexible, synchronous resources like hydro; however, the ride-through standard applies primarily to inverter-based resources and does not directly change hydro's existing obligations. Hydro operators in PJM should monitor August 3 deadline compliance requirements and the June 5 MSPP Rules of Procedure webinar for any emerging standards that might extend ride-through or frequency-support mandates to synchronous units.

title: Item 11 – Mtopoc Update
relevance_reason: MTOPOC coordinates major transmission outages that constrain interregional flows and affect generator dispatch flexibility and deliverability — directly impacting hydro owner outage scheduling options and revenue dependability during periods when transmission is constrained.
stakeholders: David Mroz (PJM); Emanuel Bernabeu (PJM); Joe Rushing (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Multi-TO Project Outage Coordination (MTOPOC) process manages ~$21 billion in transmission upgrades and associated major outages (230 kV and above, 20+ days) across 2026–2027 to minimize system reliability risk and construction conflicts. Generator outage coordination is becoming critical as load growth and generation retirements outpace new capacity additions; PJM is encouraging generators to submit outages early with detail and to maintain scheduling flexibility, as transmission constraints and emergent system conditions are triggering increased outage adjustments and recalls. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators in PJM face tightening outage windows and must coordinate outage timing with major transmission work affecting their deliverability corridors (Peach Bottom nuclear, northern Dominion, North-South flows); generators should engage with Emanuel Bernabeu and Joe Rushing early in the outage-planning cycle.

title: Item 12 – Regulation Market Phase Ii Simulation Results
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: regulation market bifurcation (splitting single bidirectional product into separate RegUp and RegDown products) changes the offer, clearing, and settlement mechanics that hydro and PSH use to compete for and monetize regulation revenue in PJM, a major market where hydro operates.
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Phase 2 of its Regulation Market redesign, effective October 1, 2026 (pending FERC approval), splitting the single bidirectional regulation product into two separate RegUp and RegDown products, each with its own requirement, clearing price, and settlement. The simulation, using FESTIV software across representative weeks in all four seasons, tested area control error performance, controller saturation, and generator movement under the bifurcated design versus the current unified product. Key findings show separate regulation products improve ACE (Area Control Error) performance and reliability metrics, though summer conditions remain challenging; improvements stem from changing resource mix and generator movement behavior. The implication for hydro and PSH operators is that the new product structure will change how their regulation capacity awards are determined and priced — previously a single award covered both up and down service; under Phase 2, RegUp and RegDown awards and prices are independently cleared, potentially altering the revenue profile for units providing regulation. The document does not contain comment deadlines or further implementation milestones beyond the October 1, 2026 effective date.

title: Item 13 – Generation Operational Testing Update
relevance_reason: PJM's Generation Operational Testing program establishes must-offer compliance obligations and performance penalties that directly affect dispatchable resource operators, including hydroelectric generators committed to RPM or FRR plans, through failure charges and retest requirements tied to start-time and minimum-run-time parameters.
stakeholders: David Mroz (PJM); Emanuel Bernabeu (PJM); Roger Cao (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Performance Compliance team reported on the Generation Operational Testing program, which subjects all capacity resources (except variable resources) committed in RPM or FRR to unannounced real-time operational tests up to twice per season. Winter 2025 saw 54 total tests with a 40% failure rate (vs. 24% in summer 2024), driven mainly by delayed start times, mechanical issues, and telemetry errors; retests failed at 64% versus 36% for initial tests. PJM emphasized that generators face financial implications from failed retests and must proactively validate operational readiness and parameter accuracy (start-up time, minimum run time, notification delay) in Markets Gateway. Hydroelectric and pumped-storage operators committed as capacity resources in PJM fall squarely under this obligation; dispatchable hydro must maintain accurate start-time parameters and be capable of reliable real-time response or face test failure and associated penalties.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: The agenda addresses conforming changes to PJM Manual 11 and 18 covering energy and ancillary services market operations, capacity market design, and dual-fuel scheduling—mechanisms that directly affect how dispatchable and storage resources, including hydro, are scheduled, priced, and compensated in PJM.
initiatives: Dual Fuel Schedule Requirements [evaluation in progress]; Fuel Cost Policy Clarifications for Affiliated Fuel Suppliers [evaluation in progress]; Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Kevin Hatch (PJM); Pete Langbein (PJM); Phil D'Antonio (PJM); Scott Baker (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee will review and vote on conforming changes to Manual 11 (energy and ancillary services operations) regarding Advanced Scheduled Resources Phase 1, FERC Order ER26-846 on economic load response regulation, and dual-fuel schedule requirements; Manual 18 (capacity market) periodic review and conforming changes will receive a First Read for endorsement at the next meeting. The agenda also addresses a periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values (capacity-market cost parameters), Phase 2 of resource scheduling prior to the day-ahead market, and fuel-cost policy clarifications for affiliated fuel suppliers. These revisions touch how hydro and other dispatchable resources are scheduled, must-offer, and how their ancillary service regulation and capacity-accreditation parameters are set. Phase 1 and dual-fuel updates are on track for endorsement June 3, 2026; Phase 2 scheduling and fuel-cost policy remain open working items without stated completion dates.

title: Informational Only – PJM Esr Model Enhancements Position Paper
relevance_reason: PJM's proposed enhancements to energy storage resource market participation rules—particularly opportunity-cost offer methodology, state-of-charge management, and must-offer requirements—directly affect how pumped-storage hydro and battery storage compete for energy and ancillary-services revenue and how their dispatch is constrained by SOC accounting, setting precedent that will shape storage treatment across RTOs.
ai_summary: PJM staff proposes enhancements to its Energy Storage Resource Participation Model to accommodate rapid battery-storage growth (4,800 MW expected by 2030). The proposal addresses four issue areas: (1) a standardized opportunity-cost calculation for real-time energy offers capping cost at max(incremental energy cost, maximum DA LMP) up to $2,000, with emergency override to $2,000; (2) SOC accounting in day-ahead dispatch (rolling calculation to prevent infeasible dispatch) and ancillary-services clearing (recognizing SOC depletion from AS deployment), but explicit rejection of centralized SOC optimization; (3) no change to inter-day offer rules; and (4) complementary work via the Reserve Certainty Senior Task Force. A stakeholder process begins July 2026. The opportunity-cost cap directly affects how pumped storage (and batteries) offer into real-time markets—tying the cap to max DA LMP rather than real-time conditions or a multiplier makes cost-based offers more predictable but may suppress PSH discharge bids during high-priced hours when SO-C is low. SOC accounting prevents infeasible dispatch but stops short of RTO-led optimization, preserving operator discretion. The proposal affects both standalone storage and MTF configurations (including hydro-battery hybrids). Comments and next steps are embedded in a July 2026 stakeholder launch.

title: Item 01 1 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 11 revision 137 includes updates to hydropower operation scheduling rules (Section 9.3) and pumped-storage modeling (Attachment B) under FERC compliance, plus regulation-market redesign affecting how hydro supplies ancillary services; this touches both hydro obligations (scheduling/dispatch) and revenue mechanisms (regulation pricing and settlement).
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 revision 137 (effective July 1, 2026) incorporates three market-design updates relevant to hydro: (1) Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 updates (FERC ER26-0527) affecting resource scheduling prior to day-ahead, reflected in sections 2.3.1, 2.3.3, and 9.1; (2) Regulation Redesign Phase 1 (FERC ER24-1772) introducing revised regulation market clearing, pricing, and obligation rules in new Section 3 and Section 4.4; and (3) updates to hydropower operation procedures in Section 9.3 and pumped-storage modeling in Attachment B. Hydro operators face new scheduling windows and must-offer obligations under the Advance Scheduled Resources framework, while regulation revenue and mileage settlement mechanisms are restructured. Pumped-storage owners are directly affected by modeling changes and participation rules in the revised regulation market. The manual is now effective; no comment period is noted in this redline document, indicating implementation is underway.

title: Item 01 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's advance resource-scheduling rule for units with >24-hour start-up times locks cost-based offers and prevents price-based rebidding, affecting how dispatchable hydro and other resources can position themselves in energy markets and real-time rebidding windows.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Phil D'Antonio (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is implementing Manual 11 revisions (Phase 1) to formalize advance scheduling of resources with notification or start-up times exceeding 24 hours, triggered by lessons from Winter Storm Elliott. Resources pre-scheduled in advance of the Day-ahead Market on binding cost-based offers will have their offer prices locked and the 'Switch to Cost Schedule' flag set by PJM; they cannot update to price-based schedules for the locked days. Resources with start-up/notification <24 hours may update cost-based offers only through the Day-ahead bid period and during real-time rebidding windows. The rule prevents advance-scheduled units from exercising price-based flexibility once committed. Implementation is scheduled for June 2026, with MIC first read May 26 and second read/endorsement in June.

title: Item 01 3 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Manual 11 revisions clarify dispatch and cost-offer rules for resources called prior to the day-ahead market, affecting how conventional hydro and other dispatchable units are scheduled and compensated when operated outside normal market gates.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM approved conforming changes to Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) effective June 24, 2026, under FERC order ER26-527-000, to implement Phase 1 of its Resource Scheduling Prior to Day-Ahead Market initiative. The revisions clarify cost-capping rules for resources with start-up or notification times exceeding 24 hours when called in advance of the day-ahead market, specify how such resources are placed on cheapest-available cost-based offers, and address application of the "switch to cost" flag in day-ahead and real-time operations. For conventional hydro and dispatchable resources operated outside standard day-ahead/real-time windows, the changes affect how dispatch costs are calculated and offers are applied—particularly relevant to run-of-river or reservoir hydro units with long notification times that may be pre-scheduled for reliability or system needs, with voting completed June 3, 2026.

title: Item 02 1 – Manual 18 Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 18 Revision 63 governs the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM) capacity market, including accreditation rules, performance assessment penalties, and participation frameworks that directly affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage resources qualify for and are compensated in PJM's capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM has released Revision 63 of Manual 18 (effective September 1, 2026), a periodic review update to the RPM capacity market rulebook. The revision spans resource accreditation, performance testing, non-performance assessment, seasonal capacity resources, and DER participation—all mechanisms that establish how hydro and PSH assets earn capacity revenue and face compliance penalties. Key sections address Summer/Winter Capability Testing (Section 8.5), Non-Performance Assessment (Section 8.4A), and Seasonal Capacity Performance Resources (Section 4.10), which directly determine whether hydro meets PJM's qualifying-MW standards and penalty exposure. The document shows this is a formal Manual revision in effect as of the meeting date; no comment period or vote deadline is stated in the excerpt, but this redline indicates the revision has been approved and is now operative.

title: Item 02 2 – Manual 18 Periodic Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review includes conforming changes to PJM's capacity market accreditation rules (AUCAP factor penalties, VRR curve parameters, sell-offer requirements) that directly affect how hydro and storage resources are valued and must participate in PJM's RPM auction.
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Pete Langbein (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Manual 18 periodic review incorporates conforming changes to the Capacity Market Manual approved via several tariff filings, including a new penalty for derate caused by decrease in final Accredited AUCAP factor (ER25-2002-000), updates to Variable Resource Requirement curve plotting and parameters (ER26-455-000), and clarifications to must-offer exception processes and resource-specific sell-offer requirements. The document reflects housekeeping and compliance work, with a FERC order expected on June 5th on GDECS changes (ER26-980) that may trigger additional conforming edits; the updated manual is expected for MIC endorsement in July 2026. Most exposed are hydro and storage operators subject to AUCAP accreditation assessment and must-offer obligations; the new penalty structure for AUCAP derate could alter effective capacity value for peaking or seasonal hydro resources whose output factors change year-to-year.

title: Item 04 – Periodic Review Of Default Gross CONE And Gross Acr Values – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM's periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values directly affects the Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) calculations that apply to all resources, including hydropower and pumped storage; these values set the floor for offers and determine whether units face mitigation, which is the canonical market-power rule applied to use-limited resources like hydro.
stakeholders: Jason Shoemaker (PJM); Pete Langbein (PJM); Stefan Starkov (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is conducting its mandatory periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values for the 30/31 delivery year, with external consultants Brattle Group and Sargent & Lundy analyzing updates. These values drive MOPR calculations for new resources and ACR-based MSOC calculations for existing units; they directly determine the cost floors against which hydro offers are tested for mitigation. PJM will present detailed methodology and revisions to the Market Implementation Committee on June 16, 2026, seek first read at the MRC, conduct an Advisory Vote at MRC/MC in August, and file changes at FERC no later than October 1, 2026. All resource types—including any hydro or pumped-storage plants in the capacity market—are subject to these mitigation rules, making the updated values material to revenue defensibility and offer strategy for PJM members.

title: Item 06 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Work Plan
relevance_reason: PJM is developing a new market mechanism (resource scheduling prior to the day-ahead market) that would materially affect the timing and rules governing how dispatchable resources including hydropower submit offers and commitments, altering both revenue timing and operational flexibility.
initiatives: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market [evaluation in progress]
ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee is developing Phase 2 of a 'Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market' framework, with design work spanning mid-2025 through mid-2026. The work plan shows PJM will identify design components, document their relative importance, develop and narrow options, and construct and compare alternative packages for stakeholder approval around June 2026. The mechanism would reshape when and how hydro (and all dispatchable resources) lock in commitments relative to the day-ahead market, potentially affecting both the value of real-time flexibility and the cost of unit-commitment, particularly for run-of-river and reservoir resources that depend on hour-ahead optionality; the outcome will be critical to PJM-area hydro operators' ability to optimize dispatch timing against price signals. No vote date or public-comment window is yet specified in this work plan.

title: Agenda
relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION planning and interregional coordination affect hydro deliverability and may influence strategic options for export-dependent hydro resources; generator deactivation notifications and reliability analysis also touch fleet-adequacy decisions.
stakeholders: Emmanuele Bobbio (PJM); Jeffrey Goldberg (PJM); Josh Stephenson (PJM); Mojgan Hedayati (PJM); Nicolae Dumitriu (PJM); Niraj Kshatriya (PJM); Saravana Kumar Rajendran (PJM); Stanley Sliwa (PJM); Wenzheng Qiu (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee (TEAC) meets June 2, 2026, to review interregional planning updates, generator deactivation notifications, market-efficiency findings, and supplemental transmission projects from major utilities (UGI, FirstEnergy, AEP, PPL, Exelon, Dominion). The agenda includes reliability analysis and 2026 RTEP capacity-expansion scenarios for the 8-year forward case (2034). While no hydro-specific proposals are flagged, the interregional-planning update and reliability analysis may surface transmission or resource-adequacy constraints affecting hydro deliverability and seasonal energy supply, and generator-deactivation notifications provide early warning of fleet retirements that alter competitive scarcity and hydro value. Next meeting: July 7, 2026.

title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumption Update
relevance_reason: RTEP transmission planning assumptions directly shape interconnection queues, generator deliverability criteria (including hydro and pumped-storage EEFORds), and transmission-constrained revenue for existing and new hydro/PSH resources.
stakeholders: Eric Hsia (PJM); Joshua Stephenson (PJM); Emmanuele Bobbio (PJM Planning); Wenzheng Qiu (PJM Planning)
ai_summary: PJM posted its 2026 RTEP modeling assumptions for the Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee (June 2, 2026). Key hydro/PSH impacts: (1) Hydro generic EEFORd set at 8.15% and pumped storage at 5.57% — these forced-outage rates determine how much capacity PJM credits each technology in planning studies and interconnection queues. (2) Battery dispatch rules in base cases now specified (summer minimum charge at ENC × (1−EEFORd)/4, winter /8, light load charging mode), setting a precedent for how storage participates in transmission planning and potentially foreshadowing market participation models. (3) Offshore wind removals from 2031 and 2034 base cases and continued modeling of deactivating units create relief in transmission-constrained zones, directly affecting the scarcity-value of existing hydro and PSH deliverability. Timeline: competitive proposal window opens July 2026; Board approval expected October 2026–February 2027. Most exposed are PSH owners with interconnection in PJM's constrained MAAC/DOM zones, where EEFORd assumptions drive whether their resource clears in capacity/interconnection analysis.

title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumptions Feedback
relevance_reason: PJM's capacity expansion modeling methodology for RTEP transmission planning directly affects how hydro and storage resources are represented in future transmission needs assessments, which determines transmission investment priorities and interregional transfer capability available to hydro exporters.
stakeholders: Bryn Baker (CEBA); John Miller (CEBA); FirstEnergy (FirstEnergy); Emmanuele Bobbio (PJM Interconnection); Wenzheng Qiu (PJM Interconnection)
ai_summary: PJM is incorporating capacity expansion modeling into its 2026 RTEP cycle to project future generation buildout and inform backbone transmission investments through 2046. CEBA and FirstEnergy submitted feedback on April 20–22, 2026, regarding technical assumptions: CEBA supports the expansion modeling but urges PJM to model broader storage durations (6-, 8-, and 10-hour batteries and longer-duration systems), age-based retirement curves, and sensitivity analysis on natural gas prices and load growth. FirstEnergy requests clarification on whether the model will incorporate PJM's proposed "Connect and Manage" load flexibility framework, which would materially affect where new capacity is sited and thus transmission needs. The methodological choices—especially the treatment of storage technologies, retirement assumptions, and interconnection queue completeness—will shape which transmission corridors PJM prioritizes and whether interregional paths serving hydro exports are reinforced. PJM is scheduled to discuss 8-year modeling results at the May 8, 2026 TEAC meeting.

title: Informational Only – PJM Statement On Public Policies Within The 2026 RTEP
relevance_reason: PJM's 2026 RTEP capacity expansion modeling directly affects transmission investment and resource adequacy assumptions that touch hydro revenue (through load-growth scarcity signals and generation mix shifts), strategic options (resource adequacy constructs and storage targets), and planning obligations for all PJM-region generators including any hydro resources.
ai_summary: PJM issued its statement on public policies incorporated into the 2026 RTEP 8-year case (2034 horizon), detailing which state and federal policies shaped two main scenarios (A.1 and B.1) plus five sensitivities. Scenario B.1 models state RPS targets and battery storage targets, including policy-driven generator deactivations; Scenario A.1 does economic resource-stack optimization without specific RPS modeling. The capacity expansion model assumes 218.3 GW summer and 204.5 GW winter peak load in 2034, forcing addition of solar, wind, gas, battery storage, and nuclear uprates. Storage targets (modeled in Sensitivity A.3 and Scenario B.1) and clean energy policies via generator retirements (Illinois CEJA, New Jersey CO₂ Rule, Virginia Clean Economy Act) reshape the assumed generation fleet PJM uses for transmission needs assessment, altering the scarcity and reserve conditions hydro will operate in and the competing resources for RA accreditation. This is the planning foundation for 2026–2034 transmission and resource-adequacy decisions across PJM.

title: Item 01 – Interregional Planning Update
relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning directly affects hydro and PSH deliverability, revenue realization from interregional trade, and strategic positioning in seams between PJM and neighboring RTOs (MISO, NYISO/New England).
stakeholders: Eric Hsia (PJM); Jeffrey Goldberg (PJM); Joshua Stephenson (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee reviewed an interregional planning update covering EIPC initiatives and scheduled IPSAC (Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee) meetings with MISO (June 8 and August 17, 2026) and with New York & New England (TBD). Interregional transmission expansion and seams governance directly shape the economic value of hydro capacity at PJM boundaries — larger transfer capability into PJM increases price realization for exporting hydro (e.g., from New York) and improves market access; conversely, congestion on interregional paths limits arbitrage and reserve-sharing opportunities. Hydro owners with generation in MISO, NYISO, or adjacent regions should monitor these IPSAC meetings for proposals affecting interregional flow capability, cost allocation, and resource adequacy coordination.

title: Item 02 – Generation Deactivation Notification Update
relevance_reason: STRATEGIC OPTION: Generation deactivations and the RMR/DACC mechanism directly affect hydro fleet retirement decisions, resource adequacy, and transmission-driven constraints that can extend profitable plant life or trigger forced operations.
stakeholders: Eric Hsia (PJM); Joshua Stephenson (PJM); Saravana Kumar Rajendran (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM's Q1 2026 deactivation status report tracks seven energy-storage and thermal units requesting exit by April 1, 2027, with reliability analyses completed and no impacts identified for any unit. The report illustrates PJM's deactivation-review process under its tariff: when a proposed exit could adversely affect transmission-system reliability due to upgrade-completion timelines, PJM may request the owner to extend operations under either a Cost of Service Recovery Rate (CSRR) filing at FERC or acceptance of a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC). While this cohort contains storage units (Beech Ridge, Grand Ridge, Mantua Creek, Green Mountain, Cape May County) rather than conventional hydro, the DACC and CSRR mechanisms are available to all dispatchable resources—including pumped-storage facilities and some run-of-river hydro—facing similar retirement pressure and transmission constraints. Hydro owners should monitor how PJM applies these mechanisms when transmission upgrades lag and scarcity conditions develop; forced-operation agreements can materially extend revenue life but require tariff-compliant cost recovery.

title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM is adopting IEEE 2800 inverter-based resource interconnection standards that will establish ride-through, frequency response, and voltage support requirements applicable to synchronous hydropower and all new generation — creating new operating obligations and potentially raising compliance costs for hydro facilities during disturbances.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14H to adopt IEEE 2800-2022 requirements for inverter-based resources (Phase 1), including voltage and frequency ride-through, primary frequency response, and enter-service criteria, with application to VSC-HVDC transmission facilities and IBR interconnections. The revision adds Section 9.15 and new Attachment K, specifying mandatory ride-through times (e.g., 6 seconds at 0.70–0.90 p.u. voltage) and default frequency-response parameters (droop 5%, dead-band 0.06% × fnom). While Phase 1 targets IBRs, the standards establish precedent for synchronous resource requirements; PJM endorsement is scheduled for June 2, 2026, with MRC endorsement August 19, 2026. Hydro operators should monitor whether these ride-through and frequency-response mandates extend to conventional generators in Phase 2, as compliance during voltage/frequency disturbances will become mandatory, and deviation penalties may apply.

title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: IEEE 2800 adoption establishes operating standards (ride-through, frequency response) that synchronous hydro units must meet, directly affecting compliance obligations and potentially the technical feasibility and cost of hydro participation in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14H to adopt IEEE Standard 2800-2022 requirements for inverter-based resources, with Phase I covering general definitions, voltage and frequency ride-through, ride-through protection, and primary frequency response. The manual changes are effective July 23, 2026, pending Planning Committee approval (MRC first read June 24, 2026; MRC voting July 23, 2026). While IEEE 2800 is designed for inverter-based resources, PJM's alignment with industry standards may establish precedent for how synchronous resources—including conventional hydropower and pumped-storage hydro—are expected to meet similar technical standards in future compliance phases, affecting both capital requirements and operational dispatch.

title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual revision addresses interconnection queue procedures, surplus interconnection service, and generator requirements (including capacity vs. energy resource elections) — core obligations and strategic options for hydro interconnection and deliverability.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) Revision 05 becomes effective July 23, 2026. This manual governs the interconnection queue process for generation, transmission, and merchant transmission requests in PJM. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, the key provisions include: Section 9 sets requirements for generating facilities (power factor, capacity vs. energy resource status elections, behind-the-meter generation, inverter-based resource rules); Section 12 covers surplus interconnection service, the mechanism by which existing hydro plants can access previously-allocated but unused interconnection capacity for uprates or expansion; and Sections 1–5 establish the phased study and agreement process that determines project viability and cost allocation. The manual revision incorporates IEEE 2800 standards (synchronous inverter modeling), affecting how hydro and hybrid resources are studied. Hydro developers and PSH operators in PJM should review Section 9's resource-status elections and Section 12's surplus service process, which determine how much capacity value their projects can claim and how efficiently they move through the queue.

title: Item 03 – Market Efficiency Update
relevance_reason: Market Efficiency analysis drives transmission expansion planning that affects hydro deliverability and resource adequacy assumptions; reserve-requirement forecasting and generation-expansion modeling directly inform the capacity and energy price environment hydro faces.
stakeholders: Eric Hsia (PJM); Joshua Stephenson (PJM); Nicolae Dumitriu (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM posts its preliminary 2026/27 Market Efficiency base case in June 2026, with stakeholder feedback due August 31, 2026, and final case in January 2027; analysis will identify congestion drivers and proposed transmission solutions through September 2027, culminating in TEAC board approval by December 2027. The study models PJM's peak demand growing from 160.5 GW (2027) to 241.4 GW (2041) and uses preliminary ELCC class ratings to forecast generation adequacy; reserve requirements and topology are informed by the 2026 RTEP reliability window. Hydro operators should note that this cycle's load forecast, generation-expansion assumptions, and transmission topology directly shape which existing hydro assets are called to deliver energy and when locational scarcity pricing—and thus ancillary-service and energy margins—will emerge; the August 31, 2026 feedback window is the actionable juncture to flag hydro-specific accreditation or deliverability concerns in PJM's modeling.

title: Item 04 – 1 M20a M22 Periodic Review – Presentation
relevance_reason: PJM Manual 20A addresses ELCC Class Rating and Resource-Specific ELCC Rating—the core accreditation mechanism for capacity value and resource adequacy showings—which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited toward peak-load obligations and RA requirements.
ai_summary: PJM is conducting a periodic cover-to-cover review of Manual 20A (Resource Adequacy Analysis, Revision 3) and Manual 22 (Generator Resource Performance Indices, Revision 19), with administrative and formulaic updates. Manual 20A now presents ELCC Class Rating and Resource-Specific ELCC Rating as formulas rather than plain text, and adds definitions for Solved Simulated Annual Peak Load and Portfolio EUE. Manual 22 updates references and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor and effective dates for PRISM reliability calculations. First read scheduled June 2, 2026 (Planning Committee); second read and endorsement scheduled June 24, 2026 (Members Resource Committee). These revisions affect how PJM calculates capacity accreditation for all dispatchable and storage resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, which depend on accurate ELCC methodology to establish their contribution to system adequacy.

title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 20a Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: ELCC accreditation methodology for capacity resources directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are valued and compensated in PJM's capacity market; the periodic review updates to ELCC calculation formulas and DER conforming changes impact hydro revenue recognition.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A Revision 3 (effective May 20, 2026) updates the Resource Adequacy Analysis procedure governing ELCC class ratings and resource-specific ELCC ratings used to accredit capacity in PJM's Reliability Pricing Model (RPM). Key changes: two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE) are added, and ELCC calculations are now presented as mathematical formulas rather than text, plus DER conforming changes based on FERC Order 2222 are incorporated (as of Revision 2, December 17, 2025). Hydro and PSH operators rely on ELCC ratings to determine their capacity offering value; reformulated accreditation methodologies can shift which resource types (dispatchable vs. variable; duration-limited vs. unlimited) receive higher accreditation factors, directly altering revenue in PJM's annual capacity auction. The manual governs RTO-wide (0.1 days/year LOLE) and LDA-level (40% × Portfolio EUE) adequacy criteria that drive capacity procurement.

title: Item 04 – 4 Manual 22 Periodic Review – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 22 defines generator performance indices and accredited capacity factors (UCAP) used in PJM's resource adequacy and capacity market, which directly affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities are valued and compensated for capacity accreditation.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 22 (Revision 19, effective May 20, 2026) establishes the technical framework for calculating generator resource performance indices, including forced outage rates, availability factors, and accredited capacity (UCAP factors) used in planning and capacity-market studies. The revision updates references to Manual 21B and 20A (resource adequacy and capability rules) and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor to the Introduction. These metrics directly determine how much capacity credit hydro and pumped-storage units receive in PJM's capacity market and reserve-margin calculations — a core revenue stream for storage and seasonal-limited resources. The manual itself is procedural and defines no new policy, but changes to UCAP accreditation methodology, outage-rate data collection, or reliability-calculation tools (PRISM) flow through this manual and affect what capacity revenue hydro owners can claim.

title: Item 04 – Ugi Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Large customer load interconnection (500 MW in Newport Township) creates transmission expansion need that affects hydro's deliverability and strategic positioning in PJM; load growth directly increases scarcity value of existing hydro capacity.
ai_summary: PJM's UGI transmission zone is evaluating a supplemental transmission project to serve a 500 MW customer load in Newport Township, with phased in-service dates of Q3 2029 (250 MW) and Q1 2030 (250 MW). This is a Need Meeting phase of the M-3 process, meaning stakeholder comments on the project scope are due within 10 days of the June 2, 2026 meeting to inform the Solutions phase. The project demonstrates demand-driven transmission expansion; for hydro owners in PJM, new firm load interconnections reduce transmission congestion risk to existing generation and increase energy scarcity value during peak periods, though the specific route and configuration are not yet determined in this early-stage need meeting.

title: Item 05 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Presentation
relevance_reason: Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) establishes a new parallel queuing process for large generators (≥250 MW UCAP) with compressed timelines, affecting interconnection strategy and deliverability for pumped storage and conventional hydro projects seeking to compete in PJM resource-adequacy procurement.
ai_summary: PJM has filed a tariff amendment (FERC Docket ER26-1563) creating a temporary Expedited Interconnection Track for generators meeting three criteria: ≥250 MW accredited capacity, ability to achieve commercial operation within 36 months, and Primary Siting Authority support. EIT operates as a parallel track outside the standard interconnection queue cycle, compressing the process into ~300 days (Application Review ~180 days, Study Phase ~60 days, Final Agreement Negotiation ~60 days). Manual 14H is being revised to reflect EIT procedures; PJM's Board ruled on the process in June 2026, with FERC ruling anticipated July 31, 2026 and a requested effective date of July 31, 2026. For PSH and large run-of-river projects with construction-readiness and state support, EIT offers a faster path to interconnection and earlier capacity accreditation timing relative to standard queue, affecting project-timing and RA-portfolio strategy; however, EIT projects forfeit the one-time milestone-extension option available to standard-queue projects, tightening execution risk.

title: Item 05 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Executive Summary
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue reform and expedited interconnection track design directly affect hydro and PSH project deliverability timelines and strategic project-development options in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) to implement an Expedited Interconnection Track, pending FERC approval in Docket ER26-1563-000, effective July 31, 2026. The revision adds a new section 16 describing the expedited process and reorganizes sections to treat it as a parallel pathway alongside the standard interconnection queue. This creates a faster-track option for qualifying projects (specifics on eligibility criteria are not detailed in this executive summary), potentially enabling hydro and PSH developers to achieve COD sooner than the standard process — a strategic advantage in tight RA markets and for repositioning existing generation or pumped-storage capacity additions. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23, 2026.

title: Item 05 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 14h revisions establish interconnection queue procedures and surplus interconnection service rules—core obligations affecting hydro and PSH uprate, new-plant, and deliverability pathways in PJM.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 14h (Revision 06, effective July 23, 2026) governs New Service Requests Cycle Process for generation and transmission interconnections, including system impact study phases, agreement negotiation, deposits, and an expedited interconnection track (EIT). The manual covers generator classification, capacity vs. energy resource election, surplus interconnection service (including deactivation of existing facilities), and modifications at cycle decision points—all mechanics that determine how hydro and PSH plants queue, reserve interconnection capacity, and pursue uprates. This redline version shows the current procedural framework; hydro owners must track revisions to site-control requirements (Section 7), deposit timelines (Section 6), and surplus service deactivation rules (Section 12.3) as they affect project economics and timelines. Most exposed are PSH facilities seeking new queue slots or surplus capacity, and run-of-river plants pursuing uprates via the surplus interconnection pathway.

title: Item 07 – PPL Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large customer load interconnection (900 MW by 2031) in PPL transmission zone triggers transmission-expansion planning that affects hydro deliverability and interregional transfer capability; PJM's supplemental-project review process determines which transmission upgrades get built, reshaping the network through which hydro exports and dispatch instructions flow.
ai_summary: PPL Transmission Zone has received a customer request to serve a 900 MW facility (by 2031) from a 230 kV source near Harrisburg, PA, with in-service date of March 2029. This is classified as a supplemental need under PJM's M-3 transmission-planning process and was presented at the June 2, 2026 TEAC meeting. The document is in the need-meeting stage; stakeholders have 10 days from the meeting to submit comments before the process advances. Large load interconnections like this one drive transmission buildout decisions that alter the network's capability to deliver hydro energy from the Northwest and other hydro-rich regions to load centers; the specifics of how PJM engineers the solution (new circuits, reinforcement, or non-wires alternatives) will shape long-term transmission scarcity rents and locational price signals that hydro operators depend on for revenue.

title: Item 07 – Reliability Compliance Update – Presentation
relevance_reason: NERC's Level 3 Alert and Reliability Guidelines on large computational loads directly affect PJM synchronous and inverter-based resource dispatch, ride-through obligations, and the revenue/operational stability of dispatchable hydro and pumped storage relied upon to manage the second-scale oscillations and frequency disturbances these loads cause.
ai_summary: NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert (responses due August 3, 2026) requiring registered entities to implement seven immediate actions addressing risks posed by computational loads (data centers) causing customer-initiated large load reductions and significant oscillations within seconds — outpacing real-time response capability and threatening BPS reliability. PJM members must comply with this alert and the accompanying NERC Reliability Guideline on risk mitigation for emerging large loads. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, this creates new dispatch obligations (faster frequency response to load swings) and qualification/ride-through requirements under PRC-029-1 Inverter-Based Resource guidance; it also signals potential scarcity value for synchronous hydro's inertial response and for PSH's second-scale ramping to absorb load shocks — contingent on PJM's eventual tariff implementation of NERC's standards revisions (currently in informal comment period through June 17, 2026).

title: Item 11 – DOM Supplemental Projects
relevance_reason: Massive data-center load growth (3–6.8 GW by 2032) in Dominion's transmission zone directly affects hydro's strategic option to serve co-located or behind-the-meter load, and transmission constraints will shape scarcity pricing and deliverability of hydro exports from the region.
stakeholders: Dominion Energy (Dominion); Northern Virginia Electric Cooperative (NOVEC) (NOVEC)
ai_summary: Dominion Energy is presenting supplemental transmission projects to PJM's TEAC to accommodate extraordinary data-center load growth in northern Virginia (North Anna–Thornburg–Bristers corridor and Elmont–Fredericksburg corridor). The Bristers project will install 230 kV conductor to serve ~3 GW of requested data-center load with in-service Q4 2030 ($33.75 M, transmission-only cost). A larger multi-phase Ladysmith/Fredericksburg initiative ($450 M, phases 1 and 2 by 07/2029) targets 4,900–6,800 MW of new load by 2029–2032 via nine new substations in the Elmont–Fredericksburg corridor and ten in the Elmont–Chickahominy corridor. These transmission bottlenecks create windows of scarcity-driven pricing and make hydro's ability to reach and serve co-located or interruptible load behind data-center infrastructure increasingly valuable; owners should monitor whether Dominion's interconnection processes will allow direct-connect hydro or PSH to these load clusters.

title: Item 13 – 2026 Capacity Expansion For RTEP Feedback Assumptions Results
relevance_reason: Capacity expansion modeling for PJM's 2034 8-year planning horizon determines which resource types (solar, wind, battery, nuclear, gas, hydro uprates) are economically selected to meet resource adequacy, directly affecting the revenue competitiveness and dispatch value of existing hydro and potential uprate projects in PJM's footprint.
stakeholders: Emmanuele Bobbio (PJM); Mojgan Hedayati (PJM); Niraj Kshatriya (PJM); Wenzheng Qiu (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is using capacity expansion (CapEx) modeling to supplement generation assumptions for the 2034 planning horizon (8-year cases), selecting the most economic resources from interconnection queues (TC2, Cycle 1) to meet a 1-in-10 resource adequacy standard. The model employs ELCC curves that adjust dynamically based on resource-mix penetration—reflecting how hydro's capacity value changes with higher renewable penetration—and includes expansion candidates: solar, onshore/offshore wind, hybrid, combustion turbine, combined cycle, batteries (4–10 hour), and nuclear uprates. PJM will use Scenario B (policy-driven deactivations and Maryland nuclear commitments) as the baseline, with sensitivities testing gas siting restrictions, state storage targets, coal retirements, and gas cost impacts. Results will inform 5-year RTEP solution selection and may highlight development potential in northwestern PJM (ComEd zone). The approach aligns with FERC Order 1920 long-term regional transmission planning requirements and will evolve in successive RTEP cycles.

title: Item 14 – 2026 Capacity Expansion For RTEP Feedback Assumptions Results
relevance_reason: PJM's capacity expansion modeling for the 2034 planning horizon determines generation-mix assumptions that directly affect the transmission needs, resource adequacy targets (ELCC-based), and economic viability of hydro and storage resources competing for capacity value in PJM's footprint.
stakeholders: Emmanuele Bobbio (PJM); Mojgan Hedayati (PJM); Niraj Kshatriya (PJM); Wenzheng Qiu (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is conducting capacity-expansion (CapEx) modeling for its 2026 RTEP 8-year case (2034 horizon) to supplement generation assumptions when the system lacks sufficient generation to meet forecast demand (218 GW summer, 205 GW winter peak). The model will select economically optimal resources—solar, wind, hybrid, battery (4–10 hour), CC/CT, and nuclear uprates—from interconnection queues (TC2 and Cycle 1), using ELCC-based resource-adequacy constraints that automatically adjust as resource mix changes. Scenario A.1 (base case) does not build gas in regulated states (NJ, MD, DE, IL, eastern PA) unless projects have ISA/GIA or state agreement backing; Scenario B models policy-driven deactivations and nuclear targets. Results will inform 5-year RTEP solution right-sizing and state commitments, with power flows to be developed for Scenario B. The CapEx approach aligns with FERC Order 1920's long-term regional transmission planning and will evolve over successive cycles. This exercise directly sets the competitive position of hydro (and especially pumped-storage) in PJM's capacity adequacy calculations—any resource sited and given ELCC credit in the 2034 baseline affects scarcity-driven revenue signals and accreditation assumptions that cascade into 5-year capacity-market and real-time dispatch outcomes.

meeting_title: OC Special Session - Manual 13 Revisions Meeting
title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Presentation
relevance_reason: Manual 13 Revision 98 establishes emergency operating procedures affecting how hydropower and other dispatchable resources participate in PJM's capacity and transmission emergency protocols, plus introduces a new Capacity Advisory mechanism that signals resource scarcity — both touch hydro operators' obligations to respond to emergency directives and may alter revenue opportunities during stress periods.
stakeholders: Emmanuel Bernabeu (PJM); Mike Handlin (PJM); Paul Dajewski (PJM)
ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 13 (Emergency Operations) to clarify its roles as Reliability Coordinator, Balancing Authority, and Transmission Operator under NERC EOP-011. The revision introduces a new Capacity Advisory (Section 2.3) to provide advance notice when forecasted available capacity approaches the operating requirement — designed to address gaps in temperature-based emergency triggers and expected to increase in frequency as demand growth outpaces generation interconnection. Revision 98 also establishes Attachment O, a conditional procedure to mobilize backup generation at large data-center loads during extreme emergencies (triggered only if the DOE issues a Section 202(c) order); this procedure was activated during Winter Storm Fern (January 2026) but not implemented. The revision cleanly separates transmission-security emergency procedures from capacity-based procedures, removes capacity-related actions from transmission protocols, and updates language for Advanced Scheduled Resources in cold/hot weather alerts. Endorsement is sought before the summer operating period; the timeline shows second reads at the MRC on June 24, 2026. The Capacity Advisory may increase call frequency for emergency ramping from dispatchable hydro, while data-center load growth (now formally recognized in procedures) creates both scarcity signals that favor hydro dispatch and potential operational constraints if backup-gen mobilization competes for transmission or reserve capacity.

meeting_title: OC Special Session - Manual 13 Revisions Meeting
title: Item 02 – 2 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Redline
relevance_reason: Manual 13 governs PJM emergency operations including load-shedding procedures, transmission security emergencies, and resource-limitation reporting that directly affect how dispatchable hydro and pumped-storage resources must respond to system stress and what operating obligations they face.
ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updates emergency-operations procedures across capacity emergencies, weather/environmental events, and transmission security. Key changes include: restructured transmission-security emergency procedures that no longer trigger PAIs or EEAs; split of Maximum Generation Emergency into separate Transmission Security–Local Maximum Generation Emergency Alert and Load Management Alert; addition of Capacity Advisory to Section 2.3; updated Advanced Schedule Resources language for cold and hot weather alerts; and new Attachment O on emergency back-up generator use. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the procedure changes govern mandatory response protocols during transmission constraints (Section 5.2) and capacity shortages (Section 2), and clarify which emergency triggers obligate generation or discharge (e.g., load-shedding directives, transmission relief actions). The removal of PAI/EEA linkage to transmission-security procedures may reduce frequency of certain dispatch signals to storage resources during local constraints. Pumped-storage operators should review Section 2 (reserve and capacity procedures), Section 5.2 (transmission-security load and generation actions), and Attachments E–F (manual load-dump allocation) to confirm charging/discharging obligations under updated protocols.

title: Item 01 – 1 Rmr Resources – Tariff Revisions – Redline
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Tariff revisions to PJM's RMR (Retained for Reliability) resource capacity-market treatment—specifically, how retained resources clear auctions, accredit UCAP, and receive capacity-market revenues—directly affect hydro and other dispatchable units that PJM may retain to address transmission reliability, and establish the revenue and settlement rules governing their participation in the capacity market.
ai_summary: PJM staff have redlined tariff language (Attachment DD, section 5.3) governing Retained-for-Reliability (RMR) generation resources' participation in the capacity market (RPM auctions and accreditation). The proposal clarifies that RMR resources not clearing an auction on their own offer are deemed to submit a $0/MW-Day Sell Offer in the Base Residual Auction for their full Accredited UCAP (up to Capacity Interconnection Rights), provided they are deliverable, operationally available, and not subject to bilateral restrictions. Any accreditation increase post-auction is automatically offered in the Third Incremental Auction at $0/MW-Day; if accreditation decreases or the resource no longer meets RMR criteria, PJM procures replacement capacity. Capacity-market revenues from RMR units are credited to the load zones paying for the resource's continuing operations under its FERC-accepted rate schedule, rather than to the RMR unit operator; credits cease if the rate schedule expires, the unit deactivates, or catastrophic failure occurs. This provision extends through the 2029/2030 Delivery Year unless FERC accepts an extension. Retained dispatchable and storage resources relying on RMR rate schedules—including some legacy hydro units—will have their capacity revenues redirected to load-serving entities paying for reliability upgrades, fundamentally altering the revenue model for RMR-designated plants. The tariff change appears to be in draft form (redline status suggests it has not yet cleared stakeholder review or FERC).

title: Item 01 – 2 Brandon Shores 12 And Wagner 34 Deactivation And Rmr – Presentation
relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must Run) arrangement extending beyond planned deactivation date directly affects hydro and thermal generator retirement decisions and the transmission-investment timeline that determines when generating resources become dispatchable rather than must-run — a core strategic option for fleet planning.
ai_summary: PJM has assessed whether Brandon Shores Units 1&2 (635 MW + 638 MW, BGE-owned, Maryland) and Wagner Units 3&4 (305 MW + 397 MW, BGE-owned, Maryland) can safely deactivate on their current RMR end date of May 31, 2029. Planning studies found thermal and voltage violations would persist through 2030 even with baseline transmission projects in service, because several major 500 kV and 765 kV lines required for reliability (e.g., Morrisville–Vint Hill–Wishing Star, North Delta–High Ridge, Peach Bottom–Conastone rebuilds, Amos-Welton–Spring–Rocky Point, Joshua Falls–Yeat) do not reach in-service dates until mid-to-late 2030 or 2031. PJM's recommendation is to extend RMR beyond May 2029 through at least 2030 to maintain reliability until these transmission enhancements complete; next steps are RMR agreement negotiation and FERC filing. This determines whether 1,975 MW of generation will remain must-run (and thus unavailable for economic dispatch or retirement) well past currently assumed end dates — a precedent affecting all generators assessing retirement timing in constrained transmission zones.

title: 04_MORWG Generation Ambient Derates.pdf  [in MRAFT Meeting Materials 20260720.zip]
relevance_reason: Must-offer obligations and fleet-performance calculations directly affect hydro's compelled supply and the data-submission infrastructure through which availability derates flow into resource-adequacy compliance and dispatch requirements.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes adding an optional MUI/API parameter called 'Ambient Capacity Adjustment' to allow market participants to submit hourly ambient derates (such as temperature-dependent derate for thermal generators, but applicable to any resource with ambient sensitivity) rather than forcing them through the CROW system, which has technical volume limits. Currently, derates affecting must-offer calculations must be submitted in CROW at high frequency, risking dataflow congestion; the MUI already handles hourly resource data for Markets+, so SPP recommends shifting hourly/frequent ambient derates there while keeping longer-term forced outages in CROW. The proposal was presented to MORWG on May 27, 2026, and will be presented to MRATF on July 20, 2026, with protocol updates and an MIR (Markets+ Issue Resolution) to follow. For hydropower operators, this changes the technical pathway for submitting capacity adjustments driven by water temperature or other ambient factors that reduce available capacity within the same day or operating horizon — shifting to a more frequent submission path may reduce compliance friction but also clarifies the must-offer framework's application to use-limited resources.

title: 05_WRAP Program Split _ Market impacts.pptx  [in MRAFT Meeting Materials 20260720.zip]
relevance_reason: SPP Markets+ is restructuring its Operations Program and Forward Showing Program (WRAP), which directly affects how use-limited resources including hydro and pumped storage participate in resource-adequacy calculations, must-offer submissions, and market dispatch — a core revenue and obligation mechanism for Western hydro owners in the WRAP/Markets+ footprint.
ai_summary: Markets+ and WPP propose to split responsibilities for WRAP (Western Resource Adequacy Program): Markets+ will assume the Operations Program (real-time sharing and holdback optimization) starting October 2028 (Wave 2 go-live), while WPP retains the Forward Showing Program. The split requires governance changes to both programs and new tariff provisions; Markets+ will move holdback calculations and sharing-zone optimization into its market clearing engine, potentially expanding the resource pool to meet operational requirements and refining the fixed uncertainty calculation. WRAP binding-season participants not in Markets+ (including BPA, Idaho Power, Puget Sound Energy, Salt River Project, Seattle City Light, and others—many of them major hydro or hydro-hybrid operators) will participate under a limited-participation tariff model and must sign a Participation Agreement; governance will shift from the sunsetted MRATF to a new Markets+ Resource Access Working Group (MRAWG) effective Q3 2026. MPEC is expected to provide feedback or decision in August 2026, with MRAWG standing up in Q3 2026 and MRATF sunsetting Q1 2027.

title: 08_MSUF Slides_Must Offer Calc Changes_MRATF July 2026.potx  [in MRAFT Meeting Materials 20260720.zip]
relevance_reason: Must-offer compliance obligations and settlement reporting affect how hydro and storage operators are assessed for capacity contribution and charged/credited under Markets+ rules, directly impacting revenue and operational/financial compliance.
ai_summary: Markets+ is revising its Settlement Calculation Guide to allocate must-offer charges and credits from the Market Participant (MP) level down to Asset Owner (AO) level using load-ratio-share methodology. Currently, must-offer is assessed only at the MP level, leaving no AO-level settlement detail for invoicing, shadow-settlement audits, or determinant reporting. The proposed fix assigns MP-level must-offer amounts proportionally to each AO modeled under the MP for reporting and reconciliation purposes, while keeping financial responsibility at the MP. These changes (sections 2.6.17, 2.6.18, 2.7.26, 2.7.27 of the Calculation Guide) were reviewed at the June 2026 MSUF meeting and incorporated into the posted Guide without formal approval (no Tariff/Protocols changes required). Hydro and pumped-storage operators modeled as separate AOs under a single MP will now see their must-offer obligations and charges/credits broken out in settlement reports and invoices, improving visibility into compliance assessments and amounts due—critical for owners auditing weekly invoices and managing capacity-contribution risk.

title: 08_MSUF Slides_Must Offer Calc Changes_MRATF July 2026.potx  [in MRATF Meeting Materials 20260720.zip]
relevance_reason: OBLIGATION: Must-offer rules at the Market Participant level are a compliance requirement for all dispatchable resources, including hydropower; this doc clarifies how SPP Markets+ will calculate and allocate must-offer charges and credits across Asset Owners, directly affecting how hydro operators settle obligations.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is revising its Settlement Calculation Guide (version 1.3a) to add Asset Owner (AO) level calculations for must-offer charges and credits, allocating MP-level must-offer amounts to AOs by load ratio share. Currently the system calculates must-offer only at the Market Participant level, creating gaps in determinant reports, settlement statements, and invoice detail lines needed for shadow settlement and tie-outs. The change preserves the tariff requirement that assessment and financial responsibility remain at the MP level but enables AO-level reporting and audit trails. No formal tariff or protocol changes are required; the revision was reviewed at the June 2026 MSUF meeting and incorporated into the posted calculation guide.

title: 09_MIR8.2 Must Offer Considerations MRATF July 2026.pptx  [in MRATF Meeting Materials 20260720.zip]
relevance_reason: MIR8.2 co-located resources constraint directly affects must-offer obligations for pumped-storage and hybrid resources (especially storage paired with variable generation), altering compliance calculations and the economic dispatch of storage charging/discharging against injection limits.
ai_summary: Markets+ (SPP successor) is implementing MIR8.2, which adds a point-of-interconnection (POI) constraint for co-located resources (e.g., battery storage paired with solar, or DVER paired with MSR) registered separately but sharing the same injection point. The proposal modifies must-offer obligations in Section 4.2.1.1 of Markets+ Protocols to prevent double-counting when a market storage resource (MSR) self-commits to charge while constrained by the shared POI limit; it caps the resource contribution at the lesser of the sum of individual maximum limits or the POI constraint, and excludes self-committed charging MWs from must-offer when the POI constraint binds. This affects pumped-storage operators co-located with generation or demand, primarily by preventing artificial inflation of must-offer amounts during charging intervals and enabling more efficient co-optimization of dispatch between generation and storage within the shared constraint. The document presents four scenarios illustrating how net contribution is calculated across different combinations of VER output and MSR self-committed charging; status and next steps are not specified in the excerpt.

title: 03-MSWG IM↔M+ Update 20260710.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260710 (1).zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Intermarket coordination between SPP and Markets+ will directly affect real-time dispatch economics, locational price signals, and cross-seam revenue capture for SPP-based hydro and pumped storage competing across seams; RTDT capacity limits and IMO framework establish new rules governing when and how hydro can participate in profitable cross-seam transactions.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is implementing three sequential workstreams to enable cross-seam coordination: (1) Real-Time Dispatchable Transactions (RTDT) with a Capacity Recovery Limit capping interchange transactions to 15-minute physical recovery, approved Q2 2026 and going live October 2026; (2) Intermarket Optimization (IMO) via SIR788.1 for SPP ↔ Markets+ operator-initiated economic transfers, targeting functional design approval Q4 2026; (3) full Market-to-Market congestion coordination following Markets+ launch (October 2027). The RTDT capacity limit and IMO framework will reshape how hydro and PSH access cross-seam arbitrage and congestion-driven revenues — particularly for fast-responding resources — and will apply immediately to SPP-Markets+ seams. Implementation and external-BA coordination (including PSCo) begin in July 2026.

Resources exporting above their EcoMax or exceeding seasonal surplus caps (defined as Total QCC minus Forward Showing Obligation) are prorated down, and system sales must now be identified on eTags with source tied to a Resource or Resource Hub. PSE submitted comments on a recent WRAP transmission-requirement change. The changes preserve export priority and WRAP-honoring intent but clarify compliance pathways; hydro exporters — especially PSH and larger run-of-river operators in or adjacent to SPP selling into WRAP-coordinated regions — must track the August vote and verify registration/tagging procedures align with their export business model.

Pumped-storage and other multi-state reservoir hydro scheduling exports across BA boundaries under Dynamic Schedules are directly affected, particularly the distinction between what can remain dispatchable for market optimization versus what becomes non-dispatchable export, and the settlement basis (LMP at external interface) for self-scheduled exports. title: 05-MIR9.3-R SPP Comments-20260702.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]

ai_summary: SPP Markets+ Design Working Group is adopting a new registration and modeling framework for 'dynamically scheduled quasi-pseudo-ties'—external resources that are modeled as pseudo-tied into a participating BAA for Markets+ purposes while remaining dynamically scheduled (not formally tied) between Native BA and Attaining BA per NAESB standards. The framework adds a new resource designation to Section 9.1.1 and creates two pathways: formal electronic pseudo-ties (Section 9.1.9.1) and dynamically scheduled quasi-pseudo-ties (9.1.9.2–9.1.9.3). Under the dynamic pathway, the Market Participant must register the resource, designate a Meter Agent to submit settlement meter data, and obtain an attestation letter from the Attaining BA confirming it understands the resource will be modeled as a pseudo-tie in Markets+; the resource continues to be e-Tagged as a Dynamic Schedule between BAs. This clarification benefits external hydro plants and other resources in adjacent balancing authorities that wish to participate in Markets+ while preserving existing dynamic-schedule operations, reducing administrative ambiguity and enabling more consistent participation pathways. The framework is documented in Markets+ Protocols version 12.0a; this is SPP's comment form on the MIR with proposed edits, indicating the proposal is in design and comment phase.

ai_summary: SPP staff (Kelli Schermerhorn) submitted MIR 60-R on May 29, 2026 to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a framework enabling market participants to schedule and e-Tag interchange transactions that result from market clearing rather than pre-bilateral arrangements. The proposal revises Markets+ Protocols (glossary, definitions, offer submittal, day-ahead and real-time inputs) to define MPSP registration, e-Tag requirements, and optional hourly interchange limits per BAA, with MPSP Interchange Limits enforced in day-ahead, RUC, and real-time market runs. Approval is targeted for Q3 2026 and implementation for Q1 2027. For hydro and storage owners in the Markets+ footprint (SPP's multistate region), this establishes operational and commercial rules for how their export and import transactions are scheduled, tagged, and settled at market interfaces, affecting transaction flexibility and compliance obligations.

For hydro and storage operators, this creates a new pathway to participate in cross-footprint energy interchange without pre-scheduled bilateral agreements, potentially improving market access and revenue opportunities for exports during high-price periods — but also subjects those transactions to BA-imposed hourly and net-position limits that constrain flexibility. The document is a protocol revision submission to the Markets+ Design Working Group; next step and vote timeline are not stated in the excerpt.

ai_summary: SPP Markets+ (the successor RTO design for the SPP region) proposes to remove outdated Real-Time Capability language carried over from the legacy Integrated Marketplace that does not apply to Markets+ dispatch of DVERs in the Real-Time Balancing Market (RTBM). The change removes Real-Time Capability as a dispatch constraint for DVERs—which would otherwise cap Desired Dispatch output—but retains it as an ICCP (Inter-Control Center Communications Protocol) data point communicated to the Market Operator for use in variable-energy-resource forecasting. The document includes detailed registration and real-time data requirements for wind and solar resources (wind-speed, irradiance, turbine availability, Real-Time Capability in MW), establishing the data infrastructure for variable-resource integration. This is part of the ongoing Markets+ Implementation Request (MIR77-R) process; the summary does not state a comment deadline or vote date. The change most directly affects owners of run-of-river hydro or variable resources participating in Markets+; the removal of Real-Time Capability as a dispatch hard limit could allow higher output from variable generators when marginal costs warrant it, shifting the price signal available to inflexible or peaking hydro.

title: 08-MIR79-R MIR Process document-SRP Comments-20260526.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document establishes the Markets+ Revision Initiative Request (MIR) process — the governance procedure by which hydro and storage owners can submit proposals affecting Markets+ Tariff, Protocols, and systems. Understanding who can submit MIRs, what qualifies as a complete submission, routing to working groups, and approval pathways directly determines hydro's strategic option to initiate rule changes affecting revenue, obligations, and operational flexibility in the SPP Markets+ footprint.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ has adopted and clarified a Revision Initiative Request (MIR) Process (effective November 13, 2025; current version 2.0 as of May 12, 2026) governing how stakeholders submit and advance proposals to change the Markets+ Tariff, Protocols, and related documents. Qualified Entities—including any Markets+ Participant, SPP Staff, the Market Monitoring Unit, governmental authorities, and SPP Members—may submit MIRs via the RMS using a standardized Submission Form; incomplete submissions are rejected and resubmitted by deadline. Submissions are routed by Primary Policy Coordination staff to the appropriate Primary Working Group (MDWG for Tariff/Protocols/MIR Process itself) and Secondary Working Groups; comments must be filed within five business days of Primary WG scheduling and are posted within three days. The Primary Working Group (e.g., MDWG) approves or rejects changes, which then route through Secondary WGs, the Markets+ Participant Executive Committee (MPEC), and the Markets+ Independent Panel (MIP) for final approval; system-change MIRs receive impact analysis (scope, budget, timeline, staffing implications, alternatives) within 30 calendar days of Primary WG approval. This process is the formal gateway by which hydro and PSH owners can propose market-design changes—faster than new tariff proceedings but still multi-stage—and affects what rule changes are feasible to pursue in Markets+.

title: 09-MIR82-R MTSP OASIS Queue Pause presentation_MTWG.pptx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]

ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes revising its OASIS Queue Pause rules (MIR82-R) to allow individual Transmission Service Providers (MTSPs) flexibility in managing transmission service requests during the Day-Ahead Market window, rather than imposing a uniform mandatory pause. The revision shifts from a hard pause to a managed-pause model, requiring each MTSP to document its own queue-management procedures and coordinate with Market Operator timelines while preserving request prioritization. The change addresses NAESB WEQ 001-4.13 timing-compliance concerns SPP Legal identified as preventing a global waiver; it moves the choice of queue-management approach to individual MTSPs. The MDWG is scheduled to approve MIR82-R on July 8, 2026, with MPEC approval targeted for August 25, 2026. Hydro and storage interconnection applicants relying on queue advancement in the day-ahead timeframe should monitor which MTSP service territories are affected and what queue-pause conditions each MTSP implements under the new flexibility provision.

The revision aims to reduce administrative burden, enhance market–transmission coordination, and improve transparency by requiring MTSPs to document and publish their queue procedures. This affects hydro and storage developers' ability to move TSRs through interconnection queues smoothly and predictably; interconnection queue timing and coordination with market operations directly shape the cost and speed of new capacity coming online and existing-plant uprates, both strategic levers for fleet adequacy decisions.

title: 11-WRAP Program Split _ Market impacts.pptx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
