ai_summary: Markets+ and the Western Renewable Power Pool (WPP) are proposing to split operational responsibilities: starting October 2028 (Wave Two go-live), Markets+ will assume WRAP's operations-program functions (including sharing calculations, holdback optimization, and reserve-zone coordination), while WPP retains the forward-showing program. WRAP binding-season participants (including Bonneville Power Administration, Avista, Idaho Power, Puget Sound Energy, and others) who are not full Markets+ members would participate under a limited-tariff model and must sign participation agreements; governance changes and a new Markets+ WRAP-focused working group (MRAWG, standing up Q3 2026) will address tariff bifurcation, settlements, and coordination between the two programs. For non-Markets+ WRAP participants and full Markets+ members alike, the move integrates holdback inputs, must-offer ceilings, and sharing calculations into the M+ market-clearing engine, potentially refining uncertainty inputs and expanding the resource pool eligible to meet load via reserve zones. MPEC feedback/decision is scheduled for August 2026.

title: 12-MIR8.1-R MSR SOC Management draft language.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Modifies state-of-charge management rules for Market Storage Resources (MSRs)—pumped storage's core participation model in Markets+. Shifts from participant-forecast to market-operator calculation of SOC within Day-Ahead and RUC studies, directly affecting PSH dispatch feasibility, financial penalties for forecast mismatches, and capacity commitment reliability—a use-limited-resource obligation that hydro owners must track.
ai_summary: SPP and the MSR Subgroup propose enhanced MSR state-of-charge management by introducing time-coupled intervals in Day-Ahead Market and RUC studies, allowing the market operator to calculate SOC rather than relying solely on participant forecasts. Currently, market participants bear full responsibility for SOC prediction in their Resource Offers, creating risk that Day-Ahead commitments become infeasible in real-time and expose operators to financial penalties and capacity shortfalls. The proposal—submitted as draft tariff and protocol language—reduces the burden on MSR participants by enabling more accurate SOC tracking across study horizons. Pumped-storage operators in Markets+ should review whether this shifts SOC management burden away from them and how it interacts with their obligation to register loss-factor parameters and directional commitment limitations.

title: 13-MIR8.2-R Co-Located Resource Constraints draft language.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
relevance_reason: Co-located resource constraint mechanism directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and paired-resource portfolios are dispatched, offered into must-offer obligations, and accounted for at injection-constrained points — a novel market design rule that creates or removes revenue optionality for PSH and hybrid hydro installations.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes a new Co-Located Resources Constraint mechanism (MIR8.2-R) that formalizes 'clipping' — transferring MWs between co-located resources behind a single interconnection point when their combined capacity exceeds the injection limit. The rule applies to Day-Ahead Market, RUC, and Real-Time Balancing Market, with the market-clearing engine respecting whichever is lower: the sum of individual resource capacities or the declared co-located constraint. Critically, must-offer obligations for co-located resources use the same lesser-of logic, meaning a PSH facility paired with solar (for example) could see its must-offer requirement capped by the injection limit rather than its full capacity. This changes the dispatch flexibility and revenue recognition for PSH in portfolios with injection constraints, and improves market visibility into actual MW availability at the point of interconnection. The draft language touches tariff definitions, participation rules, must-offer requirements, and settlement guidance; status and comment deadline not stated in this excerpt.

ai_summary: Markets+ is proposing to define default electric pricing hubs for the Seasonal Hydroelectric Offer Curve (SHOC), which currently contains a 'TBD' placeholder in Appendix D, Section D.7.f of the Markets+ Protocols. SHOC's short-term component is indexed to peak day-ahead prices and on-peak monthly futures at a default hub; the hub choice determines the price signal hydro generators see when structuring seasonal offers. Candidate hubs include Mid-Columbia (Northwest) and Palo Verde (Arizona), selected by liquidity (≥2,000 MWh average daily volume or ≥5 transactions/counterparties) and location (footprint representation, regional fit). The MMU will submit an MIR for MDWG approval in August 2026, with MPEC approval targeted for August or November; hydro operators in Northwest and Southwest regions should monitor hub selection, as basis between their resource location and the default hub will shape the financial foundation of SHOC bids.

ai_summary: SPP staff (Dan Kheloussi) submitted MIR 36.1-R to clarify Markets+ Protocol sections 4.2.3.3 and 9.1.14, refining the validation criteria and operational mechanics for High Priority Export System Sales tied to LRE slice-of-system resources during WRAP Binding Seasons. The proposal designates exports as "High Priority" when they originate from an LRE's WRAP-reserved seasonal surplus (monthly positive difference between QCC capacity, contract adjustments, and forward-showing obligation) or are backed by WRAP forward showings or holdbacks; pro-rata reduction rules apply when aggregate High Priority e-tags exceed available seasonal surplus. This streamlines administrative handling of export bids and aligns LRE seasonal-surplus accounting with export authorization, reducing financial risk for market participants maximizing seasonal flexibility sales. title: 3. GHG Tracking and Reporting Backstop Emissions Report 20260701.pptx  [in MGHGTF Meeting Materials 20260701.zip]
relevance_reason: Markets+ GHG attribution and accounting rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage exports are valued and marketed in clean-energy programs and cross-RTO trade, particularly for Northwest hydro entering WEIM/EDAM footprints.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ GHG Task Force proposes replacing EPA eGRID and EIA fuel-type emissions factors with two superior backstop sources: GRETA (a real-time heat-rate curve model already used by MISO, SPP IM, and APS) and Open Grid Emissions (OGE, a peer-reviewed, generator-specific annual dataset). The proposal would establish a four-tier pecking order—Market Participant submission first, GRETA second, OGE third, and Market Operator calculation as fallback—shifting away from outdated annual averages to dynamic and sub-plant-specific emissions accounting. This matters to hydro owners because zero-carbon hydro and pumped-storage assets will receive more accurate (and favorable) emissions-factor treatment in clean-energy programs and multiregional GHG accounting; the use of real-time heat-rate modeling and open-source peer-reviewed data strengthens the defensibility of hydro's zero-carbon claim in export markets and capacity-procurement contexts where GHG attribution determines eligibility and value. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in the document.

title: 5. 2026-06-17_Revised Unspecified Emissions Factor Conclusions & Recommendation_DRAFT 20260701.docx  [in MGHGTF Meeting Materials 20260701.zip]

ai_summary: Markets+ GHG Task Force proposes adopting Washington Department of Ecology's unspecified emissions factor for pricing imports into GHG zones, rather than attempting to calculate a real-time marginal-emissions rate. The proposal addresses a foundational issue: if the unspecified EF is set too high, specified/internal resources are economically undercut; too low, it underrepresents emissions costs and can raise marginal energy prices. The task force recommends proceeding with an adopted static factor and establishing post-implementation monitoring, with open questions on how SPP distributes unspecified EF revenue to market participants and how unspecified imports are tracked and reported. This directly affects hydro exporters' energy revenue in the GHG zone and their ability to compete against unspecified imports when dispatch incorporates the emissions adder.

ai_summary: SPP's Markets+ GHG Task Force is developing a greenhouse-gas pricing and tracking framework for the SPP footprint, with ongoing work on tariff language, protocols for emissions reporting, treatment of specified-source (imported) energy, and accounting for storage and hybrid resources. For hydro owners, the mechanism is two-fold: (1) GHG-based pricing will create new scarcity signals and marginal-cost adders that alter dispatch merit order, affecting when hydro generation is economically selected; (2) the tracking and reporting requirements (particularly for imports, exports, and storage state-of-charge) will impose operational and compliance obligations on hydro operators. Pumped-storage resources are explicitly targeted by the HSMR definition revisions and multi-configuration resource (MCR) merit-order stacking proposals (Action Item 180), which will reshape how PSH offers energy and frequency support. ai_summary: Markets+ Markets and Operations working groups will discuss and vote on six Market Implementation Requests in June 2026, with August 2026 MPEC approval targets for most items. MIR8.1 (State of Charge Management) and MIR8.2 (Co-Located Resource Constraints) introduce new participation and operational frameworks for multi-hour storage resources—both drawn from MSR (multi-service resource) subgroup whitepapers and directly affecting how pumped storage and hybrid generators (including co-located hydro + solar) can operate and earn revenue. MIR9.2-R and MIR9.3-R expand dynamic-scheduling and pseudo-tie pathways, opening participation for neighboring resources and potentially enabling smaller or out-of-footprint hydro to access Markets+ balancing. MIR60-R and MIR81-R address real-time transaction settlement and dynamic multi-transaction-interchange pricing, altering how interchange flows (critical for Northwest-to-SPP hydro exports) settle and are valued. Comment deadlines range from June 18 to June 25, 2026; August MPEC vote is the approval milestone for most items.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 03a - May MORWG Meeting Minutes.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MIR9.1-R establishes rules for non-dispatchable physical resources (likely including run-of-river hydro and variable generation), and MIR78-R adjusts market-clearing rules for MCR transition states in Markets+—both touch hydro participation mechanics and obligations in SPP's evolving regional market.
stakeholders: Libby Kirby (Bonneville Power Administration); Peter Graf (Clark County PUD); Chris McDarment (Grant County PUD); Evan Sorrell (Puget Sound Energy); AJ Holley (SPP); Adam Cornelius (SPP); Caleb Mayden (SPP); Gentry Crowson (SPP); Kelli Schermerhorn (SPP); Sherri Maxey (SPP); Skylar Kincaid (SPP); Harley Johnson (Tacoma Power)
ai_summary: The Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group approved two market-initiative requests at its May 27, 2026 meeting. MIR9.1-R (Non-Dispatchable Physical Resources) creates a framework for resources whose owners are legally or contractually unable to submit financial offer information, allowing them to be modeled in the market without unavailable offer data—directly relevant to run-of-river hydro and other variable resources that cannot control output. MIR78-R (MCR Transition) removes a 30-minute transition-state eligibility constraint on reserve clearing that was ported from the Integrated Marketplace but does not apply in Markets+, simplifying multi-commitment-request behavior. Both motions passed unanimously; no comment deadlines or further action items were assigned in these minutes. The group also received education on MIR1 (Market System Outage BA Overrides) and MIR79-R (MIR Process terminology revision), with updates scheduled for June and July MORWG meetings.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 04-MIR75-R SPP Comments-20260520.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This MIR75-R proposal directly affects high-priority export scheduling and adjustment procedures for hydro and other dispatchable resources in SPP Markets+, touching revenue realization for exports and operational constraints on resource commitment and discharge.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ MIR75-R proposes clarified mechanics for adjusting high-priority export e-Tags when a resource's maximum economic limit or Variable Energy Resource forecast falls below committed export volumes. The proposal specifies that adjustments will be made pro-rata when multiple e-Tags share a single resource source, with a 1 MW minimum threshold and whole-MW rounding. Hydro and pumped-storage operators exporting power into or through SPP Markets+ will face automatic downward adjustment of export schedules when their available capacity (Max ECOL for conventional hydro, Maximum Discharge Limit for MSRs, VER forecast for wind/solar) cannot support the export commitment — a constraint that directly reduces realized export revenue and requires operators to manage export bids against real-time resource availability, especially during low-water or variable-generation conditions. The language resulted from an April 2026 MSWG/MDWG policy decision; this MIR specifies the operational algorithm. SRP supports the threshold addition.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 05-MIR9.2-R Dynamic Schedule Resources June 2026.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ Dynamic Schedule Resource rules establish how dispatchable hydro and other resources physically located in one balancing authority but serving load in another are accounted for in dispatch, power-balance settlement, and interbalancing-authority transfer accounting — directly affecting how hydro output is cleared, metered, and compensated across BA boundaries, particularly for multi-state/multi-BA hydro operators.
ai_summary: Markets+ proposes a new 'Dynamic Schedule Resource' participation type (MIR 9.2-R, June 2026) to solve a double-counting problem: when a dispatchable resource in one Balancing Authority (Native BA) is dynamically scheduled to serve load in another (Attaining BA), current rules treat its dispatch change in both the dynamic net scheduled interchange (NSI) and the market-to-interchange (MTI), forcing both BAs to regulate redundantly. The solution links a Dynamic Schedule Resource to a Dynamic Schedule Resource Interface; the resource remains dispatchable by Markets+ in real-time (RTBM), but only the interface value (not the resource's full output) is counted in the Attaining BA's power balance, preventing double-counting. The proposal defines five use cases: participating-to-participating BAs (resource-coupled or not), participating-to-non-participating (resource-coupled or not), and non-participating to participating. Most exposed are multi-BA hydro operators and pumped-storage facilities dynamically scheduled across BA lines; use-case 1a (participating-to-participating, resource-coupled) preserves dispatchability and shifts regulation responsibility via the interface, altering how dynamic hydro transfers compete in dispatch and how their locational and marginal-loss signals propagate across seams. Tariff and protocol sections are newly drafted; implementation timeline and stakeholder comment opportunity are not detailed in this presentation.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 05-MIR9.2-R SPP Comments-20260602.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic Schedule Resources clarifies how Markets+ will treat dispatchability and real-time ICCP management of hydro and other resources subject to physical energy transfers across BA boundaries—a revenue and compliance issue for hydro sellers using dynamic dispatch to serve load or other BAs, and for multi-state resource compliance with e-Tag and regulation-burden rules.
ai_summary: SPP staff proposes tariff and protocol revisions (April 2025/1.0a) to clarify registration, e-Tagging, and operational treatment of Dynamic Schedule Resources and Interfaces across Markets+ timeframes (DA, RUC, pre-RTBM, RTBM). The proposal distinguishes resource-coupled versus non-resource-coupled dynamic schedules and specifies when a resource may remain dispatchable under a dynamic schedule (internal transfers) versus when transactions must be non-dispatchable (certain exports). Key change: MSRs (multi-state resources, of which hydro is the canonical example) registered as Dynamic Schedule Resources can represent import or export depending on dispatch, but must coordinate separate e-Tags with the Native and Attaining BAs and accept ICCP-reported real-time quantities as fixed; Dynamic e-Tags referencing these resources are ignored by settlements, shifting compliance burden to the Market Participant. This reduces regulation burden on receiving BAs by eliminating pseudo-ties but locks the Dynamic Schedule Resource's output into the native BA's power balance and removes it from real-time economic dispatch in the attaining BA—a material constraint for hydro operators managing multi-BA export strategies. Status: tariff/protocol revisions have been proposed; next step and comment deadline are not stated in this excerpt.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 06-MIR9.3-R Dynamic Schedule Resource Pseudo-Ties June 2026.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: Markets+ is refining pseudo-tie registration rules for external resources, which directly affects how hydropower and other dispatchable generation located outside the Markets+ footprint can participate, be dispatched, and settle — a strategic option for NHA members operating in non-participating balancing areas seeking access to SPP Markets+.
ai_summary: Markets+ (SPP's combined market and reliability coordinator) is adopting a new registration pathway (MIR 9.3-R) that allows external resources physically located in non-participating balancing authorities to be modeled as pseudo-tied within Markets+ using dynamic schedules instead of formal NAESB pseudo-tie agreements. External resources registered under this new option will be dispatchable by Markets+, settled at their physical-location LMP, and participate identically to formally pseudo-tied resources, but the underlying dynamic schedule between native and attaining balancing authorities will remain in place (and ignored by the market system and settlements). The change preserves all formal pseudo-tie requirements and adds two new attributes: the market participant must designate the resource as a "dynamically scheduled pseudo-tie" in the registration workbook, and the participating attaining BA must provide an attestation letter confirming its understanding that the resource will be modeled as pseudo-tied. This creates operational flexibility for BA arrangements that cannot or prefer not to establish formal pseudo-ties, potentially broadening external-resource participation. The document shows protocol language updates to sections 9.1.1, 9.1.9, 9.1.9.1, and 9.1.9.2 but states no vote date or comment deadline.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 06-MIR9.3-R Dynamic Schedule Resource Pseudo-Ties submission.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: External resource pseudo-tie registration and modeling rules directly affect how hydropower resources outside the Markets+ footprint (including those in adjacent balancing authorities) can participate in the day-ahead and real-time energy market and settle, and clarifies the framework for pseudo-tied hydro to remain on dynamic schedules while competing in Markets+—a critical pathway for cross-BA hydro participation and revenue capture.
ai_summary: Markets+ (SPP's organized market launching 2027) is codifying registration and operational requirements for two forms of external resource pseudo-ties: formal pseudo-ties with binding BA agreements, and dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties where the resource is modeled as pseudo-tied into the attaining BAA in Markets+ commercial and network models while energy continues to flow as a dynamic schedule under NAESB/WECC standards between native and attaining BAs. The MIR (Markets+ Initiative Request 9.3-R) adds a new resource designation ('dynamically scheduled pseudo-tie') and defines meter-agent, attestation, and settlement responsibilities in Protocols Sections 9.1.1, 9.1.9, 9.1.9.1 (new), and 9.1.9.2 (new). SPP staff rates this as medium design, implementation, and operational complexity; MPEC approval is targeted Q3 2026 with Q1 2027 go-live. This standardizes a workaround already in Markets+ base design, reducing ad hoc handling and participation ambiguity for external hydro resources seeking to serve load within the footprint—directly relevant to northwest hydro exports and cross-border hydro from Mexican utilities or other external basins.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 07-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) establish a new framework for import/export transactions across the Markets+ boundary and internal transfers within the footprint, affecting how hydro and other resources can participate in interregional energy trading and settlement — a direct revenue vector for hydro owners seeking to export power or manage interchange scheduling within the expanding Markets+ footprint.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is implementing the Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) framework, which creates generic system points (MPSP) within each Markets+ Balancing Authority Area to facilitate scheduling and tagging of import/export transactions across the Markets+ boundary and internal market transfers. The protocol revisions establish registration, operational, and settlement requirements for MPSPs as sources and sinks for interchange transactions, with optional hourly limits managed by each BA in the Day-Ahead and Real-Time Balancing Markets. For hydro owners exporting from or importing into Markets+ BAAs, MITSPS enables participation in interchange transactions that are not pre-scheduled bilateral trades but are cleared by the market — expanding the scheduling and revenue-participation pathways available to hydroelectric resources. The MIR seeks MPEC approval in Q3 2026 with implementation targeted for Q1 2027, affecting how existing hydro plants can access interregional markets and how new capacity (especially in growing Markets+ footprint) will schedule transactions.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 08-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM submission.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: MTI/MOTI tagging and BAA Scheduling Constraints directly affect how SPP Markets+ dispatches interregional power flows in real-time; distinguishing Normal (DA-locked) from Dynamic (RTBM-only) transaction types changes the sequencing and flexibility of resource commitment and redispatch — a structural change to market dispatch that hydro operators use to optimize revenue from flexible ramping and interregional sales.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ (MIR 81-R) proposes to distinguish Normal and Dynamic MTI/MOTI transaction types, with Normal types locked after Pre-RTBM SCED and Dynamic types eligible only in RTBM. This allows BAAs to create separate BAA Scheduling Constraints on Dynamic paths without retroactively constraining the day-ahead and RUC commitment plan. The change is ranked High priority, with MPEC approval targeted Q4 2026 and implementation Q1 2027. Hydro operators in the SPP footprint benefit by avoiding artificial redispatch and binding constraints when BAs adjust transmission limits for reliability mid-day; it preserves the optimization value of DA and RUC schedules and lets BAs manage incremental transfer changes without forcing costly RTBM repricing and potential VRL violations. Most exposed are pumped-storage and flexible reservoir operators using MTI/MOTI paths for interregional arbitrage and capacity sales.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 08-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Modifies balancing-area scheduling constraints and market-transfer-interface (MTI) rules that affect real-time dispatch flexibility and locational price signals — hydro's revenue from ramping/flexibility procurement and locational scarcity pricing depends on how transmission constraints are enforced across market intervals.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes MIR81-R, which distinguishes Normal (day-ahead/RUC/pre-RTBM) and Dynamic (real-time only) MTI/MOTI transaction types to allow balancing authorities to set different scheduling constraints in RTBM than were used in DA commitment, preventing large NSI swings without undermining DA/RUC optimization. Normal MTIs become fixed quantities in real-time; Dynamic MTIs operate only in RTBM with nonbinding limits (defaulting to 99,999 MW unless constrained by BA-to-BA scheduling restrictions). The proposal updates tariff sections 4.2.7, 8.2, 8.2.1.1, 8.2.2.1, 8.2.6, and 9.4.1 to implement transaction-type registration and constraint-submission requirements via the MUI. This affects real-time dispatch optimization and price formation: by allowing separate constraints for incremental transfers, the scheme constrains BAA scheduling-constraint binding in RTBM while preserving DA-cleared transfers, potentially reducing real-time price separation and limiting the incremental scarcity premiums hydro can capture on flexible ramping in congested intervals. All market participants must submit Normal MTIs/MOTIs prior to DA market close and register at least one Dynamic MTI at resource registration.

committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
title: 09-10-MIR8.1 and MIR8.2 Update to MORWG June 2026.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
relevance_reason: MIR8.1 restructures how pumped-storage and battery resources manage state-of-charge forecasting across Markets+ unit-commitment studies, shifting from market-participant prediction to market-operator calculation of SOC trajectories — a material change to how storage revenue exposure and dispatch predictability are handled under Markets+ rules.
ai_summary: Markets+ MIR8.1 proposes to introduce time-coupled intervals in all unit-commitment studies (Day-Ahead Market, Day-Ahead RUC, Intra-Day RUC, and Short-Term RUC), allowing market operators to calculate state-of-charge for all intervals after the first, rather than requiring market participants to submit a SOC forecast for every interval. The change is optional: participants can elect either the current design (submitting SOC forecast for each interval) or the new coupled design (submitting SOC forecast only for the first interval, letting the market operator time-couple and calculate subsequent SOC values). The proposal targets a FERC filing timeline, with MPEC approval targeted for August 2026. For pumped-storage owners, this addresses a key exposure: misforecasting dispatch leads to infeasible SOC trajectories in real-time, triggering capacity shortfalls and financial penalties; time-coupling within studies mitigates that risk by ensuring the market solver respects physical charging/discharging constraints. The optionality preserves flexibility for hybrid resources and those charging/discharging outside the market (where the operator lacks full visibility). MORWG convened June 30, 2026; MPEC approval is scheduled August 25, 2026.

title: 00-MTWG Agenda 20260625.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: Markets+ transmission and interconnection queue governance directly affects hydro deliverability, strategic options for resource participation, and transmission planning that allocates interregional transfer capability — core to NHA members' market-seams choices and transmission-dependent revenue.
stakeholders: Caleb Mayden (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+); Carman Ruder (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+); Cullen Reid (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+); Garrott Granholm (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+); Gentry Crowson (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+); Joe Taylor (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+); Kelli Schermerhorn (Southwest Power Pool / Markets+)
ai_summary: The Markets+ Transmission Working Group (MTWG) agenda for June 25, 2026 covers four substantive items with direct relevance to hydro and storage participation in the emerging Markets+ construct: (1) MMU Transmission Market Power Terminology (MIR32-R), establishing how market-power mitigation applies to use-limited resources; (2) OASIS Queue Pause (MIR82-R), addressing interconnection queue management and deliverability gating; (3) MTI/MOTI Update (MIR81-R, Option 1A), on market-to-interchange coordination protocol affecting how hydro exports across seams; and (4) Dynamic Schedule Resources and Pseudo-Ties (MIR9.2-R and MIR9.3-R), governing how variable and storage resources integrate operationally into the interchange and day-ahead market. These topics touch transmission planning, seams governance, interconnection queue administration, and the operational rules under which hydro must participate — all foundational to NHA members' decisions on Markets+ membership versus EDAM/WEIM participation. No comment deadlines or votes are stated in this agenda.

title: 05-MIR82-R MTSP OASIS Queue Pause presentation_MTWG.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Interconnection and transmission-service request processing during Markets+ Day-Ahead Market operations — changes to queue-pause design affect the timing and feasibility of transmission-service submissions for hydro generators seeking operational flexibility and transmission rights, and indirectly touch deliverability during scarcity events.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is revising the OASIS Queue Pause design (MIR82-R) to allow individual Transmission Service Providers (MTSPs) to pause, limit, or manage transmission-service requests flexibly — rather than mandate a uniform pause — during the window between Day-Ahead Market close and Energy Schedule updates. The revision addresses NAESB WEQ 001-4.13 timing-compliance concerns raised by individual MTSPs and gives each MTSP discretion to document and implement queue-management procedures consistent with its operational requirements. MIR82-R was posted June 1, 2026, with comment deadline June 19, 2026; MTWG approval is scheduled June 25, 2026, followed by MDWG (July 8), MORWG (July 29), and MPEC (August 25, 2026). Hydro operators in SPP Markets+ should assess whether the proposed flexibility in queue management creates new opportunities or risks for intra-day transmission-service requests (e.g., for storage recharge or ramping adjustments) or whether MTSP-specific procedures may disadvantage certain resource types.

title: 05-MIR82-R MTSP OASIS Queue Pause submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: Interconnection queue management and transmission service provider operational procedures directly affect hydro project development timelines, queue priority, and market participation eligibility under Markets+ governance.
ai_summary: Markets+ MIR 82-R proposes replacing the mandatory MTSP queue-pause requirement with flexible queue-management discretion, allowing each Transmission Service Provider to pause, limit, or otherwise manage transmission service request processing between Day-Ahead Market close and schedule updates based on its own operational needs. The change enhances MTSP operational flexibility while requiring documented procedures and coordination with Market Operator timelines; it is ranked low priority with MPEC approval targeted Q3 2026 and implementation Q1 2027. For hydro developers and operators seeking transmission service interconnection in the Markets+ footprint, this shifts queue predictability from a uniform pause rule to provider-specific management protocols — potentially affecting interconnection timelines and the transparency of queue advancement between developers.

title: 05-MIR82-R Puget Sound Energy Comments-20260609.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Queue-processing rules directly affect transmission interconnection timelines and deliverability coordination for new or upgraded hydro resources, and the flexibility granted to individual transmission service providers may alter how hydro interconnection requests are evaluated and sequenced relative to day-ahead market operations.
ai_summary: Puget Sound Energy (Vincent Ching) comments on Markets+ Initiative Request MIR82-R, which proposes revising the MTSP OASIS Queue Pause language in the Markets+ Tariff and Protocols to permit individual transmission service providers (MTSPs) to flexibly manage transmission service request (TSR) processing rather than applying a universal pause requirement. Currently MTSPs must pause TSR processing between day-ahead market close and schedule updates; MIR82-R allows each MTSP to set its own queue-management conditions aligned with operational requirements, provided they document and publish procedures and coordinate with Market Operator timelines. PSE seeks clarification on protocol language defining 'queue processing,' pause conditions, and which TSR durations must be paused. The revision trades a predictable, universal pause schedule for operator-specific flexibility, which could affect hydro interconnection request timing and prioritization in a capacity-constrained Markets+ region.

title: 06-Stakeholder Training Update.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: SPP Markets+ is a strategic governance option for NHA members considering RTO membership and market participation in the Southwest Power Pool region; this training-readiness document illuminates which market-design concepts (must-offer obligations, GHG attribution, storage resource registration, transmission congestion, settlements) Markets+ participants must master, affecting entry barriers and operational complexity for hydro and PSH owners evaluating Markets+ membership.
ai_summary: SPP's Stakeholder Training team is rolling out a multi-level training curriculum to onboard Markets+ participants ahead of go-live. Level 1 covers market fundamentals (energy markets, roles, DAM/RTBM/RUC components, settlements, congestion rent); Level 1.5 includes podcasts and registration deep-dives (Market Storage Resources, pseudo-tied assets, JOU/CIR configurations, DER); Level 2 offers virtual instructor-led topical courses (Transmission, GHG, Must-Offer, Day-Ahead/RUC/RTBM, Market Monitoring, BA Considerations, Settlements, Congestion Rent) with three-time scheduling starting late 2026; Level 3 will be task-based "day-in-the-life" modules (Analyze, Design, Develop, Implement, Evaluate). RC Services training on outage management, congestion, and communications begins October 2026. For PSH and hydro operators evaluating Markets+ membership, the curriculum's emphasis on storage-resource registration, must-offer rules, transmission-constraint management, and GHG tracking signals the operational and compliance obligations they will assume; the Level 2 and 3 course design (and timing through late 2026) indicates Markets+ launch readiness is imminent and market participation will require significant trainer engagement.

title: 07-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic vs. Normal transaction-type rules for market-transfer interfaces (MTIs/MOTIs) affect interregional scheduling and dispatch flexibility that hydro and storage use to manage net-load volatility and capture arbitrage; BAA scheduling constraints on dynamic transfers create new operational and revenue risks for resources optimized by day-ahead and RUC runs.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ MIR 81-R proposes to distinguish Normal (eligible DAM through pre-RTBM, then fixed for RTBM) and Dynamic (RTBM-only) transaction types on MTIs/MOTIs, permitting separate BAA scheduling constraints on each type to prevent post-DA constraint changes from forcing redispatch and artificial binding constraints. The MIR identifies a financial risk: absent this change, dynamic BAA constraint tightening could override the DA/RUC commitment plan, triggering costly real-time redispatch and potential VRL exceedances. Status: submitted for normal-track review; MPEC approval targeted Q4 2026, implementation Q1 2027. SPP staff identified this as High-rank, Low complexity; no impact analysis deemed necessary because design is already accounted for in current work.

title: 07-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MIR81-R establishes a dual-transaction-type framework for Market Transfer Interfaces (MTIs) that affects how BAA scheduling constraints and transmission paths are managed across day-ahead, pre-real-time, and real-time markets—directly impacting transmission-constrained hydro's dispatch, transfer capability accreditation, and real-time pricing exposure.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes MIR81-R, which distinguishes 'Normal' MTIs (active in day-ahead, RUC, and pre-RTBM; then fixed in RTBM) from 'Dynamic' MTIs (active only in RTBM). A BA may register separate scheduling constraints for the same path using different transaction types, allowing it to restrict incremental real-time transfers independently of day-ahead commitment without disrupting the prior optimized dispatch plan. The mechanism allows BAs to limit swing in net scheduled interchange (NSI) between RTBM dispatch intervals while preserving the DA/RUC commitment. This affects transmission-constrained hydro by potentially reducing real-time flexibility access on congested paths and creating dynamic constraint shadow prices in RTBM that were previously zero or absorbed in DA/Pre-RTBM; hydro with limited upstream flexibility or PSH relying on real-time inter-BA transfers for arbitrage or reserve charging face higher exposure to binding Dynamic constraints. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the presentation.

title: 08-Draft_ M+ Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual SRP Comments submitted 05.21.2026.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This reference manual governs e-tagging and interchange scheduling within Markets+, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources schedule energy transfers across balancing authority boundaries, settle transactions, and participate in day-ahead and real-time market operations — touching revenue settlement, transmission-service obligations, and market participation pathways for hydro owners participating in or evaluating Markets+ membership.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ has issued draft version 0.1 of the Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual (April 16, 2026), a technical guidance document for market participants on e-tagging, scheduling roles, and transaction types (import, export, through, internal) for energy transfers crossing Markets+ balancing authority boundaries. The manual establishes roles for the Market Operator (SPP/MPLS), participating BAs, transmission service providers, and purchasing/selling entities, and specifies validation rules and settlement-location registration requirements. This is a reference document, not a binding tariff, but hydro owners evaluating or active in Markets+ must understand these scheduling mechanics to ensure compliance with interchange-tagging requirements and to optimize transaction settlement and revenue recognition across BA boundaries. The document was submitted for comment by SPP staff on May 21, 2026; next steps and comment deadlines are not stated in this excerpt.

title: 08-Draft_ M+ Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual_updated 20260625.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: Interchange scheduling rules, settlement location definitions, and transmission service treatment for Markets+ directly affect how hydro and PSH owners schedule energy exports/imports, settle transactions across balancing authority boundaries, and interface with transmission service providers—core revenue and operational mechanics.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ releases Version 0.2 of its Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual (June 8, 2026), a procedural guide for energy transfers crossing Markets+ participating balancing authority areas. The manual specifies e-tagging requirements, roles of market operators, transmission service providers (MTSPs), participating balancing authorities (M+ BAs), and purchasing/selling entities (PSEs) in scheduling interchange transactions (imports, exports, through-flows, and internal transfers). The Markets+ Market Operator (designated MPLS in NAESB registry) is SPP, not a transmission service provider; each MTSP and M+ BA has distinct approval authority over schedules on their facilities or within their balancing area. Hydro and PSH owners participating in Markets+ must register as market participants and PSEs, understand transmission-loss treatment and settlement-location mapping on tagged schedules, and comply with validation requirements imposed by both the MTSP and M+ BA on each path segment. The manual covers day-ahead and real-time market interchange, self-scheduled vs. economic schedules, and dynamic schedules; it is a reference document only and subordinate to the Markets+ Tariff and Protocols in case of conflict.

title: 08-Draft_ M+ Interchange Scheduling Reference-APS Comments submitted 05.29.2026.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document defines the e-tagging and interchange scheduling rules for Markets+ (SPP's expansion platform), which directly governs how hydropower and other resources moving energy across balancing authority boundaries will be registered, scheduled, and settled — a foundational obligation for any hydro owner participating in or exporting through the Markets+ footprint.
ai_summary: SPP has published a draft Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual (v0.1, dated April 16, 2026) establishing business rules for scheduling energy transfers that cross Markets+ Participating Balancing Authority boundaries. The manual defines roles for the Market Operator (SPP, designated as MPLS in NAESB), participating BAs, transmission service providers (MTSPs), and purchase/selling entities (PSEs), and specifies the e-tagging process, transmission service mechanics, validation checkpoints, and loss accounting for day-ahead and real-time market schedules under NAESB standards. This is a reference document (not official governing policy) intended to help market participants and tagging entities understand what system changes they must make to comply with Markets+ interchange scheduling. The document reflects Markets+ Tariff and Protocols but the formal next step (board adoption, tariff filing, comment deadline) is not stated in this excerpt. Hydro owners exporting from or transacting within the Markets+ footprint will need to understand these tagging, settlement-location, and transmission-service rules to participate; the treatment of transmission losses and interchange transaction types directly affects revenue settlement.

title: 10-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes the Markets+ System Points (MPSP) and Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) framework, which defines how hydropower and other resources can schedule interchange transactions (imports and exports) across the Markets+ boundary and within the footprint. This affects hydro operators' ability to execute bilateral and market-cleared energy transfers, their interchange transaction offer requirements, must-offer obligations treatment, and their access to generic scheduling points—central to how hydro monetizes peaking and load-following services across balancing authority areas in the SPP/Markets+ footprint.
ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is implementing a new Markets+ System Points (MPSP) and Interchange Transaction System Point Schedules (MITSPS) protocol framework, targeting Q1 2027 implementation with Q3 2026 MPEC approval. MPSPs are generic (not resource- or load-specific) scheduling points within Markets+ BAAs that facilitate exports from and imports to the Markets+ footprint, and also enable internal market transfers. MITSPS transactions (uncommitted import/export interchanges using an MPSP as source or sink) are excluded from net-position calculations and do not satisfy must-offer obligations—a key difference from bilateral transactions. The framework establishes hourly MPSP interchange limits (import, export, and net) set by each BA and enforced in Day-Ahead and Real-Time markets, plus e-Tag confirmation requirements between the MPSP and the Markets+ boundary. Hydro operators relying on interchange flexibility to manage run-of-river or PSH output will be directly affected by these new scheduling rules, limit-setting authority, and the clarification that MITSPS transactions are not must-offer-eligible. The revision is primarily a Markets+ Protocols change (multiple sections), classified as an Enhancement with low design and implementation complexity.

title: 11-MIR9.2-R Dynamic Schedule Resources June 2026.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
relevance_reason: Dynamic scheduling rules directly affect how hydro and other dispatchable resources physically sited in one balancing authority can be marketed and dispatched to serve load in another BA — a fundamental strategic option for multi-BA hydro portfolios and interregional export optimization, especially relevant to Northwest hydro supplying load across SPP Markets+ participant boundaries.
ai_summary: Markets+ is establishing tariff and protocol language (MIR 9.2-R) to create a new "Dynamic Schedule Resource" participation type for dispatchable resources physically located in one participating BA but dynamically scheduled to serve load in another BA. The proposal addresses a double-counting problem in current power-balance accounting: when a dynamically scheduled resource is dispatched to a new output level, both the MTI (market transmission interface) and the dynamic NSI (net scheduled interchange) reflect the change, forcing both BAs to regulate separately. MIR 9.2-R introduces a Dynamic Schedule Resource Interface tied to each such resource, so only the interface — not both the output and the NSI — enters the attaining BA's power balance, eliminating double regulation. The framework allows resource-coupled dynamic schedules (Use Case 1a: participating BA to participating BA with a specific dispatchable resource) to remain under Markets+ dispatch control while transferring regulation responsibility to the attaining BA, with e-tagging requirements and registration mandates for market participants. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the document; it appears to be a technical briefing of tariff and protocol changes already adopted or in final preparation.

Resources designated as 'dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties' would be dispatchable by Markets+, modeled within the attaining participating BA for power-balance and settlement purposes (LMP at physical location), and governed by the same obligations as formal pseudo-ties—but the underlying dynamic schedule between native and attaining BAs would be submitted but ignored by Markets+ systems. Key requirements include: resource-market-participant designation in the registration workbook, an attestation letter from the attaining BA confirming awareness of the pseudo-tie modeling, and a meter-agent export pseudo-tie submission matching resource output. This lowers the barrier to entry for external resources (including small and mid-sized hydro plants and pumped storage in adjacent non-participating BAAs) that operate under dynamic-schedule arrangements rather than formal transmission agreements, expanding the pool of dispatchable, settlable resources in Markets+ without requiring new transmission arrangements.

ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is codifying registration and tariff language for two forms of external-resource pseudo-ties: formal pseudo-tie agreements (where a Native BA and Attaining BA electronically tie a resource per NAESB standards) and dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties (where energy continues to be scheduled as a Dynamic Schedule between BAs but the resource is modeled as pseudo-tied into the Attaining BAA for Markets+ network and commercial models). The rule applies in Day-Ahead Market (adjustment posted at market close) and Real-Time Balancing Market (adjustment in Pre-RTBM SCED study). This clarifies policy intent from prior MSWG/MDWG decisions (April 16, 2026 meeting) by specifying the pro-rata allocation method — eliminating ambiguity that could have forced discretionary cuts. Most affected are conventional hydro and pumped-storage operators with multiple simultaneous export tags from a single resource, where the pro-rata rule now guarantees fair allocation rather than first-come-first-served exposure. ai_summary: SPP submitted a Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR75-R) specifying the operational mechanics for adjusting export e-Tags when a resource's maximum economic limit or (for pumped-storage and variable energy resources) available output falls below the MW amount specified on the e-Tag. This clarifies language from an April 2026 MSWG/MDWG policy decision and applies to high-priority exports (WRAP forward showings, WRAP holdbacks, and NERC Priority 6–7 transmission service from internal resources). The proposal distinguishes between conventional resources (limited by Max ECOL) and pumped-storage/hybrid resources (limited by Maximum Discharge Limit or VER forecast). Most exposed are pumped-storage operators exporting during tight resource conditions and run-of-river hydro operators whose unforecasted generation changes mid-day; the pro-rata settlement rule means their export revenue depends on how many other same-source e-Tags are active. For hydropower owners participating in Markets+—particularly those seeking to export renewable generation or manage interchange flexibility across the SPP-region boundary—this creates a new operational pathway that could alter revenue stacking (energy cleared in DAM/RTBM plus potential bilateral premiums) and strategic choices around scheduling vs. market participation; the mechanism is most relevant to larger hydro and PSH facilities managing outflow across seams into non-Markets+ balancing areas, since it enables market-cleared rather than pre-scheduled boundary transactions.

ai_summary: Markets+ is evaluating whether to accept e-Tags at the 'Valid' (pending approval) or 'Confirmed' (approved) stage for import/export/through interchange transactions. SPP and its vendor recommend 'Confirmed e-Tags' — only those with completed multi-party approvals — to avoid settlement discrepancies, divergence between day-ahead awards and real-time operations, and failed transactions affecting market assumptions. The 'Valid' option offers earlier visibility and participant flexibility but creates operational and settlement risk: ~10% of Western Interconnect e-Tags in May 2026 were ultimately denied, and using unapproved schedules in market solutions could force settlements on transactions that never become operational. The recommendation aligns with existing Integrated Marketplace practices and reduces vendor development costs. Markets+ protocol sections 4.2.2.7, 4.2.3.3, and 4.2.4 currently reference 'valid' e-Tags; this decision will either codify or revise that language. Discussion topics and a decision timeline (MSWG → MDWG → MPEC) are outlined but no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

ai_summary: Black Hills Energy presents a proposal to align Markets+ High Priority Export surplus calculations with Operations Program obligations, rather than keeping them static across the entire WRAP binding season. Currently, surplus capacity is calculated once per season against forward RA obligations but cannot be updated as operational conditions change, forcing high-priority exports to be classified as non-firm (uncommitted) even when actual operational capacity exists. The proposal would allow surplus recalculation on a near-term basis to reflect real availability, addressing a misalignment between WSPP Schedule C (firm-service language) and Markets+ treatment of uncommitted exports as economically dispatchable. This affects hydropower owners relying on firm bilateral export trades across the SPP-WEIM seam, particularly in constrained regions like Rocky Mountain; the change would restore liquidity of firm bilateral trades and planning certainty for load-serving entities. Status and next steps are not stated in this presentation.

title: 04) Proposed Changes Must Offer.potx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
relevance_reason: Must-offer is a core compliance obligation for dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage operators under Markets+ tariff; changes to settlement calculation and reporting directly affect how hydro owners are charged or credited for must-offer compliance and how those outcomes are tracked and billed.
ai_summary: Markets+ is revising how must-offer charges and credits are calculated and reported in settlement systems. Currently, must-offer assessment occurs at the Market Participant (MP) level only, creating gaps in Asset Owner (AO)-level determinants for shadow settlement and invoicing. The proposed resolution allocates MP-level must-offer charges proportionally by load ratio share across each AO modeled under an MP, preserving MP-level assessment (and financial responsibility) while enabling AO-level reporting. Changes affect sections 2.6.17, 2.6.18, 2.7.26, and 2.7.27 of the Markets+ Settlement Calculation Guide. A two-week public comment period runs through June 24, 2026, via the SPP RMS system; comments require no formal tariff vote if the guide changes stand alone. Most exposed are hydro operators managing multiple asset owners under a single market participant profile, since the new load-ratio-share allocation will determine how aggregate must-offer penalties or credits flow back to individual facilities for purposes of settlement reporting and invoice tie-out.

MIR77 addresses removal of inapplicable Integrated Marketplace language regarding dispatchable variable energy resource (DVER) deployment in the real-time balancing market, clarifying that Markets+ does not co-optimize for operating reserves (a responsibility left to balancing authorities). For hydro and pumped-storage operators, these rules affect how startup, shutdown, and GHG-related costs are treated in make-whole uplift payments — a key revenue recovery pathway when energy prices fall below operating costs. The mechanism is tightened cost disallowance for manual-status resources, which may constrain recovery for storage units or hydro plants operating outside economic dispatch.

ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is conducting preliminary calibration testing in July–August 2026 to validate meter-data submissions and settlement-area constructs ahead of data exchange testing and market trials. Market participants and meter agents in each settlement area must submit meter data via SPP's portal, participate in calibration exercises to identify and resolve unaccounted-for-energy issues, and agree to share meter data during settlement-area-specific troubleshooting meetings. Hydro and PSH operators will be required to submit accurate generator meter data and tie-line data (with correct sign convention for imports/exports) and to resolve calibration disputes at the settlement-area level; high unaccounted-for-energy balances will shift settlement MWhs via load-ratio-share distribution, creating financial impacts if meter data is inaccurate or submission deadlines are missed. No formal vote or comment deadline is stated; next steps include SPP identifying meter agents and market participants by settlement area, distributing spreadsheet templates and exercise intervals in July, and scheduling settlement-area meetings for August.
